56
Generalized anxiety disorder (GAD) has been conceptualized as the “basic” anxiety
disorder given its persistent course and the features it shares with other anxiety
disorders (Brown, Barlow, & Liebowitz, 1994). Accordingly, understanding the etiol-
ogy and maintenance of GAD may have implications for understanding all anxiety
disorders (Roemer, Orsillo, & Barlow, 2002).
TYPICAL SYMPTOMS AND ASSOCIATED FEATURES
Worry, a central feature of GAD, has been defined as “a chain of thoughts and
images, negatively affect-laden and relatively uncontrollable . . . an attempt to engage
in mental problem-solving on an issue whose outcome is uncertain but contains the
possibility of one or more negative outcomes . . . [and] relate[d] closely to the fear
process” (Borkovec, Robinson, Pruzinsky, & DePree, 1983, p. 10). The definition was
later extended to include anxious apprehension for future negative events (Borkovec,
Ray, & Stoeber, 1998). Although worry is a common phenomenon, it can become a
persistent and disabling source of emotional discomfort in GAD. Persons with GAD
worry not only about major life issues (e.g., health, finances, relationships) but also
minor issues that others would not perceive as threatening (e.g., household chores).
Pathological worry often generates additional distress due to its catastrophic thought
content, and it deviates from normal worry due to its pervasiveness (e.g., Niles, Leb-
eau, Liao, Glenn, & Craske, 2012) and uncontrollability (e.g., Hallion & Ruscio, 2013).
C H A P T E R 3
Generalized Anxiety Disorder
Michelle G. Newman
Paul F. Crits‑Christoph
Lauren E. Szkodny
Gavin N. Rackoff
Jeremy T. Schwob

-- 77 of 498 --

Generalized Anxiety Disorder 	57
Cognitive and physiological features of GAD elucidate the nature and func-
tion of worry. Worry is predominated by negative verbal thought activity, such that
when people worry, they talk to themselves about negative events that may occur in
the future (Borkovec et al., 1998). Because of its threat-related content, worry gener-
ates increases in subjective distress and sympathetic nervous system activity, while
also reducing parasympathetic nervous system activity (e.g., Llera & Newman, 2010,
2014; Steinfurth, Alius, Wendt, & Hamm, 2017). By generating distress and somatic
activation, worry reduces the likelihood of further reactivity associated with a fear-
ful event (e.g., Llera & Newman, 2010, 2014). Thus, worry may account for the auto-
nomic inflexibility characteristic of GAD (e.g., Hoehn-Saric, McLeod, Funderburk, &
Kowalski, 2004) and serve as a strategy to avoid emotional processing of unexpected
negative events (Newman & Llera, 2011). Accordingly, persons with GAD report that
worry creates sustained levels of negative emotion, while also helping them avoid
sudden increases in negative emotion and prepare for the worst (Borkovec & Roemer,
1995; Llera & Newman, 2017).
GAD and worry impact information processing in ways that interfere with
learning from experience and contribute to the maintenance of anxious meanings
attributed to various stimuli. People with GAD demonstrate a bias for attending to
threat cues (Goodwin, Yiend, & Hirsch, 2017) and hold exaggerated beliefs about the
probability of negative events (Butler & Mathews, 1983). However, given that there
is no actual danger, as it only exists in their future-oriented thought, the threat can-
not be physically avoided. As long as danger is perceived, individuals with GAD
continue to search for ways to avoid it or prepare themselves to cope with it. Thus,
worry is a mental response to perceived threat when no behavioral solution exists.
It primarily functions as a maladaptive coping behavior, a means to prepare for the
worst and avoid large shifts in negative emotions (Borkovec & Roemer, 1995; New-
man & Llera, 2011).
Cognitive biases in GAD also extend to memory for positively and negatively
valenced information. For example, compared to persons without anxiety disor-
ders, persons with GAD have a bias toward greater memory for threat-related words
(Coles, Turk, & Heimberg, 2007). Persons with GAD also show deficiencies in prob-
abilistic learning, with a particular tendency to underestimate the probabilities of
positive outcomes (LaFreniere & Newman, 2019). Memory biases and probabilistic
learning deficits may account for the persistent apprehensive expectations charac-
teristic of GAD. For example, when individuals with GAD were asked to track the
outcomes of their worries, 91% of worries did not come true, and 30% of worries
that did come true turned out better than expected (LaFreniere & Newman, 2020).
Furthermore, recording a greater number of untrue worries predicted a reduction in
worry over time (LaFreniere & Newman, 2020), suggesting that memory for positive
and negative information may be involved in the maintenance of GAD.
GAD is also associated with significant distress, disability, and impairment in
quality of life. The most detailed information on disability and quality-of-life effects
of GAD comes from the Epidemiologic Catchment Area (ECA) community survey
(Robins & Regier, 1991), one of the largest investigations of the prevalence of men-
tal disorders, and from a multisite survey of psychiatric outpatients (Massion, War-
shaw, & Keller, 1993). The overall picture for GAD is one of significant psychosocial
impairment, with 38% of ECA subjects and 71% of outpatients characterizing their

-- 78 of 498 --

58 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
emotional health as only fair to poor; 27 and 25%, respectively, were receiving dis-
ability payments, and only about 50% were working full-time. Of those who were
working, 38% had missed at least 1 week of work in the past year due to their anxiety
(Massion et al., 1993). In the ECA, GAD was associated with a threefold greater likeli-
hood of working at a low occupational level, and a more than a twofold likelihood
of earning less than $10,000 per year (Robins & Regier, 1991). Studies suggest that
role and quality of life impairment in those with pure GAD (without comorbidity) is
comparable to pure major depressive disorder (MDD; Hoffman, Dukes, & Wittchen,
2008; Wittchen, Carter, Pfister, Montgomery, & Kessler, 2000). Moreover, high rates of
suicidality can be found in persons with GAD. In one longitudinal epidemiological
study in the United States, young adults (ages 16–25 years) diagnosed with GAD had
a sixfold increased likelihood of suicidal ideation and a more than twofold greater
likelihood of attempted suicide, after researchers controlled for other mental disor-
ders and stressful life events (Boden, Fergusson, & Horwood, 2007).
GAD also has consequences for physical health, with 35% of individuals with
GAD in the ECA sample and 23% of those in the Massion and colleagues (1993)
sample rating their physical health as only fair to poor. GAD is associated with a
number of comorbid medical problems such as asthma (Goodwin, Jacobi, & The-
feld, 2003), chronic pain conditions (Von Korff et al., 2005), irritable bowel syndrome
(Gros, Antony, McCabe, & Swinson, 2009), and insomnia (Brenes et al., 2009). Fur-
thermore, GAD is a risk factor for cardiovascular disease independent of depression
(e.g., Batelaan, ten Have, van Balkom, Tuithof, & de Graaf, 2014; Martens et al., 2010).
Persons with GAD are among the heaviest users of primary care, specialty clinic,
and emergency room resources, contributing substantially to the nonpsychiatric cost
associated with anxiety disorders in the United States (e.g., Fogarty, Sharma, Chetty,
& Culpepper, 2008; Jones, Ames, Jeffries, Scarinci, & Brantley, 2001). Even individuals
who do not meet the full criteria for GAD but have a subthreshold level of symptoms
experience significant impairment, as well as greater health care use compared to
individuals without GAD symptoms (Haller, Cramer, Lauche, Gass, & Dobos, 2014).
Finally, GAD has been associated with interpersonal dysfunction (Crits-
Christoph, Gibbons, Narducci, Schamberger, & Gallop, 2005; Newman & Erickson,
2010; Przeworski et al., 2011). Individuals with GAD tend to report a predominance
of worrisome thoughts related to relationship concerns (Breitholtz, Johansson, & Öst,
1999; Roemer, Molina, & Borkovec, 1997). They also exhibit a tendency to perceive
social threats in their environment (Goodwin et al., 2017). In a like manner, GAD is
more associated with marital discord or dissatisfaction than other anxiety or mood
disorders (Whisman, 1999). Compared to those without GAD, individuals with GAD
are more likely to have enmeshed relationships or to engage in role reversal, such as a
child assuming parental responsibility (Cassidy, 1995; Cassidy, Lichtenstein-Phelps,
Sibrava, Thomas, & Borkovec, 2009). People with GAD have disrupted perceptions
of their negative interpersonal impact on others (Erickson & Newman, 2007), which
can impact their relationships and maintain interpersonal dysfunction.
Treatment Implications
Individuals with GAD anxiously anticipate and engage in mental attempts to
problem-solve and prepare for possible negative events. They frequently detect and
interpret threat in their environment, even when no threat exists (Goodwin et al.,

-- 79 of 498 --

Generalized Anxiety Disorder 	59
2017). Their mental experience is dominated by worry, which is elicited by internal or
external cues that signal real or imagined danger. Individuals with GAD use worry
to figure out how to prevent bad things from happening or to prepare for the worst
(Borkovec & Roemer, 1995). They spend a significant amount of time preoccupied
with their worrisome thoughts and therefore tend to describe themselves as having
been worriers all their lives. Chronic worry becomes a way of life to the point that
these individuals may not realize there are treatments available to them. Since many
people with GAD might not even seek treatment, dissemination of information about
the helpfulness and availability of psychotherapy for GAD is an important first step.
Individuals with GAD may be so preoccupied with worries that they neglect the
world around them. Generally anticipating the worst, they tend to absorb informa-
tion in accordance with their negative expectations. They do not utilize informa-
tion available to them via their emotional experience, which can adaptively motivate
them, guide their behavior, or facilitate learning and subsequent growth (Borkovec,
2002). In their efforts to focus on imagined threat, they are distracted from the pres-
ent, including any information that may be useful and/or make life less stressful.
Despite its negative impact, GAD is unique compared to other anxiety disorders, in
that worry is inherently reinforcing. Some individuals with GAD hold the super-
stitious belief that their worry can actually prevent negative things from happen-
ing. The relief they experience when the feared event does not occur reinforces their
worry. They are firm in their conviction that if they had not engaged in worrisome
thinking, the aversive event might have taken place. Other individuals with GAD
believe that their motivation hinges on their worry. Without it, they would no lon-
ger be goal-oriented. They feel that they need to be in a constant state of activation,
anticipating future catastrophes so they can avoid them.
Based on empirical findings on the symptoms and clinical features of GAD
reviewed earlier, we can make a number of recommendations about how treatment
might best proceed with such patients. Because worry is negatively reinforced, indi-
viduals with GAD need to gain insight that their worry is ultimately maladaptive.
Although it is likely to take considerable therapeutic effort for patients with GAD to
learn that their worry is not helpful, clinicians should assist them in developing a
new perspective. As many individuals with GAD tend to associate their worry with a
motivation to succeed, they need to learn to differentiate between emotional distress
and drive and ambition. It is important for them to realize that they can still be goal-
oriented in the absence of anxiety. The majority of their experience centers on trying
to prevent future disasters; thus, their actions are perceived to be externally moti-
vated. Accordingly, treatment should focus on helping them rediscover the intrinsic
motivation behind their actions and come to understand that it is possible to engage
in something even when not actively fearing negative consequences. Patients with
GAD do not fully experience life because they are consumed by mentally creating,
and subsequently avoiding, new dangers and threats. To maximize their experience,
they need to refrain from attending to external consequences to the extent that they
are disengaged from meaningful and rewarding activities. They need to recognize
the negative impact their worry has on their well-being. Thus, before beginning to
work with clients on decreasing worry, therapists need to help clients see that their
worry is neither helpful nor necessary.
Given the potency of worry, it is also important to target and track this cogni-
tive process consistently and not to treat GAD as simply diffuse anxiety. Therefore,

-- 80 of 498 --

60 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
self-monitoring is likely to be a beneficial component of therapy, irrespective of the
clinician’s theoretical orientation. Clinicians need to help patients identify experi-
ences that are inconsistent with their expectations. These patients are constantly
attuned to imagined negative events that may happen in the future, so it is necessary
for them to return to the present moment. Being present enables accurate tracking
of information that can disabuse patients of maladaptive beliefs. When focused on
what may happen in the future, people with GAD tend to lose information available
to them that they can use to their benefit in their present environment. Therefore, it
is important that clinicians help them actively make an effort to track and observe
aspects of the world around them that are inconsistent with their apprehensions.
Moreover, individuals with GAD need more adaptive ways to perceive, interpret,
and predict events. They tend to reject information that contradicts their negative
cognitions, often regarding positive experiences as flukes. Therefore, therapists need
to teach clients to change negative beliefs. Overall, a major goal of therapy is to help
these patients shift from inaccurate negative expectations to beliefs that are less anxi-
ety provoking.
In addition to teaching new ways of seeing the world, more recently developed
treatments have targeted the predilection of individuals with GAD to avoid emotional
processing via worry. Given that emotional processing avoidance may be maintain-
ing the generalized anxiety symptoms, active engagement in emotional processing
may be an important component of treatment, particularly the processing of fear
(Llera & Newman, 2010; Newman, Castonguay, Borkovec, Fisher, & Nordberg, 2008).
However, given that worry precludes such processing, and because worry increases
and maintains anxiety and stress, an important therapeutic task is to engage clients
in the use of applied relaxation. In a relaxed state, emotional processing is more
likely. Within therapy, exposing clients to sharp shifts in anxiety by having them
relax and then imagine a worrisome outcome might be helpful. Outside of therapy,
it is necessary for clients to expose themselves to sharp shifts in negative emotions,
especially those occurring in worry-related situations, such as interpersonal rela-
tionships (Newman, Llera, Erickson, & Przeworski, 2014). Care should be taken to
ensure that clients attempt to lower their baseline state of anxiety and tension during
normal activity to one that mimics euthymia and naturally allow any mood shifts
that may occur both during and outside of therapy.
Because GAD is associated with impairment in health and occupational func-
tioning, it is recommended that clinicians subscribing to all theoretical orientations
assess associated disability at different phases of treatment in order to determine
ways in which clients can improve those important aspects of functioning. Similarly,
therapists should assess for suicidal ideation, intent, and plan throughout the course
of treatment, especially if it has been determined that the patient is at risk for suicide.
Moreover, some of the somatic symptoms associated with GAD (e.g., fatigue, sleep
disturbance, irritability, difficulties with concentration) can be addressed with spe-
cific interventions, such as relaxation and sleep hygiene techniques, and should be
incorporated into treatment.
Also, because interpersonal problems are highly associated with GAD, thera-
pists should assess interpersonal difficulties at different phases of therapy, including
the end of the treatment. Remaining interpersonal problems at the end of therapy
have been linked to poorer outcome at follow-up. Thus, addressing such problems
before the end of treatment may prevent relapse. Both theory and research evidence

-- 81 of 498 --

Generalized Anxiety Disorder 	61
have suggested that interpersonal problems (past and current) are linked to the
cause and/or maintenance of GAD. As such, interpersonal difficulties are further
addressed in the section on etiology.
The aforementioned recommendations are beneficial in targeting the core cog-
nitive, emotional, and behavioral symptoms of individuals diagnosed with GAD.
However, individuals who fall below threshold may still be significantly affected by
their GAD symptoms. Although their overall dysfunction may not be as severe as
that of individuals with full GAD, their symptoms can be just as debilitating during
periods of excessive worry and high anxiety. Therefore, it is important to assess clini-
cally for the presence of subthreshold levels of GAD. Whereas a patient is generally
treated within an overall diagnostic and therapeutic framework, including consid-
eration of personality disorders, it may be necessary to consider the patient’s needs
from an individual standpoint. This is especially true if they do not meet criteria for
a particular disorder but report some associated symptoms requiring stabilization.
COURSE AND OUTCOME
GAD has a common onset during middle age (median = 31–39 years; Kessler et
al., 2005; Ruscio et al., 2017) and assumes a chronic course. In the Harvard–Brown
Anxiety Research Program (HARP), a naturalistic prospective study of psychiatric
patients, the probability of recovery from GAD over a 12-year period was 58%, and
the probability of subsequent recurrence among those who did recover was 45%
(Bruce et al., 2005). A separate prospective study of primary care patients with GAD
revealed a 39% probability of full recovery after 2 years, as well as a 52% probability
of recurrence of moderate or greater symptoms among recovered patients (Rodri-
guez et al., 2006). Thus, full recovery from GAD is rare and often short-lived. Factors
associated with a longer course of GAD in the United States include being female
and being of African American or Latinx ethnicity (Bjornsson et al., 2014; Rodri-
guez et al., 2006; Sibrava et al., 2013). Internationally, GAD takes on a more prolonged
course in lower-income countries and among individuals with lower socioeconomic
status (Ruscio et al., 2017).
Individuals with GAD experience varying levels of symptom severity and
impairment over time. Among HARP participants with GAD, disorder severity
declined modestly over 14 years, though participants did not improve beyond partial
recovery on average (Ramsawh, Raffa, Edelen, Rende, & Keller, 2009). Just as some
persons with GAD experience decreases in severity, others experience increases
in severity, rising from subthreshold to threshold levels (Angst, Gamma, Baldwin,
Ajdacic-Gross, & Rossler, 2009). Thus, while experiencing a chronic course, the typi-
cal person with GAD experiences fluctuations in symptom severity (e.g., Wittchen,
Lieb, Pfister, & Schuster, 2000). As such, there may be periods when the severity of
GAD symptoms is below threshold levels, without necessarily indicating recovery.
Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) is currently the “gold standard” treatment
for GAD. CBT for GAD produces significant improvement that is maintained for
up to 2 years following treatment termination (Borkovec & Ruscio, 2001; Cuijpers et
al., 2014). CBT has also been found to generate greater improvement than no treat-
ment, psychodynamic therapy, pharmacotherapy, nondirective therapy, and placebo
therapy (Borkovec & Ruscio, 2001; Cuijpers et al., 2014; Salzer, Winkelbach, Leweke,

-- 82 of 498 --

62 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
Leibing, & Leichsenring, 2011). CBT is well liked by clients and associated with a
lower dropout rate compared to pharmacotherapy (Mitte, 2005). Though findings
are mixed, the literature generally demonstrates superior efficacy of CBT compared
to either individual cognitive or behavioral therapy components alone (Borkovec &
Ruscio, 2001; Cuijpers et al., 2014). Moderator analyses suggest that individual com-
ponent treatments may be more effective for GAD clients with a longer course of
the disorder (Newman & Fisher, 2013; Newman, Shin, & Lanza, 2019). These find-
ings suggest that, while full CBT packages provide a range of efficacious therapeutic
techniques, prolonged and intensive focus on individual techniques may help clients
break deeply entrenched habits.
In addition to showing statistically significant improvement, CBT has also dem-
onstrated clinically significant improvement. CBT reduces worry to normative levels
and leads to recovery in a majority of clients, and these gains are typically main-
tained for up to 1 year (Covin, Ouimet, Seeds, & Dozois, 2008; Hanrahan, Field,
Jones, & Davey, 2013). CBT interventions include self-monitoring, stimulus con-
trol, relaxation, self-control desensitization, and cognitive therapy. As described by
Woody and Ollendick (2006) and Newman, Stiles, Janeck, and Woody (2006), these
techniques reflect five principles of change (i.e., challenge misconceptions, actively
test the validity of erroneous beliefs, use repeated exposure, eliminate avoidance,
and improve skills) that can be implemented by interventions espoused in various
approaches.
Benzodiazepines (BZs), buspirone, and tricyclic antidepressants were the first
classes of medications to be approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration
(FDA) for the treatment of GAD. However, BZs carry risk of sedation, motor and
cognitive abnormalities, as well as physical dependency and withdrawal problems
(e.g., van Laar, Volkerts, & Verbaten, 2001; Wang, Bohn, Glynn, Mogun, & Avorn,
2001). Because of their adverse effects, BZs have been relegated by most psychiatrists
to treat only acute anxiety symptoms (Baldwin, Allgulander, Bandelow, Ferre, & Pal-
lanti, 2012). In clinical trials, buspirone (Rickels et al., 1982; Volz, Möller, & Sturm,
1994) and the tricyclic antidepressant imipramine (Kahn et al., 1986; Rickels, Down-
ing, Schweizer, & Hassman, 1993) have shown efficacy.
Since 1999, a number of studies have documented the efficacy of selective sero-
tonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) and serotonin–norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor
(SNRI) antidepressants in the treatment of GAD. Venlafaxine XR (extended release)
was the first of these drugs to be approved by the FDA for the indication of GAD.
In large-scale studies, venlafaxine XR demonstrated significantly better clinical effi-
cacy than placebo after 8 weeks (Rickels, Pollack, Sheehan, & Haskins, 2000; Shee-
han, 1999), and after 6 months (Allgulander, Hackett, & Salinas, 2001; Gelenberg et
al., 2000) of treatment. Paroxetine was also studied in several GAD trials (Davidson,
2001; McCafferty, Bellew, Zanelli, Iyengar, & Hewett, 2000; Pollack et al., 2001; Rickels
et al., 2003; Stocchi et al., 2003) and approved by the FDA for the treatment of GAD.
Additional SSRI/SNRI drugs approved by the FDA for the treatment of GAD include
duloxetine and escitalopram.
Despite the efficacy of SNRIs and SSRIs in the treatment of GAD, low remission
rates have been observed during the acute phase of treatment of GAD for venlafax-
ine XR (37%; Gelenberg et al., 2000) and paroxetine (33–39%; Rickels, Rynn, Iyengar,
& Duff, 2006). Although remission rates are higher after longer-term treatment with
venlafaxine XR (79%; Rickels et al., 2013) and paroxetine (73%; Stocchi et al., 2003),

-- 83 of 498 --

Generalized Anxiety Disorder 	63
it is equally important to attempt to increase these rates during acute treatment in
order to improve compliance and relieve suffering and functional impairment as
quickly as possible. Thus, it is important to test new ways of improving short-term
pharmacological treatment of GAD.
Treatment Implications
Given GAD’s chronic fluctuating course, it is unlikely that GAD symptoms and
associated features will permanently resolve themselves over time in the absence
of treatment. Although symptoms may decrease unaided, they can resurface to the
same degree, especially in response to stressors. In patients seeking treatment, GAD
symptoms may appear to have been successfully treated when assessed at discrete
time points. Likewise, patients presenting with subthreshold GAD may be at risk to
develop full-blown GAD. Psychotherapists should use standards employed in epi-
demiological studies to track GAD symptoms and better understand their ebb and
flow, such as the symptom course indicative of sustained recovery. Also, it is rec-
ommended that treatment not be discontinued until clients have shown sustained
recovery.
Although data suggest that GAD is chronic, clinicians should also be aware that
patients may inaccurately report their symptoms. For instance, if they are feeling
anxious in a given moment or for an extended period, they are more likely to report
that they have been that way for the past 6 months, thus meeting the chronicity
criterion for GAD. Therefore, it is important for therapists to help patients increase
their awareness of their patterns of functioning. Furthermore, individuals with
GAD rarely experience full or sustained recovery, so it is imperative that therapists
approach treatment with realistic expectations and aim not for a “cure” but to help
the client to learn how to cope with the disorder. The chronicity and recurrence of
GAD also suggests that clients may need long-term therapy. Therapists should incor-
porate methods to predict and prevent relapses, as well as cope with possible resur-
gences of the disorder. Acceptance-based therapy techniques, such as mindfulness,
may also help clients to stop bracing for the worst and prevent full-blown relapses
(Whiteside et al., 2007).
EPIDEMIOLOGY
Variations in definition, including subsequent changes to DSM criteria, have inter-
fered with precise prevalence estimates for GAD. However, several epidemiological
studies employing large representative samples have approximated actual preva-
lence rates. Lifetime prevalence of DSM-III-R GAD, excluding comorbid panic and
depression, was 3.6% (Kendler, Neale, Kessler, Heath, & Eaves, 1992). The National
Comorbidity Survey found a 12-month prevalence of 3.1% and a lifetime prevalence
of 5.1% (Wittchen, Zhao, Kessler, & Eaton, 1994). The National Comorbidity Survey
Replication study indicated a lifetime prevalence for DSM-IV GAD of 5.7% and a
12-month prevalence of 3.1% (Kessler et al., 2005). Projected lifetime prevalence at age
75 is 8.3%. Compared with DSM-IV GAD prevalence rates, the World Mental Health
Surveys report a higher 12-month prevalence of DSM-5 GAD of 4.0% and a lifetime
prevalence of 7.8% for the United States (Ruscio et al., 2017). Globally, a lifetime

-- 84 of 498 --

64 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
DSM-5 prevalence of 3.6% and a 12-month prevalence of 1.8% has been found in the
26 participating countries (Ruscio et al., 2017).
The presence of GAD is associated with a number of sociodemographic features.
Gender is one of the factors most consistently associated with GAD, with rates of
the disorder approximately two times greater in women than in men in community
(Wittchen et al., 1994) and clinical (e.g., Woodman, Noyes, Black, Schlosser, & Yagla,
1999) samples. Furthermore elevated rates of GAD have been found in previously
married (Wittchen et al., 1994) and never married (Ruscio et al., 2017) individuals
compared to married individuals. Homemakers, individuals not working outside the
home, and unemployed individuals also have elevated rates of GAD (Ruscio et al.,
2017; Wittchen et al., 1994). Within the United States, prevalence is greatest in the
Northeastern region (Wittchen et al., 1994), and globally, prevalence is greatest in
higher income countries (Ruscio et al., 2017). Prevalence increases across adulthood,
beginning at 4.1% for ages 18–29, 6.8% for ages 30–44, rising to 7.7% for ages 45–59,
before decreasing to 3.6% for ages 60 and older (Kessler et al., 2005). Epidemiological
surveys in the United States have found few differences in the prevalence of GAD as
a function of ethnicity, religion, income, education and urbanicity (Wittchen et al.,
1994).
Treatment Implications
GAD prevalence rates depend on the current diagnostic criteria. However, it is not
uncommon for anxious patients to worry excessively or meet criteria for subthresh-
old GAD. Therefore, therapists should understand the determinants of GAD and
related symptoms, since they are likely to encounter worrisome patients. Although
patients may not meet full criteria for GAD, clinicians of all experience levels should
not neglect subthreshold GAD symptoms because the emergence of full GAD may be
prevented through early detection of symptoms. Given a patient’s sociodemographic
features, therapists should be aware that differential risk of GAD may be associ-
ated with different determinant stressors, such as divorce or the death of a loved
one. Whereas ethnicity, education, religion, and income are not related to increased
incidence of GAD, the content of patients’ worries may differ depending on their
sociodemographic background. Thus, clinicians need to understand the manifesta-
tions of GAD within the context of an individual’s culture, ethnicity, religion, educa-
tion, and income level.
COMORBIDITY
GAD is frequently comorbid with other psychological disorders. Current comorbid-
ity for GAD has been reported to range from 6 to 29% for dysthymia, 39 to 76%
for major depression, 23 to 44% for social phobia, 12 to 44% for panic disorder, and
12 to 25% for specific phobia—the five most common comorbid diagnoses (Brown,
Campbell, Lehman, Grisham, & Mancill, 2001; Lamers et al., 2011; Ruscio et al., 2017;
Wittchen et al., 1994). Reported rates of lifetime comorbidity for GAD range from 53
to 86% for major depression and 52 to 74% for other anxiety disorders (Brown et al.,
2001; Judd et al., 1998; Lamers et al., 2011; Ruscio et al., 2017). High levels of lifetime

-- 85 of 498 --

Generalized Anxiety Disorder 	65
comorbidity have also been reported between GAD and substance used disorders
(22%) and personality disorders (38%) (Dyck et al., 2001; Ruscio et al., 2017).
Comorbidity occurs both concurrently and sequentially in GAD (Moffitt et al.,
2007). GAD has been found both to precede and to follow substance use disorders,
panic disorder, and major depression (Grant et al., 2009; Moffitt et al., 2007; Wittchen,
Kessler, Pfister, & Lieb, 2000). Moreover, the presence of GAD at one time point
increases the probability of developing a different disorder at a later time point. In
the National Comorbidity Survey Replication, current GAD significantly predicted
subsequent panic disorder, posttraumatic stress disorder, mood disorders, and sub-
stance use disorders; even subthreshold GAD significantly predicted new onsets of
panic disorder, mood disorders, and substance use disorders (Ruscio et al., 2007).
Because GAD is highly comorbid with other disorders, particularly MDD, some
researchers have questioned whether GAD is a distinct disorder. However, con-
siderable evidence supports the distinctiveness of GAD. Pure GAD and MDD pro-
duce comparable impairment, and they produce further impairment when comor-
bid (Hoffman et al., 2008). Moreover, the relationships among GAD symptoms are
stronger than their relationships with MDD symptoms, and these relationships are
stable over time (Beard et al., 2016). Beyond differences in clinical presentation, GAD
and MDD have distinct risk factors (Beesdo, Pine, Lieb, & Wittchen, 2010; Kendler,
Gardner, Gatz, & Pedersen, 2007) and neurobiological features (Oathes, Patenaude,
Schatzberg, & Etkin, 2015; Weinberg, Kotov, & Proudfit, 2015). GAD and MDD are
also equally likely to be temporally primary (Moffitt et al., 2007), refuting the notion
that GAD is a mere prodrome for MDD. Thus, the literature supports the validity of
GAD as a distinct disorder.
Comorbidity is associated with further impairment. Compared to those with
pure GAD, persons with comorbidity in addition to GAD report more disability days
(Hunt, Issakidis, & Andrews, 2002), interpersonal problems (Judd et al., 1998), and
lower life satisfaction (Henning, Turk, Mennin, Fresco, & Heimberg, 2007). More-
over, compared to those with pure GAD, those with comorbidity generate greater
health care costs due to medication use, hospitalization, and outpatient care (Sand-
elin, Kowalski, Ahnemark, & Allgulander, 2013). Having comorbid disorders in addi-
tion to GAD predicts a more prolonged course of both GAD (Bruce et al., 2005; Bruce,
Machan, Dyck, & Keller, 2001; Kessler et al., 2002) and the comorbid disorders (Bruce
et al., 2005; Kessler et al., 1996, 1998). Finally, comorbidity in GAD is associated with
greater risk of suicide and all-cause mortality (Boden et al., 2007; Phillips et al., 2009).
Comorbid interpersonal factors such as marital tension (Durham, Allan, &
Hackett, 1997) and personality disorders (Skodol, Geier, Grant, & Hasin, 2014) predict
a more prolonged course of GAD, including poorer treatment outcomes. Borkovec,
Newman, Pincus, and Lytle (2002) found that CBT for GAD failed to make a signifi-
cant change in most interpersonal problem domains. Clients endorsing pretherapy
interpersonal problems associated with dominance (e.g., domineering/controlling,
intrusive/needy, vindictive/self-centered) responded least favorably to CBT. Tailor-
ing CBT to address interpersonal problems may improve treatment outcomes for
some clients. For example, behavior therapy outperformed both CBT and cognitive
therapy in the treatment of clients with problems related to dominance (Newman,
Jacobson, Erickson, & Fisher, 2017), and the addition of interpersonal and emotion
processing techniques to traditional CBT led to better treatment outcomes among

-- 86 of 498 --

66 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
clients with dismissive attachment styles (Newman, Castonguay, Jacobson, & Moore,
2015). Identifying and addressing clients’ interpersonal problems can maximize the
effectiveness of treatment for GAD.
Treatment Implications
GAD is a highly comorbid disorder that is associated with significant impairment
whether or not it is the primary disorder. Clinicians are advised to periodically
assess for the presence of other disorders when treating GAD, in order to adjust
treatment in accordance with the client’s difficulties. Assessing psychopathology at
the end of treatment also allows clinicians to determine whether therapy alleviated
comorbidity. The detection of residual comorbid problems might call for additional
targeted interventions, particularly for interpersonal problems, because these tend
to predict poor outcome after the completion of therapy. Similarly, given the high
level of comorbid GAD observed in other disorders, especially depression, clinicians
are advised to assess for GAD at different phases of treatment, even when it is not
the primary disorder.
Conceptualization and treatment planning may differ depending on comorbid-
ity, such that one disorder or related symptoms can affect the expression of another
disorder. For example, a diagnosis of panic disorder alongside GAD can be associ-
ated with increased behavioral avoidance and may require situational exposure tech-
niques. GAD can also be associated with symptoms of other disorders in the absence
of those disorders. For instance, individuals with GAD may engage in behavioral
avoidance of worry triggers. Thus, a thorough assessment of symptoms of associated
disorder symptoms, including avoidance, is important. Addressing comorbid prob-
lems in patients with GAD may be critical for long-term recovery from GAD.
ETIOLOGY
The development of GAD is caused by the interplay of genetic, biological, environ-
mental, and psychological factors. According to twin studies (e.g., Kendler et al.,
1992), GAD is moderately regulated by genetic factors, such that about 30% of the
variance is due to heritability. Similarly, heritable traits related to temperament, such
as neuroticism, are strongly associated with anxiety disorders (Hettema, Neale,
Myers, Prescott, & Kendler, 2006). Although genetic influences alone do not cause
GAD, heritability contributes to a general biological predisposition to anxiety, which
interacts with other elements in the emergence of a distinct disorder (e.g., Beesdo et
al., 2010). A genetic vulnerability to anxiety manifests as GAD when the necessary
biological, psychological, and environmental elements converge.
Learning theories illuminate factors in the development and maintenance of
anxiety, and therefore suggest possible pathways in the etiology of GAD. Classical
conditioning theory suggests that a child comes to fear a conditioned stimulus (CS)
following repeated pairings with an aversive unconditioned stimulus. The CS gener-
ates anxiety because the child expects it to result in an aversive outcome. Anxious
behavior is reinforced and maintained upon avoiding the CS (Mowrer, 1939). Oper-
ant conditioning also contributes to the development and maintenance of anxiety

-- 87 of 498 --

Generalized Anxiety Disorder 	67
when some parents foster children’s anxious behavior by providing attention, reas-
surance, and assistance with avoidant behavior (Ayllon, Smith, & Rogers, 1970).
Learning from social modeling may also contribute to anxious behavior (Bandura
& Menlove, 1968).
Early attachment likely plays a role in the development and maintenance of
GAD. Bowlby (1982) suggested that when a child’s attachment figure is in some way
absent, the child fails to develop a secure base from which to explore their environ-
ment. Subsequently, the child may view the world as unpredictable, uncontrollable,
and threatening, thereby exaggerating the perceived likelihood and negative impact
of feared events, and undermining their perceived ability to cope with these events.
In support of Bowlby’s model, adults with GAD have been found to report elevated
rates of both anxious–ambivalent and avoidant attachment to their parents, com-
pared to nondisordered control participants (Newman, Shin, & Zuellig, 2016).
It is possible that cognitive biases characteristic of GAD develop prior to the
onset of GAD and serve as risk factors for the disorder. Although there are cur-
rently no empirical data to support this, a child might learn that bad things happen
unexpectedly and develop the maladaptive coping strategy of repeatedly interpret-
ing ambiguous information as an indication that something bad has happened or
will happen, as a means to prepare for the worst. Models of GAD implicate processes
such as worry about worry (Wells, 2005), intolerance of uncertainty (Dugas, Buhr, &
Ladouceur, 2004), and fear of a negative emotional contrast (i.e., the fear of a sharp
shift in negative emotion or being emotionally caught off guard) as possible cogni-
tive factors that precede the development of GAD (Newman & Llera, 2011).
Children with GAD tend to have apprehensive expectations for the future, hold
a negative self-image, and seek constant reassurance (Masi et al., 2004). These chil-
dren also tend to have fewer friends and are less assertive than nonanxious chil-
dren (Scharfstein, Alfano, Beidel, & Wong, 2011; Scharfstein & Beidel, 2015). Anxious
children’s fear and negative expectations of social situations may result in negative
interpersonal cycles (Safran & Segal, 1990) in which these children influence oth-
ers’ behavior in a manner that reinforces their negative expectations and contributes
to the maintenance of their anxiety. Indeed, children displaying anxious behavior
tend to be judged less favorably by peers (Baker, Hudson, & Taylor, 2014), and ado-
lescents’ GAD symptoms predict greater levels of maternal criticism in subsequent
years (Nelemans, Hale, Branje, Hawk, & Meeus, 2014). Thus, interpersonal dysfunc-
tion may be a pathway by which GAD develops and is maintained.
During childhood, individuals with GAD may learn that caring for others elicits
love, approval, and nurturance. Extending this pattern into adulthood, they are fre-
quently concerned about interpersonal relationships (Breitholtz et al., 1999; Roemer
et al., 1997). Thus, worry may function as a means of anticipating the needs of, and
threats to, significant others in the pursuit of satisfying interpersonal needs. Accord-
ingly, persons with GAD display an attentional bias to social threat information
(Goodwin et al., 2017) and are especially attuned to others’ negative mental states
when worried (Zainal & Newman, 2018). At the same time, persons with GAD and
elevated worry are inaccurate in perceiving the impact of their social behavior on
others (Erickson et al., 2016; Erickson & Newman, 2007; Shin & Newman, 2019). Con-
sidering these cognitive tendencies in concert, it follows that individuals with GAD
would show not only greater interpersonal distress but also greater rigidity in their

-- 88 of 498 --

68 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
interpersonal styles across differing situations than nonanxious people (Przeworski
et al., 2011). Histories of role-reversed and enmeshed relationships may also cause
persons with GAD to experience unresolved anger toward their primary caregivers
(Cassidy, 1995; Cassidy et al., 2009), exacerbating their interpersonal difficulties.
Treatment Implications
Although many biological, psychological, and social factors are likely to be involved
in the development of GAD, it is important to keep in mind that the etiology of a dis-
order does not necessarily have a bearing on its maintenance. Whereas people come
to behave in certain ways as a result of some etiological factor, the behavior often
persists in the absence of its origin. Often it is difficult to determine how a disorder
develops, but recovery is possible if maintaining factors are identified and addressed.
Therapists should investigate factors that may contribute to their GAD patients’ wor-
risome and anxious states, especially since these patients tend to engage in behaviors
that are perceived to be helpful but ultimately may exacerbate their problem. Addi-
tionally, it is important to monitor for secondary gains that enable patients’ condi-
tions. For example, GAD is often associated with reassurance seeking. Individuals
with this disorder work themselves into an excessively worried state, then want vali-
dation from significant others that they are OK. They learn to associate worry with
reassurance, which promotes further worry.
Once maintaining factors have been identified, therapy can center on eliminat-
ing them. Many of these factors have been targeted in CBT protocols. We should
mention, however, that CBT techniques such as relaxation, stimulus control, social
skills training, and exposure can be incorporated into treatment, irrespective of the
therapist’s theoretical approach. As we mentioned earlier, these interventions can be
viewed as manifestations of general principles of change that could be implemented
by techniques typically prescribed in other orientations (e.g., cognitive distortions
and processes can be addressed by the use of confrontation and interpretation, as
well as by cognitive restructuring; see Castonguay, 2000; Newman et al., 2006). Simi-
larly, treatments for GAD have involved interpersonally focused psychotherapies to
address interpersonal problems in GAD (Crits-Christoph et al., 2005; Newman et al.,
2008, 2011), underscoring the importance of integrating therapy techniques in the
treatment of GAD.
CONCLUSION
Worry is a universal experience that enables people to deal with challenging future
events. Since this thought style is more abstract and less concrete (less image pro-
ducing) than other types of thinking (Borkovec & Inz, 1990), it allows for abstract
processing of emotional information, thereby facilitating avoidance of reactivity and
surprising negative events. Thus, it functions as a strategy to manage the unpre-
dictability and uncontrollability of potentially threatening future events. Neverthe-
less, the worry experienced by individuals with GAD is a pervasive, uncontrollable
daily activity that often elicits emotional discomfort. Although patients with GAD
engage in worry as a means to combat potential threat, it serves to exacerbate their

-- 89 of 498 --

Generalized Anxiety Disorder 	69
anxiety. Accordingly, GAD can be associated with debilitating distress, disability,
and impairment in quality of life.
Overall, GAD is a highly chronic and comorbid anxiety disorder whose etiology
is linked to a combination of genetic, biological, environmental, and psychological
factors. Its core and associated symptoms are the products of the interaction among
cognitive, imaginal, and physiological responses to continuously perceived threat
(Borkovec & Inz, 1990; Newman & Borkovec, 2002). Although GAD is the least suc-
cessfully treated anxiety disorder given its chronicity and pervasiveness (Brown et
al., 1994), CBT is an efficacious treatment for GAD. CBT targets each of the aforemen-
tioned response systems, and interventions include self-monitoring, stimulus con-
trol, relaxation, self-control desensitization, and cognitive therapy. Although DSM-5
brought significant changes to the diagnostic criteria for anxiety disorders, worry
continues to play a central, prominent role in GAD’s conceptualization (American
Psychiatric Association, 2013). Therefore, when treating GAD, clinicians and thera-
pists should assist patients in recognizing the detrimental impact of their worry on
their psychological and emotional functioning, and identifying and tracking their
worrisome thinking, as well as underscore those experiences and aspects in the
patients’ environment that are inconsistent with their schemas to remediate mal-
adaptive thought and behavior patterns.

77
Four types of anxiety disorders are discussed in this chapter: (1) panic disorder, typi-
fied by recurrent attacks that involve the rapid onset of multiple physical symptoms
(e.g., shortness of breath and racing heart), as well as fear of future attacks; (2) ago-
raphobia, characterized by avoidance of situations in which anxiety or panic symp-
toms might occur, especially when escape may be difficult; (3) social anxiety disor-
der (previously termed “social phobia”), which involves intense anxiety or avoidance
of interpersonal and/or performance situations fueled by fear of negative evaluation
by others; and (4) specific phobias, characterized by excessive fear toward a particu-
lar object or situation (e.g., animals), certain natural environments (e.g., heights), and
closed spaces.
The anxiety disorders overlap considerably with one another, both in terms of
their typical symptoms and the models of etiology to explain their development.
Thus, rather than review all of the data for each disorder separately, we highlight
those aspects of panic disorder and phobias that are shared, then consider the evi-
dence specific to a given disorder. We outline each disorder’s common symptoms
and related clinical features, natural course and treatment outcome findings, epide-
miological data, and the high rates of comorbidity for panic and phobic disorders.
C H A P T E R 4
Panic Disorder,
Social Anxiety Disorder, and Phobias
Bethany A. Teachman
Marvin R. Goldfried
Elise M. Clerkin
Alexandra L. Silverman

—Old Chinese proverb
You must do the things you think you cannot do.
—Eleanor Roosevelt

-- 98 of 498 --

78 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
Finally, we review general and specific models of etiology, emphasizing the many
shared biological and psychological vulnerability factors across the anxiety difficul-
ties. Throughout the chapter, we raise potential clinical implications that follow from
the psychopathology literature and consider some of the many exciting challenges
that arise in helping patients reduce their disabling anxiety symptoms.
TYPICAL SYMPTOMS AND ASSOCIATED CLINICAL FEATURES
The anxiety disorders, including panic disorder, social anxiety, and the various pho-
bias, share a number of features, such as avoidance behaviors and excessive fear rela-
tive to what others would experience in a given situation. These common features
(as we discuss below) have led many researchers to suggest that a dimensional per-
spective may more appropriately characterize the range of anxiety problems rather
than the current classification system that emphasizes specific categories. Relatedly,
certain transdiagnostic models (e.g., Nolen-Hoeksema & Watkins, 2011) integrate dis-
tal (e.g., childhood sexual abuse) and proximal (e.g., rumination) risk factors, as well
as moderators (e.g., social stress), to help explain why a given risk factor may lead
to multiple different disorders (this concept is known as “multifinality”). Further-
more, these models can help to explain why one individual with a given risk fac-
tor may develop one set of symptoms, another individual with the same risk factor
may develop a different set of symptoms, and a third individual may not have any
significant mental health symptoms (this concept is known as “divergent trajecto-
ries”). Thus, we consider symptom dimensions that are common across panic, social
anxiety, and phobic disorders, as well as the unique ways these difficulties play out
across specific disorders. This focus on both common and disorder-specific features
is important from a treatment perspective because seeing patients with multiple
rather than isolated anxiety difficulties is the norm (see Brown, Campbell, Lehman,
Grisham, & Mancill, 2001).
Subjective distress that is excessive given the objective danger in the environ-
ment is present across anxiety difficulties, on a continuum from diffuse worry and
future-oriented anxious apprehension (often present in agoraphobia), to more spe-
cific targeted fears (seen in specific phobia and social anxiety disorder), to immi-
nent threat and panic (characteristic of panic disorder). Related to the experience of
emotion (e.g., fear, anxiety, distress), there are also impairments in the regulation of
emotions (Cisler, Olatunji, Feldner, & Forsyth, 2010). For instance, anxious individ-
uals typically strive to make their world feel “safe.” These maladaptive strategies
to reduce the sense of danger and vulnerability often result in extensive efforts to
avoid or escape from the feared situations or feelings, which adds considerably to the
impairment in occupational, academic, and social functioning typical of panic, social
anxiety, and phobic disorders. Similarly, anxious individuals often rely too heavily
on cues or signals in their environment that help to reduce their distress at entering
feared situations (see Rachman, 1994). For instance, a person with agoraphobia may
only go out to the mall if their spouse accompanies them; a person with panic disor-
der may insist on always carrying their medication or water bottle when leaving the
house; a person with social anxiety disorder may only attend a party after consum-
ing three beers. Finally, although much previous research has focused on negative

-- 99 of 498 --

Panic Disorder, Social Anxiety Disorder, and Phobias 	79
emotions and emotion regulation, to better understand the experience of those with
pathological anxiety, there is an increasing focus on impairments in positive emotions
and emotion regulation (e.g., Eisner, Johnson, & Carver, 2009; Kashdan et al., 2013).
This is reflected in the individual with social anxiety who does not emotionally ben-
efit from savoring the memory of their date and even fears being evaluated positively
by their date, or the person with panic disorder who actively dampens positive emo-
tions when they arise.
Anxious individuals also tend to process information in distorted ways that
are thought to increase their beliefs about the world being a dangerous, uncontrol-
lable place and the individual being vulnerable to threat (see MacLeod & Mathews,
2012). Examples include spider-fearful individuals orienting their attention quickly
to pictures of spiders (e.g., Mogg & Bradley, 2006); individuals who are afraid of dogs
being more likely than nonphobic individuals to use ferocity features in classifying
dogs (Landau, 1980); people with panic disorder misinterpreting benign changes in
bodily sensations as catastrophic signs of illness (e.g., interpreting a slightly racing
heart as indicative of an impending heart attack; Teachman, Smith-Janik, & Saporito,
2007); or people with social anxiety disorder judging themselves more harshly on a
social performance (e.g., giving a speech) than do outside observers (e.g., Norton &
Hope, 2001). Furthermore, there is compelling evidence that threat-oriented cogni-
tions occur both at relatively explicit (controlled, deliberative) and automatic (outside
one’s conscious control) levels (Teachman, Clerkin, Cunningham, Dreyer- Oren, &
Werntz, 2019). Additionally, there is some indication that individuals with phobic
symptoms may actually see their environment as more threatening at a perceptual
level, in addition to judging it as more dangerous (e.g., Teachman, Stefanucci, Cler-
kin, Cody, & Proffitt, 2008).
Just as thinking patterns often become highly geared toward managing per-
ceived threats when individuals become anxious, so too can physiological respond-
ing. There is considerable variability in the physiological changes that occur across
the anxiety disorders, but some degree of the “fight-or-flight” reaction (initially
described by Cannon, 1929) is common to both panic and phobic reactions. The fight-
or-flight system involves dramatic surges of autonomic arousal that motivate individ-
uals either to escape or avoid a situation or defend themselves. A freezing response
may also occur in some individuals. During the heightened activation of the sym-
pathetic branch of the autonomic nervous system, a series of physical reactions that
occur very rapidly help to prepare the body for action. These include increased heart
rate, rapid breathing, inhibition of digestive processes, constriction of blood vessels
in some parts of the body with dilation of blood vessels for muscles, pupil dilation,
endorphin release, and acceleration of reflexes (see Gray & McNaughton, 1996; Gun-
nar & Quevedo, 2007). This response prompts the individual for immediate action
and is adaptive when a real danger exists in the environment, but it essentially (mal)
functions as a false alarm when no objective danger exists, as occurs in panic and
phobias (see Barlow, 2002).
Thus, across a variety of emotional response systems (e.g., subjective distress,
behavior, physiology, cognitive processing; Eysenck, 2014; Lang, 1985), we see com-
mon signs and consequences of anxious arousal associated with both panic disor-
der and phobias, pointing to their shared phenomenological presentation. These
common features (combined with frequent difficulties in determining the clinical

-- 100 of 498 --

80 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
threshold for a given disorder, as well as the high level of comorbidity across them)
have led to many questions about the adequacy of a categorical classification system
for these disorders (see review in Barlow, 2002, Chapter 9). Simply put, questions
have arisen as to whether the current system effectively “carves nature at its joints.”
However, the heuristic value of categories (e.g., for communication about diagnoses)
and advantages conferred by the specificity of categorical criteria for research and
clinical decision making make it unlikely that the categories will be fully abandoned
any time soon (see Brown & Barlow, 2009).
Taking into consideration the overlap across anxiety disorders also recognizes
that the typical symptoms unique to each disorder represent important variation in
the way anxiety pathology manifests across individuals, depending on their particu-
lar vulnerability factors and learning experiences. We turn to these specific symp-
toms next.
Panic Disorder and Agoraphobia
Panic disorder is defined by recurrent and unexpected panic attacks, which are fol-
lowed by at least 1 month of fear about future panic attacks, concern about the con-
sequences of the attacks (e.g., worries about “going crazy”), or changes in behavior
related to the attacks (e.g., avoidance or introduction of extensive safety cues). The
term “panic attack” is used frequently by the general public, but it has a specific
set of criteria in the context of anxiety pathology: The attack must involve a dis-
tinct period of intense discomfort and/or fear that rises to a peak within minutes.
It must involve at least four of the following symptoms: “palpitations, pounding
heart, or accelerated heart rate; sweating; trembling or shaking; sensations of short-
ness of breath or smothering; feeling of choking; chest pain or discomfort; nausea
or abdominal distress; feeling dizzy, unsteady, light-headed, or faint; chills or heat
sensations; paresthesias (numbness or tingling sensations); derealization (feelings
of unreality) or depersonalization (being detached from oneself); fear of losing con-
trol or going crazy; fear of dying” (list from American Psychiatric Association, 2013,
p. 208). Attacks that involve fewer than four symptoms are termed “limited symp-
tom” attacks.
Importantly, panic attacks can occur within the context of many anxiety prob-
lems. As one example, a person with social anxiety disorder may experience a panic
attack before having to give a speech; however, in these cases the fear focuses on the
negative evaluation by others and expected social embarrassment. In panic disorder,
the attacks themselves become a problem, as indicated by the fear of future attacks
and altered behavior in response to the attacks. For this reason, panic disorder is
often described as a “fear of fear” (Goldstein & Chambless, 1978). It is notable that fre-
quent panic attacks are not actually required for the diagnosis, provided that impair-
ment or fear of future attacks is evident. Furthermore, the occurrence of panic attacks
alone is not sufficient to warrant a diagnosis of panic disorder. In fact, roughly 11% of
adults in the United States experience a panic attack during a given 12-month period,
which is clearly much higher than the prevalence rate of actual panic disorder (e.g.,
American Psychiatric Association, 2013). Thus, tracking and reducing how often
panic attacks occur are useful in treatment but not the sole goal of treatment; rather,
helping people reduce their fear of future attacks and diminish associated avoidance

-- 101 of 498 --

Panic Disorder, Social Anxiety Disorder, and Phobias 	81
is paramount. Along these lines, simply asking patients to monitor the percentage
of time they are worried about becoming anxious can provide the therapist—and
the patient—with a useful index of change. This marker is meaningful because, at
the outset of treatment, the lives of these patients are often very much about their
anxiety; indeed, in most of their waking hours, they are preoccupied with concern
about becoming anxious.
Panic attacks may be entirely unexpected, in that they seem to come on “out of
the blue” (when a person cannot identify a trigger), especially during early stages
of the disorder. It has been our clinical experience that more often than not, such
uncued attacks occur in situations in which the person is unaware of the precipitat-
ing factors (e.g., a fleeting thought and emotion). Alternatively, they may be expected,
in that an attack is more likely to occur following certain triggers (e.g., when in a
crowded place), or when the attack regularly occurs after exposure to a particular
situation. Situational events may involve a place, such as a restaurant, but they may
also be a physical state or symptom (e.g., when experiencing arousal following caf-
feine intake), or an emotional circumstance (e.g., during conflict with significant oth-
ers). Not surprisingly, people often start to avoid situations or feelings that tend to
trigger their panic attacks. When this becomes extreme and impairing in its own
right, the person may qualify for diagnoses of both panic disorder and agoraphobia.
Currently in the field, we consider agoraphobia as an independent disorder,
even though it often co-occurs with panic disorder. A person with agoraphobia expe-
riences unusually high anxiety about situations or places that they perceive would
be hard to escape from, or in which they would not be able to find help if they have
a panic attack or similar symptoms. We think of such experiences as a “disorder”
when they last for a while (months), and cause a person enough stress that they find
themself going out of their way to avoid those situations and places. Common situ-
ations that trigger agoraphobic avoidance include various modes of transportation
(e.g., car, bus), going outside of the home alone, or standing in crowds or lines. Avoid-
ance of internal sensations (known as “interoceptive cues”) that make a person fear-
ful is also common. These include sensations associated with dizziness, shortness
of breath, or hyperventilation (Schmidt & Trakowski, 2004), as well as sensations
associated with normal arousal in response to exercise, sex, eating heavy meals, or
strong emotional reactions.
Given the considerable avoidance behaviors and fear of bringing on panic symp-
toms that characterize panic disorder and/or agoraphobia, it is not surprising that
significant impairment is associated with these disorders. The morbidity associated
with panic disorder and agoraphobia cuts across many domains of biopsychosocial
functioning. For instance, in terms of relationship functioning, couples may have
stopped having sexual intercourse because of the patient’s fears about experiencing
intense physical sensations. In addition, difficulties with driving and related travel
fears often lead to considerable dependence on loved ones. Work impairment is also
common (Alonso et al., 2004) as the triggers associated with panic become more
ubiquitous. In severe cases of agoraphobia, the person may literally be homebound,
which clearly limits work opportunities. Individuals with panic disorder and/or ago-
raphobia are also at an elevated risk for experiencing a range of other anxiety, mood,
impulse-control, and substance use disorders (Kessler et al., 2006). Additionally,
sleep dysregulation is common and may be tied in part to the frequent occurrence of

-- 102 of 498 --

82 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
nocturnal panic attacks (Craske et al., 2002). Patients who experience nocturnal panic
attacks, which occur when a person wakes from sleep in a state of panic, show simi-
lar panic symptom severity and general impairment to that of panic patients who do
not experience nocturnal panic. However, patients with nocturnal panic attacks tend
to show lower levels of agoraphobic avoidance, possibly because they have more fear-
ful associations with sleep than with external situational factors.
One of the most serious clinical features connected to panic disorder is the
increased risk for suicide attempts (see Schmidt, Woolaway-Bickel, & Bates, 2001). In
an early meta-analysis (Harris & Barraclough, 1997), panic disorder was associated
with a 10-fold increase in suicide risk, relative to expected rates in the population.
Moreover, although there is some support for the “depression mediation hypoth-
esis” (i.e., that panic disorder is indirectly linked to suicide via its relationship with
heightened depression; Schmidt et al., 2001), there is little doubt that clinicians need
to be alert to the increased risk in this population. This is especially true given that
comorbidity is the norm rather than the exception (Kessler et al., 2006). It is possible
that elevated rates of suicide risk among individuals with panic disorder may be tied
to beliefs that anxiety sensations are dangerous, potentially indicating a cognitive
disaster such as going crazy or losing control of one’s mind. Referred to as “anxiety
sensitivity cognitive concerns,” researchers theorize that these beliefs may amplify
the distress brought on by the sensations associated with mental illness, such as dif-
ficulty sleeping or concentrating (Capron, Norr, Macatee, & Schmidt, 2013). In turn,
after the “amplified” distress reaches a potently high level, suicidal ideation and
attempts may occur.
Social Anxiety Disorder
Social anxiety disorder is characterized by marked and persistent fear of social inter-
actions or performance situations in which scrutiny by others is possible (American
Psychiatric Association, 2013). Individuals with social anxiety disorder consistently
experience anxiety when confronted with social and/or performance situations, and
this disproportionate anxiety typically lasts for at least 6 months to qualify for a
diagnosis. Given the common tendency by socially anxious individuals to overes-
timate threat in social situations, individuals suffering from social anxiety disorder
need not recognize that their fears are unreasonable or excessive. Typically, indi-
viduals with social anxiety disorder avoid feared social or performance situations.
Less frequently, social situations are not avoided but are endured with considerable
dread. Significant anticipatory anxiety is also common.
Although early theorists believed that social anxiety is caused by deficits in
social skills (see Trower, Bryant, & Argyle, 1978), empirical findings are inconsistent
regarding the extent to which individuals with social anxiety disorder actually have
social skills deficits (e.g., Stopa & Clark, 1993). What is clear is that compared with
healthy control participants, people with social anxiety disorder are typically more
critical of their social performances, even after accounting for actual differences in
social abilities (e.g., Rapee & Lim, 1992).
Recent research has focused on understanding moderators and mediators link-
ing social anxiety with biased self-evaluations. For instance, there is evidence that
among socially anxious individuals, a tendency to engage in postevent processing

-- 103 of 498 --

Panic Disorder, Social Anxiety Disorder, and Phobias 	83
(i.e., to ruminatively dwell on previous social interactions) is associated with more
negative judgments about one’s social performance (Brozovich & Heimberg, 2011).
Other research points to the importance of experiential avoidance (i.e., attempts to
control or avoid one’s internal emotions and thoughts; Kashdan et al., 2014) for under-
standing why social anxiety symptoms may lead to less positive self-evaluations
across time (Sarfan, Cody, & Clerkin, 2019). Moreover, research from Wallace and
Alden (1997) indicates that individuals with social anxiety disorder often believe
that they will be unable to make desired impressions on others, or that they will fail
to live up to others’ expectations. This fear is tied to one of the defining symptoms
at the heart of cognitive models of social anxiety disorder—extreme fears of nega-
tive evaluation (e.g., Clark & Wells, 1995). Interestingly, there is also evidence that
individuals with social anxiety disorder fear positive evaluation (Weeks, Heimberg,
& Rodebaugh, 2008).
Many theorists contend that the distinction between social anxiety disorder and
the more commonly experienced forms of shyness is a matter of degree rather than
kind, with symptoms of social anxiety ranging along a continuum from mild and
transient social unease to shyness to social anxiety disorder (see Heimberg & Becker,
2002). Thus, while social anxiety disorder and shyness have many similarities, symp-
toms of social anxiety disorder are more extreme across a variety of domains, and
the vast majority of people (~88%) who report that they are shy would not meet diag-
nostic criteria for social anxiety disorder (American Psychiatric Association, 2013).
The issue of diagnosis becomes further complicated when one considers the
relationship between social anxiety disorder and avoidant personality disorder.
Given the striking similarities and high rates of comorbidity between these two dis-
orders (as they were defined in DSM-IV, which mainly guided this research), some
theorists believe that there is not a “distinct symptomatic delineation” between them
(see Reich, 2001, p. 157), and avoidant personality disorder is sometimes conceptual-
ized as an extreme form of social anxiety disorder. Interestingly, there also does not
appear to be strong evidence for the notion that there are meaningful subtypes of
social anxiety disorder with distinct symptom profiles (for reviews, see Heimberg,
Holt, Schneier, Spitzer, & Liebowitz, 1993; Rapee, 1995).
The subjective anxiety experienced by individuals with social anxiety disorder
is often extremely impairing and distressing, as are the physical sensations typically
tied to social anxiety, such as heart racing, sweating, gastrointestinal discomfort,
blushing, and so forth. Avoidance is also particularly problematic. For instance, a
student who fears appearing inarticulate or stupid may avoid raising a question dur-
ing class; a teenager who fears being rejected may never risk asking someone on a
date; a middle-aged man who fears that others will notice his hands shaking or voice
trembling may avoid giving presentations at work. In some cases, this avoidance
behavior can even lead to employment or financial difficulties (e.g., when individu-
als pass up promotions at work because the new position would involve increased
social interactions). Moreover, experiential avoidance appears to be critically impor-
tant in differentiating between adults with social anxiety disorder and healthy con-
trol participants (Kashdan et al., 2013).
Alcohol problems within the context of social anxiety are also quite high. Epi-
demiological research indicates that social anxiety disorder and alcohol use disorder
commonly co-occur (Grant et al., 2004; Schneier et al., 2010), possibly because some

-- 104 of 498 --

84 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
individuals with social anxiety disorder drink to minimize anxiety when interacting
socially. Indeed, there is evidence that coping motives (i.e., drinking to reduce nega-
tive affect) mediate the relationship between social anxiety symptoms and drinking
problems, particularly for emerging adults (Clerkin, Werntz, Magee, Lindgren, &
Teachman, 2014). The presence of social anxiety disorder may also increase the prob-
ability of suicide ideation and attempts (Cougle, Keough, Riccardi, & Sachs-Ericsson,
2009), and individuals with social anxiety disorder have lower well-being ratings
compared to individuals without a mental illness (Wersebe et al., 2018).
Specific Phobia
Specific phobias are defined by disproportionate and persistent fear toward a partic-
ular object or situation that typically lasts at least 6 months to qualify for a diagnosis
(see American Psychiatric Association, 2013). Unlike less intense, subclinical fears,
a specific phobia must almost always evoke an extreme and immediate fear reac-
tion. Furthermore, the phobic target must be avoided or endured with severe distress
and result in some impairment in functioning. Similar to social anxiety disorder,
individuals with a specific phobia need not recognize their fear is unreasonable or
excessive because of the tendency by many phobic individuals to have exaggerated
perceptions of the danger in phobic situations. Note that we focus in this chapter on
panic and phobias among adults, but given the relatively high prevalence of phobias
in children, the interested reader is encouraged to see excellent summaries of fear
and phobias in children and adolescents (e.g., Ollendick, White, & White, 2018).
The overlap in criteria and feared situations for panic disorder, agoraphobia,
social anxiety disorder, and specific phobias can make their differential diagnosis a
challenge. More generally, determining whether a fear target constitutes a specific
phobia or another anxiety problem is sometimes difficult. For example, a fear of dirt
can reflect either obsessive– compulsive disorder for a person with contamination
obsessions or be part of a specific phobia. Similarly, fear of a particular situation
may be tied to a traumatic event in the context of posttraumatic stress disorder or
reflect a more circumscribed specific phobia. Distinguishing among these possibili-
ties involves a functional analysis that includes a careful assessment of the triggers
and feared consequences associated with encountering the feared object or situation.
In theory, a phobia can be directed toward almost any target, and identifying
some of the more esoteric phobias, often by their Greek or Latin names, can be an
entertaining party game (e.g., “apeirophobia,” a fear of infinity). However, in clini-
cal practice, a smaller set of phobias tends to be represented, and these have been
organized into subtypes in recent DSM versions (i.e., animal, natural environment,
blood–injection–injury, situational, and other). Although these types have some heu-
ristic value for research and communication purposes, the utility of using subtype
specifiers as part of the diagnostic label has been questioned (see Antony & Barlow,
2002).
As with panic disorder and the other phobias reviewed in this chapter, spe-
cific phobias are associated with a strong physiological response, characterized by
increased heart rate and skin conductance. In fact, a panic attack is a common reac-
tion for persons with specific phobias upon encountering their feared object/situ-
ation (Antony, 1994). Although increased arousal is common across various types

-- 105 of 498 --

Panic Disorder, Social Anxiety Disorder, and Phobias 	85
of specific phobias, blood–injection–injury phobia has a unique, additional compo-
nent to its physiological profile that includes a tendency to faint. Another interesting
characteristic of certain specific phobias, such as spider, snake, and blood–injury–
injection fears, is that patients often feel a strong disgust reaction to their feared
object (see review in Cisler, Olatunji, & Lohr, 2009). One theory (which requires more
empirical validation), known as the “disease avoidance model,” is that disgust serves
an adaptive function, in that it promotes avoidance, which helps to prevent the
transmission of disease (Davey, 1992). Supporting this theory, meta-analytic work
suggests that although disgust proneness is elevated for a range of anxiety disor-
ders, the effect is especially pronounced for individuals whose anxiety symptoms
are linked to contagion fears (Olatunji, Armstrong, & Elwood, 2017). Thus, applying
transdiagnostic frameworks may be helpful to understand conditions under which
disgust proneness leads to one set of symptoms (e.g., spider phobia) versus another
(e.g., health anxiety). For instance, someone with a tendency toward disgust prone-
ness may be more likely to develop spider phobia if their environment emphasizes
concerns tied to small animals and disease. Furthermore, the avoidance of disgust
elicitors to prevent disease may function similarly to the avoidance in panic disorder
of bodily sensations to prevent physical illness; both cases involve an oversensitive
“false alarm” system that exaggerates the presence of danger.
Finally, for individuals with specific phobias, avoidance is problematic because
it can result in limited functioning in social, recreational, and work situations (see
Rowa, McCabe, & Antony, 2006), and persons with blood–injection–injury fears often
do not adhere to recommended medical regimens (Mollema, Snoek, Ader, Heine, &
van der Ploeg, 2001). However, the impairment associated with specific phobias has
more often been described as moderate rather than severe (e.g., Becker et al., 2007),
in part because it is often possible to avoid encountering one’s feared situation/object
effectively because of the circumscribed nature of the fear. For instance, organizing
one’s life to avoid seeing a snake is far easier than trying to avoid all social perfor-
mance situations or triggers of bodily sensations (as might be desired in social anxi-
ety or panic disorder, respectively). Thus, it is not unusual for people to meet all of
the diagnostic criteria for a specific phobia except for the impairment in functioning
requirement (e.g., Romano, Tremblay, Vitaro, Zoccolillo, & Pagani, 2001).
Clinical Implications
The typical symptoms associated with anxiety disorders can be manifested in one
or more responses systems: behavioral, physiological, expressive emotion, and/or
cognitive. We deal with some of the clinical implications associated with each of
these domains, considering the behavioral (e.g., avoidance of therapy), physiological
(e.g., difficulty tolerating physiological reactions), expressive emotion (e.g., dysregu-
lation of multiple emotions), and cognitive (e.g., beliefs about not feeling in control,
selective processing of threatening information) features of anxiety. One consid-
erable challenge in assessing and treating anxiety difficulties is that these differ-
ent response systems are often not closely aligned and may not change in tandem
(see seminal work by Lang, Cuthbert, & Bradley [1998] and recent perspective by
Taschereau-Dumouchel, Kawato, & Lau [2020], a process termed “desynchrony”
or “uncoupling”). Thus, it is important to consider multiple outcome markers to

-- 106 of 498 --

86 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
determine the success of therapy because there is no one true measure of fear or
anxiety. Analogously, evaluating other markers of impairment in functioning, such
as quality of relationships and work life, is critical.
Avoidance of Therapy Itself
Reducing the avoidance of cues associated with panic and phobic targets is typically
a key focus in therapy, but avoidance symptoms also have other important treatment
implications. In particular, individuals who use avoidance as a coping strategy, albeit
a maladaptive one, to reduce their anxiety in the short term also may sometimes
desire to avoid treatment and therapy homework, especially if exposure to fear cues
is involved in therapy. This may be particularly true in a group treatment context, in
which it can be slightly more challenging to permit total flexibility for each individ-
ual to set the pace for their exposures. Thus, building a strong therapeutic alliance
is essential to help patients become willing to face their fears, and enhancing moti-
vation for treatment may be needed to reduce patients’ ambivalence about making
changes in their lives, or even about being in treatment (motivational interviewing
approaches can be very helpful in this regard; see Miller & Rollnick, 2012). This, of
course, would be in conjunction with careful monitoring of patients’ avoidance, as
well as successful exposure practice between sessions.
Physiological Reactions
A salient characteristic of anxiety disorders is the increased activation of the sym-
