151
The adverse psychological effects of being exposed to a traumatic event have been
documented for many years. Earlier conceptualizations of traumatic stress typically
regarded these reactions as transient responses that would normally abate shortly
after trauma exposure. In the first edition of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of
Mental Disorders (DSM; American Psychiatric Association, 1952), traumatic stress
reactions were classified as acute posttrauma responses under gross stress reaction,
and longer lasting reactions were subsumed under the anxiety or depressive neuro-
ses. A major change occurred in the third edition of the DSM (American Psychiatric
Association, 1980), in which the diagnosis of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD)
was first introduced. This formal recognition of the syndrome was partly influenced
by the need to understand and meet the needs of veterans returning from Vietnam
with PTSD reactions. Our goal in this chapter is to provide an overview of the current
state of research and clinical guidelines in acute and posttraumatic stress disorders.
In the following sections, we first describe typical symptoms, course, classification,
associated clinical features, epidemiology, comorbidity, etiology, and risk factors.
Then we review PTSD treatment, clinical implications, and principles of change in
the treatment of PTSD. We address clinical considerations throughout the text to aid
in the translation of basic research into clinical practice.
TYPICAL SYMPTOMS
Posttraumatic stress disorder describes severe and persistent stress reactions after
exposure to a traumatic event (American Psychiatric Association, 2013). How one
C H A P T E R 6
Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
Richard A. Bryant
Abigail Powers Lott
Terence M. Keane

-- 172 of 498 --

152 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
defines “trauma” varies, and the fifth edition of the DSM has specific criteria for what
constitutes trauma exposure (see pp. 271–274 of DSM-5 for a description of trauma
and full diagnostic criteria). PTSD comprises four symptom clusters reflecting intru-
sive symptoms, avoidance symptoms, negative alterations in mood and cognitions,
and alterations in arousal and reactivity. Criterion D represents a new component
of the PTSD diagnosis with the release of DSM-5 in that it separates avoidance from
other cognitive and emotional facets of the condition. This revision was made based
on accumulating evidence that effortful avoidance was distinct from mood and
numbing symptoms (Elhai & Palmieri, 2011). Cognitive distortions are also a central
part of PTSD and symptoms that were added to the negative alterations in cognitions
and mood cluster in DSM-5 now better capture trauma-related cognitive symptoms,
including negative beliefs about oneself and the world and distorted blame of self or
others related to the event (LoSavio, Dillon, & Resick, 2017).
Acute stress disorder (ASD), a diagnosis first included in DSM-IV, was intro-
duced for two primary reasons: to describe acute stress reactions that occur in the
initial month after trauma exposure, and to identify trauma survivors who are high
risk for developing subsequent PTSD (Harvey & Bryant, 2002). A major rationale
for the introduction of this diagnosis was because PTSD can only be diagnosed at
least 1 month following trauma, and there was a diagnostic gap in the initial month
of trauma. The lack of a formal diagnosis to describe posttraumatic stress in the
initial month potentially limited some trauma survivors’ ready access to mental
health services and, in this sense, a formal diagnosis was intended to alleviate this
potential barrier to care. In part due to the fact that ASD is not a clear predictor of
who develops PTSD, the disorder was reconceptualized in DSM-5 to broaden the
required symptoms and focus on individuals with severe stress reactions in need of
immediate clinical attention (Bryant, 2018). In DSM-5, the stressor criterion (Crite-
rion A) for acute stress disorder was also changed from DSM-IV. The criterion now
requires, as with PTSD, being explicit as to whether the qualifying traumatic events
were experienced directly, witnessed, or experienced indirectly. Also, DSM-IV Cri-
terion A2 regarding the subjective reaction to the traumatic event (e.g., “the person’s
response involved intense fear, helplessness, or horror”) was eliminated. Based on
evidence that acute posttraumatic reactions are very heterogeneous and that DSM-
IV’s emphasis on dissociative symptoms was too restrictive, individuals may meet
diagnostic criteria in DSM-5 for acute stress disorder if they report any nine of 14
listed symptoms in these categories: intrusion, negative mood, dissociation, avoid-
ance, and arousal. Support for the inclusion (and previous focus on DSM-IV) of dis-
sociative symptoms in the ASD diagnosis to predict subsequent PTSD came from
evidence demonstrating an association between “peritraumatic dissociation” (i.e.,
dissociation occurring in the immediate aftermath of a traumatic experience) and
subsequent levels of PTSD, a finding that has been replicated across numerous longi-
tudinal studies (Murray, Ehlers, & Mayou, 2002).
COURSE
There is strong evidence that most people recently exposed to a traumatic experience
commonly report posttraumatic stress reactions in the initial weeks after trauma.

-- 173 of 498 --

Posttraumatic Stress Disorder 	153
Despite the prevalence of acute stress reactions, the majority of these stress responses
are transient (Bryant, 2003; Galatzer-Levy, Huang, & Bonanno, 2018). For example,
whereas 94% of rape victims displayed sufficient PTSD symptoms 2 weeks post-
trauma to meet criteria (excluding the 1-month time requirement), 11 weeks later this
rate dropped to 47% (Rothbaum, Foa, Riggs, Murdock, & Walsh, 1992). In another
study, 70% of women and 50% of men were diagnosed with PTSD at an average of
19 days after an assault; the rate of PTSD at 4-month follow-up dropped to 21% for
women and zero for men (Riggs, Rothbaum, & Foa, 1995). Similar patterns have been
observed following motor vehicle accidents (Blanchard, Hickling, Barton, & Taylor,
1996), the New York terrorist attacks (Galea et al., 2003), and the 2004 tsunami (van
Griensven et al., 2006).
Despite a general trend for people to adapt after trauma, it is also important to
note that there are different trajectories from acute reactions to chronic PTSD. The
main exemplar of a different trajectory is delayed-onset PTSD. Delayed onset is an
uncommon response in civilian populations, although there is some evidence that
it is more common in military contexts (for a review, see Andrews, Brewin, Phil-
pott, & Stewart, 2007). Although the mechanisms underpinning delayed-onset PTSD
are not well understood, several explanations have been offered. These include the
implementation of numbing responses that inhibit expression of distress (Horowitz
& Solomon, 1975), preoccupation with more immediate needs (e.g., pain, surgery)
that distract attention from one’s symptoms (Andreasen, 2004), the increase of stress-
ors in the posttrauma period that compound the initial stress response (Bryant &
Harvey, 2002), or rising demands on cognitive resources that are required to man-
age emotional responses and then lead to increased distress (Grossman, Levin, Kat-
zen, & Lechner, 2004). There is evidence that it represents subsyndromal levels of
PTSD that accumulate over time, potentially as a function of increasing posttrau-
matic stress; that is, prospective evidence indicates that cases described as delayed
onset actually display elevated levels of posttraumatic stress in the initial period
after the trauma (Andrews et al., 2007; Horesh, Solomon, Keinan, & Ein-Dor, 2013).
Interesting evidence on this issue emerged in a study on the trajectory of adaptation
after Hurricane Katrina, which indicated that rates of PTSD actually increased over
time following Hurricane Katrina (Kessler et al., 2008). It is possible that the lengthy
periods of relocation, lack of housing, and loss of basic infrastructures resulted in
increased psychological strain, which led to rising rates of PTSD. As mentioned ear-
lier, delayed-onset PTSD appears to be more common in military settings than in
civilian contexts, with accumulating evidence from military populations that sol-
diers do not appear to have PTSD immediately after deployment but do develop the
disorder months later (Andrews et al., 2007; Horesh, Solomon, Keinan, & Ein-Dor,
2013). One possibility is that the relief of leaving the immediate context of threat
and the attention given to soldiers in the immediate postdeployment environment
result in a period of minimal posttraumatic stress; however, as time passes, they are
increasingly aware of the negative aftereffects of their trauma.
It is also worth noting that our understanding of the course of traumatic stress
has become more sophisticated in recent years, as studies have included multiple
time points that allow closer analysis of the course of PTSD over time. For example,
one large study of traumatic injury patients revealed that rather than following a
linear course, at least half of people who have PTSD at one time may not have it at

-- 174 of 498 --

154 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
a subsequent assessment—and then have a worsening of their condition at another
subsequent assessment (Bryant, O’Donnell, Creamer, McFarlane, & Silove, 2013).
This evidence highlights that PTSD can be a dynamic condition that tends to fluctu-
ate according to current life stressors. The course of PTSD has also been the subject
of many studies that have used latent growth mixture modeling, which allows iden-
tification of classes of individual variation over time and describes distinct classes
of trajectory. These studies have reliably demonstrated (1) a resilient class that con-
sistently shows few PTSD symptoms, (2) a recovery class with initial distress then
gradual remission, (3) a delayed reaction class with initial low symptom levels but
increased symptoms over time, and (4) a chronic distress class with consistently high
PTSD levels (Bryant et al., 2015; deRoon-Cassin et al., 2010; Galatzer-Levy et al., 2018).
There is also evidence using network analysis that PTSD as a cohesive construct
develops in the months after trauma exposure; this statistical approach focuses
on relationships between symptoms, which shows that PTSD symptoms are more
loosely interconnected in the acute posttrauma phase, and become more cohesively
interrelated in the following months (Bryant et al., 2017).
This convergent evidence underscores the challenges of predicting subsequent
PTSD from acute reactions because PTSD does not appear to follow a linear course.
Although there is evidence of an association between elevated PTSD symptoms in
the acute phase and development of later PTSD (Jehel, Paterniti, Brunet, Duchet, &
Guelfi, 2003; Karstoft, Galatzer-Levy, Statnikov, & Shalev, 2015; Shalev & Freedman,
2005), these reported associations do not provide us with adequate cutoffs to reli-
ably determine who will develop PTSD. One way of improving possible early detec-
tion of who will develop PTSD comes from a consortium that recently combined
2,473 trauma survivors from 10 longitudinal studies that used a likelihood estimate
approach (Shalev et al., 2019). This study revealed that if a patient reported elevated
PTSD severity, female gender, less than secondary-level education, and history of
interpersonal trauma, that person had a 34% greater likelihood of developing PTSD
than someone without these risk factors. The type of trauma is also a critical factor to
consider, with assaultive injuries being particularly predictive of subsequent devel-
opment of PTSD (Lowe et al., 2020).
CLASSIFICATION
Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
In the discussions preceding DSM-5, considerable attention was devoted to the clas-
sification of PTSD, in terms of both where it belongs in the broader spectrum of
psychiatric disorders and its own definition. One important decision was to reclas-
sify PTSD in a new category of trauma-related and stressor-related disorders (rather
than with the anxiety disorders, where it appeared in DSM-IV). Debate focused on
the possibility of identifying a subset of anxiety disorders termed “fear circuitry dis-
orders” that arise after a traumatic/stressful event. Some experts proposed that this
category would include PTSD, panic disorder/agoraphobia, social anxiety disorder,
and specific phobia (Andrews, Charney, Sirovatka, & Regier, 2009). Although there is
some evidence that aversive or traumatic experiences do precede onset of panic dis-
order (Manfro et al., 1996) and social phobia (McCabe, Antony, Summerfeldt, Liss, &
Swinson, 2003), other evidence suggests a different pattern (Rapee, Litwin, & Barlow,

-- 175 of 498 --

Posttraumatic Stress Disorder 	155
1990). There is also strong evidence that a range of other disorders develop after
trauma exposure. Depression is a commonly observed psychological disorder in sur-
vivors of traumatic events, along with various anxiety disorders (Bonde et al., 2016;
Knowles, Sripada, Defever, & Rauch, 2019; Rytwinski, Scur, Feeny, & Youngstrom,
2013). After the Oklahoma City bombing, 22% of people suffered depression, 9% had
alcohol use disorder, 7% suffered panic disorder, 4% had generalized anxiety disor-
der, and 2% had drug use disorder; overall, 30% of people had a psychiatric disorder
other than PTSD (North et al., 1999). In another study of over 1,000 traumatic injury
survivors, depression was the most common disorder (16%), followed by generalized
anxiety disorder (11%), agoraphobia (9%), and PTSD (9%) (Bryant et al., 2010). These
patterns highlight that PTSD is not the only condition that can develop following
trauma. However, the evidence that these other disorders are not distinctive to the
aftermath of trauma is not consistent with their being part of a fear circuitry cat-
egory. Another challenge is that the fear circuitry construct does not give adequate
attention to other emotions associated with PTSD, including sadness, grief, anger,
guilt, and shame (Friedman, Resick, Bryant, & Brewin, 2011).
One line of research supporting the fear circuitry category comes from neu-
roimaging research. The prevailing model of fear circuitry posits that these disor-
ders are characterized by impaired medial prefrontal cortex activation, which leads
to excessive amygdala response, and this network is responsible for fear reactivity
(Charney, 2003). An increasing body of evidence indicates that whereas fear circuitry
disorders tend to be characterized by excessive amygdala reactivity, and to a lesser
extent impaired regulation of that response by the medial prefrontal cortex (e.g.,
Shin & Liberzon, 2010), different neural networks appear to be involved in nonfear
circuitry anxiety disorders (e.g., Duval, Javanbakht, & Liberzon, 2015; Rauch et al.,
2007). It is important to acknowledge the heterogeneity of PTSD as a disorder and
its components beyond fear-based symptoms. For example, in considering the anhe-
donic symptoms of PTSD, deficits in reward processing for individuals with PTSD
have also been found (see Nawijn et al., 2015, for a review). Additionally, there is
evidence for the dissociative subtype characterized by increased activation of medial
prefrontal regions and hyperinhibition of the amygdala and other limbic regions
(Lanius, Brand, Vermetten, Frewen, & Spiegel, 2012).
Although we have thus far focused primarily on the diagnosis of PTSD based
on DSM-5, it is important to note differences between how PTSD is represented
in DSM-5 and in the 11th revision of the International Classification of Diseases and
Related Health Problems (ICD-11; World Health Organization [WHO], 2019), and how
that may affect case conceptualization and treatment. As mentioned earlier, DSM-5
(American Psychiatric Association, 2013) includes 20 symptoms and four symptom
clusters: intrusions, active avoidance, negative alterations in cognitions and mood,
and arousal. A dissociative subtype reflecting the presence of either derealization
or depersonalization is also included. The ICD-11 has a narrower definition of PTSD
than does the DSM-5. Specifically, PTSD includes only six symptoms, categorized
into three core symptom clusters: intrusions, avoidance of trauma-related thoughts
and behaviors, and an ongoing sense of threat (hyperarousal and startle response)
(Maercker et al., 2013). The symptoms must cause significant functional impairment
and have been present for several weeks. A major goal of the revision to ICD-11 PTSD
criteria was to reduce symptom overlap with other disorders and focus on symptoms
that best discriminate PTSD from other disorders (Brewin et al., 2017). There has

-- 176 of 498 --

156 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
been some evidence that the ICD-11 definition of PTSD better fits data across varied
trauma types and reduces comorbidity with depression in comparison to the DSM-5
definition of PTSD (Hansen, Hyland, Armour, Shevlin, & Elklit, 2015; Morina, van
Emmerik, Andrews, & Brewin, 2014). Other research suggests that the narrow ICD-
11 definition of PTSD may result in underdiagnosis of PTSD and may miss individu-
als in need of treatment (Hafstad, Thoresen, Wentzel-Larsen, Maercker, & Dyb, 2017;
Wisco et al., 2016).
Complex PTSD (CPTSD) is a new diagnosis in ICD-11 characterized by distur-
bances in self-organization that often result from exposure to chronic trauma that
cannot be easily escaped (e.g., childhood sexual abuse, intimate partner violence,
torture) (Maercker et al., 2013). The ICD-11 CPTSD diagnosis requires the presence
of PTSD based on the three ICD-11 symptom clusters described earlier and includes
three additional symptom clusters related to disturbances in self-organization,
including affect dysregulation, negative self-concept, and difficulties in relation-
ships (Cloitre, Garvert, Brewin, Bryant, & Maercker, 2013; WHO, 2019). CPTSD was
first introduced by Herman (1992), who described the impact of repeated traumatic
stressors on self-organization that can develop uniquely from PTSD symptoms. The
ICD-11 CPTSD diagnosis stems from the DSM-IV disorder of extreme stress not
otherwise specified (DESNOS) diagnosis (van der Kolk, Roth, Pelcovitz, Sunday, &
Spinazzola, 2005). Numerous studies have supported CPTSD as a distinct disorder
(Brewin et al., 2017; Cloitre et al., 2013). However, there remains controversy over the
clinical utility of CPTSD beyond other disorders with overlapping symptoms, such
as PTSD and borderline personality disorder (Jowett, Karatzias, Shevlin, & Albert,
2020; Wolf et al., 2015), and as a result, CPTSD was not included as a diagnosis in
DSM-5 (Resick et al., 2012).
Acute Stress Disorder
Since its introduction, the ASD diagnosis has been widely criticized for several rea-
sons. In terms of describing acute posttraumatic stress, approaches that focus on fear
responses alone may neglect other psychological reactions (Isserlin, Zerach, & Solo-
mon, 2008). In contrast to ASD, the construct of acute stress reactions described in
ICD-11 (WHO, 2019) conceptualizes acute stress reaction as a transient reaction that
can be evident immediately after the traumatic event and usually resolves within
2–3 days after trauma exposure. This transient reaction may include a range of emo-
tional, cognitive, behavioral, or somatic reactions. Importantly, acute stress reaction
is no longer included as a diagnosis in ICD-11, but instead is included in the factors
influencing health section in the context of problems associated with harmful or
traumatic events. This change was made in part in order to normalize reactions to
trauma, while still allowing for clinical intervention as a response to amorphous
acute stress reaction as needed (Maercker et al., 2013). It is important to note that
ICD-11 does not hold the same 1-month posttrauma requirement for PTSD diagnosis
as DSM-5, so the need to address symptoms within the first month following trauma
exposure may be somewhat reduced.
A secondary goal of the ASD diagnosis is to discriminate between recent trauma
survivors who are experiencing transient stress reactions and those who are suffer-
ing reactions that will persist into long-term PTSD. A large number of prospective
studies have assessed trauma survivors for ASD in the initial month after trauma

-- 177 of 498 --

Posttraumatic Stress Disorder 	157
and subsequently assessed for PTSD months or years later (for a review, see Bryant,
2011). Across these studies, at least half of trauma survivors with ASD subsequently
meet criteria for PTSD. In contrast, the majority of survivors who eventually devel-
oped PTSD did not initially meet ASD criteria. This pattern suggests that if a major
goal of ASD is to predict who will subsequently develop PTSD, it is failing to identify
half of those who will meet criteria for PTSD at some later time.
DSM-5 reformulated the construct of ASD so that its basis is not solely as a state
that predicts PTSD. Evidence over the past two decades suggests that ASD does not
seem to be able to identify the majority of people who develop PTSD upon exposure
to a traumatic event (Bryant, Friedman, Spiegel, Ursano, & Strain, 2011). For the cur-
rent DSM edition, the goal is to describe severe acute stress reactions that may or
may not be transient responses but might nonetheless benefit from mental health
interventions. The ASD definition now requires that the patient satisfy a minimum
of nine out of a total 14 potential symptoms in recognition that ASD presentations
are heterogeneous.
Clinical Considerations with Classification
Since trauma is so ubiquitous and certain types of trauma exposure are related to
increased risk for PTSD, it is important to assess for trauma exposure and related
PTSD symptoms in clinical assessments to ensure that something critical is not
missed. As described earlier, PTSD is not the only psychological disorder to develop
as a result of trauma, so an individual’s presenting concern may not include trauma-
specific symptoms. Clinicians should keep in mind that the client’s understanding of
the potential connectedness between psychological symptoms and trauma exposure
may vary. Additionally, the dynamic nature of PTSD means that symptoms may
come and go, and may be influenced by changing environmental factors or stress.
When asking about trauma exposure, it is helpful to ask about specific traumas and
obtain enough information to understand broadly what happened without asking
for so much detail as to unnecessarily retraumatize the individual during the assess-
ment process. As described later, a major component of trauma-focused treatment
is going into significant detail about the trauma memory; however, in the context of
initial clinical assessment and case conceptualization, that level of detail is unnec-
essary. If a patient is sharing significant detail early on, it can be helpful to praise
their openness and acknowledge that there will be later opportunities to go into
detail, while gently redirecting to the goals of the assessment sessions. Exact diagno-
sis will clearly depend on the classification system that is used; however, it is help-
ful to remember that even clinically significant subthreshold symptoms that cause
functional impairment can be addressed in the context of trauma-focused treatment.
ASSOCIATED CLINICAL FEATURES
There is much evidence that PTSD is linked to a wide range of other mental health
and psychosocial problems, both of which contribute to impaired functioning and
also compound the PTSD itself. PTSD has been linked to a large range of impairment
indicators, including difficulties with employment, homelessness, social engage-
ment, intimate partner relationships, and parenting, as well as poorer self-rated

-- 178 of 498 --

158 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
health and higher utilization of medical services (Rodriguez, Holowka, & Marx,
2012; Tuerk et al., 2013). Impairment is associated with a wide range of PTSD symp-
toms (Ross, Murphy, & Armour, 2018), although there is evidence that avoidance and
numbing are the PTSD symptoms that most contribute to impairment (Shea, Vuja-
novic, Mansfield, Sevin, & Liu, 2010). There is convergent evidence that trauma expo-
sure is linked to a range of poor physical health outcomes, and it appears that this
effect can be largely attributed to PTSD (Schnurr, Wachen, Green, & Kaltman, 2014).
Several mechanisms have been proposed for this relationship, including biological
(e.g., increased cardiovascular reactivity, altered hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal
[HPA] activity), behavioral (e.g., increased substance use, smoking), and psychologi-
cal (e.g., chronic depression, hostility) factors that may impair health (Friedman &
McEwen, 2004). PTSD is also linked to impaired sleep function (Germain, McKeon,
& Campbell, 2017); not only do nightmares and hyperarousal lead to sleep distur-
bance, but also sleep disturbance immediately prior to trauma exposure enhances
risk for PTSD (Bryant, Creamer, O’Donnell, Silove, & McFarlane, 2010). Finally, it is
important to note that suicide risk is increased with PTSD across veteran and civilian
populations (Gradus, 2018; Krysinska & Lester, 2010). The association between PTSD
and suicide is complex, and the issue of suicide risk in PTSD is particularly relevant
because of the elevated rates of suicidality in other mental disorders (e.g., depression)
that are highly comorbid with PTSD.
EPIDEMIOLOGY
Population studies have shown that the majority of people in the community have
been exposed to traumatic stressors. The U.S. National Comorbidity Survey indi-
cated that 61% of randomly sampled adults reported exposure to a traumatic stressor
(Kessler, Sonnega, Hughes, & Nelson, 1995). More recent national- and community-
level assessments indicate that closer to 90% of adults are exposed to a traumatic
stressor (Breslau, 2009; Kilpatrick et al., 2013). Despite the frequency of exposure to
potentially traumatizing events, relatively few people actually develop PTSD. For
example, the National Comorbidity Survey revealed that only 20.4% of the women
and 8.2% of the men ever developed PTSD (Kessler et al., 1995). Similarly, based on
DSM-5 criteria, the National Stressful Events Survey revealed that only 12.8% of the
women and 5.7% of the men had developed PTSD (Kilpatrick et al., 2013). Consistent
evidence supports greater risk for the development of PTSD in women than men that
is not attributable to type or frequency of trauma exposure (Breslau, 2009); although
factors increasing women’s risk are not fully understood, there is growing evidence
of the importance of neurobiological differences that may contribute to women’s
increased risk (Pineles, Hall, & Rasmusson, 2017).
Prevalence rates are not the same everywhere. Across countries and cultural
settings there is marked variability in the occurrence of PTSD. The World Mental
Health Survey has conducted the most global analysis of PTSD rates, and this study
found that DSM-IV-defined PTSD (12-month prevalence rates) was higher in high-
income countries (Northern Ireland = 3.8%; United States = 2.5%; New Zealand =
2.1%) than in low-income countries (Columbia = 0.3%; Mexico = 0.3%) (Karam et al.,
2014). These studies indicate that the normative response following trauma exposure
is to adapt to the experience and to not develop PTSD.

-- 179 of 498 --

Posttraumatic Stress Disorder 	159
There is good evidence that PTSD risk is associated with different types of trau-
matic events. The risk for PTSD is lower following natural disasters (5–10%) relative
to sexual assault (>40%) (Creamer, Burgess, & McFarlane, 2001; Kessler et al., 1995).
Also, interpersonal violence typically is associated with higher risk for PTSD, and
particularly following sexual violence (Forbes et al., 2012, 2014). The World Mental
Health Survey reported that organized, physical, or sexual violence increased risk
for PTSD (Liu et al., 2017). In short, a person is more likely to develop PTSD when
they experience prolonged and severe trauma. This includes individuals living in
civil conflict and refugees (Charlson et al., 2019). Military combat is also associated
with increased risk for PTSD (Kilpatrick et al., 2013).
It is worth noting that comparing prevalence rates across studies is difficult
because of methodological differences, different patterns in trauma exposure, vari-
ability in access to health care, and cultural distinctiveness in the definition of post-
traumatic symptoms. Accordingly, to address the role of race and ethnicity in PTSD,
it is important to consider racial and ethnic variation in similar settings, with expo-
sure to comparable traumatic events. In this context, there are different prevalence
rates across racial and ethnic groupings. For example, in the wake of the September
11, 2001, terrorist attack in New York, Hispanic, Black, and other racial and ethnic/
minority individuals were more likely than White individuals to develop PTSD
(Adams & Boscarino, 2005; DiGrande et al., 2008). Of note, a recent review shows evi-
dence of increased conditional risk for PTSD in Latinx adults (Alcántara, Casement,
& Lewis-Fernandez, 2013). In addition to civilian samples, there is also evidence in
military samples that minorities are particularly at risk of PTSD development (Sch-
lenger et al., 1992; Smith, Goldstein, & Grant, 2016). It is difficult to identify the reason
for this pattern; it may be attributable to lower socioeconomic status and associated
resource availability to manage trauma, the ongoing chronic stress of racial discrimi-
nation, or cultural differences in expressing and coping with adversity (Ruef, Litz,
& Schlenger, 2000). Evidence of lifetime rates of PTSD as high as 50% in low-income
urban minority communities (Gillespie et al., 2009) highlights how critical it is to
understand the intersection between sociocultural factors that increase risk and
ensure that interventions are developed to target those at highest risk.
Clinical Considerations for Epidemiology
There is significant awareness of the need to treat PTSD and other trauma-related
symptoms in veterans, and many resources have been put toward these efforts.
However, for other at-risk groups, such as low-income minority individuals, aware-
ness and resources are more limited. While stigma and barriers exist for treatment
engagement in both veterans and civilians, these are particularly present for civil-
ians who often are unaware that PTSD is something other than a “war-related” prob-
lem. For those working in settings that serve underserved and underprivileged com-
munities, trauma-informed care and screening for trauma and PTSD are critical.
COMORBIDITY
There is overwhelming evidence that PTSD is more often associated with other psychi-
atric disorders than occurring as the sole diagnosis. Lifetime comorbidity prevalence

-- 180 of 498 --

160 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
rates with PTSD have been reported in at least two-thirds of cases (Kessler et al., 1995).
Recent estimates in veterans with PTSD show similar rates (Smith et al., 2016). The
major overlap is between PTSD and depression, anxiety disorders, and substance use
disorders. Although it is possible that a degree of comorbidity may be attributed to
overlapping symptoms between PTSD and both depressive and anxiety disorders,
evidence indicates that there is marked comorbidity with these disorders and PTSD,
even when researchers control for this overlap (Flory & Yehuda, 2015). There is also
considerable comorbidity with personality disorders (Smith et al., 2016). PTSD has fre-
quently been shown to be comorbid with borderline personality disorder, which argu-
ably is not surprising, as there is often a significant trauma history in the profiles of
those with borderline personality disorder (Golier et al., 2003). There is also significant
overlap between PTSD and antisocial personality disorder (Sareen, Stein, Cox, & Has-
sard, 2004); it has been suggested that the antisocial tendencies may precede the PTSD,
and that dangerous behaviors may predispose the person to suffer traumatic events.
Two major mechanisms have been proposed to explain these high rates of
comorbidity. One pathway may involve people developing a psychiatric disorder,
which predisposes them to experiencing trauma and associated PTSD. The other
pathway is the primary onset of PTSD, which leads to simultaneous or subsequent
comorbid disorders. It appears that a significant proportion of comorbid cases reflect
the development of PTSD after the initial onset of another disorder (Breslau, 2009).
Anxiety disorders and substance use disorders may be particularly influential in
increasing risk for trauma exposure and PTSD (e.g., Stein et al., 2002). There is also
strong evidence that much comorbidity development follows PTSD onset, implying
that PTSD can increase the likelihood of developing distinct disorders (Breslau, 2009;
Perkonigg, Kessler, Storz, & Wittchen, 2000). One important consequence of PTSD is
increased substance use, which has been conceptualized as a form of self-medication
and can lead to marked long-term dysfunction in the context of chronic PTSD (Hawn,
Cusack, & Amstadter, 2020; Ouimette, Read, Wade, & Tirone, 2010).
ETIOLOGY
Learning and Biological Theories of PTSD
Biological models of PTSD have predominantly focused on fear conditioning concepts
to explain the genesis and maintenance of PTSD (Milad, Rauch, Pitman, & Quirk,
2006). Fear conditioning models posit that when a traumatic event (unconditioned
stimulus) occurs, people typically respond with fear (unconditioned response). It is
argued that the strong fear elicited by the trauma will lead to strong associative
conditioning between the fear and the events surrounding the trauma. As remind-
ers of the trauma occur (conditioned stimuli), people then respond with fear reac-
tions (conditioned response). It is postulated that release of stress neurochemicals
(including norepinephrine and epinephrine) in the context of the traumatic experi-
ence results in very strong conditioning and overconsolidation of trauma memories
(Rauch, Shin, & Phelps, 2006). This model proposes that successful recovery from
trauma involves extinction learning, in which repeated exposure to trauma remind-
ers or memories results in new learning that these reminders no longer signal threat
(Davis & Myers, 2002).

-- 181 of 498 --

Posttraumatic Stress Disorder 	161
In support of these proposals, there is evidence that people who eventually
develop PTSD display elevated resting heart rates (Shalev et al., 1998) and respiration
rates (Bryant, Creamer, O’Donnell, Silove, & McFarlane, 2008) in the initial days after
trauma. There have been reports that other indices of elevated arousal in the imme-
diate posttrauma phase are associated with subsequent PTSD, such as lower gamma-
aminobutyric acid (GABA) levels (Vaiva et al., 2006). Psychophysiological studies
during the acute trauma phase have also explored the responses of individuals using
their own recollections of the trauma as the eliciting stimulus. A study of patients
in the Emergency Department posttrauma found that higher skin conductance
response during a bedside trauma interview was predictive of the development of
chronic PTSD (Hinrichs et al., 2019). Other paradigms using trauma narratives in the
acute trauma phase also find larger psychophysiological responses, including heart
rate and skin conductance among those who go on to develop PTSD (Goodman &
Griffin, 2018; Gutner et al., 2010). Importantly, psychophysiological response (acous-
tic startle response, skin conductance) is also an indicator of treatment response in
trauma-focused treatment (Maples-Keller et al., 2019; Wangelin & Tuerk, 2015).
Biological models propose that PTSD occurs when the amygdala is hyperre-
sponsive to fear-related stimuli and there is a concomitant lack of “top-down” inhi-
bition from the medial prefrontal cortex (MPFC; Rauch et al., 2006). There is reduced
MPFC recruitment in PTSD during fear processing, particularly in rostral anterior
cingulate cortex (Hayes, Hayes, & Mikedis, 2012; Shin et al., 2005). There is also evi-
dence for increased amygdala response to fear stimuli in PTSD (see review by Patel,
Spreng, Shin, & Girard, 2012), although individuals with the dissociative subtype of
PTSD may not show this pattern and instead show hyperinhibition of the amygdala
and other limbic regions (Lanius et al., 2012). Impairment in hippocampal activation
is also implicated in individuals with PTSD, representing deficits in memory and in
the ability to use contextual cues appropriately in the context of fear learning (Joshi,
Duval, Kubat, & Liberzon, 2020). It is worth noting that trauma-focused therapy is
conceptualized as a form of extinction learning, in which conditioned fear responses
are inhibited by learning of new, safe associations (Rothbaum & Davis, 2003). In
this context, it is interesting to note that the same neural networks implicated in
extinction learning are also involved in successful cognitive-behavioral treatment
(CBT; described in the treatment section below). The neural mechanisms implicated
in extinction learning (i.e., MPFC–amygdala network) have been shown to predict
positive response to CBT in patients with PTSD in both functional (Bryant, Felming-
ham, Kemp, et al., 2008) and structural (Bryant, Felmingham, Whitford, et al., 2008)
magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). Furthermore, as patients with PTSD success-
fully respond to CBT, they are better able to recruit the MPFC during fear processing
(Felmingham et al., 2007; Shou et al., 2017).
Cognitive Models of PTSD
Cognitive models of PTSD posit that the development and maintenance of PTSD
rely heavily on two processes: (1) maladaptive appraisals of the trauma and its after-
math, and (2) disturbances in autobiographical memory (Ehlers & Clark, 2000). These
models posit that trauma memories are encoded in a fragmented manner because
of the elevated arousal that occurs at the time of trauma. It is proposed that the

-- 182 of 498 --

162 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
disorganized nature of these memories precludes a coherent narrative, and this can
impede adaptive processing of the experience and limit the extent to which the trau-
matic experience can be placed in the context of other nontraumatic memories the
person may retrieve. It is also proposed that the manner in which people appraise
the traumatic event, their responses to it, and their environment after the trauma are
pivotal in terms of perpetuating their sense of threat or sense of vulnerability.
There is a great body of evidence supporting this model. Individuals with PTSD
have an attentional bias to threat stimuli (e.g., Fani et al., 2012; McNally, Kaspi, Rie-
mann, & Zeitlin, 1990; Naim et al., 2015; Powers et al., 2019), are deficient in recalling
specific positive memories (McNally, Lasko, Macklin, & Pitman, 1995; Schönfeld &
Ehlers, 2017), and engage in maladaptive thoughts about how they responded to the
trauma or to future negative events that may happen to them (Dekel, Peleg, & Solo-
mon, 2013; Warda & Bryant, 1998; Zuj et al., 2017). Catastrophic appraisals about the
experience shortly after the trauma are also predictive of subsequent PTSD (Ehlers,
Mayou, & Bryant, 1998; Nixon & Bryant, 2005).
RISK FACTORS
The robust finding that only a minority of trauma survivors develop PTSD raises
the issue about variables that increase risk for PTSD development. In terms of pre-
existing risk factors, certain biological and cognitive characteristics (e.g., HPA axis
dysfunction, pretrauma cognitive biases), prior psychological disturbance, fam-
ily history of psychological disorders, childhood maltreatment, and female gender
are predictive of PTSD development (Bomyea, Risbrough, & Lang, 2012; Brewin,
Andrews, & Valentine, 2000; Trickey, Siddaway, Meiser-Stedman, Serpell, & Field,
2012). Recent studies in trauma patients have also indicated that higher levels of emo-
tion dysregulation at the time of trauma exposure increases risk for the development
of PTSD independent of co-occurring psychiatric symptoms (Forbes et al., 2020; Pen-
cea et al., 2020). In terms of factors associated with the trauma, a major predictor of
PTSD is the nature of the traumatic event itself. The more one is exposed to threat
and to grotesque or distressing events, the more likely one is to develop PTSD (Bre-
slau, 2009; Brewin et al., 2000). As noted earlier in the discussion of ASD, there is
considerable evidence that peritraumatic responses can predict subsequent PTSD.
Symptom severity, including dissociation, reexperiencing, avoidance, and arousal,
several weeks after exposure can be predictive of subsequent PTSD. Consistent with
cognitive models, there is evidence that catastrophic appraisals of the stressor and
of one’s reactions to the stressor influence whether chronic PTSD develops (Ehlers et
al., 1998). In relation to posttrauma factors, there is evidence that self-reported levels
of low social support and the presence of ongoing stressors are important predictors
of PTSD (Brewin et al., 2000; Nillni et al., 2013).
Most of the literature on risk factors for PTSD has been limited by inferring
risk on the basis of data collected retrospectively after trauma exposure; this data
can be confounded by mood biases in retrospective reports. In more recent years,
researchers have focused on populations with high risk for trauma exposure and
have assessed these people prior to trauma exposure, with subsequent follow-up
after trauma exposure. A systematic review (DiGangi et al., 2013) of all prospective

-- 183 of 498 --

Posttraumatic Stress Disorder 	163
studies from 1991 to 2013 that included measures both pre- and posttrauma expo-
sure, found that lower cognitive ability, negative cognitive bias, personality charac-
teristics (e.g., neuroticism, hostility), prior psychopathology and trauma exposure,
higher levels of physiological arousal, negative family environment, poor social sup-
port, and poverty all increased risk for PTSD. Recent evidence with soldiers pre- and
postdeployment indicates related patterns. For example, predeployment neuroticism
and dissociation are associated with postdeployment development of PTSD (Ponce
de León, Andersen, Karstoft, & Elklit, 2018). Lower levels of heart rate variability
(HRV) predeployment predict higher levels of PTSD postdeployment (Minassian et
al., 2015). Additionally, certain attention biases predeployment, namely, cognitive
avoidance of threat-related stimuli, are predictive of greater PTSD symptoms post-
deployment even in the context of less war zone stress (Beevers, Lee, Wells, Ellis,
& Telch, 2011). There is also evidence that the tendency to engage in catastrophic
thinking before trauma exposure predicts subsequent PTSD in firefighters (Bryant
& Guthrie, 2005, 2007).
Twin studies indicate the role of genetic contribution, in that heritability accounts
for up to 30% of the variance in PTSD symptoms (True et al., 1993). It is important to
note, however, that the genetic influences on PTSD are common to other (frequently
comorbid with PTSD) disorders, including depression, other anxiety disorders, and
substance use (Koenen et al., 2008; Smoller, 2016). Using MRI to study monozygotic
co-twins, Gilbertson and colleagues (2002) found that Vietnam veterans with PTSD
were characterized by smaller hippocampi than those without PTSD, but that the
co-twins of veterans with PTSD (who had not served in Vietnam) had hippocampi
that were just as small. These findings suggest that small hippocampal volume may
constitute a vulnerability factor for PTSD among people exposed to trauma, sup-
ported by a recent treatment study demonstrating that smaller hippocampal volume
is not the result of trauma exposure and does not increase with successful PTSD
treatment (van Rooij et al., 2015). There is evidence that genetic differences may
influence the development of PTSD, although clear understanding of genetic risk
has been hampered by small sample sizes and varying results in candidate gene
studies (see Sheerin, Lind, Bountress, Nugent, & Amstadter, 2017 for a review). For
example, while it is the most widely studied polymorphisms, meta-analyses of the
serotonin transporter (5-HTTLPR) have found no overall association with PTSD,
although it may be important in consideration of potential gene × environment inter-
actions with level of trauma exposure (Armbruster et al., 2009; Gressier et al., 2013;
Navarro-Mateu, Escámez, Koenen, Alonso, & Sánchez-Meca, 2013). Candidate genes
involved in the regulation of the HPA axis such as FK-506 binding protein (FKBP5;
Watkins et al., 2016) and PACAP (pituitary adenylate cyclase-activating polypeptide,
coded by ADCYAP1 gene, associated with estrogen response) (Stevens et al., 2014)
may be important to risk for PTSD. Genomewide association studies (GWAS) offer an
unbiased approach to test associations of genetic variants across the entire genome
with a construct of interest, and there have been numerous GWAS studies of PTSD
to date (see Daskalakis, Rijal, King, Huckins, & Ressler, 2018, for a review). Epigenetic
research on PTSD is also promising and may help us to better understand the ways
that trauma exposure (among other environmental stressors) impact outcomes of
DNA through changes to the epigenome, which then confer risk for the development
of PTSD (Howie, Rijal, & Ressler, 2019).

-- 184 of 498 --

164 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
It is increasingly clear that function of the inflammatory system is also impor-
tant to consider in relation to PTSD (Michopoulos, Powers, Gillespie, Ressler, & Jova-
novic, 2017). In the context of trauma exposure, the immune system is activated and
with chronic exposure can become dysregulated; immune function and HPA axis
function are closely interconnected, and the dysregulation of both are implicated in
PTSD. While accumulative evidence shows immune dysregulation in PTSD (Passos
et al., 2015), the directionality of the association remains unclear. Numerous prospec-
tive studies have shown that higher levels of inflammation pretrauma are associated
with increased risk of PTSD posttrauma (see Michopoulos et al., 2017, for a review).
There is also evidence that people’s capacity for conditioning may be a risk factor
for PTSD development. One study compared startle responses in pairs of Vietnam
combat veterans and their non-combat-exposed monozygotic twins (Orr et al., 2003).
Orr and colleagues (2003) found evidence of more slowly habituating skin conduc-
tance to startle responses in veterans with PTSD and their non-combat-exposed co-
twins compared to veterans without PTSD and their non-combat-exposed co-twins.
This finding suggests that more slowly habituating skin conductance responses to
startle stimuli may represent a pretrauma vulnerability factor for PTSD. A prospec-
tive study of newly recruited firefighters indicated that skin conductance and eye-
blink electromyographic (EMG) startle responses before trauma exposure in a cohort
of trainees predicted acute stress reactions after subsequent trauma exposure (Guth-
rie & Bryant, 2005). Other research indicates that impaired extinction learning prior
to trauma exposure is predictive of subsequent posttraumatic stress in firefighters
and paramedics (Guthrie & Bryant, 2006).
TREATMENT
Chronic PTSD
The treatment of choice for chronic PTSD is trauma-focused psychotherapy. Com-
prehensive treatment based on systematic reviews of the evidence including guide-
lines from the U.K. National Institute for Clinical Excellence (2018), the U.S. Veterans
Department/Department of Defense (VA/DoD, 2017), the U.S. Institute of Medicine
(2011), the Australian National Health and Medical Research Council (Phoenix Aus-
tralia, 2013), the American Psychological Association (2017), and the International
Society for Traumatic Stress Studies (Bisson et al., 2019) have reached this conclusion.
A similar conclusion has been drawn from major meta-analyses (Bradley, Greene,
Russ, Dutra, & Westen, 2005; Cusack et al., 2016). Importantly, trauma-focused psy-
chotherapy has been shown to be efficacious across different trauma-exposed popu-
lations, including survivors of traumatic injury and assault (Bryant, Moulds, et al.,
2008), sexual assault (Foa, Rothbaum, Riggs, & Murdock, 1991), combat (Castillo et
al., 2016; Schnurr et al., 2003), terrorist attacks (Duffy, Gillespie, & Clark, 2007), and
child sexual abuse (McDonagh et al., 2005).
The major form of trauma-focused psychotherapy, CBT, usually commences
with psychoeducation about the trauma responses, then focuses on three major
strategies: anxiety management, exposure, and cognitive restructuring. In psycho-
education, the patient is informed about common symptoms following a traumatic
event and the way in which the core symptoms will be treated during the course of

-- 185 of 498 --

Posttraumatic Stress Disorder 	165
therapy. Anxiety management techniques aim to reduce anxiety through a range
of techniques that may include breathing retraining, relaxation skills, and self-talk.
Exposure therapy usually involves both imaginal and in vivo exposure. Imaginal
exposure typically requires patients to imagine their traumatic experience vividly
for prolonged periods, usually for at least 30 minutes. The therapist asks patients
to provide a narrative of their traumatic experience in a way that emphasizes all
relevant details, including sensory cues and affective responses. In vivo exposure
involves graded exposure to feared stimuli, in which patients are asked to remain in
close proximity to mildly fearful reminders of the trauma, then repeat this exercise
with increasingly fearful situations until they feel comfortable with most reminders
of their experience. Exposure may be therapeutic because of habituation of anxiety,
integration of corrective information, knowledge that the trauma is a discrete event
that is no longer threatening, and self-mastery through management of exposure
(Rothbaum & Schwartz, 2002). Cognitive restructuring involves teaching patients
to identify and evaluate the evidence for negative automatic thoughts, as well as
helping them to evaluate their beliefs about the trauma, the self, the world, and the
future. In most controlled trials, CBT programs usually require eight to 14 sessions
conducted on an individual basis.
The majority of studies indicate that treatments incorporating exposure either to
trauma memories or situations that remind the individual of trauma are beneficial.
There is some debate over the relative contributions of different CBT components,
although evidence of the specific comparable contributions of various components of
CBT remains insufficient (Cusack et al., 2016). Several studies suggest that exposure
and cognitive restructuring provide comparable treatment gains, and others indi-
cate that combining cognitive restructuring with exposure does not provide addi-
tive effects above beyond exposure alone (Foa et al., 2005). There is some evidence
that the addition of cognitive restructuring may enhance the effect of exposure (Bry-
ant et al., 2003, 2008). The overall finding that other CBT components do not mark-
edly enhance exposure suggests that exposure is a critical ingredient in facilitating
recovery from PTSD (Cahill, Rothbaum, Resick, & Follette, 2009). Because exposure
typically includes a form of cognitive reframing in which the therapist discusses
the mastery of exposure to trauma reminders, it seems that the change mechanisms
involved in both exposure and cognitive restructuring may overlap. In summary,
the largest body of evidence supports the use of exposure-based CBT that typically
employs exposure to trauma memories and feared situations that result in a sense of
safety or mastery over those stimuli.
Prolonged exposure (PE) is a variant of CBT and has received strong support
for its effectiveness in treating PTSD across varied populations for a range of trauma
types (Cusack et al., 2016; Powers, Halpern, Ferenschak, Gillihan, & Foa, 2010). PE is
a manualized treatment that includes many of the components already described
earlier but with one major difference, in that there is not an explicit cognitive restruc-
turing component. The goal of PE is to alter fear structures so that they are no longer
problematic; thus, the imaginal and in vivo exposures are focused on activating the
fear structure and incorporating new corrective information into the structure (Wat-
kins, Sprang, & Rothbaum, 2018). While PE generally is provided in eight to 15 ses-
sions, reduced formats (five sessions in primary care settings) (Rauch, Cigrang, Aus-
tern, Evans, & Strong Star Consortium, 2017) and application in intensive outpatient

-- 186 of 498 --

166 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
program settings (e.g., Zalta et al., 2018) have been developed and showed promise
for improving outcomes.
Another variant of trauma-focused CBT, cognitive processing therapy (CPT),
was developed as a form of cognitive therapy to address the specific cognitive
responses of survivors of sexual assault (Resick & Schnicke, 1993); since its devel-
opment CPT has shown effectiveness for a wide range of trauma types (Watkins et
al., 2018). In CPT, attention is focused on reframing unrealistic beliefs about safety,
trust, control, esteem, and intimacy. Originally, CPT also engaged trauma memories
by requiring the patient to write detailed accounts of the trauma and relating these
accounts to the therapist. However, a trial that compared (1) full CPT, (2) the writing
component, and (3) the cognitive component found that the writing component did
not provide any additive gain over the cognitive element (Resick et al., 2008) led to
the removal of the written narrative component in the most recent manual (Resick,
Monson, & Chard, 2016). CPT and PE have shown similar effectiveness, and both are
recommended as frontline psychotherapy approaches for PTSD.
A third variant of CBT is eye movement desensitization and reprocessing
(EMDR). This treatment differs from CBT insofar as it requires patients to focus their
attention on a traumatic memory, while simultaneously visually tracking the thera-
pist’s finger as it is moved across their visual field, then to engage in restructuring of
the memory (Shapiro, 1995). The patient is asked to identify more adaptive or posi-
tive thoughts related to the memory or traumatic experience, and to again track the
therapist’s fingers. Although the specific mechanisms by which eye movements are
purported to enhance treatment gains are not well specified, moving one’s eyes after
engaging the trauma memory is intended to facilitate information processing (Sha-
piro & Maxfield, 2002). Across a series of controlled trials, EMDR has been shown
to be an effective treatment for PTSD relative to wait list and to perform comparably
to standard CBT. Accordingly, it is recommended, along with trauma-focused CBT,
PE, and CPT, as a frontline treatment in a number of treatment guidelines (Phoe-
nix Australia, 2013; VA/DoD, 2017). In evaluating EMDR, however, it is important to
note that eye movements are not pivotal in the treatment effects of EMDR (Phoenix
Australia, 2013). This conclusion is supported by evidence from studies that manipu-
lated eye movement, in addition to other strategies, and found that the eye move-
ments themselves were not important in relieving the symptoms relative to the other
components of EMDR (Foley & Spates, 1995). Additionally, the Australian National
Health and Medical Research council (NHMRC) guidelines concluded that EMDR
was as efficacious as CBT only when EMDR also included in vivo exposure (Phoenix
Australia, 2013).
There has been concern that CBT techniques may not be appropriate for patients
with symptoms consistent with ICD-11 CPTSD. On the premise that patients with
severe emotion regulation deficits may have difficulty managing the emotional
demands of exposure-based therapy, attempts have been made to prepare these
patients for trauma-focused CBT approaches by teaching them skills to regulate
emotions and improve interpersonal relations. These skills involve distress toler-
ance, labeling emotions, and emotion management, as well as interpersonal compo-
nents related to maladaptive schemas, assertiveness, and flexibility in interpersonal
engagement. One approach designed specifically to treat CPTSD is skills training in

-- 187 of 498 --

Posttraumatic Stress Disorder 	167
affective and interpersonal regulation (STAIR; Cloitre, Koenen, Cohen, & Han, 2002).
Two randomized trials have now demonstrated that this modified form of CBT is
efficacious in treating CPTSD, and there is evidence that it is more effective than
standard CBT for patients with CPTSD (Cloitre et al., 2010). Considering that people
who suffer more severe traumatic exposures, and often endure prolonged childhood
trauma, can have difficulty regulating emotions, this approach appears to augment
the efficacy of CBT. Overlap exists between CPTSD and borderline personality dis-
order, and dialectical behavior therapy with PE (DBT-PE) has shown promise in
improving outcomes for individuals with comorbid borderline personality disorder
and PTSD (Harned, Korslund, Foa, & Linehan, 2012). It is important to acknowledge
that while research regarding STAIR continues (Oprel et al., 2018), current evidence
for a phase-based approach to treating CPTSD is limited and general guidelines
remain for the use of frontline treatment approaches (De Jongh et al., 2016), while
balancing patient preferences and service availability.
An important caveat exists for the support for trauma-focused CBT. Although it is
without doubt the best treatment we have for PTSD, and most patients respond posi-
tively, between one-third and one-half of patients are not responsive to this approach
and continue to experience ongoing symptoms (Bradley et al., 2005; Loerinc et al.,
2015). This has led to many attempts to augment trauma-focused CBT, and these
have typically tried to promote extinction processes in combination with exposure
therapy. Pharmacological agents to achieve this have included the partial glutama-
tergic agonist D-cycloserine (DCS), which has yielded mixed findings in enhancing
the benefits of trauma-focused CBT (de Kleine, Hendriks, Kusters, Broekman, & van
Minnen, 2012; Litz et al., 2012; Rothbaum et al., 2014); glucocorticoid administration
to facilitate extinction, which has been shown in one pilot study to augment trauma-
focused CBT (Yehuda et al., 2015) but has not been proven in full trial; yohimbine,
which was found in one study that combined administration with trauma-focused
CBT to yield no effect for yohimbine at follow-up; and MDMA (3,4-methylenedioxy-
methamphetamine), which has been shown in several pilot studies to augment the
effects of trauma-focused CBT but not replicated in proper trials (Mithoefer et al.,
2018; Ot’alora et al., 2018). Other studies have employed brain stimulation techniques,
such as transcranial direct current stimulation and transcranial magnetic stimula-
tion, but they currently lack adequate evidence (Kozel et al., 2018). Overall, although
some of these approaches appear to show promise and deserve further investigation,
the current evidence does not support these approaches (Lebois, Seligowski, Wolff,
Hill, & Ressler, 2019).
CBT for Children
Compared to studies of adults, relatively less attention has been given to treatment
of childhood PTSD. In general terms, children appear to experience PTSD as often
as adults. Some differences across developmental stages have been shown, partic-
ularly in young children (Scheeringa, Myers, Putnam, & Zeanah, 2012). In DSM-5
(American Psychiatric Association, 2013), PTSD diagnosis is the same for children
over the age of 6, although some of the symptoms allow for different presentation
(e.g., repetitive play with themes or aspects of the trauma as a component of intrusive

-- 188 of 498 --

168 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
symptoms). For children age 6 and younger, there is a new developmental subtype of
PTSD: PTSD in children 6 years and younger. Treatment programs for children often
modify CBT by integrating strategies that teach regulation of affect or behavior,
including parents in treatment, adapting treatment delivery to the developmental
stage of the child, and placing strong emphasis on rapport factors between therapist
and patient (Cohen, Mannarino, Deblinger, & Berliner, 2009). Consistent with the
adult literature, available trials support the conclusion that trauma-focused psycho-
therapy, and in particular, trauma-focused CBT for children and adolescents is the
treatment of choice for childhood PTSD (de Arellano et al., 2014; Dorsey et al., 2017;
Jensen, Holt, & Ormhaug, 2017).
Global Early Interventions
A major debate that has persisted over recent years concerns the merits of inter-
vening shortly after a traumatic event potentially to prevent PTSD. For many years,
psychological debriefing has been the most common approach immediately after
trauma exposure, and critical incident stress debriefing (CISD) was arguably the
most common variant of this approach (Mitchell, 1983). In the original iteration of this
strategy, a debriefing session that typically occurred within 48 hours of the trauma
involved education about trauma reactions; required participants to describe what
occurred and their cognitive, emotional, and physical responses to the event; and
provided suggestions for stress reduction (Mitchell & Everly, 1996). Although dif-
ficult to assess because of the many uncontrollable factors that exist in a postdisaster
setting, a series of studies have compared trauma survivors who received CISD and
those who did not receive the intervention (Rose, Brewin, Andrews, & Kirk, 1999).
Systematic reviews of these studies indicate that people who receive CISD did not
have better outcomes than those who do not (Rose, Bisson, & Wessely, 2001). Fur-
thermore, some tentative evidence indicates that very distressed people who receive
debriefing immediately after a trauma may subsequently have worse PTSD symp-
toms than those who do not receive debriefing (Hobbs, Mayou, Harrison, & Worlock,
1996). It has been suggested that requiring people to ventilate their emotions within
days of trauma exposure may hasten arousal and strengthen their trauma memories,
which may impede natural recovery (McNally, Bryant, & Ehlers, 2003). In the wake
of this evidence, the field has moved toward recognition that single-session inter-
ventions after trauma may not be sufficient to avert subsequent PTSD. At present,
the most common alternative to debriefing is psychological first aid (PFA; Young,
2006), which attempts to assist adaptation by ensuring that patients’ needs are met,
facilitating problem solving, reducing acute arousal, and accessing social supports.
PFA attempts to enhance coping skills in the immediate aftermath of a disaster with-
out causing undue distress by directing the survivor to revisit the traumatic experi-
ence. Importantly, PFA has not yet been tested, and one obstacle to evaluation is that
PFA does not have clearly stated goals or outcomes. Therefore, it is not a standard-
ized intervention that is amenable to controlled evaluation (Dieltjens, Moonens, Van
Praet, De Buck, & Vandekerckhove, 2014; Fox et al., 2012). Whereas CISD explicitly
attempts to reduce subsequent PTSD, PFA assists coping rather than serving a sec-
ondary prevention role.

-- 189 of 498 --

Posttraumatic Stress Disorder 	169
Targeted Early Interventions
In contrast to interventions provided to all trauma survivors shortly after exposure,
there has also been a focus on interventions for survivors who are deemed to be at
high risk for developing PTSD. The focus of most of these studies has been on trauma
survivors who display ASD in the first month after trauma exposure because of evi-
dence that these individuals are more likely than other survivors to develop chronic
PTSD (Bryant, 2003). Attempts at secondary prevention have typically modified
standard CBT approaches by abridging them to five or six sessions. The common fac-
tor across interventions for ASD/acute PTSD is that their trauma-focused exposure
approach involves imaginal or in vivo exposure. Across a series of controlled trials,
there is now convergent evidence that exposure-based CBT provided within a month
of trauma exposure prevents PTSD in the majority of cases relative to nondirective
counseling (Bryant, Mastrodomenico, et al., 2008). These treatment gains have been
shown to maintain up to four years after treatment (Bryant et al., 2006). Addition-
ally, providing CBT to trauma survivors with acute PTSD in the initial months after
trauma exposure results in reduced levels of PTSD at subsequent time points (Ehlers
et al., 2003). There is also preliminary evidence of the benefits of a three-session
modified PE for individuals immediately posttrauma, with the intervention initi-
ated bedside in the Emergency Department (Rothbaum et al., 2012). Since many indi-
viduals show natural recovery, it is critical that reliable early risk factors for PTSD
directly following trauma exposure in Emergency Department settings are identi-
fied to improve targeted early intervention approaches (Maples-Keller et al., 2020).
Overall, the evidence indicates that exposure-based therapies are indicated for early
intervention with people with ASD/acute PTSD.
CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS AND PRINCIPLES OF CHANGE
This review has highlighted the heterogeneity of PTSD. The potential variability and
complexity of PTSD highlights how clinicians need to be cognizant of the various
techniques from evidence-supported research that can be administered to posttrau-
matic presentations. One of the first decisions is whether a patient is appropriate for
exposure-based therapy. Although it is the treatment of choice, some patients may
not be able to manage it appropriately for several reasons. Patients who are poten-
tially psychotic, severely suicidal, or substance dependent may be better managed
initially with medication, skills-based interventions (e.g., STAIR, PE), or programs
specifically designed for comorbid PTSD and substance use disorders (i.e., Seeking
Safety), until these conditions are adequately controlled. Any condition that may
undermine extinction learning during exposure should be managed prior to expo-
sure.
Table 6.1 presents a summary of principles and related strategies that can be
applied to the variety of PTSD presentations, and the sequence in which one imple-
ments these strategies is usually determined by several factors. As always, with any
clinical intervention, any presentation that poses a risk for a patient (e.g., self-harm,
reckless behavior) needs to be addressed first.

-- 190 of 498 --

170 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
Having ensured the safety of the patient, the next principle is to sequence those
strategies that lead to generalized improvement. In the context of PTSD, it is often
understood that many of the related problems, including depression, panic symp-
toms, sleep disturbance, or substance use, may be exacerbated by the symptoms of
PTSD. In fact, these related problems often diminish following successful treatment
of the PTSD; accordingly, treatment should focus on exposure and cognitive restruc-
turing strategies, with the expectation that reduction of trauma-related distress will
generalize to improvements in other domains. If residual problems remain, then
specific techniques can be applied to these remaining problems (e.g., sleep hygiene,
panic). In the case of patients who have panic disorder, the clinician who wants to
reduce the likelihood of full panic attacks in the course of exposure can conduct
interoceptive exposure prior to exposure.
TABLE 6.1. Principles of Change and Treatment Choices
Principle 	Treatment strategy
Understanding of trauma reactions 	Psychoeducation and normalization of symptoms
Enhancing distress tolerance 	Emotion regulation skills training (breathing
retraining, emotion labeling, mindfulness)
Extinction learning of trauma memory 	Prolonged imaginal exposure
Extinction of situational reminders 	In vivo exposure
Modifying maladaptive appraisals of the
trauma or one’s response
Cognitive restructuring
Managing harmful substance use 	Initially provide exposure to reduce source of
self-medication. Give medication and implement
behavioral control and motivational interviewing to
address substance abuse
Reducing sleep disturbance 	Address nightmares through imaginal exposure and,
if necessary, implement a sleep hygiene regimen to
reduce sleep-incompatible behaviors
Reducing rumination on trauma
or one’s response
Prolonged exposure to minimize ruminative
response, motivational interviewing to understand
the impact of rumination, and distraction or
mindfulness to manage rumination
Managing anger 	Motivational interviews concerning the benefits
and costs of anger, cognitive restructuring of beliefs
driving anger, and mindfulness practice to reduce
anger-related thoughts
Addressing comorbid depression 	Prolonged exposure to reduce PTSD symptoms,
cognitive restructuring to address self-esteem
and hopelessness beliefs, and event scheduling to
increase positive activities

-- 191 of 498 --

Posttraumatic Stress Disorder 	171
Another common comorbidity to consider in treatment is traumatic brain injury.
These cases pose a challenge for exposure because they typically involve some
degree of amnesia of the event that triggered the disorder. In mild traumatic brain
injury, there are usually islands of memory that allow exposure to be administered
in the normal manner (Bryant, Moulds, Guthrie, & Nixon, 2003). In more severe cases
of brain injury, when there is often no discursive memory of the trauma, patients
often experience psychological or physiological distress in response to reminders
of the trauma, without actually recalling the event (Bryant, Marosszeky, Crooks, &
Gurka, 2000). In these cases, it is more useful to focus on in vivo rather than imaginal
exposure because situational factors tend to be the major triggers of fear.
It is also crucial to determine whether the patient is ready to allocate sufficient
resources to therapy. It is common in the posttraumatic period, especially in the acute
phase, for the trauma survivor to be challenged with a range of environmental and
personal demands, including pain, surgery, legal processes, housing disturbance, or
ongoing threat. PTSD is also associated with a range of functional challenges, such as
employment problems, health difficulties, marital and interpersonal problems, sleep,
and sexual dysfunction; thus, systematic assessment of these clinical features should
be used in order to determine whether patients can focus appropriately on therapy
when they are attempting to manage the demands of their environment. There is
growing support for the use of comprehensive services that include both evidence-
based therapies and social work services within the same program in medical set-
tings in which high volumes of acute trauma patients are seen (e.g., Trauma Recovery
Centers). In order to respond most effectively to clients’ needs and to reduce possible
interference to therapy, some of these issues may need to be addressed before and/or
during exposure-based therapy. These issues should also be assessed at the end of
successful exposure-based treatment to determine whether additional interventions
are necessary to improve clients’ health and functioning.
CONCLUSION
Much has been learned about human response to psychological trauma since the
introduction of the PTSD diagnosis. In the wake of large-scale natural and man-
made disasters, interpersonal and community violence exposure, and the continu-
ing effects of war, research continues to shed light on the biological, cognitive, and
social factors that influence the different trajectories of response to trauma. Evidence
emerging from these studies continues to shape policy and practice to enhance the
ways we attempt to prevent, assess, and treat PTSD. There is no doubt that traumatic
experiences will continue to affect people at a very frequent rate, and it is critical that
we constantly adopt scientific approaches to help people respond to these events in
ways that enhance adaptation.

185
This chapter presents an overview of the clinical features and implications, risk fac-
tors, and treatment of four eating disorders: anorexia nervosa (AN), bulimia ner-
vosa (BN), binge-eating disorder (BED), and avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder
(ARFID).
TYPICAL SYMPTOMS, COURSE, COMORBIDITY, AND ASSOCIATED FEATURES
Anorexia Nervosa
AN is characterized by failure to maintain a minimally acceptable weight (e.g., a
body mass index [BMI] of less than 18.5 kg/m2, failure to meet expected growth in
youth) and intense fear of being or becoming fat. The revision of the World Health
Organization’s (2019) International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health
Problems (ICD-11) also notes that rapid weight loss (e.g., >20% of total body weight
within 6 months) may satisfy the low weight guideline as long as other diagnostic
requirements are met (Claudino et al., 2019). Patients with AN also place undue value
on their own body shape or weight, and they deny that their low weight is a seri-
ous health concern. Females with AN commonly experience an interruption in men-
strual activity, but as this does not meaningfully distinguish clinical status, it is not
an essential diagnostic feature. There are two subtypes of AN—one for patients who
regularly engage in binge-eating or purging behaviors while underweight, and the
other for patients who simply restrict their food intake. ICD-11 also includes a sever-
ity qualifier to ensure that individuals can retain the AN diagnosis until a “full and
lasting recovery is achieved” (e.g., “healthy weight and the cessation of behaviors
C H A P T E R 7
Eating Disorders
Deborah R. Glasofer
Alexandra F. Muratore
Evelyn Attia
Kathleen M. Pike

-- 206 of 498 --

186 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
leading to weight loss independent of the provision of treatment”; Claudino et al.,
2019).
Psychological symptoms associated with AN include depression, anxiety,
insomnia, and increased obsessionality. These symptoms have also been observed
in non-eating-disordered individuals undergoing an induced state of semistarvation
and in the case of AN are often secondary to underweight status (Attia, 2010). Medi-
cal complications of AN include cardiac abnormalities, osteopenia or osteoporosis,
and endocrine disturbances. Electrolyte imbalances may be present, including hypo-
kalemia in individuals with vomiting or laxative use, and hyponatremia in those
who ingest large amounts of liquids (Attia, 2010).
AN is a serious psychiatric illness associated with increased morbidity and
mortality. Over the long term, while approximately one-half of the individuals with
AN achieve full recovery, approximately one-third remain partially symptomatic,
and approximately one-fifth remain chronically ill. A meta-analysis of 35 published
studies describing mortality rates for AN determined the crude mortality rate to be
5.1 deaths per 1,000 person-years (Arcelus, Mitchell, Wales, & Nielsen, 2011). Mortal-
ity estimates rise for patients who require hospitalization, with as much as a sixfold
increase in comparison to the general population. The cause of death in AN is equally
or more likely to be suicide than to be medical problems secondary to compromised
nutritional status (Papadopoulos, Ekbom, Brandt, & Ekselius, 2009).
High rates of depression, dysthymia, social phobia, generalized anxiety disorder,
specific phobia, and obsessive– compulsive disorder (OCD) are commonly reported
in clinical samples (American Psychiatric Association, 2013). Individuals with AN
report elevated lifetime alcohol use disorder that typically begins after onset of AN;
however, the rate of alcohol use disorder for individuals with AN, restricting sub-
type (AN-R), is significantly lower than for individuals with AN, binge-eating/purg-
ing subtype (AN-B/P), BN, or BED (Bulik et al., 2004).
AN is associated with personality traits including perfectionism, obsessive–
compulsiveness, neuroticism, negative emotionality, harm avoidance, low self-
directedness, low cooperativeness, and avoidant personality disorder (Cassin & von
Ranson, 2005). Individuals with AN-R report high levels of constraint and persis-
tence and low levels of novelty seeking, though these features are not ubiquitous.
Perfectionism and harm avoidance are traits that appear to be premorbid to AN,
enduring even after recovery (Strober et al., 2006).
AN can also be associated with a range of psychosocial problems. Significant
interpersonal problems or stressors often occur in the year prior to onset of AN
and cumulatively increase risk for AN (Pike et al., 2008). Individuals with AN often
report social isolation and lack of pleasure in interpersonal relationships, and the
majority of women with AN report dramatically decreased sexual interest and plea-
sure (Zemishlany & Weizman, 2008). Adults with AN are likely to report lower rates
of employment than non-eating-disordered peers given the all-consuming nature of
the disorder. This is particularly true during the acute stages of the disorder; how-
ever, for individuals with long-standing AN and for those with significant comorbid
psychopathology, employment remains a problem even following recovery (Hjern,
Lindberg, & Lindblad, 2006).
For many, the impact of the disorder can be pervasive and long-lasting; however,
most follow-up studies of AN have been conducted with individuals who pursued

-- 207 of 498 --

Eating Disorders 	187
intensive treatment and are therefore likely to represent a more severely ill subset.
Among those with AN who develop their eating disorder in adolescence, and for
whom the disorder is not enduring, associated psychopathology and related prob-
lems are accordingly more limited. Even for those with more long-standing AN, cer-
tain interpersonal problems and personality dimensions (and even disorders) can
improve with recovery from AN. Thus, clinicians should be careful not to overinter-
pret personality disorders and interpersonal problems during the acute stage of AN,
but rather should monitor an individual’s clinical presentation, including these fea-
tures, and address them as an integrated part of the clinical work.
Bulimia Nervosa
Individuals with BN engage in repeated binge-eating episodes and use compensa-
tory behaviors, such as self-induced vomiting or taking laxatives or other pills, over
at least a period of several months. An episode of binge eating involves taking in an
objectively large amount of food (i.e., an amount that most people would agree is
large) and simultaneously experiencing a sense of loss of control over eating. Accord-
ing to ICD-11, it is not necessary to consume an objectively large amount of food
during a binge-eating episode; this definitional change is likely to expand the num-
ber of individuals diagnosed with BN (and reduce the number with an other speci-
fied feeding and eating disorder; Claudino et al., 2019). Patients with BN overvalue
body shape and weight. The medical complications associated with BN are primarily
related to the deleterious effects of purging. The most common problems are ero-
sion of dental enamel, swollen salivary glands, edema, electrolyte abnormalities, and
esophageal tears (Mehler, Birmingham, Crow, & Jahraus, 2010).
Given the lack of agreement on definitions of remission, recovery, and relapse in
