BN (Shapiro et al., 2007), the evaluation of outcome for this patient population remains
problematic. A longitudinal study reported that approximately 75% of female college
students diagnosed with BN by structured interview were in remission at 20-year
follow-up (Keel & Klump, 2003). In treatment-seeking samples, between 40 and
45% of patients achieve full recovery, 27% experience symptom improvement, and
14–23% remain chronically ill in the long term (Quadflieg & Fichter, 2019; Steinhau-
sen & Weber, 2009). The most common diagnostic crossover is from BN to another
specified (or unspecified) feeding and eating disorder, followed by AN, then BED
(Quadflieg & Fichter, 2019; Steinhausen & Weber, 2009). Crude mortality rates for
individuals with BN are generally lower than those in AN (Papadopoulos et al.,
2009), with one meta-analysis finding the crude mortality rate in BN to be 1.7 per
1,000 person-years (Arcelus et al., 2011).
Among individuals with a lifetime history of BN, mood and anxiety disorders
are common; approximately 43–75% of patients with BN report comorbid mood dis-
orders (Duncan et al., 2005; Fichter & Quadflieg, 2004), while approximately 68%
experience some form of anxiety or OCD (Kaye, Bulik, Thornton, Barbarich, & Mas-
ters, 2004). Depressive symptoms endorsed by individuals with BN may include dis-
tress about binge-eating episodes, and negative affect or emotion dysregulation may
also be associated with the onset of aberrant eating (Fischer, Smith, & Cyders, 2008;
Roberto, Grilo, Masheb, & White, 2010). Compared to people with AN, those with BN
are more likely to report a lifetime history of an alcohol use disorder (46.1%); similar

-- 208 of 498 --

188 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
to AN, the majority of individuals with BN who develop an alcohol use disorder do
so subsequent to the onset of their eating disorder (Bulik et al., 2004).
A high percentage of individuals with BN also meet criteria for comorbid per-
sonality disorders, with borderline and avoidant personality disorders being most
common (Rø, Martinsen, Hoffart, & Rosenvinge, 2005). Impulsivity and, more spe-
cifically, negative urgency, is commonly associated with BN and is significant in pre-
dicting treatment outcome (Fischer et al., 2008). Suicide attempts are not uncommon
in individuals with BN, with one study reporting that 26.9% of women with a life-
time history of BN have attempted suicide. However, the rate of completed suicides
for individuals with BN is reported to be lower than that for those with AN.
Individuals with BN report significant difficulties in relationships, and inter-
personal problems are correlated with treatment outcome (Hartmann, Zeeck, & Bar-
rett, 2010). However, in comparison to those with AN, individuals with BN are much
more likely to be able to continue working and to be socially engaged in their peer
communities, even while symptomatic.
Binge‑Eating Disorder
BED is characterized by recurrent episodes of binge eating that occur in the absence
of any compensatory behaviors. As in BN, there is a disagreement about diagnostic
standards, with DSM-5 requiring the presence of objectively large binge episodes
for a BED diagnosis, but ICD-11 including intake of both objective and subjective
amounts of food with loss of control (Claudino et al., 2019). Binge eating in the context
of BED is accompanied by significant emotional distress and is further distinguished
from normal eating by features such as eating quickly, until uncomfortably full, or
when not hungry. BED has no diagnostic criterion specifying the cognitive feature
of undue influence of body shape or weight on self-evaluation. However, individuals
with BED tend to report more concern with body shape and weight than overweight
(Grilo, Masheb, & White, 2010) and normal-weight (Goldschmidt et al., 2010) controls.
The degree of overvaluation reported by BED patients is similar to that reported by
individuals with BN (Grilo et al., 2010). Body shape and weight concerns indicate a
more severe variant of BED associated with greater psychosocial impairment (Gold-
schmidt et al., 2010).
Individuals with BED are more likely than those without the disorder to be
obese (Hudson, Hiripi, Pope, & Kessler, 2007), but they can also be distinguished
reliably from non-eating-disordered obese individuals (Wonderlich, Gordon, Mitch-
ell, Crosby, & Engel, 2009). When compared to weight-matched individuals without
BED, those with BED endorse significantly more eating disorder psychopathology
and lower quality of life (Wonderlich et al., 2009). BED is associated with co-occurring
medical problems such as diabetes, irritable bowel syndrome, and fibromyalgia
(Olguin et al., 2017), and psychological correlates, such as depressive symptoms and
low self-esteem (Hrabosky, Masheb, White, & Grilo, 2007). A meta-analysis of BED
studies from 2004 to 2009 reported a cumulative mortality rate of 0.5%, but more data
are needed from studies with longer follow-up periods (Keel & Brown, 2010).
The course of BED appears quite distinct from that of AN, and although BED
shares core features with BN and diagnostic crossover can occur, there are also
clear differences in risk, course, and outcome. Individuals with BED who receive

-- 209 of 498 --

Eating Disorders 	189
treatment are more likely to experience symptom reduction and less likely to experi-
ence diagnostic crossover (Wonderlich et al., 2009).
Compared to obese controls, individuals with BED show higher rates of mood
and personality disorders, with depression as the most commonly reported (lifetime
rate of 50–60%) (Wonderlich et al., 2009). Negative affect, state anxiety, anxiety dis-
orders, and alcohol use disorders are also elevated in clinical samples (Dingemans,
Danner, & Parks, 2017). The degree of psychopathology reported by individuals with
BED appears to be associated with the eating disorder, not degree of obesity (Wilfley,
Citrome, & Herman, 2016).
Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder
ARFID, as its name suggests, is an eating disorder defined by highly restrictive
eating and is associated with significant medical and psychosocial consequences.
Added to the eating disorders section in DSM-5 (American Psychiatric Association,
2013) and soon to be added to ICD-11 (Claudino et al., 2019), ARFID is a reformulation
of DSM-IV’s and ICD-10’s feeding disorder of infancy and early childhood (American
Psychiatric Association, 1994). The age-of-onset criterion was removed because indi-
viduals of all ages can have clinically significant avoidant/restrictive eating, and it
does not always begin in childhood. Unlike those with AN, individuals with ARFID
attribute their food avoidance or restrictive eating to sensory features (e.g., texture,
color), fears related to the process of eating (e.g., choking, vomiting), and/or a lack of
interest (e.g., low hunger, lack of pleasure of eating) rather than body shape or weight
concern. ARFID is not synonymous with picky eating. Though roughly 25% of youth
experience feeding or eating problems (Benjasuwantep, Chaithirayanon, & Eiamu-
domkan, 2013), ARFID is distinguished by its severity and persistence.
Although DSM-5 offers a clear definition of ARFID, this diagnostic group is
rather heterogeneous. ARFID patients appear more likely to be younger, male, and
to have a higher incidence of comorbid psychiatric and medical disorders compared
to those with other eating disorders (Norris, Spettigue, & Katzman, 2016). Common
comorbid psychiatric disorders shared with AN, BN, and BED include anxiety dis-
orders, OCD, and depression. Unlike the other eating disorders, autism spectrum
disorders, oppositional defiant disorder, and attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder
may more commonly co-occur.
EPIDEMIOLOGY
Global Burden of Disease and Prevalence
It is widely recognized that eating disorders occur across culture, socioeconomic
class, and race (Claudino et al., 2019), with risk being a function of biological, envi-
ronmental, cultural, and psychological factors. The Global Burden of Disease (GBD)
study has dramatically enhanced our understanding of the worldwide distribution
and burden of eating disorders. Utilizing the metric of disability-adjusted life-years
(DALYs), the GBD study provides rates of hundreds of health conditions and an esti-
mate of burden based on an algorithm that incorporates years lived with the dis-
order, years of life lost due to the disorder, and associated degree of impairment.

-- 210 of 498 --

190 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
Current GBD estimates comparing AN and BN to 306 other health conditions indi-
cate that combined, these disorders were the 12th leading cause of DALYs in females
ages 15–19 in high-income countries, responsible for 2.2% of all DALYs. Globally,
this ranking did not change a great deal between 1990 and 2013; however, within
low- and middle-income countries, AN and BN increased from 58th to 46th (Erskine,
Whiteford, & Pike, 2016). This higher ranking is largely a function of improvements
in preventing and treating other communicable diseases in these countries, which
shifted the proportion of total burden attributed to eating disorders (Hoek, 2016).
The number of DALYs per 100,000 members of the population is highest in Europe
(Erskine et al., 2016). Despite lower prevalence rates, given the large populations
of India and China, along with the United States, these countries have the highest
contributions of total DALYs caused by eating disorders among women ages 15–49
(Thomas, Lee, & Becker, 2016).
Epidemiological estimates are largely derived from studies in Western Europe
and North America. Epidemiological data from Asia and Pacific Island countries
remain scarce (Thomas et al., 2016); the same holds true for Latin America (Kolar,
Rodriguez, Chams, & Hoek, 2016) and even more so for Africa (van Hoeken, Burns,
& Hoek, 2016).
Anorexia Nervosa
AN, while a severe psychiatric illness, is also relatively uncommon, and rates
appear to be relatively stable in high-income countries, with the exception of a noted
increase among females ages 15–19 (Smink, van Hoeken, & Hoek, 2013). Estimates of
incidence and prevalence of AN may be complicated by the rarity of the illness and
patient characteristics (i.e., secrecy/denial around symptoms that may interfere with
accurate reporting; Grilo, 2006). With changes in the formal diagnostic criteria of
AN, individuals who had previously met criteria for eating disorder not otherwise
specified (EDNOS) are now more likely to be diagnosed with AN, but the overall
rates do not actually appear to be increasing (Mustelin et al., 2016). The most recent
studies in Europe show that AN affects 1–4% of women in Europe; in the United
States, the prevalence rate is approximately 0.8% in adults and 0.3% in adolescents
(Swanson, Crow, Le Grange, Swendsen, & Merikangas, 2011; Udo & Grilo, 2018). Best
estimates indicate the disorder affects one-tenth as many males as females (Udo &
Grilo, 2018). Rates of AN remain low in Africa (van Hoeken et al., 2016), consistent
with the absence of cases in both a large-scale prevalence study among Black women
in the United States (Striegel-Moore et al., 2003) and a large study of incidence among
the majority Black population of Curaçao (Hoek et al., 2005; van Hoeken et al., 2016).
Bulimia Nervosa
Estimates of the prevalence of BN suggest it is a slightly more common disorder than
AN. Hoek and van Hoeken’s (2003) epidemiological review described a 1.0% aggre-
gated prevalence rate for BN (range = 0.0–4.5%), with higher rates for more broadly
defined BN (Hail & Le Grange, 2018). Studies based on surveys of large, nationally
representative, U.S. samples have yielded similar rates in adolescents and lower
rates (0.28%) in adults, with a slightly higher ratio of men affected than seen in AN

-- 211 of 498 --

Eating Disorders 	191
(Swanson et al., 2011; Udo & Grilo, 2018). The wide variation of prevalence estimates
may be attributed to changing definitions of the illness since its introduction (Crow
& Brandenburg, 2010). Given the secrecy and ease of hiding bulimic behaviors, inci-
dence rates are generally considered to be underestimates (Grilo, 2006); however,
there does appear to be reduced incidence over the past few decades (Smink et al.,
2013).
Nationally, research suggests that BN may be more prevalent among Latinos
and Blacks than non-Latino Whites (Marques et al., 2011). Associated distress with
bulimic symptoms may vary cross-culturally, with preliminary data suggesting
binge eating as the best predictor of distress among Whites, Blacks, and Latinos,
in contrast with vomiting as the most salient predictor of distress among Asians
(Franko, Becker, Thomas, & Herzog, 2007). Cross-cultural variations support the
development of flexibility within the classification system to promote cultural sensi-
tivity and allow for phenomenological distinctions (Becker, 2007).
BN has also been described in a variety of non-Western cultures (Nobakht &
Dezhkam, 2000; Pike & Mizushima, 2005). Unlike in AN, the transmission of West-
ern ideals (e.g., thin beauty) and economic factors (e.g., an abundance of food avail-
able for binge eating) is thought to play an important role in the prevalence of BN in
non-Western cultures (Keel & Klump, 2003).
Binge‑Eating Disorder
BED is estimated to affect 1.6% of adolescents and between 0.8 and 2.8% of adults
(Hudson et al., 2007; Swanson et al., 2011; Udo & Grilo, 2018). In addition to being
more common than either AN or BN, BED is associated with later age of onset and
longer duration (Hudson et al., 2007). Full and subthreshold BED is more commonly
seen in adolescent (0.8–2.6%) and adult (1.9–2.0%) males than is AN or BN, though the
available data indicate that full-syndrome BED is still more common in women (Udo
& Grilo, 2018). Prevalence estimates range from 2 to 5% in obese community sam-
ples, and as high as 30% among individuals seeking bariatric surgery or alternative
weight loss treatments (de Zwaan, 2001; Kalarchian et al., 2007). In a sample of over-
weight and obese veterans seeking assistance with weight management, estimates
were higher, with 78.2% of individuals reporting binge eating, though these findings
are based on self-report data and likely capture both sub- and full-threshold symp-
toms (Higgins et al., 2013). Relative to other eating disorders, BED is known to be
more prevalent across a variety of racial and ethnic groups (Wonderlich et al., 2009).
Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder
There has been little epidemiological research on ARFID to date. In a population-
based survey of individuals at least 15 years old, the 3-month point prevalence of
ARFID was 0.3% (Hay et al., 2017), placing it in a range comparable to AN and BN.
In a school-based survey of children ages 8–13 in Switzerland, 3.2% of students
reported features consistent with a diagnosis of ARFID (Kurz, van Dyck, Drem-
mel, Munsch, & Hilbert, 2015). Medical record review studies have looked at the
rates of ARFID in specialty services, reporting estimates ranging from 5 to 22.5%
in pediatric or adolescent medicine eating disorders programs (Nicely, Lane-Loney,

-- 212 of 498 --

192 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
Masciulli, Hollenbeak, & Ornstein, 2014; Norris et al., 2014), and a 1.5% prevalence
rate in a pediatric gastroenterology specialty clinic (Eddy et al., 2015). It appears that
a greater number of males than females are affected by ARFID relative to AN and
BN. Even less is known about the prevalence of ARFID across different racial and
ethnic groups. However, the disorder has been described in adolescents and adults
receiving eating disorders treatment in Japan (Nakai, Nin, Noma, Teramukai, &
Wonderlich, 2016; Tanaka et al., 2015).
ETIOLOGY
The empirical literature on the etiology of eating disorders is still limited, with few
reports utilizing prospective and experimental research designs that could identify
directional and causational effects. Several case– control studies have identified risk
correlates (Fairburn et al., 1998; Karwautz et al., 2001; Pike et al., 2008; Striegel-Moore
et al., 2005). These studies offer the advantage of examining actual clinical cases, but
they are subject to potential reporting bias given that the data are gathered retro-
spectively from individuals with eating disorders. However, the majority of prospec-
tive and experimental studies of eating disturbance are limited because they do not
examine true cases of eating disorders (for a review, see Jacobi, Hayward, de Zwaan,
Kraemer, & Agras, 2004).
In 1986, Striegel-Moore, Sliberstein, and Rodin posed the following set of heu-
ristic questions to guide the study of the development of eating disorders: Why now?
Why women? Why some women and not others? These questions reflect several
important understandings of risk for eating disorders (Striegel-Moore et al., 1986).
First, eating disorders are a contemporary problem; prevalence rates have increased
during the past century in Western cultures, and rates of eating psychopathology
continue to increase across the globe (Becker, Thomas, & Pike, 2009). Second, gender
is perhaps the most significant and unequivocal risk factor for eating disorders, par-
ticularly AN and BN. Finally, despite widespread sociocultural and environmental
factors, only a small percentage of individuals actually develop significant eating
disturbances, implying that individual risk factors are essential to understanding
why some individuals develop eating psychopathology while others from the same
environmental context do not.
Why Now?
At the broadest level, sociocultural factors set the general stage of risk. Sociocultural
studies of the development of eating disorders suggest a complex interplay between
increased abundance in the food supply (especially highly palatable, caloric foods),
increased sedentary lifestyles, and societal overvaluation of a thin beauty ideal
(Brownell, 2010). In addition, as societies modernize and industrialize, traditional
gender roles are often put in flux. It has been argued that the dynamic of social tran-
sition increases risk for eating disorders at the broadest level because such societal
flux increases the developmental challenges of coming of age given the lack of role
models and societal norms to guide the course (Katzman & Lee, 1997).

-- 213 of 498 --

Eating Disorders 	193
Why Women?
In addition to biological vulnerabilities that likely predispose women to develop eat-
ing disorders, environmental factors associated with industrialization and modern-
ization have profoundly impacted women’s place in society. Across the globe, issues
of women’s roles and rights have been at the forefront of much societal change, and
although this has ushered forth many positive changes in women’s roles, a concomi-
tant phenomenon has been the rise in societal overvaluation of a thin beauty ideal
for women. The confluence of these factors results in significant pressure conveyed
by family, peers, and mass media to achieve an unrealistic ideals within a social
context associated with increased caloric consumption and reduced energy expendi-
ture. To the extent that an individual internalizes the thin beauty ideal, the tension
that ensues has been shown to be associated with increased body dissatisfaction and
dieting, and prospective studies suggest that body dissatisfaction and dieting are
significant risk factors for eating psychopathology, particularly bulimic symptom-
atology (Stice & Agras, 1998).
Why Some Women and Not Others?
Exposure to particular life events, individual characteristics, and genetic differences
and vulnerabilities have been examined in an attempt to identify why particular
individuals are at increased risk for the development of eating disorders.
The genetics data suggest some biological heritability of risk for AN, BN, and
BED; however, it is unclear what the genetic vulnerability is, whether it is the same
across eating disorders, and what degree of variance is accounted for by genetic fac-
tors in an etiological model (Bulik, Kleiman, & Yilmaz, 2016). It appears that one
biological component of risk centers on weight regulation. Individuals with AN may
have a biology that can sustain extreme weight loss that the majority of the popula-
tion could not tolerate. Those with BED and BN are more likely to have a history of
being overweight. There are likely other biological factors that contribute to risk;
however, without the existence of specific biological or genetic markers, studies must
identify variables that may further be explored in terms of both biological and envi-
ronmental transmission.
In terms of the development of AN, no prospective and experimental stud-
ies exist to inform the discussion of risk factors. Case– control studies suggest that
perfectionism, negative affectivity, family discord and high parental demands, and
childhood physical and sexual abuse are potential risk correlates in the developmen-
tal history reported by individuals in treatment for AN (Fairburn, Cooper, Doll, &
Welch, 1999; Karwautz et al., 2001; Pike et al., 2008). Interestingly, individuals with
AN do not report a long history of weight and shape concerns; instead, the salience
of these factors appears to be greatest in the year immediately preceding the onset of
their eating psychopathology. In fact, it may be that dieting and weight concern are
more accurately understood as prodromal correlates of AN rather than potential risk
factors. Moreover, it is possible that the risk factors identified to date are most rel-
evant to individuals with long-standing AN given that the existing data are derived
from studies of individuals with enduring pathology.

-- 214 of 498 --

194 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
It is commonly understood that a combination of biological vulnerabilities inter-
act with certain environmental exposures to increase risk for AN. For example, a
majority of girls engage in dieting at some point during adolescence. In the case of
AN, it may be that dieting and weight loss trigger a certain biological vulnerability
among individuals who also have some anxious and perfectionistic traits, such that
they would be able to lose and sustain significant weight loss when others could not.
In the case of BN, extant data converge to suggest that genetic vulnerability,
childhood obesity, weight concern, negative body image and dieting, sexual abuse,
and adverse family factors (including parental obesity, parental mood or substance
abuse disorder, and highly critical parenting), along with individual factors of social
anxiety, negative affectivity, low self-esteem, impulsivity, and externalizing behav-
iors may contribute to increased risk (Fairburn et al., 1998; Jacobi et al., 2004; Stice,
2001).
BED shares with BN the common feature of binge eating at the core of the syn-
drome; thus, certain risk correlates also appear to be common across these disor-
ders. In particular, childhood obesity, adverse family factors (including family dis-
cord, high parental demands, and parental history of mood and substance disorder),
mood disorder, and negative affectivity have been identified as risk correlates in
case– control studies (Fairburn et al., 1998; Striegel-Moore et al., 2005). It is likely that
both biological and environmental factors contribute to increased risk for childhood
obesity, which is often associated with teasing and body dissatisfaction.
Of note, many of the possible risk factors associated with eating disorders may
be shared with other psychiatric disorders. For example, childhood abuse increases
risk for not only eating disorders but also anxiety and mood disorders (Jonas et al.,
2011). Yet unlike the presence of a certain cellular abnormality that indicates without
a doubt the presence of a particular cancer, the correlates associated with eating dis-
turbances represent increased risk but do not definitively determine the presence of
an eating disorder.
MULTIVARIATE MODELS OF ONSET AND MAINTENANCE OF EATING DISORDERS
It is widely recognized that eating disorders are multidetermined and that the fac-
tors contributing to their onset are not necessarily those that contribute to mainte-
nance of the disorder. Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) interventions emphasize
this point (Fairburn, Marcus, & Wilson, 1993). These models maintain that certain
variables likely increase risk during a developmentally vulnerable period of time
(e.g., overvaluation of shape and/or weight), and that other variables contribute to the
persistence of the eating psychopathology (e.g., strict dietary rules). Specific compo-
nents of the eating disorders (e.g., severe weight loss, binge eating and purging) have
significant biological, behavioral, and psychological effects that serve to complicate
the clinical picture and perpetuate the disturbance. CBT and other evidence-based
treatments for eating disorders largely focus on identifying maintenance factors
based on the tenet that addressing these issues first results in the most significant
and tangible clinical improvement initially. Resolution of the acute issues brings
clarity of vision in analyzing the more distal, developmental factors because by the

-- 215 of 498 --

Eating Disorders 	195
time the individual presents for treatment, the eating disorder has often taken on a
life of its own. Cognitive-behavioral theory maintains that achieving some symptom
resolution offers immediate relief and increases the patient’s sense of self-efficacy.
Long-standing issues can then be more readily addressed once eating disorder main-
tenance behaviors have sufficiently resolved.
Although CBT was initially developed for patients with BN, Fairburn intro-
duced an enhanced version of CBT (CBT-E) proposed to be “transdiagnostic,” in
that it focuses on commonalities among the eating disorders (Fairburn, 2008). The
core treatment is similar to that in the earlier manual (Fairburn, Marcus, et al., 1993),
with two substantial changes. First, there is a reformulated strategy and method
for addressing dysfunctional thought processes regarding the overvaluation body
shape and weight. Second, a new treatment module tackles “mood intolerance” as
a trigger of aberrant eating episodes. The core focus on eating disorder symptoms
is supplemented by flexible, personalized use of modules to address maintaining
mechanisms theorized to perpetuate the illness, including low self-esteem, clini-
cal perfectionism, and interpersonal difficulties. Given the additional mechanisms
addressed throughout treatment, CBT-E is proposed to be a more suitable treatment
for particularly complex cases (de Jong, Schoorl, & Hoek, 2018). As we discuss in
more detail later, CBT and CBT-E have strong empirical support in the field.
CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS, TREATMENT PLANNING,
AND EVIDENCE‑BASED TREATMENT OF EATING DISORDERS
Anorexia Nervosa
Individuals with AN are unlikely to present with complaints about their change
in weight or nutritional intake. They may be identified on routine medical assess-
ment or may present for evaluation following concern expressed by family, friends,
coworkers, or coaches. Evaluation is challenging because of the thought content fre-
quently associated with the illness, including a belief that one is fat or would become
fat with weight gain, and a denial of the seriousness of the low weight state. Life
events and psychological traits that were present before the onset of eating behav-
ioral change are part of a comprehensive evaluation, but the clinician should use
caution in making any causal hypotheses regarding the development of the eating
disorder. Evaluation should include detailed assessment of daily food and beverage
intake, weight history (e.g., lifetime highest–lowest weights; time course of signifi-
cant weight changes; associated changes in menstrual status), body image concerns,
and exercise routines. Assessments should include direct questioning about purging
behaviors, including vomiting, as well as the use of laxatives, diuretics, and diet pills
in traditional pharmaceutical, as well as alternative, preparations. Parallel history
from family members may be necessary. In younger patients, weight loss may not
be present; rather, the diagnosis may be made if expected goals for weight gain and
growth are not met (Rosen, 2010). Evaluation will likely include medical assessment,
including vital signs such as heart rate, blood pressure, and temperature, in addition
to height and weight. Low weight patients will frequently have changes consistent
with a hypometabolic state, and both these findings and other physical signs can be

-- 216 of 498 --

196 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
useful indicators of the severity of illness. Some patients are concerned enough about
these changes that they are willing to consider engaging in weight restoration. Even
in the context of significant low weight, laboratory assessments may not identify
abnormalities. Clinicians should not be reassured by these results because total body
levels of micronutrients and minerals may not be reflected in blood tests.
Case formulation should include a narrative that helps the patient understand
the emergence and perpetuation of symptoms. The narrative should include the
patient’s predisposing factors such as preillness anxiety or obsessionality, family his-
tory of an eating disorder, dieting behaviors, or activities (e.g., athletics, modeling)
that may reinforce leanness. Dietary changes, even those that the patient dismisses
as distinct from the formal eating disorder, such as lists of disliked foods, recently
acquired vegetarian practices, gluten sensitivity and food allergies, should be con-
sidered as possible symptoms of the illness itself. Patients should be informed about
the physical and psychological risks of malnutrition, including the development or
intensification of distressing symptoms such as depression, anxiety, and social iso-
lation. Basic principles of nutritional rehabilitation should be reviewed, such as the
fact that individuals usually require 1,800–2,400 kcal (kilocalories) for weight main-
tenance, and that 3,500 kcal above maintenance requirements are necessary in order
to gain 1 pound. Patients (and families) may have to hear this repeatedly in order to
process the fact that nutritional plans must include 500–1,000 kcal/day above mainte-
nance requirements in order to gain 1–2 pounds per week.
Treatment plans need to emphasize weight restoration and normalization of eat-
ing behaviors. After evaluation of illness severity, available psychosocial supports,
prior treatment history, and treatment availability, evaluations should conclude with
recommendations for treatment type and setting. Patients, and their families, when-
ever possible, should participate in treatment planning. Most treatments include
a team of clinicians with medical, psychological, and nutritional expertise. Target
weight goals (and the rationale for these recommendations) should be provided,
along with explicit expectations about rate of weight gain and plans for weight mon-
itoring. Once a recommended weight has been achieved, goals can be reevaluated
to incorporate weight maintenance. Psychoeducation about normal fluctuations in
weight and healthy weight ranges often is required.
Clearly, treatment can be both challenging and complicated, and the lack of
clear empirical support for any single treatment approach may be a result of different
factors, including several difficulties inherent to treating this clinical group (Attia,
2010). Associated features of AN, such as the ego-syntonic nature of the disorder
and the medical complications that can arise during treatment, can have profound
implications on patient motivation, compliance, and outcome. Ambivalence about
recovery and denial about the seriousness of the problem can be framed as symp-
tomatic of the illness and should be explicitly addressed at the onset of treatment and
repeatedly as needed.
Treatment research in individuals with AN is complicated by their varying needs
in terms of intensity of services and the tendency for patients to be withdrawn from
outpatient treatment for a higher level of care (Bulik, Berkman, Brownley, Sedway,
& Lohr, 2007). The rarity of the illness also makes it challenging to recruit adequate
samples for rigorous study. Overall, psychotherapeutic interventions for AN target
either acute treatment for underweight or the prevention of relapse to a low weight.

-- 217 of 498 --

Eating Disorders 	197
Family Therapy
Evidence supports the use of family-based treatment of adolescents with AN, though
only a handful of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) have been completed. One
type of family-based therapy (FBT), also called the Maudsley approach because of its
origins at the Maudsley Hospital in England (Russell, Szmukler, Dare, & Eisler, 1987),
stands out from the empirical database. FBT assumes that normal adolescent devel-
opment has been disturbed by the presence of the eating disorder; thus, parents’
involvement in treatment is essential to this therapeutic intervention (Lock & Le
Grange, 2012). Parents temporarily oversee the refeeding process, and once success-
ful in this task, gradually return an age-appropriate degree of control and autonomy
back to the patient. After eating and weight have normalized, treatment focuses on
feelings underlying the disordered behaviors and the developmental issues that may
have been ignored due to the illness. This treatment modality is best suited to those
under age 18, who live at home with their families, and whose families are able to set
aside other issues in the service of treating the eating disorder. The entire household
(e.g., parents, siblings, child care providers) is typically involved and must be com-
mitted to the approach and the day-to-day mealtime routines.
Overall, this and other family-based treatments are considered the “gold stan-
dard” for adolescents with AN (Lebow et al., 2018). Moreover, existing data suggest
long-term benefit of FBT (Loeb & Le Grange, 2009). One study of adolescents receiv-
ing FBT observed that 78% of patients no longer met criteria for an eating disor-
der at 36-month follow-up (Paulson-Karlsson, Engstrom, & Nevonen, 2009). Studies
comparing subtle variations of FBT suggest that parental involvement may be the
most salient feature of the treatment, prompting the development of parent-focused
treatment (PFT; Hughes, Sawyer, Loeb, & Le Grange, 2015). RCTs comparing PFT
to FBT have consistently found PFT to yield greater reduction in symptoms and
increased remission rates (Hughes, Sawyer, Accurso, Singh, & Le Grange, 2019). The
evaluating clinician must consider carefully whether parents and patients can be
adequately committed to this psychotherapy and, accordingly, what interventions
to employ.
Cognitive‑Behavioral Therapy
CBT, pioneered by Beck (1976), has demonstrated efficacy for a wide range of psy-
chiatric illnesses (Hofmann, Asnaani, Vonk, Sawyer, & Fang, 2012). As previously
described, CBT was initially adapted for patients with BN and found to be effica-
cious (CBT-BN; Fairburn, 1981); it has since evolved into CBT-E.
A central tenet of the CBT model of AN (CBT-AN) is that symptoms are main-
tained by the interaction between disturbances in thoughts and behaviors that
impact eating and weight control. Though available data on CBT-AN remain lim-
ited, there is some indication that this approach may be of use preventing relapse in
weight-restored patients: Studies comparing CBT-AN to alternative approaches have
consistently reported lower rates of premature dropout and longer time to relapse
in weight-restored individuals receiving CBT (Agras, 2019; Galsworthy-Francis &
Allan, 2014). Despite this, meta-analyses examining CBT-AN suggest its efficacy is
not consistently superior to other treatments (Galsworthy-Francis & Allan, 2014).

-- 218 of 498 --

198 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
CBT-AN’s efficacy in treating acute AN is somewhat ambiguous. In the first
published case series, Cooper and Fairburn (1984) reported that two patients did
well, one showed some improvement, and two did not benefit. Although subsequent
RCTs have found no differences in outcome for underweight patients receiving CBT-
AN compared to other treatments (Galsworthy-Francis & Allan, 2014), a trial com-
paring CBT-AN with specialist supportive clinical management in a sample of adults
with severe and enduring AN found CBT-AN to yield greater social adjustment and
symptom reduction at 6- and 12-month follow-up (Touyz et al., 2013). While there is
minimal evidence to support this approach over others, existing studies in this sub-
group are limited by the methodological problems outlined previously.
Research on CBT-E for AN remains relatively limited, though early reports sug-
gest its suitability for a substantial proportion of patients, with reports of up to 96%
of patients successfully completing treatment (Dalle Grave, El Ghoch, Sartirana, &
Calugi, 2016). There is some indication that this approach yields increased weight
and decreased symptoms among the majority of patients (60–80%) who complete
treatment (Dalle Grave et al., 2016) and that gains are relatively well maintained a
year after treatment completion (Frostad et al., 2018). Among patients with severe
AN (defined as those with BMI < 16 kg/m2), 50% successfully completed outpatient
CBT-E, and 71.4% of these individuals had a substantial increase and maintenance of
BMI up to 12 months later (Frostad et al., 2018). The guidelines for eating disorders
therefore now recommend CBT-E as a first-line treatment for adults with AN.
Additional Approaches
Given the limited evidence in support of any single psychotherapeutic intervention
for AN, clinical researchers have adapted treatments originally studied in other
patient populations, such as cognitive remediation therapy (CRT), exposure therapy
and response prevention (EX/RP), and acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT)
for use in this population.
Developed as an intervention for patients with impaired cognitive functioning
due to brain lesions, CRT aims to identify and strengthen impaired cognitive processes
through repetitive cognitive and behavioral exercises (Tchanturia & Hambrook, 2010).
The therapy addresses areas of relative cognitive weakness, based on neuropsycho-
logical assessment, neuroimaging findings, and studies comparing individuals with
AN to healthy counterparts. Areas of impairment include “set shifting” (to transition
easily back and forth between tasks or mental sets) or “cognitive flexibility” (to adapt
behavior to changing demands in the environment), and “central coherence” (holistic
information processing whereby details are integrated and put in a broader context).
In CRT, patients work on versions of different tasks to address inflexibility in thinking
and overly detailed processing biases (for details, see Tchanturia & Hambrook, 2010).
CRT appears to yield improvement on neuropsychological measures in patients with
AN (Tchanturia, Lloyd, & Lang, 2013) and may reduce treatment attrition rates relative
to CBT (Dahlgren & Rø, 2014). Preliminary results from RCTs suggest that when com-
bined with treatment as usual, CRT may enhance certain outcomes, including improve-
ments in eating disorder psychopathology and quality of life (Dahlgren & Rø, 2014).
In EX/RP for AN, disturbances in eating behavior are explicitly targeted. This
intervention, predicated on patients’ learning through experience that anticipated

-- 219 of 498 --

Eating Disorders 	199
negative consequences do not occur (or can be managed) when a fearful stimulus
is approached, is the psychotherapy of choice for OCD, posttraumatic stress disor-
der, and some anxiety disorders (Kaczkurkin & Foa, 2015). In EX/RP for AN, the
avoidance of food likely to produce weight gain is hypothesized to result from anxi-
ety. Rigid dietary rules and ritualized eating behaviors (e.g., eating slowly, cutting
food in small pieces) are conceptualized as strategies to avoid or minimize distress
around eating (Steinglass et al., 2011, 2014). To promote experiential learning, EX/RP
for AN utilizes session time to put the patient in direct contact with feared foods and
eating situations. In an open case series, Steinglass and colleagues (2012) observed
increased caloric intake during a laboratory meal following a brief trial of EX/RP. An
RCT of EX/RP and CRT in inpatients showed that EX/RP was associated with better
intake in a laboratory meal; furthermore, improvement in intake was associated with
improvement in eating-related anxiety (Steinglass et al., 2014).
ACT (Hayes, Strosahl, & Wilson, 2002) emphasizes a need for psychological flex-
ibility and acceptance of the human experience. In this approach, eating disorders
are conceptualized as a means of avoiding unpleasant experiences. ACT aims to shift
a patient’s perspective on how to instead approach and accept these experiences. In
case series and larger samples incorporating ACT into existing treatments, improve-
ments in weight and symptom severity are comparable with treatment as usual
(Juarascio et al., 2013; Parling, Cernvall, Ramklint, Holmgren, & Ghaderi, 2016). ACT
holds promise but is not currently considered superior to other treatments.
Bulimia Nervosa
While individuals with BN are more likely than individuals with AN to present
with clear interest in symptom resolution, it is not unusual for them to delay their
presentation because of shame. All comprehensive psychological evaluations should
include questions about eating, body shape, and weight concerns, and individuals
who report concerns in these areas should be asked about binge-eating and purg-
ing behaviors. As with AN, assessment should include gathering information about
typical overall eating pattern (and associated dietary rules) and binge episodes (e.g.,
amounts/types of food, context/triggers). Patients with BN may report an escalating
pattern of binge and purge frequency. They may even have had an earlier period of
restrictive food intake and may have previously met full criteria for AN. Younger
patients may report less frequent binge eating and purging. Self-induced vomiting at
any frequency should be identified as a problem and addressed in the evaluation and
treatment plan. Assessments for BN should include questions about social function-
ing because patients may eat secretively, or pair restrictive eating when alone with
apparently “normal” eating with friends, accompanied by purging following a fuller
meal. Patients should be made aware of the risks of frequent vomiting, including
dental erosion and decay, and electrolyte instability associated with serious medical
risks, such as cardiac arrhythmia or seizures. Individuals who abuse laxatives may
develop significant difficulties with intestinal motility, as well as fluid and electro-
lyte abnormalities.
As symptoms are often the source of great shame, patients should be commended
for speaking honestly and seeking help. Patients require education about the cycle of
thoughts and behaviors that frequently perpetuate symptoms and need support in

-- 220 of 498 --

200 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
order to break this cycle, introduce healthier eating behaviors, and manage anxiety
about whether eating without purging can be accomplished without feared weight
gain. Treatment planning should include education about effective treatments for
BN, including psychotherapy and antidepressant medication. Regular medical eval-
uation should be required if patients continue to be notably symptomatic.
There has been great progress in the development and rigorous empirical study
of several psychotherapies for BN in the past two decades. This body of research
has identified substantial differences in the effectiveness of distinct psychotherapies,
allowing for clearly delineated treatment guidelines (Grilo, 2006). In contrast to AN,
the prognosis for patients with BN who receive adequate treatment is good. Approxi-
mately 50% of individuals with BN who receive manual-based CBT, for example,
achieve either near or full remission of symptoms that appears to be maintained
over time (Wilson, Grilo, & Vitousek, 2007). Given the robust empirical database of
treatment research in BN, in 2017 the U.K.’s National Institute for Health and Care
Excellence (NICE) updated their recommended list of evidence-based treatment
guidelines, designating CBT-guided self-help (CBT-GSH) as the treatment of choice,
followed by CBT-E.
Cognitive‑Behavioral Therapy
CBT targets behavioral disturbances by prescribing a regular pattern of eating, pro-
viding problem-solving skills to cope with high-risk situations, and teaching cogni-
tive restructuring to modify problematic body shape and weight beliefs (Fairburn,
Marcus, et al., 1993).
RCTs of CBT-BN support its efficacy in helping patients achieve abstinence from
or a decreased frequency of binge–purge behaviors. Consistent with the approach’s
conceptual model, the reduction in dietary restraint achieved by CBT partly medi-
ates the intervention’s efficacy in eliminating binge eating and purging (Wilson,
Fairburn, Agras, Walsh, & Kraemer, 2002). CBT has been demonstrated to be superior
to other psychological treatments in the short term and to antidepressant medica-
tions for achieving abstinence from bulimic symptoms (Linardon, Wade, de la Pie-
dad Garcia, & Brennan, 2017).
By addressing difficulties external to the eating disorder (as described earlier),
CBT-E may be an effective treatment for complex or severe cases, though there does
not appear to be a consistent difference between the focused and broad versions
(Thompson-Brenner et al., 2016). Studies do provide relatively robust support for
CBT-E as an effective treatment, yielding global symptom reduction and significantly
improved outcomes when compared with psychoanalytic psychotherapy, interper-
sonal psychotherapy (IPT), and wait-list control (de Jong et al., 2018). Despite this,
CBT-E has relatively high attrition rates, and there is preliminary evidence to suggest
alternatives, such as integrative cognitive–affective therapy, may also be effective (de
Jong et al., 2018).
GUIDED SELF‑HELP USING CBT
Despite the effectiveness of CBT in the treatment of BN, access to this modal-
ity of treatment remains limited (Wilson et al., 2007). CBT-GSH was developed to

-- 221 of 498 --

Eating Disorders 	201
overcome barriers in treatment dissemination. It combines a self-help manual with
limited, brief “therapy” sessions administered by health care providers with differ-
ing degrees of expertise and experience.
Interpersonal Psychotherapy
Originally developed as a short-term, structured psychotherapy for depression
(Klerman, Weissman, Rounsaville, & Chevron, 1984), IPT has been adapted for BN
by Fairburn and colleagues (Fairburn, Jones, Peveler, Hope, & O’Connor, 1993). IPT
for BN does not overtly focus on the modification of disturbed eating behaviors or
overvaluation of shape and weight. Binge eating is theorized to occur as a response
to interpersonal disturbances and consequent negative mood (Fairburn, Jones, et al.,
1993). Thus, the primary emphasis of IPT is on helping patients identify and address
interpersonal problems hypothesized to maintain the eating disorder. Treatment
goals evolve from the four domains of interpersonal problems, as outlined by Kler-
man and colleagues (1984)—grief, interpersonal role disputes, role transitions, and
interpersonal deficits—and encourage mastery of current social roles, as well as
adjustment to evolving interpersonal situations (Wilfley et al., 2002). Eating disorder
symptoms are linked consistently back to their role in the perpetuation or mainte-
nance of the patient’s interpersonal domain of focus (Tanofsky-Kraff et al., 2010).
IPT has some empirical support and therefore might be considered an alternative
to CBT. However, RCTs show this approach to be less effective than CBT in reducing
bulimic symptoms by the end of the active phase of treatment (Agras, Walsh, Fair-
burn, Wilson, & Kraemer, 2000; Fairburn et al., 1991) and rates of remission are higher
in CBT-E (Fairburn et al., 2015). Nevertheless, studies have shown that individuals
receiving either treatment report a similar degree of improvement in bulimic symp-
toms at follow-up. Thus, there appear to be clear temporal differences in response to
treatment, with IPT taking longer to achieve its effects than CBT (Fairburn, Jones, et
al., 1993; Wilson et al., 2002). Proponents of IPT assert that if delivered in such a way
that interpersonal problems are repeatedly linked to the maintenance of the eating
disorder, response to IPT may occur more rapidly (Tanofsky-Kraff et al., 2010).
Additional Approaches
Given the need for psychological treatments to address a broader range of individu-
als with BN (Wilson et al., 2007), dialectical behavior therapy (DBT) and FBT for BN
have garnered increased interest. Some attention has also been paid to leveraging
technology to increase access to evidence-based care.
DBT, developed to treat borderline personality disorder (Linehan, 1993), is an
empirically supported treatment that focuses primarily on affect regulation and
development of adaptive coping strategies and interpersonal skills. The skills taught
in DBT, including mindfulness, distress tolerance, and emotional regulation, may
be a particularly good match for the high levels of negative affect frequently experi-
enced by patients with BN (Safer, Telch, & Agras, 2001; Wilson et al., 2007). Findings
from initial trials suggest that DBT for BN can yield a large reduction of bulimic
symptoms. Rigorous studies comparing DBT to an active treatment are needed to
better evaluate its efficacy.

-- 222 of 498 --

202 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
The Maudsley form of family therapy described previously has also been
adapted to meet the needs of younger patients with BN. Research suggests that FBT
is useful in reducing bulimic symptoms (Le Grange, Crosby, Rathouz, & Leventhal,
2007). While previous studies suggested FBT may not be more effective than other
approaches, more recent findings indicate that when compared to CBT for adoles-
cents, FBT for BN results in greater abstinence from binge eating and purging at end
of treatment and 6-month follow up (Le Grange, Lock, Agras, Bryson, & Jo, 2015).
Beyond meeting the needs of a broader range of patients with BN, there has been
a growing interest in telemedicine as a strategy to reach more individuals and pro-
vide cost-effective treatment. Internet-based CBT has shown promise for individuals
with anxiety disorders (Andersson, 2009). Some data suggest telemedicine CBT-BN
yields outcomes greater than control conditions and is comparable to face-to-face
CBT, but findings are mixed (Loucas et al., 2014; Pittock, Hodges, & Lawrie, 2018).
More research is needed to address logistical considerations in delivering telemedi-
cine, including the use of smartphone apps, and to identify possible long-term differ-
ences in outcome between this and other treatment modalities.
Binge‑Eating Disorder
BED is the most prevalent eating disorder and occurs in many individuals who pres-
ent for treatment for other psychiatric disorders, and in some who present at obesity
clinics and other medical venues. Therefore, individuals with mood disorders, psy-
chotic disorders, anxiety disorders, and obesity should be assessed for binge eating.
Treatment planning for BED requires patient education and participation. It is not
synonymous with weight loss treatment, and treatments that effectively decrease
bingeing may not be associated with weight loss. Psychotherapy for BED is often one
of several treatment components that overweight or obese individuals with BED may
want to consider in moving toward improved eating and health.
Multiple therapeutic modalities show promise. Initial treatment studies came
from the field of obesity, where mixed findings led to concern that behavioral weight
loss interventions and calorically restrictive diets might be unsuitable for obese indi-
viduals endorsing features of eating disorders (Yanovski, 1993). For the many obese
individuals with BED, there continues to be debate in the eating disorder and obesity
fields as to whether weight status or eating disorder symptoms should be first-line
treatment goals (Krysanski & Ferraro, 2008). Given limited data supporting mainte-
nance of significant weight loss following behavioral weight loss, targeting a reduc-
tion in binge eating initially may allow for a slow, modest weight loss following BED
treatment (Krysanski & Ferraro, 2008). However, research including long-term follow-
up is limited. Additionally, more studies comparing obese and nonobese individuals
with BED are warranted to clarify the extent to which the eating disorder diagnosis
offers clinically relevant information in relation to obesity (Wonderlich et al., 2009).
The clinical concerns of practitioners treating obese individuals with BED
inspired research on psychological treatments adapted from those for BN. Currently,
CBT-GSH enjoys the designation as the treatment of choice for BED by the U.K.’s
NICE. In considering the treatment needs of individuals with BED, however, it is
important to bear in mind that these patients struggle across domains (e.g., binge
eating, attitudinal features of eating disorders, general psychological symptoms and
distress, and, at times, obesity with its associated health risks; Grilo, 2006).

-- 223 of 498 --

Eating Disorders 	203
Cognitive‑Behavioral Therapy
Treatment studies evaluating CBT for BED (CBT-BED) have typically relied upon
the manual developed by Fairburn, Marcus, and colleagues (1993) for the treatment
of BN. Patients are encouraged to challenge negative cognitions related to having a
larger body size and to develop structured, healthy eating patterns, with an empha-
sis on moderation of amounts and types of foods. Adjustments to the theoretical
understanding of maintenance mechanisms recognize key differences between the
disorders, namely, the absence of inappropriate compensatory behaviors and more
generally chaotic, less restrictive eating characteristic of BED (Grilo, 2006).
In RCTs of CBT-BED, substantial reductions in binge eating, and general and
eating-disordered psychopathology are evident posttreatment and at follow-up (Wil-
fley et al., 2002). Though CBT interventions do not reliably result in substantial or
sustained weight loss, they yield greater reductions in binge eating than behavioral
weight loss (Iacovino, Gredysa, Altman, & Wilfley, 2012). CBT is generally associated
with good treatment completion rates, reduction in binge-eating episodes in more
than half of patients, and improvement in psychosocial functioning and mood. CBT
has proven more effective than pharmacological treatments as a stand-alone treat-
ment and as an adjunct to behavioral weight loss treatment (Iacovino et al., 2012).
While data clearly and consistently support its use, little is known about how
CBT-BED produces its effects. Some have proposed that the current CBT model pays
insufficient attention to low levels of dietary restraint, high negative affect, and eat-
ing in response to emotion characteristic of individuals with this eating disorder
(Wilson et al., 2007). A review of recent research on CBT-E provides evidence that
this is also an effective treatment for BED and results in faster treatment response
and higher remission rates than IPT (de Jong et al., 2018).
GUIDED SELF‑HELP USING CBT
Similar to what we described for BN, self-help based on the CBT model of eating dis-
orders has been studied extensively, primarily using the Fairburn (1995) manual. Sev-
eral investigations have tested CBT-GSH and pure self-help approaches (for a com-
plete list, see Sysko & Walsh, 2008); in BED, data suggest some advantage of CBT-GSH
over pure self-help (Iacovino et al., 2012). In one study, 10 sessions of CBT-GSH deliv-
ered by beginning psychology graduate students with limited therapy experience
proved to be as effective in reducing BED symptoms as 20 sessions of IPT conducted
by intensively trained and supervised doctoral-level psychologists (Wilson, Wilfley,
Agras, & Bryson, 2010), providing further support that CBT-GSH may be a viable,
appropriate intervention with advantages in dissemination and cost-effectiveness.
Interpersonal Psychotherapy
IPT for BED (Wilfley et al., 2002) is based on IPT for depression (Klerman et al., 1984)
and BN (Fairburn, Jones, et al., 1993). Although connections are made throughout
therapy between interpersonal events and binge eating, the treatment does not
overtly address eating behavior. Instead, patients’ work focuses on making changes
in specifically identified problematic areas of interpersonal functioning. In adapting
IPT for BED, Wilfley and colleagues (2002) developed a group format. In group IPT,

-- 224 of 498 --

204 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
patients are encouraged to use the group to decrease isolation, enable the formation
of relationships, and practice new communication skills. The group format provides
a unique environment for participants, who may otherwise keep eating behaviors
hidden due to guilt and shame (Tanofsky-Kraff et al., 2010).
At present, NICE (2017) guidelines for BED do not designate IPT as a treatment
of choice, possibly because of the paucity of RCTs investigating this approach rela-
tive to those of CBT-based treatments. Still, research underscores the potential util-
ity of an IPT approach in a subgroup of patients known to suffer across domains of
functioning (Grilo, 2006). In the largest RCT to date, IPT was found to be superior
to behavioral weight loss in patients with low self-esteem at 2-year follow-up, and
superior to CBT-GSH in patients with low self-esteem and high eating disorder psy-
chopathology (Wilson et al., 2010). While this suggests that IPT holds promise as a
treatment for a subset of individuals with BED, it remains secondary to CBT-based
approaches.
Additional Approaches
Over the past several years, ACT’s theoretical basis has been used to inform novel
treatments for BED. For example, an acceptance-based behavioral treatment (ABBT)
for BED, designed to combine acceptance-based principles from ACT and DBT with
behavioral strategies from CBT, has been found effective in reducing global eating
disorder symptoms, including binge-eating frequency (Juarascio, Manasse, Schum-
acher, Espel, & Forman, 2017). Despite this, the reported binge-eating abstinence
rates following this treatment suggest it is comparable to CBT (Pisetsky, Schaefer,
Wonderlich, & Peterson, 2019). In a study of the feasibility and acceptability of ABBT
combined with behavioral weight loss to target both binge eating and weight loss in
overweight and obese adults with BED, patients were able to concurrently achieve
minimal weight loss while managing eating disorder symptoms (Evans, Murray,
Muratore, Lantz, & Juarascio, 2019). ACT may be a suitable treatment for BED, but it
requires additional investigation.
Another treatment approach with preliminary support is DBT (Linehan, 1993).
DBT has led to a greater reduction of binge-eating frequency and decreased eating-
disordered psychopathology compared to no treatment, and has been associated with
greater initial efficacy and fewer treatment dropouts when compared with an active
comparison group therapy (Iacovino et al., 2012; Wilson, 2011). Although symptom
reduction appears to be maintained at follow-up (Wilson et al., 2010), findings sug-
gest DBT is not superior to CBT or CBT-GSH (Chen et al., 2017) and, when delivered
in routine practice settings, may be associated with increased attrition rates (Klein,
Skinner, & Hawley, 2012).
Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder
Unlike individuals with other types of eating disorders, those with ARFID experi-
ence a problematic eating pattern that is not motivated by body shape or weight
concerns. Concerns about appearance and weight may be absent or within a normal
range. Differential diagnoses of ARFID and AN can be tricky because the clinical
presentation of both disorders can involve low weight, and individuals with AN

-- 225 of 498 --

Eating Disorders 	205
may deny or minimize fear of fat, or body image or weight concerns. However, those
with ARFID who are underweight sometimes describe feeling unhappy about being
thin and endorse feeling proud of or happy about weight gain during treatment.
Moreover, the foods preferred by individuals with ARFID tend to be energy-dense,
high-fat, and high-carbohydrate. Food restriction or avoidance is often guided by
principles related to color or texture, or due to lack of interest in food and eating,
rather than macronutrient content. Compared to individuals with AN, those with
ARFID consume a daily diet with a significantly lower ratio of protein (Thomas &
Eddy, 2018). Weight trajectories over time offer another possible distinction between
ARFID and AN. Those with low-weight ARFID tend to have been at chronically low
weight prior to seeking treatment, rather than describing an acute period of weight
loss (Strandjord, Sieke, Richmond, & Rome, 2015).
Assessment of ARFID includes careful detailing about typical eating patterns,
with an emphasis on clarifying which foods are overly relied on or avoided. Col-
lateral information from parents or other individuals close to the patient is helpful.
Evaluation should also include inquiries about the rationale for food restriction or
avoidance, with questions about sensory sensitivity, fear of aversive consequences,
disinterest in eating, and psychosocial impairment. Finally, assessment of the medi-
cal sequelae of restrictive eating is essential. This involves measurement of height
and weight, consideration of the growth trajectory in children and adolescents, and
assessment of vitamin deficiencies and use of supplements.
The current evidence base for ARFID treatment primarily comprises case
reports, case series, chart reviews, and a few controlled trials in young children.
Hospital-based refeeding programs appear to be helpful for children and adoles-
cents with low-weight ARFID and medical instability (Thomas, Wons, & Eddy, 2018).
Outpatient psychological treatments are currently being studied, but results have
not yet been published.
Cognitive‑Behavioral Therapy
Common components of CBT for ARFID include regular eating, self-monitoring
of food intake, exposure (to new foods and feared eating-related situations) and
response prevention, relaxation training, and behavioral experiments (Thomas et al.,
2018). The proposed disorder-specific CBT model hypothesizes that some individu-
als have a biological predisposition to sensory sensitivity (e.g., heightened response
to unfamiliar tastes and smells), fear of aversive consequences such as choking or
vomiting (e.g., high trait anxiety), and/or lack of interest in food or eating (e.g., low
hedonic appetite). Individuals with this predisposition may be particularly apt to
developing negative feelings about food and assumptions about eating that lead to
the restriction or avoidance of foods. For example, someone with ARFID may think,
“Whenever I try a bite of an apple, I gag because it tastes so terrible, so I will probably
not like any fruit,” and over time repeatedly rely on items from any other food group,
limiting opportunities for exposure and putting them at risk for compromised nutri-
tional status.
CBT for ARFID (CBT-AR; Thomas & Eddy, 2018) is a 20- to 30-session interven-
tion being evaluated in individuals age 10 and up (through adulthood) that aims to
(1) provide education, (2) normalize intake by increasing food volume and variety,

-- 226 of 498 --

206 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
and (3) address variants of avoidant/restrictive eating using systematic desensitiza-
tion to novel foods, guided exposures to feared eating situations, and/or interocep-
tive exposure to bloating, fullness or nausea.
Family‑Based Therapy
FBT for ARFID is similar to FBT for AN, in that parents are empowered to take
charge of feeding. However, it differs in that parents are additionally explicitly
tasked with helping their children increase dietary variety with repeated exposure
to novel foods and, depending on the subtype of ARFID, involvement of other family
members may not be required (Lock et al., 2019). In addition, a key focus in FBT for
ARFID is helping caregivers to counter what may be long-standing accommodations
that have been made to disordered eating behaviors.
MEDICATION USE
Anorexia Nervosa
Currently, there is no clearly established role for medication use in the treatment
of acute AN. Given the prevalence of AN symptoms that are commonly responsive
to medication in other clinical populations (e.g., depression, anxiety, and obses-
sionality), several classes of medication (e.g., antidepressants, anxiolytics, appetite-
enhancing drugs) have been examined in patients with AN in small RCTs (Hay &
Claudino, 2012), without effect on weight or psychological symptoms. Investigators
have theorized that the lack of treatment response across these medication classes
may result from physiological changes that accompany malnutrition. Generally, psy-
chological symptoms improve significantly with weight restoration alone (Meehan,
Loeb, Roberto, & Attia, 2006). Recently, however, a large, multisite study comparing
the atypical antipsychotic medication olanzapine to placebo found that this medica-
tion assists in achieving modest weight gain for outpatients (Attia et al., 2019), con-
firming reports from prior smaller studies (Attia et al., 2011; Bissada, Tasca, Barber,
& Bradwejn, 2008).
Several studies have also considered the utility of medication for relapse pre-
vention in weight-restored patients. Despite initial reports that fluoxetine might be
helpful (Kaye et al., 2001), a large RCT observed fluoxetine to be no more beneficial
than placebo in preventing relapse during the first year after hospitalization among
patients receiving CBT (Walsh, Sysko, & Parides, 2006). However, once patients have
achieved and maintained normal weight for some time, a medication evaluation may
be warranted to assess for persistent co-occurring symptoms.
Bulimia Nervosa
Medication treatment for BN should be considered when an individual prefers a
medication approach, in the case of poor or partial response to CBT, or when CBT is
unavailable. Generally, medication trials have demonstrated that a variety of medi-
cation classes are superior to placebo in reducing binge–purge behavior (Bacaltchuk
& Hay, 2003; Shapiro et al., 2007). Fluoxetine is commonly tried first, as it is the

-- 227 of 498 --

Eating Disorders 	207
only medication with a specific indication for the treatment of BN by the U.S. Food
and Drug Administration (FDA; Broft, Berner, & Walsh, 2010). Psychotherapists are
advised to collaborate with prescribing physicians to monitor bulimic symptoms
if patients are using or are deciding to come off medication as part of treatment.
Patients should be monitored for symptom relapse after discontinuing medication.
Binge‑Eating Disorder
Most, but not all, controlled medication studies for BED have observed a signifi-
cant advantage for medication versus placebo in the short-term reduction of binge
eating (Reas & Grilo, 2008). Investigations have largely evaluated the efficacy of
different antidepressants, an anticonvulsant (topiramate), and weight loss medica-
tions including orlistat and sibutramine. Recently, the stimulant lisdexamfetamine
has been identified as superior to placebo in both its effect on binge frequency and
weight change (McElroy et al., 2017), leading to FDA approval for its use in BED and
representing the only medication that has received this indication. There does not
appear to be substantial difference in the efficacy of these different agents relative
to placebo in treating BED psychopathology (Grilo, 2006). Fluoxetine has also been
associated with improvement in depressive symptoms in the short term (Devlin et
al., 2005) and at follow-up (Devlin, Goldfein, Petkova, Liu, & Walsh, 2007) for indi-
viduals with BED. Not surprisingly, the use of obesity medication has been observed
to result in more clinically meaningful weight loss concomitant with a reduction in
binge frequency (Wilfley et al., 2008). A comprehensive meta-analysis of BED phar-
macotherapy studies also determined that orlistat and topiramate offer enhanced
weight loss as adjunctive treatment to CBT and behavioral weight loss (Reas & Grilo,
2008). Thus, medication might be recommended as part of a multidisciplinary treat-
ment approach for BED, particularly for those who are significantly overweight or
suffering from additional psychological problems.
TREATMENT MOTIVATION
The issue of motivation for treatment is embedded in the previous discussion of
treatment approaches. As described, individuals with AN most frequently do not
self-present for treatment, and the ego-syntonic nature of the disorder is perhaps
its most dramatic and particularly challenging clinical feature (comparable to indi-
viduals with substance abuse disorders). Because investment in an excessively low
weight is core to AN, treatment interventions must address motivational issues
from the start. One of the particularly salient components of the FBT approach is to
engage parents to assist with and lead in the recovery process, until their child is in a
healthier state, such that the child is aligned with and able to assume responsibility
for recovery. In the case of BN and BED, goals regarding weight loss may compete
with recovery from the eating disorder. For ARFID, fear of or disinterest in particu-
lar foods can impact the stamina required for consistent, repeated exposure trials.
Long-term success of eating disorders treatment ultimately depends on the patient’s
sense of ownership about the treatment goals and commitment to recovery goals.
Clinicians’ creativity (e.g., finding meaningful metaphors as patients grapple with

-- 228 of 498 --

208 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
ambivalence) and flexibility (e.g., developing and operationalizing mutually agreed-
upon intermediate goals while working toward long-term goals) may enhance thera-
peutic alliance and motivation for change.
THE PROCESS OF CHANGE IN EVIDENCE‑SUPPORTED TREATMENTS
There is increasing interest in the field of psychotherapy research in exploring treat-
ment mechanisms and identifying factors that may contribute to behavioral change
in the context of successful treatment. While Castonguay and Beutler (2005) have
outlined participant, technique, and clinical relationship factors that should be con-
sidered in examining principles of therapeutic change associated with evidence sup-
ported treatments, very little has been written about such principles associated with
treatments for eating disorders. Treatment elements relevant to CBT that may repre-
sent principles for change include the challenge of cognitive distortions about body
shape and weight, as well as the reinforcement of structured, healthy eating behav-
iors. Elements relevant to IPT and possibly reflective of principles of change associ-
ated with this approach include encouragement of normal socialization, cessation of
social isolation, and development of communication skills to improve relationships
and decrease eating in response to emotion. Elements relevant to FBT for AN and
ARFID include structured eating and changing the environment to make eating and
weight gain more likely.
Several investigators have examined variables that may predict treatment out-
come or serve as mediators for change. Across studies of different treatments, early
treatment response appears to predict both shorter- and longer-term outcomes.
Specifically, improvement in binge eating and purging during the first 4 weeks of
CBT or IPT treatment (Agras et al., 2000), as well as during the first few weeks of
antidepressant treatment (Walsh et al., 2006), predicts symptom remission at the
end of treatment for individuals with BN. While it is unclear whether there is some-
thing inherently different (and more likely to remit) about the individuals who
respond quickly to treatment, these findings suggest that treatment should empha-
size behavioral change and that individuals who do not experience symptom reso-
lution early in a treatment course may need a change in the intervention. Relat-
edly, in an analysis of variables thought possibly to mediate treatment response,
Wilson and colleagues (2002) found that improvement in dietary restraint mediated
improvement in binge eating in patients with BN receiving either CBT or IPT in a
randomized trial. This finding was surprising, as normalization of eating behav-
iors was a direct treatment focus in CBT and not IPT, yet normalization of restraint
appeared to be a mediator of both therapeutic approaches, questioning whether the
mechanisms of therapeutic effect for these two seemingly different treatments are
really so different (Wilson et al., 2002). Among weight-restored individuals with
AN, consumption of a diet higher in variety and caloric density predicts likelihood
of maintaining normal weight during the year following acute weight restoration.
Taken together, these findings on predictors and mediators of outcome indirectly
suggest that normalizing eating behavior is central to better outcomes in eating
disorder treatments.

-- 229 of 498 --

Eating Disorders 	209
TREATMENT INTENSITY
Available services for treating patients with eating disorders range from varying
levels of outpatient care (e.g., weekly or biweekly psychotherapy, nutritional coun-
seling, psychiatric care) to intensive outpatient, residential, and inpatient programs.
Because specialized programs are not available in all geographic regions, it can be
difficult to access appropriate care. Nonetheless, we present guidelines below for
how clinicians might choose the most appropriate level of care based on patients’
clinical status. Relevant available data on treatment intensity are included.
Anorexia Nervosa
While the specific mechanism responsible for psychological changes in individuals
with AN remains uncertain, the improved nutritional state that accompanies refeed-
ing is presumed to enable better cognitive functioning and emotional regulation
(Attia & Walsh, 2009). Therefore, weight restoration is an essential primary treat-
ment goal. However, because patients with acute AN have considerable difficulty
gaining weight outside of a highly structured treatment, many of those who seek
treatment are ultimately guided to an inpatient or residential treatment program
(Guarda, 2008).
Admission to an inpatient unit is recommended for adult patients who have lost
a substantial amount of weight, particularly if the weight loss has been rapid, and for
those whose weight is extremely low for their height (e.g., <75% ideal body weight;
Attia & Walsh, 2009). Inpatient treatment may also be appropriate for youth with
AN who do not gain the expected amount of weight for their height and age (Attia
& Walsh, 2009). Some clinicians may be reluctant to recommend inpatient treatment,
but data suggest that outcome is better if inpatient treatment is pursued before the
onset of medical instability secondary to low weight (Yager, 2007).
The limited availability of specialized inpatient units that treat eating disorders,
together with the significant expense of hospital treatments and challenges of third-
party reimbursement, often make it difficult for patients to find appropriate hospi-
tal programs or to stay in hospital programs for as long as may be recommended.
Changes in U.S. health care economics have dramatically shortened hospital stays,
and the limited weight gain achieved by patients with AN on inpatient units raises
the question of whether shorter stays and lower discharge weights may contribute to
higher relapse rates (Wiseman, Sunday, Klapper, Harris, & Halmi, 2001).
As hospital programs have become less available, newly developed residential
and structured behavioral day treatment programs have filled the gap. To date, clini-
cal outcome information from residential treatment programs is limited, and while
many programs provide some data on their websites as part of promotional market-
ing efforts, this information has not typically been through a rigorous peer review
process (Attia, Blackwood, Guarda, Marcus, & Rothman, 2016; Guarda, Wonderlich,
Kaye, & Attia, 2018). Structured day treatment programs have demonstrated some
efficacy in helping patients to normalize weight, albeit at a more modest pace. One
uncontrolled comparison of specialized inpatient versus day treatment behavioral
programs observed an average weight gain in patients of approximately 4 pounds

-- 230 of 498 --

210 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
per week and 2 pounds per week, respectively (Guarda, 2008). Overall, outcomes
from specialized day treatment programs are strongly associated with treatment
intensity. More successful programs require treatment participation at least 5 days
per week, 8 hours per day (Olmsted, Kaplan, & Rockert, 2003). While intensive, struc-
tured programming helps in the short-term management of AN, the long-term ben-
efit remains unknown (Attia & Walsh, 2009).
Outpatient treatment for acute AN may be considered for adolescents whose
families are committed to FBT aimed at weight restoration (Lock & Le Grange, 2012).
Otherwise, outpatient intervention is typically indicated for the prevention of relapse
following weight restoration in a structured setting or for patients with symptoms
of AN that are at borderline diagnostic threshold (e.g., individuals at approximately
85% ideal body weight with associated features of AN). Weekly psychotherapy is
an opportunity for patients to normalize their attitudes about weight, body shape,
and eating behavior. Outpatient appointments also provide an opportunity for cli-
nicians to monitor the weight status. If patients are unable to achieve or sustain a
healthy weight range, clinicians should recommend an increase in session frequency
to twice per week, adjunctive nutritional counseling sessions, or a structured behav-
ioral program.
Bulimia Nervosa
Many patients with uncomplicated BN do not require hospitalization. Depending on
the frequency of binge–purge behaviors and severity of associated symptoms, clini-
cians should consider biweekly psychotherapy appointments. In a standard course
of CBT-BN, for example, it is recommended to start with two scheduled sessions per
week for the first month of treatment to help patients implement self-monitoring
techniques, adopt a regular eating pattern, and break the binge–purge cycle (Fair-
burn, Marcus, et al., 1993). In initial biweekly IPT sessions, the clinician completes a
detailed analysis of the interpersonal context in which the eating disorder has been
developed and maintained (Agras et al., 2000). After the early phase of treatment,
with some symptom remission, psychotherapy sessions may occur weekly, then
bimonthly.
If a patient with BN does not respond to an adequate trial of outpatient psycho-
therapy, a higher level of care should be considered. Additional indications for more
structured treatment include severely impairing symptoms that have not responded
to adequate trials of psychotropic medication, serious concurrent medical problems,
or psychiatric conditions requiring an inpatient treatment independent of the eating
disorder (Yager, 2007).
Though inpatient treatment has long been recognized as the viable alternative
to outpatient care for treatment-resistant BN, day treatment is an alternative along
the continuum of care. Day treatment programs offer a cost-effective alternative to
