345
Problems in interpersonal relationships are core or associated features of most forms
of psychopathology. Psychopathology occurs in a social context, and this observa-
tion dates back well over a century. Relational discord plays a role in the etiology,
maintenance, and relapse of a range of mental health problems (Foran, Whisman,
& Beach, 2015; Whisman & Baucom, 2012) and physical health outcomes (Robles,
Slatcher, Trombello, & McGinn, 2014) in adulthood; family relationships also provide
the context for most forms of psychopathology that begin in childhood and adoles-
cence (Wamboldt & Wamboldt, 2000). Furthermore, couple-based treatments have
been shown to be effective for some psychiatric disorders and physical health prob-
lems (Baucom, Whisman, & Paprocki, 2012; Snyder, Castellani, & Whisman, 2006).
In this chapter, we review what is known about the presentation, classification,
prevalence, course, comorbidities, and available treatment options for marital and
relational discord (MRD). We limit the scope of our review on MRD to adult marital
or long-term committed partnerships, which we call “marriage-like relationships,”
given that the most immediate and relevant interpersonal context for adults is mar-
riage or a marriage-like partnership. From our perspective, the inclusion of a chapter
on MRD in a volume on psychopathology–psychotherapy integration is a positive
sign of the times; it is inarguable that MRD is associated with considerable suffer-
ing, both in its own right and in its association with mental and physical health and
well-being.
Although MRD can be understood in many of the same ways we typically think
about individual psychopathology, MRD also contains at least two major elements
that make it distinct from the other disorders considered in this volume. First, MRD
C H A P T E R 1 2
Marital and Relational Discord
David A. Sbarra
Atina Manvelian
Julia M. Salinger
Mark A. Whisman

-- 366 of 498 --

346 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
is inherently dyadic (First et al., 2002); people cannot have marital or relational dis-
cord unless they are in a relationship. It is therefore important to think about MRD as
a systemic problem when considering assessment, etiology, and treatment. Second,
the relationships that define the context for MRD are—in most of the Western indus-
trialized world—based on choice. Most love relationships hinge on free will and the
choice two people make to live together. A corollary of this point, then, is that people
can choose to greatly mitigate the psychological toll of MRD by ending the relation-
ship. It is clear that the same cannot be said for major depression or panic disorder,
for example; we cannot disavow the part of ourselves that causes us to feel sad and
guilty in the same way we can attempt to solve MRD by ending a dysfunctional rela-
tionship. We return to this point at the end of the chapter when we review literature
on the available treatments for MRD.
TYPICAL SYMPTOMS
MRD is a heterogeneous and broad construct. In DSM-5 (American Psychiatric Asso-
ciation, 2013), MRD is included under “other conditions that may be a focus of clini-
cal attention.” Specifically, “relationship distress with spouse or intimate partner”
(V61.1) is to be used when the major focus of treatment is intimate relationship qual-
ity or when intimate relationship quality is impacting the course, prognosis, or treat-
ment of a mental or other medical disorder. As defined in DSM-5, MRD is typically
associated with impaired functioning in the domains of behavior (e.g., withdrawal,
overinvolvement, or difficulty with conflict resolution), cognition (e.g., chronic nega-
tive attributions of a partner’s actions or intentions, dismissal of a partner’s posi-
tive behaviors), and affect (e.g., chronic sadness, apathy, and/or anger). DSM-5 also
includes codes for problems involving adult maltreatment and neglect in the section
on other conditions that may be a focus of clinical attention. These include physical
or sexual spousal or partner violence and psychological spousal or partner abuse.
MRD is significantly associated with intimate partner violence (Stith, Green, Smith,
& Ward, 2008). However, because intimate partner violence has its own set of risk
factors (Stith, Smith, Penn, Ward, & Tritt, 2004) and its own treatment considerations,
we omit a thorough discussion of this topic and point the interested reader to more
in-depth coverage elsewhere (e.g., Eckhardt et al., 2013).
Clinical Implications
Although a detailed description of assessing MRD and couple functioning is beyond
the scope of this chapter, we encourage clinicians to assess partners’ global evalua-
tion of their relationship (i.e., relationship satisfaction), as well as specific domains
of functioning that reflect relationship quality (as outlined by Snyder, Heyman,
& Haynes, 2005). First, couple-based assessment evaluates relationship behaviors,
including partners’ ability to communicate and successfully solve problems and
resolve conflict. Second, couple-based assessment examines relationship cognitions,
such as causal and responsibility attributions for partner behavior, and relationship
assumptions, standards, and expectancies. Third, couple-based assessment evalu-
ates relationship affect, including partners’ intensity, duration, and reciprocity of
negative feelings about each other and their relationship. These domains are broadly

-- 367 of 498 --

Marital and Relational Discord 	347
consistent with those outlined by Lawrence and colleagues (2011) and included in
the Relationship Quality Interview, which is a semistructured interview of relational
behaviors that evaluates the following domains: (1) quality of emotional intimacy,
(2) quality of the couple’s sexual satisfaction, (3) quality of support transactions, (4)
quality of the couple’s ability to share power in the relationship, and (5) quality of
problem solving in the relationship. A thorough couple-based assessment should
also incorporate an evaluation of individual differences that may impact relationship
quality, such as psychopathology, health problems, personality characteristics, and
attachment; within-couple differences, such as cultural norms and family histories;
and external factors (e.g., environmental stress, discrimination, neighborhood vio-
lence) that may impact relationship quality. Assessment should be done regularly
during the course of treatment, not just at the onset of treatment. Repeated assess-
ment allows clinicians to evaluate whether partners continue to exhibit individual
distress, even after the relationship has improved, which may suggest the need for
extending treatment or supplementing couple-based treatments with individual-
based treatments.
ASSOCIATED CLINICAL FEATURES
One of the most common clinical features of MRD is high rates of psychopathol-
ogy, and we consider this topic later in the section on comorbidity. In this section,
we address issues of quality of life, physical health, and sexual functioning, all of
which are important associated clinical features of MRD. First, MRD is associated
with lower quality of life as operationalized in terms of lower subjective well-being.
For example, in a large-scale, nationally representative sample of middle-aged adults
in the United States, marital satisfaction demonstrated the strongest association with
overall life satisfaction, surpassing the associations obtained for finances, children,
health, work, sexuality, and contributions to others (Fleeson, 2004). Similarly, results
from a meta-analysis of the associations between different domains of satisfaction
indicated life satisfaction was more strongly associated with MRD than job or social
satisfaction (Heller, Watson, & Illies, 2004). Longitudinal studies have attempted
to elucidate the directional relationship between MRD and life satisfaction. In a
population-based sample of middle-aged and older couples, lower levels of MRD
at baseline were associated with higher levels of life satisfaction 2 years later and
vice versa, for both husbands and wives (BE, Whisman, & Uebelacker, 2013). Among
women, baseline MRD was a stronger predictor of subsequent life satisfaction than
baseline life satisfaction was of subsequent MRD. More recently, Roberson and col-
leagues (2018) examined cross-lagged associations between quality of life and two
aspects of relational quality (MRD vs. satisfaction) across three data collection time
points spanning 25 years, in a national sample of U.S. adults. Results indicated that
(1) life satisfaction predicts later MRD and (2) life satisfaction and relationship satis-
faction have a bidirectional relationship.
Second, marital quality is associated with health-relevant indicators in adults.
Robles and colleagues (2014) conducted a meta-analysis of 126 studies that had
examined associations between MRD and physical health, operationalized either as
objective and subjective health outcomes (i.e., “clinical endpoints”) or as biological
markers of immune, neuroendocrine, or cardiovascular functioning implicated in

-- 368 of 498 --

348 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
downstream health outcomes (i.e., “surrogate endpoints”). MRD was found to have
a significant association with health, with small effect sizes that were comparable in
magnitude to associations between health and factors such as diet and exercise. A
particularly clear link was supported between MRD and cardiovascular reactivity
during conflict discussions. MRD was also significantly and positively associated
with mortality across several studies of individuals diagnosed with chronic health
conditions (e.g., renal or cardiovascular disease); this finding has since been repli-
cated in a probability sample of married adults not recruited for preexisting health
conditions (Whisman, Gilmour, & Salinger, 2018). Recent reviews have elaborated on
interactive psychological, behavioral, and biological pathways through which MRD
may influence health. Kiecolt-Glaser and Wilson (2017), for example, posit a model
in which the elevated risk for depressive symptoms associated with MRD plays a
key role in couples’ health trajectories. Within this model, stress and depressive
symptoms associated with MRD are thought to up-regulate the acute and sustained
production of proinflammatory cytokines, thereby contributing to downstream risk
for conditions such as cancer, diabetes, obesity, and cardiovascular disease; these
effects may be especially pernicious for women relative to men and for older relative
to younger couples. The association between MRD and inflammatory processes is
thought to be mediated by complex bidirectional feedback loops between biological
processes (i.e., immune, neuroendocrine, metabolic, and cardiovascular function-
ing) and health behaviors (i.e., sleep, appetitive behavior). Other recent reviews have
elaborated on the role of the microbiome (Kiecolt-Glaser, Wilson, & Madison, 2019)
and affective processes (Sbarra & Coan, 2018) as potential key links in mediational
paths between MRD and health, including in relation to inflammatory processes.
Third, MRD is also associated with aspects of couples’ sexual relationships. For
example, in a probability sample of people in the United States, MRD was associ-
ated with elevated risk of arousal problems, orgasmic dysfunction, and inhibited
enjoyment in women (Dunn, Croft, & Hackett, 1999). Although Dunn and colleagues
did not find an association between MRD and sexual problems for men, subsequent
research has indicated there may be an association between MRD and aspects of
male sexual functioning, including sexual desire and erectile dysfunction (Boddi et
al., 2015). Sexual problems are far from the only sex-related factor relevant to MRD;
sexual satisfaction, for example, is a reliable correlate of MRD (Christopher & Spre-
cher, 2000). Longitudinal studies investigating the direction of this association have
yielded mixed results; one study of newlyweds found a significant and negative
bidirectional association between MRD and sexual satisfaction (McNulty, Wenner,
& Fisher, 2016), whereas studies of longer-term married or cohabiting couples have
found that sexual satisfaction is predictive of MRD but MRD is not predictive of
sexual satisfaction (Fallis, Rehman, Woody, & Purdon, 2016), or vice versa (Vowels
& Mark, 2020). MRD and sexual satisfaction have also been conceptualized as recip-
rocal developmental processes that change together over time, with high baseline
sexual satisfaction buffering subsequent increases in MRD (Quinn-Nilas, 2019).
Clinical Implications
A primary implication that emerges from considering the clinical features associ-
ated with MRD is the need to assess a wider range of functioning than MRD alone.

-- 369 of 498 --

Marital and Relational Discord 	349
Quality of life, physical health, and sexual functioning, for example, impact and are
impacted by MRD, and clinicians should consider all of these issues when assess-
ing the broader context of MRD. A useful tool for assessing these domains is the
Treatment Outcome Package (TOP; Kraus, Seligman, & Jordan, 2005), which provides
clinical cutoff scores in a variety of different domains of functioning. When assess-
ing these domains, a critical issue for clinicians to consider is whether the associated
features exacerbate or are exacerbated by MRD. In most cases, it is likely that the
causal arrow of effect is reciprocal and operates in both directions.
CLASSIFICATION
Categorizing individuals or couples as “discordant” implies that there is a mean-
ingful qualitative distinction between discordant and nondiscordant individuals or
couples, and that currently available measures can correctly assign individuals or
couples to the appropriate category. For example, individuals or couples are com-
monly defined as discordant if they score ≤97 on the Dyadic Adjustment Scale (DAS;
Spanier, 1976), which was selected because it is the score that is halfway between
married and divorced populations (Jacobson et al., 1984). Using data from a large
Internet sample, Funk and Rogge (2007) provided cutoff scores for several self-report
measures of relationship adjustment that correspond to this DAS cutoff score. Other
research indicates that marital discord is “taxonic”—MRD exists as a distinct cat-
egory that differs in kind from an underlying distribution of relational distress—and
this work has been replicated in large samples (Beach, Fincham, Amir, & Leonard,
2005; Whisman, Beach, & Snyder, 2008). People who score in the MRD taxon are pre-
sumed to comprise a discrete latent class of individuals who differ qualitatively, not
just quantitatively, from people who are not members of the taxon.
Clinical Implications
If MRD is taxonic, then methods are needed for identifying individuals or couples
that are members of the taxon and therefore may need treatment. Whisman, Snyder,
and Beach (2009) developed a reliable and easy to use 10-item screen for the taxon
that represents a nonarbitrary way of classifying relationships as discordant, thereby
identifying couples who may be in need of treatment. A self-report version of this
screening measure (the Marital Satisfaction Inventory – Brief Form [MSI-B]) is avail-
able from Western Psychological Services (www.wpspublish.com), and an interview
version can be found in Whisman and colleagues (2009). Both versions can be admin-
istered by people with minimal expertise in family psychology, and the measures
are scored by counting the number of coded responses, with couples scoring >4 clas-
sified as discordant and therefore potentially in need of treatment.
The availability of a clinical cutoff on the MSI-B has obvious implications for not
only couple therapists focused on MRD but also for therapists who primarily work
with individuals. As we discuss later in this chapter, couple-based interventions are
effective in treating several different forms of psychopathology. Therefore, because
the administration and scoring of the MSI-B is straightforward, it makes it easy for
clinicians to determine whether someone is a member of the relationship discord

-- 370 of 498 --

350 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
taxon and may therefore be in need of referral for more extensive relationship assess-
ment or treatment.
EPIDEMIOLOGY, COURSE, AND OUTCOMES
Understanding the epidemiology of MRD hinges on understanding the general epi-
demiology of marriage and romantic relationships. Although there are roughly 2.2
million new marriages each year in the United States (6.9 new marriages per 1,000
adults in the United States in 2017; National Vital Statistics System, 2017), when it
comes to the family structure, perhaps one of the greatest demographic changes in
the United States is that marriage rates are declining, the age at first marriage is
increasing, and cohabitation is quite common (Lavner & Bradbury, 2019a, 2019b).
Divorce rates have also decreased over the last 35 years, from 5.0/1,000 marriages in
1970 to 2.9/1,000 in 2017 (Tejada-Vera & Sutton, 2009). Using data from the National
Survey of Family Growth, which involved face-to-face interviews with 10,847 women
ages 15–44 years, Bramlett and Mosher (2002) estimated the divorce rate for first mar-
riages in the United States to be 43%. Among adults who do divorce, 15% remarry
within 1 year, 39% within 3 years, and 75% with 10 years; close to 40% of second mar-
riages break up within 5 years (Bramlett & Mosher, 2002). Despite a general decline
in the average divorce rate across the last two decades, the divorce rate among peo-
ple over 50—referred to as the so-called “gray divorce”—doubled over the past two
decades and now accounts for 25% of all divorces in the United States, with a total
of 600,000 people over the age of 50 becoming divorced in 2010 (Brown & Lin, 2012).
This increase has occurred despite recent stability in the overall divorce rate, sug-
gesting that younger adults are divorcing less frequently, whereas older adults are
divorcing at an increased rate.
Among intact relationships, approximately three out of every 10 couples are
likely to experience MRD (Beach et al., 2005; Whisman et al., 2008), and as such, are
likely to differ in kind, as well as degree, from couples in nondiscordant relationships.
Insofar as it is estimated that over 90% of Americans will marry in their lifetime (Kre-
ider & Fields, 2001), and that most people who do not marry will form other types of
committed, intimate partnerships (e.g., cohabiting relationships), a large percentage
of the population is likely to be exposed to clinically significant MRD at some point
in a lifetime. Although future research needs to determine the rate and predictors
of movement into (or out of) the MRD taxon, it is informative to consider norma-
tive changes in continuous measures of martial satisfaction over time. Whereas a
fair amount of evidence indicates that, normatively, marital satisfaction declines over
time, especially in the early years of marriage, more recent work suggests this view
might be overly pessimistic (Lavner & Bradbury, 2010). For example, in a study span-
ning 20 years, Amato and James (2018) reported that marriages that remained intact
showed little evidence of deterioration over time. Across the entirety of study period,
spouses who did not divorce showed relatively stable levels of marital happiness and
shared activities, with discord steadily decreasing over time; the opposite was true
for spouses who ultimately divorced, and these adults reported relatively precipi-
tous declines in happiness, with fast increases in discord as well. A synthesis of the
empirical work on trajectories of relationship satisfaction over time concluded that

-- 371 of 498 --

Marital and Relational Discord 	351
many of the negative attributions and interactions, as well as limited positivity in the
relationship, were evident at the earliest phases of the relationship and did not often
develop among otherwise happy couples (Lavner & Bradbury, 2019b).
Rates of distress are not evenly distributed across gender, race, and socioeco-
nomic circumstances. In an analysis of marital satisfaction data from the 1973–2006
General Social Survey, Corra, Carter, Carter, and Knox (2009) found independent
effects for both race and gender, with Whites and husbands reporting greater marital
satisfaction than Blacks and wives. Furthermore, they found that when comparing
four subgroups (White husbands, White wives, Black husbands, and Black wives)
over time, although White husbands consistently report the highest level of mari-
tal satisfaction, the difference between groups has been steadily declining. Lower
income couples are at substantially increased risk for divorce relative to higher
income couples (Bramlett & Mosher, 2002). Risk for marital distress is significantly
greater for low-income couples, and the major explanation for this risk focuses on the
context in which these relationships develop; specifically, external negative stress-
ors limit partners’ capacities to attend to and respond constructively to one another
(Neff & Karney, 2017). This account, however, does not appear to translate directly
into decreases in average satisfaction over time. Across the first 4 years of marriage,
lower income couples do not have less satisfying marriages or steeper decreases in
satisfaction, but they do appear to have greater fluctuations in their MRD over time
and more variability between husbands and wives (Jackson, Krull, Bradbury, & Kar-
ney, 2017), which may translate into more turbulent relationships.
Clinical Implications
Taxometric data suggest that marital discord is widespread, affecting approximately
30% of all couples, and 20–25% of newly married couples (Beach et al., 2005; Whis-
man et al., 2008). This suggests clinicians should anticipate that a sizable proportion
of partnered individuals are likely to be unhappy with their relationship; therefore,
clinicians should routinely assess level of relationship quality. The chronic nature
of relationship discord in couples seeking treatment underscores the importance of
obtaining a history of relationship quality, including information about the onset of
relationship problems. Such information may be useful in helping clinicians deter-
mine whether MRD preceded or followed the onset of psychopathology. In situa-
tions in which MRD is believed to be an important contributing factor in the etiology
or maintenance of psychopathology, couple therapy may be indicated, either by itself
or in combination with individual-focused treatments such medication or individual
therapy.
COMORBIDITY
MRD co-occurs with a variety of manifestations of psychopathology. Results from
meta-analyses have found that MRD correlates with depression (Whisman, 2001),
posttraumatic stress disorder (Taft, Watkins, Stafford, Street, & Monson, 2011), and
overall well-being (Proulx, Helms, & Buehler, 2007). Epidemiological studies sug-
gest that many people with an active (i.e., 12-month) psychiatric disorder are not in

-- 372 of 498 --

352 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
treatment (Wang et al., 2005). To make sure that the association between relation-
ship functioning and psychopathology is not limited only to people in treatment—a
minority of all people with psychopathology—researchers have studied the associa-
tion between MRD and psychopathology in probability samples.
MRD has been found to covary with psychiatric disorders in several probability
samples. For example, in a probability sample from Ontario, Canada, 24.5% of people
with a current psychiatric disorder were likely to report troubled relationships with
their spouse, compared to only 8.9% of people without a current psychiatric disor-
der (Whisman, Sheldon, & Goering, 2000). Similarly, in two probability samples of
people across the 48 coterminous United States, MRD was associated with DSM-
III (Whisman, 1999) and DSM-IV (Whisman, 2007) diagnosed mood, anxiety, and
substance use disorders, defined in terms of broad-band classifications of groups of
disorders, and narrow-band classification of specific disorders. Results from other
probability samples have similarly found greater MRD among people with specific
psychiatric disorders, and that the strength of the association between MRD and
psychiatric disorders is consistent across race and ethnicity in the United States
(McShall & Johnson, 2015).
MRD is also associated with personality pathology. For example, in a probabil-
ity sample, borderline personality disorder (BPD) symptom severity was positively
associated with MRD, perpetration of minor and severe marital aggression, and
marital disruption; these associations remained significant when adjusting for other
past-year psychiatric disorders (Whisman & Schonbrun, 2009). Similarly, personal-
ity disorder symptoms, particularly symptoms of BPD and dependent personality
disorder, were associated with MRD and verbal aggression, and perpetration of
physical violence in a community sample of couples; in addition, spouses’ reports
of personality disorder symptoms added incrementally to self-report in predicting
marital functioning (South, Turkheimer, & Oltmanns, 2008). Another study revealed
that both partners’ personality disorder symptoms were associated with MRD in a
community sample of couples (Stroud, Durbin, Saigal, & Knobloch-Fedders, 2010).
Longitudinal studies evaluating MRD and psychopathology have found that
MRD predicts increased risk for psychiatric symptoms and onset of psychiatric dis-
orders. For example, a meta-analysis of prospective studies evaluating the longitudi-
nal association between MRD and subsequent well-being obtained a weighted mean
effect size (r) of .25 (Proulx et al., 2007). Furthermore, the longitudinal association
between MRD and depressive symptoms is comparable in magnitude to the longitu-
dinal association between depressive symptoms and MRD (Davila, Karney, Hall, &
Bradbury, 2003; Whisman & Uebelacker, 2009). With respect to onset of psychiatric
disorders, a 12-month prospective study involving a probability sample of adults
who did not meet criteria for the corresponding disorder at baseline revealed that
MRD at baseline predicted incidence of a major depressive episode (Whisman &
Bruce, 1999) and alcohol abuse or dependence (Whisman, Uebelacker, & Bruce, 2006)
at 12-month follow-up; these associations remained significant when adjusting for
sociodemographic variables and prior history of the respective disorder. Similarly, in
a Dutch probability sample, MRD predicted 2-year incidence of the broad-band diag-
nosis of mood, anxiety, and substance use disorders, and the narrow-band diagnosis
of major depressive disorder, dysthymia, social phobia, and alcohol abuse (Overbeek
et al., 2006).

-- 373 of 498 --

Marital and Relational Discord 	353
Clinical Implications
Evidence that MRD co-occurs with psychopathology, predicts increases in psychiat-
ric symptoms, and is associated with the onset of psychiatric disorders has important
clinical implications. First, it is reasonable to assume that people who are unhappy
with their relationship may be less likely to respond to individually oriented treat-
ments for psychopathology because these treatments do not focus on improving rela-
tionship functioning. Indeed, MRD predicts poorer outcomes for individual-based
treatments for a variety of disorders. For example, a higher level of MRD at pre-
treatment was associated with poorer outcome for (1) depressed individuals receiv-
ing medication, cognitive therapy, or interpersonal psychotherapy (Whisman, 2001);
(2) chronically depressed individuals receiving medication, Cognitive Behavioral
Analysis System of Psychotherapy, or their combination (Denton et al., 2010); and (3)
individuals with recurrent depression receiving cognitive therapy (Trombello et al.,
2019). Similarly, MRD predicted poorer treatment outcome (i.e., greater likelihood of
relapse, fewer days abstinent, and shorter time to relapse) for men with substance
use disorder (Fals-Stewart, O’Farrell, & Hooley, 2001), and greater marital tension
predicted poorer treatment outcome for people with generalized anxiety disorder
(Durham, Allan, & Hackett, 1997). Second, as reviewed in greater detail later in this
chapter, couple-based interventions may be effective in treating psychopathology
(Baucom et al., 2012; Snyder et al., 2006).
ETIOLOGY
Most marriages begin happily and are characterized by optimism. Baker and Emery
(1993) conducted a study to reveal just how optimistic people are about their mar-
riage. One hundred thirty-seven adults who had recently filed for a first marriage
license were surveyed about their knowledge of the general divorce rate and their
estimate of the likelihood that their own marriage would end in divorce. In terms of
the general divorce rate, participants accurately reported that 50% of marriages (in
the early 1990s) would end in divorce, but the median response was 0% when assess-
ing the likelihood that they personally would divorce (Baker & Emery, 1993). Similarly,
when a sample of newlyweds was asked to predict changes in their marital satisfac-
tion over the subsequent 4 years, almost all participants reported that satisfaction
would remain at the present level or increase; nevertheless, there was an average
decline in marital satisfaction at follow-up (Lavner, Karney, & Bradbury, 2013). The
transition from an optimistic beginning to teetering on the brink of divorce appears
to be a developmental process that unfolds over time (Bradbury & Karney, 1993)
and displays significant heterogeneity, impacting a subset of couples (Lavner &
Bradbury, 2019b). Gottman (1994) referred to this process as the “cascade model of
marital dissolution.” Here we detail several major approaches to understanding the
cascade into MRD. It is important to recognize that decline in relationship quality
and emergence of MRD occurs at multiple levels, including interactions between
partners, psychological processes within individuals, and contextual stressors that
impact both between- and within-person dynamics. Following our review of etio-
logical models of MRD, we describe the treatments that stem from these frameworks.

-- 374 of 498 --

354 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
The Cognitive‑Behavioral Model of Relationship Functioning
The cognitive-behavioral model of relationship functioning emphasizes partner
behaviors, as well as people’s interpretations and evaluations of their own and their
partners’ behavior. This approach emphasizes the frequency and range of recipro-
cal positive reinforcers exchanged between partners: Relationship satisfaction is
high when rates of relationship rewards (or reinforcers) exceed rates of relationship
costs, and when the differences between the rates of rewards and costs received
exceed one’s standard of what one thinks one deserves (Kelley & Thibaut, 1978).
This approach also emphasizes partners’ ability to communicate, successfully nego-
tiate behavior change, and resolve problems and conflict in their relationship. For
example, compared to people who are satisfied with their relationship, dissatisfied
partners are more likely to engage in negative verbal and nonverbal communication,
including negative reciprocity (i.e., the tendency for negative behavior by one person
to be followed by negative behavior by the partner; for a review, see Salazar, 2015);
and demand–withdraw communication, in which one person pressures the part-
ner with demands, complaints, or criticisms and the partner withdraws with defen-
siveness and inaction (Christensen & Heavey, 1990; for a meta-analysis, see Burrell,
Kartch, Allen, & Hill, 2014).
The cognitive-behavioral approach emphasizes not only behaviors exchanged
between partners but also partners’ interpretations of these behaviors. For example,
compared to partners who are satisfied with their relationship, dissatisfied partners
are more likely to attribute each other’s negative behavior to broad and stable traits,
and to view negative partner behavior as selfishly motivated and worthy of blame
(Bradbury & Fincham, 1990). Dissatisfied partners are also more likely to hold dys-
functional or extreme assumptions (i.e., beliefs about how things are), standards (i.e.,
beliefs about how things should be), and expectations (i.e., beliefs about how things
will be) with respect to themselves, their partners, and their relationship (Baucom,
Epstein, Sayers, & Sher, 1989). Additionally, people who desire to form or maintain
close bonds with a partner tend to engage in motivated cognition, which is defined as
thinking in ways that produce conclusions consistent with one’s desires (Lemay &
Clark, 2015). In other words, motivation to bond with a partner can positively bias
people’s perceptions of their relationships and their relationship partners. For exam-
ple, people may utilize a variety of cognitive processes—such as selectively attend-
ing to information that is consistent with their desires, remembering information
in a biased manner, or making biased interpretations of situations—that help them
draw the conclusion that they are valued, cared for, and understood by their partner
(Lemay & Clark, 2015).
Attachment Theory and MRD
A great deal is written about the role of attachment theory and relationship dis-
tress. Basic research has demonstrated that individual differences in attachment
are associated with MRD. Attachment insecurity can be characterized along two
dimensions—anxiety and avoidance—with people high in anxiety hyperactivat-
ing their attachment needs and people high in avoidance deactivating their attach-
ment needs (Mikulincer & Shaver, 2007). A large body of evidence indicates that

-- 375 of 498 --

Marital and Relational Discord 	355
attachment insecurity is associated with difficulties with self-disclosure, trust, and
intimacy, as well as lower levels of commitment and satisfaction in romantic rela-
tionships, higher levels of marital conflict, poor conflict management skills, and
problems seeking or receiving support from one’s partner (Banse, 2004). Adults with
high-anxiety or high-avoidance dimensions are at-risk for MRD, with effect sizes (r)
of –.29 and –.42 between relationship satisfaction and anxious attachment and avoid-
ant attachment, respectively (Hadden, Smith, & Webster, 2014). In addition, the larger
the attachment discrepancy between partners, the greater the risk for MRD (Banse,
2004; Milad, Ottenberger, & Artigas, 2014).
In one of the first studies to examine mechanisms of the association between
attachment and marital satisfaction, Davila, Bradbury, and Fincham (1998) found that
“negative affectivity,” which they defined as the stable tendency to experience and
express negative emotion, mediated the association between husbands’ and wives’
attachment anxiety and marital satisfaction. The results of this study also revealed
an interesting partner effect, in which husbands’ satisfaction was negatively associ-
ated with wives’ anxiety (with no effect from husbands’ satisfaction to wives), sug-
gesting that it is important to consider gender-specific effects when studying the link
between attachment and marital satisfaction or discord (Davila et al., 1998). Other
research suggests that marital partners who are both securely attached report the
highest levels of marital adjustment (Siegel, Levin, & Solomon, 2019). High levels of
attachment security associated with high levels of marital satisfaction may promote
positive perceptions of one’s partner, which, in turn, are associated with greater
social support behavior (Cobb, Davila, & Bradbury, 2001). Attachment orientations
demonstrate how mental representations of interpersonal dynamics—that is, the
degree to which we believe we are worthy of love and the extent we believe we can
trust intimate partners—can profoundly impact rates of MRD.
Contextual Variables and MRD
The last two decades have witnessed a surge of research dedicated to understanding
how life circumstances and stressors are associated with relational quality (Karney
& Bradbury, 2005; Randall & Bodenmann, 2009, 2017; Story & Bradbury, 2004). It is
well established that financial hardship and daily stress increase risk for divorce
(Lavee, McCubbin, & Olson, 1987), and a major focus of the research in this area is
to understand how external forces are associated with increases in MRD. Important
distinctions in this area include intensity of stress (major vs. minor stress), duration
of stress exposure (acute vs. chronic stress), and source or locus of stress (outside
or inside the relationship), and whether individuals and couples can cope with and
adjust to the experience of stress (Randall & Bodenmann, 2009). The experience of
stress in domains of life outside the relationship (e.g., work) may spill over into one’s
relationship, causing stress within the relationship (Neff & Karney, 2017; Randall
& Bodenmann, 2017). For instance, Neff and Karney (2004) assessed 82 newlywed
couples every 6 months for the first 4 years of marriage and found that wives who
experienced the highest levels of stress spillover demonstrated the greatest declines
in marital satisfaction over the study, and that increased negative perceptions of rela-
tionship problems mediated the association between stress and relationship quality.
Building on this research, Neff and Karney (2007) also found evidence for a dyadic

-- 376 of 498 --

356 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
crossover effect, whereby husbands reported lower satisfaction when their wives
experienced higher stress.
Clinical Implications
The brief overview of etiological models of MRD offers several directions for treat-
ment that may help in preventing or treating MRD, which we discuss in detail below.
For example, the cognitive-behavioral model suggests that increasing the rates of
positive relationship behaviors, improving communication and problem-solving
skills, and recognizing and modifying unrealistic or distorted cognitions should
improve relationship quality. Attachment theory suggests that helping couples
change their emotional engagement, responsiveness, and soothing behaviors may
improve the quality of their relationship by helping partners attend to one another.
Finally, helping couples cope during times of stress, including during the transition
to parenthood, may improve MRD.
TREATMENTS AND TREATMENT OUTCOME
Preventing MRD
Given that most couples begin marriage with minimal discord and a high degree of
optimism, preventive interventions have been developed to capitalize on relation-
ship strengths and reduce risk for downstream MRD. Many of the existing evidence-
based preventive interventions for MRD draw from the cognitive-behavioral frame-
work focus on increasing healthy communication and relationship behaviors, while
decreasing unhealthy behaviors (Jakubowski, Milne, Brunner, & Miller, 2004).
Although differing terms have been used to refer to interventions intended to pre-
vent MRD, one of the most common terms is “relationship education” (RE), which
can be defined as “efforts or programs that provide education, skills, and principles
that help individuals . . . and couples . . . increase their chances of having healthy and
stable relationships” (Markman & Rhoades, 2012, p. 169).
Several meta-analyses of randomized controlled trials of relationship education
programs have supported their efficacy, with small and statistically significant pos-
tintervention differences between RE and control groups; however, findings regard-
ing maintenance of intervention effects are mixed (see Cowan & Cowan, 2014, for a
review of meta-analyses). The pattern of moderation findings across meta-analyses
indicates that effect sizes may range from small to medium, depending on the length
of follow-up, type of measure, intervention dose (or more than or less than 8 hours),
and lay or professional delivery. Jakubowski and colleagues (2004) designated four
RE programs as empirically supported, on the basis of significant findings in at least
two randomized clinical trials conducted by different researchers; three were con-
sidered possibly efficacious, and six were empirically untested.
A criticism of RE research has been that clinical trials largely consist of middle-
class, nonminority couples, and may not be efficacious for couples experiencing
contextual stressors (Johnson, 2012). A recent meta-analysis provided support for
the efficacy of RE interventions for low-income couples; pooled positive relationship
outcomes were significantly higher postintervention for couples randomized to RE

-- 377 of 498 --

Marital and Relational Discord 	357
programs relative to control groups, though with a smaller effect size than estimates
obtained in prior meta-analyses (Arnold & Beelman, 2019). There is also evidence
that increases in MRD during the transition to parenthood are reducible through
the use of RE programs (Schulz, Cowan, & Cowan, 2006), and this may be the case
for women in particular (Daley-McCoy, Rogers, & Slade, 2015). These findings are
promising and demonstrate that preventive interventions may forestall the onset of
MRD for couples experiencing stressors such as low income or the transition to par-
enthood.
Outcome Research on the Prevention and Relationship Enhancement Program
Of the established RE programs, the Prevention and Relationship Enhancement Pro-
gram (PREP) in particular has received substantial research attention (Markman &
Rhoades, 2012). PREP was developed with the primary goal of enhancing couples’
communication and problem-solving skills to enable them to proactively and produc-
tively navigate conflict, thereby reducing risk for MRD (Renick, Blumberg, & Mark-
man, 1992). Early research on PREP generally found significant associations between
PREP receipt and lower levels of MRD (for a summary, see Stanley, 2001). However,
as Stanley noted, not all studies of PREP have supported its efficacy for reducing
risk for MRD; moreover, methodological problems, including selection effects and
higher attrition rates in control groups, can complicate interpretation of follow-up
findings of PREP studies. A review of studies that have utilized data from one of the
largest randomized controlled trials of a PREP variant to date, implemented among
Army couples, drew mixed conclusions about the efficacy of PREP (Allen, Stanley,
Rhoades, & Markman, 2015). At one of the two study sites, couples who participated
in PREP were significantly less likely to have divorced at 1- and 2-year follow-ups.
However, there was not a significant difference in MRD between PREP and control
groups across sites 2 years postintervention. The effect of group (i.e., PREP vs. con-
trol) on MRD differed, based on history of infidelity and premarital cohabitation.
PREP appears to be more effective for couples who are experiencing MRD risk
factors, including contextual stressors. For example, PREP participation has been
associated with decreased risk for MRD, particularly for minority couples and for
couples who cohabited prior to committing to their relationship (Allen et al., 2015).
Participation in PREP has also been associated with a significant reduction in divorce
rate, especially for couples wherein one or both individuals are members of a racial
minority, with a trend toward couples experiencing higher levels of financial strain
also benefiting more from PREP (Allen et al., 2015). Another study documented sig-
nificant decreases in MRD post-PREP, with a large effect size, in a sample of racial/
ethnic minority low-income couples (Owen, Quirk, Bergen, Inch, & France, 2012).
Reducing MRD
Several approaches to couple therapy have been developed for reducing MRD once
it is present. In the following sections, we review approaches to couple therapy that
have been evaluated in multiple trials (for a review of approaches evaluated in single
trials, see Snyder et al., 2006). The interested reader is referred to Gurman, Lebow,
and Snyder (2015) for a more detailed description of these approaches to treatment.

-- 378 of 498 --

358 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
Cognitive‑Behavioral Couple Therapy
In line with the cognitive-behavioral models of MRD, behavioral and cognitive-
behavioral approaches to therapy focus on behavior exchange (i.e., increasing posi-
tive behaviors), communication, and problem-solving skills training, and, in the case
of cognitive-behavioral approaches, cognitive restructuring. In a meta-analysis of
20 couple therapy outcome studies targeting couple distress, Byrne, Carr, and Clark
(2004) reported the mean effect size for couple distress at the end of treatment was
0.95, indicating that couples who received treatment fared better at the end of treat-
ment than 83% of untreated couples. The mean percentage of couples falling into
the nondistressed range of functioning 6 months to 4 years after treatment was 54%
(Byrne et al., 2004). These effect sizes are comparable to (if not stronger than) the
effects frequently observed for individual-based treatments of other disorders.
Integrative Behavioral Couple Therapy
Building on the foundation of what has come be labeled “traditional behavioral cou-
ple therapy” (TBCT), integrative behavioral couple therapy (IBCT) was developed in
the 1990s, and it combines traditional behavioral techniques for promoting change
(e.g., behavior exchange, communication, and problem-solving skills training) with
techniques for promoting emotional acceptance. IBCT focuses on MRD being the
result of the (1) dynamic series of interactions that occur between partners, (2) con-
textual factors that exacerbate relational problems, and (3) differences between part-
ners that influence these interactions (Christensen & Doss, 2017). IBCT theory of
change is that issues in the relationship are the result of triggering behaviors and
sensitive responses to those behaviors. Unlike TBCT, which focuses on altering trig-
gering actions, IBCT therefore aims to change both the triggering event (i.e., trig-
gering action or inaction from Partner A) and the sensitive response (i.e., the way
Partner B responds when triggered). An IBCT therapist will align with both partners;
gather information about their history, context, and individual differences; promote
emotional acceptance of triggers in the relationship; and cultivate more helpful reac-
tions to these triggers.
A review of the research on the efficacy of couple therapy interventions estab-
lishes IBCT as an evidence-based treatment comparable to TBCT (Christensen &
Doss, 2017). For example, in a 5-year longitudinal study, 134 chronically distressed
couples were randomly assigned to IBCT or TBCT. Both IBCT and TBCT produced
significant improvements in relationship satisfaction at the end of treatment.
Importantly, more than half the participants in both groups (69% of IBCT couples
and 60% of TBCT couples) showed clinically significant improvements 2 years post-
treatment (Christensen, Atkins, Yi, Baucom, & George, 2006). This durability find-
ing is especially significant because couples in the BCT–IBCT trial were selected
for being chronically distressed and dissatisfied with their relationship (see Chris-
tensen, Atkins, Yi, Baucom, & George, 2006). Two smaller studies (N = 17) and (N =
21) also demonstrated that IBCT was superior to a wait-list control group and TCBT,
respectively. More recently, IBCT has been adapted and formulated into an impres-
sive online program called OurRelationship. This 8-hour program helps partners
observe and understand the variety of external factors and underlying emotions that

-- 379 of 498 --

Marital and Relational Discord 	359
may be driving their partner’s behavior, then provides them with opportunities to
have key conversations to respond, sharing strategies about how they might develop
better communication skills and help one another discuss core issues. Results from
a randomized controlled trial with 300 couples indicate that the OurRelationship
program demonstrated improvements in relationship satisfaction (d = 0.69), with
effects that were smaller than IBCT’s original efficacy trials (Doss, Benson, Georgia,
& Christensen, 2013).
Baucom, Atkins, Simpson, and Christensen (2009) investigated the predictors
of 2-year outcomes in the TBCT–IBCT trial, with the goal of identifying those who
might benefit most from treatment. Moderation analyses revealed that none of the
intrapersonal distress (e.g., overall mental health, high neuroticism) or interper-
sonal variables (e.g., commitment, sexual satisfaction, power bases) predicted long-
term treatment outcome, nor did any self-reported communication variables (e.g.,
demand–withdraw patterns, constructive communication); instead, several of the
behavioral communication variables interacted with type of treatment to predict
long-term treatment response. For example, the use of “soft influence” tactics (i.e.,
couple-level patterns of language that reflect a collaborative style free of manipu-
lation and pressure for change) predicted improved response in IBCT, with high-
influence tactics predicting an increased likelihood of a couple being classified as
having deteriorated functioning at follow-up. Influence patterns were not associated
with treatment response within TBCT.
Researchers have also examined change mechanisms in TBCT and IBCT. Doss
and colleagues (2004) found both therapies were effective in increasing emotional
acceptance and improving communication behaviors across the course of therapy;
however, these changes differed by treatment in a manner consistent with their
hypothesized change mechanisms. Specifically, acceptance increased significantly
more for couples in IBCT than for couples in TBCT, whereas couples in TBCT showed
larger gains in positive communication. In addition, examination of change sepa-
rately in the first and second halves of therapy indicated that change in targeted
behaviors predicted changes in relationships satisfaction early in therapy, whereas
in the second half of therapy, emotional acceptance was more strongly related to
changes in satisfaction.
Emotion‑Focused Couple Therapy
Emotion-focused couple therapy (EFCT; Wiebe & Johnson, 2016) is an experien-
tial couple therapy intervention rooted in attachment theory that draws from sys-
temic and humanistic traditions. This model suggests that couple distress is main-
tained by maladaptive interaction patterns that promote emotional disconnection
and insecure attachment. EFCT focuses on alleviating couple distress by creating a
greater sense of secure attachment between partners. Specifically, EFCT therapists
aim to help couples understand one another’s primary emotions and attune to one
another’s attachment needs, helping them become more accessible, responsive, and
engaged (Johnson, 2019). EFCT is divided into the following three stages: (1) deesca-
lation of the negative cycle—the identification of negative interaction cycles as the
issue in the relationship and simultaneous uncovering of hidden primary emotions/
core beliefs that maintain couple distress; (2) restructuring interactions—the shaping

-- 380 of 498 --

360 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
of more helpful emotional experiences and interactions by encouraging partners to
explore and share their attachment needs with one another; and (3) consolidation—
reinforcing new interaction patterns and promoting couples’ abilities to solve prob-
lems (Johnson, 2019). An EFCT therapist focuses on creating a safe alliance with
partners, identifying their individual attachment histories/core beliefs around rela-
tionships, helping them articulate their emotions, and choreographing emotional
interactions to cultivate secure attachment patterns.
A variety of clinical trials suggest that EFCT is an evidence-based couple therapy
intervention (Wiebe & Johnson, 2016). In the previously referenced meta-analysis of
20 couple therapy outcome studies specifically targeting couple distress, Byrne and
colleagues (2004) reported large effect sizes for EFCT. The mean effect size for post-
treatment couple distress was 1.27 for EFCT, indicating that couples in EFCT fared
better at the end of treatment than 89% of untreated couples. Among couples treated
with EFCT, 53% of couples fell into the nondistressed range of functioning 2 months
to 2 years after treatment (Byrne et al., 2004).
In line with attachment theory, process-level research demonstrates that changes
in emotional engagement, responsiveness, and soothing are associated with positive
outcomes in EFCT. For example, a study using a task analysis methodology reported
on three EFCT studies, demonstrating that couples were significantly more support-
ive, affirming, and understanding with each other at the end of treatment than at the
beginning; that greater affiliation and depth of experiencing were highly associated
with couple-rated “peak” session events, as were self-focused disclosing and expres-
sions of emotion; and that therapist-facilitated, intimate self-disclosures increased
affiliative behaviors within a couple (Greenberg, Ford, Alden, & Johnson, 1993).
Together, these findings suggest that EFCT exerts its effects in a manner consistent
with attachment theory. Although more comprehensive studies on mechanisms of
change are needed, it appears that teaching partners to use each other as a secure
base and to engage in behaviors that soften and deepen the emotional connection
within the relationship are among the key elements for the successful treatment of
MRD using EFCT.
Clinical Implications
There are several empirically supported approaches to working with couples present-
ing with MRD. When looking across the multiple efficacious treatments for couples,
what factors can be derived that would be considered general principles, or unify-
ing themes, of change? Although entire volumes are dedicated to different perspec-
tives on this issue (Gurman et al., 2015), efforts have been made to integrate diverse
themes in the treatment of MRD. Sprenkle and Blow (2004), for example, highlight
three nonspecific (general) treatment variables that are common to marriage and
family therapies: behavioral regulation (i.e., changing interactional patterns among
and between people), cognitive mastery (i.e., clients gaining new perspectives about
themselves and their partners, as well as new insights into interaction processes), and
emotional experiencing (i.e., clients changing the way they experience and respond
to emotional events). These therapeutic processes are common to many individual
interventions as well, and Sprenkle and Blow further suggest three common factors
unique to couple and family treatments: relational conceptualizations (i.e., a view

-- 381 of 498 --

Marital and Relational Discord 	361
of family problems as relational in nature and embedded in the relationship con-
text), expanded direct treatment system (i.e., a push in most couple treatments to
treat the couple over the individual), and the expanded therapeutic alliance (i.e., the
therapist must form a strong alliance with both members of the couple). As another
example, Snyder, Schneider, and Castellani (2003) proposed an organizational model
for sequencing and pacing couple interventions using a pluralistic approach that
draws on diverse theoretical perspectives and interventions. In short, these authors
proposed six levels of intervention that progress across three broad domains. This
model begins with the use of interventions that establish a collaborative alliance,
contain crises (e.g., physical aggression, infidelity), and strengthen the couple’s rela-
tionship. This is followed by behavioral techniques (e.g., communication skills train-
ing) that promote relevant relationship skills. Finally, the model encourages the use
of cognitive and insight-oriented approaches to address intrapersonal factors (e.g.,
unrealistic standards, previous relationship injuries) linked to relationship function-
ing.
Beyond these common factors, one way to think about unifying principles for
treating MRD is to consider what changes need to occur for most couples who experi-
ence clinically significant MRD. Of course, there exists no mean or modal couple, and
functional-analytic assessment of idiographic, controlling variables plays as impor-
tant a role in couple treatment as in individual treatment. The dangers of generali-
ties notwithstanding, the most common reasons that couples give for seeking couple
therapy are problematic communication and lack of emotional affection (Doss, Simp-
son, & Christensen, 2004). Similarly, couple therapists report that the most common
problems in couples presenting for therapy are communication, power struggles,
and unrealistic expectations in the relationship or one’s partner (Whisman, Dixon,
& Johnson, 1997).
Treating MRD and Co‑Occurring Psychopathology
Because MRD predicts the onset and course of some forms of psychopathology, it is
reasonable to assume that involving partners of people with psychopathology may
improve both partners’ mental health and well-being. Indeed, there is good evidence
that couple-based intervention can be effective in terms of improving not only rela-
tionship quality but also mental health outcomes. In a mega-analysis of 20 meta-
analyses of different couple therapies, Shadish and Baldwin (2003) reported that the
average person receiving treatment for couple therapy was better off at the end of
treatment than 80% of members of the no-treatment control group. A more focused
meta-analysis of 30 studies comparing BCT to a no-treatment control found a smaller
effect size (d = 0.59) but clearly demonstrated that BCT is effective over a range of
representative clinical conditions (Shadish & Baldwin, 2003).
There are several specific forms of psychopathology for which couple therapy is
effective. First, couple therapy is an effective treatment for major depression. Results
from a meta-analysis of eight controlled trials indicated no differences between cou-
ple therapy and individual psychotherapy in the treatment of depression (measured
in terms of either depressive symptoms or persistence of depression) and that couple
therapy was more effective than individual psychotherapy in improving MRD, par-
ticularly among couples in distressed relationships at the beginning of treatment

-- 382 of 498 --

362 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
(Barbato & D’Avanzo, 2008). Second, TBCT has been shown to be an effective treat-
ment for substance use disorders. A meta-analysis of 12 controlled trials reported
that couple therapy was more effective than individual-based treatments in improv-
ing relationship satisfaction at posttreatment, and more effective than individual-
based treatments in impacting frequency of use, consequences of use, and relation-
ship satisfaction at follow-up (Powers et al., 2008). Other reviews have concluded
that compared to individual-based treatments for substance abuse, TBCT results in
significantly higher reductions in partner violence, greater improvements in psycho-
social functioning of children, and better cost–benefit results and cost-effectiveness
(Fals-Stewart, O’Farrell, Birchler, Cordova, & Kelley, 2005). Finally, there is emerging
evidence that couple therapies based on behavioral principles may be effective in the
treatment of PTSD (Monson et al., 2012) and anorexia nervosa (Baucom et al., 2017),
and a couple-assisted CBT program may be helpful in the treatment of obsessive-
compulsive disorder (Abramowitz et al., 2013).
CONCLUSION
Humans are naturally motivated to form and maintain interpersonal bonds, and
most people spend much of their adult life married or in a marriage-like relation-
ship. Consequently, many people experience MRD, and the associated emotional and
behavioral sequelae, at least some time in their life. In this chapter, we have provided
an overview of the presentation, classification, prevalence and course, comorbidities,
and available treatment options of MRD. An important limitation of the literature on
MRD is that the majority of studies have recruited and focused on Western or white
samples, with more diverse couples only recently being prioritized and studied. To
promote a better understanding of how MRD functions in diverse groups of couples,
researchers might choose to focus more specifically on MRD across diverse contexts
(e.g., within different cultures and sexual orientations). It is our hope that contin-
ued research on the onset, prevention, and treatment of MRD will help not only to
improve intimate relationships but also enhance partnered individuals’ quality of
life and improve their physical and mental health.

-- 389 of 498 --

369
Over 50% of individuals from general population samples report being dissatisfied
with the sexual aspects of their relationship (Laumann & Gagnon, 1995). Among
these, 43% of women and 31% of men report a sexual issue, such as low desire, prob-
lems with vaginal lubrication, and erectile difficulties (Laumann, Paik, & Rosen,
1999). Based on these data, sexual issues overall appear to be common. We focus in
this chapter on sexual dysfunctions—sexual issues that are associated with clini-
cally significant distress and are experienced for more than 6 months—as defined
by the fifth edition of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-5;
American Psychiatric Association, 2013). Specifically, we highlight the symptoms,
prevalence, etiologies, course, and treatment outcome of the sexual dysfunctions,
and discuss clinical implications.
TYPICAL SYMPTOMS AND EPIDEMIOLOGY
Sexual dysfunctions are characterized by disturbances in a person’s ability to respond
sexually or to experience sexual pleasure. Sexual dysfunctions consist of clinically
significant issues with desire, interest, arousal (e.g., erection), orgasm (e.g., ejacula-
tion), and genito-pelvic pain and penetration. It should be noted, however, that if the
presence of sexual dysfunctions can be explained by comorbid nonsexual mental
disorders (e.g., a mood disorder), only the nonsexual disorder diagnosis should be
made (American Psychiatric Association, 2013). In addition, the diagnosis of a sexual
dysfunction requires that issues better explained by the effects of a medical condi-
tion or by severe relationship distress, partner violence, or other stressors are ruled
out (American Psychiatric Association, 2013). However, sexual dysfunctions may be
diagnosed as being due to the use of medications and/or substances.
C H A P T E R 1 3
Sexual Dysfunctions
Caroline F. Pukall
Sophie Bergeron

-- 390 of 498 --

370 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
DSM-5 sexual dysfunctions are organized into four main categories of sexual
response: (1) desire/interest dysfunctions, which describe issues with sexual fanta-
sies and the wish to engage in sexual activity; (2) arousal dysfunctions, which are
characterized by concerns related to the subjective feelings of sexual pleasure and
associated physiological changes (e.g., erection); (3) orgasm dysfunctions, which are
problems with the timing of the release of sexual tension; and (4) genito-pelvic pain/
penetration disorder (GPPD), which describes issues that prevent vaginal penetra-
tion (e.g., pain, severe anxiety about penetration). It is important to note that these
dysfunctions are highly comorbid, especially for women (McCabe et al., 2016). For
example, genito-pelvic pain can potentially interfere with any or all aspects of sexual
response, and a client presenting with orgasm difficulties may also experience prob-
lems with desire/interest. The overall prevalence of sexual dysfunction in women
has been reported to be 40–50% regardless of age; however, an overall prevalence
rate of sexual dysfunction in men is difficult to ascertain given the large number of
studies focusing on erectile disorder and premature (early) ejaculation to the near
exclusion of the other male sexual dysfunctions (McCabe et al., 2016). Nevertheless,
a Swiss longitudinal study of men and women ages 20–50 reported higher lifetime
risks for any sexual problem in women (67.0%) as compared to men (46.0%), and func-
tional, emotional, and sexual desire issues more frequently overlapped in women
(16.9 vs. 5.0% for men) (Angst, Hengartner, Rossler, Ajdacic-Gross, & Leeners, 2015).
As displayed in Table 13.1, DSM-5 lists seven sexual dysfunctions and three
“other” designations (i.e., substance/medication-induced sexual dysfunction, other
specified sexual dysfunction, and unspecified sexual dysfunction) that can be diag-
nosed when the sexual problem is associated with significant distress and has been
present for a minimum of 6 months (American Psychiatric Association, 2013). Ques-
tions asked during the clinical interview should determine the specifications (i.e.,
onset, context, and distress severity) of each sexual dysfunction (for more informa-
tion regarding the clinical assessment of sexual dysfunctions, see Pukall, Eccles, &
Gauvin, 2019). Onset is “lifelong” when a sexual dysfunction appears to have always
been present; if the sexual dysfunction developed after a period of nonproblematic
experiences, the onset would be specified as “acquired.” Context is specified as “situ-
ational” when the sexual dysfunction is restricted to certain types of stimulation, sit-
uations, or partners, whereas a “generalized” dysfunction indicates that the dysfunc-
tion occurs in all experienced circumstances. “Distress severity” can be designated
as mild, moderate, or severe, and should be documented for all sexual dysfunctions,
except for premature (early) ejaculation (PE). In the latter case, the severity designa-
tion corresponds to the client’s estimate of ejaculatory latency during penetrative
sexual activity; mild would be specified if the ejaculatory latency is between 30 and
60 seconds; moderate, between 15 and 30 seconds; and severe, within 15 seconds or
prior to or at the start of sexual activity) (American Psychiatric Association, 2013).
Clinically, such accurate distinctions are rarely made; rather, general time estimates
(e.g., “just before penetration,” “as soon as I penetrate,” “within a few seconds,”
“within a minute or two”) are used to inform the severity of PE.
Sexual desire disorders affect 15–25% of women and men under the age of
60 and around 40–50% of those over the age of 60 (McCabe et al., 2016). The sex-
ual desire and arousal disorders include male hypoactive sexual desire disorder
(HSDD), female sexual interest/arousal disorder (SIAD), and erectile disorder (ED).

-- 391 of 498 --

Sexual Dysfunctions 	371
The diagnosis of male HSDD is made when a client describes persistent or recur-
rent deficient (or absent) sexual/erotic thoughts or fantasies and desire for sexual
activity (American Psychiatric Association, 2013). Although DSM-5 considers desire
dysfunctions as distinct from arousal dysfunctions for men (i.e., there are separate
diagnoses for HSDD and ED; see Table 13.1), this distinction is not made for women.
Based on research indicating that women report a higher degree of overlap in per-
ceptions of sexual desire and arousal than men (Graham, Sanders, Milhausen, &
McBride, 2004) and that the parallel criteria for HSDD in men and women do not
accurately represent women’s experiences of sexual desire—thereby leading to over-
diagnosis of HSDD in women (Brotto, 2010)—DSM-5 includes the diagnosis of SIAD,
which replaces the former and distinct diagnoses of female hypoactive sexual desire
disorder and female sexual arousal disorder. The experiences of women with SIAD
are likely to be similar to those of women with these previous diagnoses; however,
recent evidence suggests that they exhibit more severe symptoms (O’Loughlin, Bas-
son, & Brotto, 2018), thereby potentially avoiding overdiagnosis of normative and
adaptive changes in desire.
SIAD is characterized by at least three of the following: absent/reduced (1) inter-
est in sexual activity; (2) sexual/erotic thoughts or fantasies; (3) sexual excitement/
pleasure during sexual activity in almost all or all (75–100%) sexual encounters;
(4) sexual interest/arousal in response to any internal or external sexual/erotic cues;

Orgasmic disorder (OD)

or multiple phases
Substance/medication-induced sexual dysfunction (i.e., when the symptoms
of a sexual dysfunction developed during or soon after substance
intoxication, substance withdrawal, or after exposure to a medication as
based on history, physical examination, or laboratory findings, and the
substance/medication is capable of producing the symptoms)

not listed in the DSM-5 is specified and diagnosed; for example, sexual
aversion, persistent genital arousal disorder, hypersexual disordera)

tive, impulse-control, and conduct disorder” given that some believe that it is not a sexual dysfunction (see
