-- 392 of 498 --

372 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
and (5) genital/nongenital sensations during sexual activity (75–100% of encounters);
as well as (6) no/reduced initiation of sexual activity and typically unreceptive to a
partner’s attempts to initiate sexual activity (American Psychiatric Association, 2013).
A diagnosis of ED is made when at least one of the following three symptoms
is experienced on all or almost all (75–100%) sexual encounters: marked difficulty in
obtaining or maintaining an erection, or a marked decrease in erectile rigidity (American
Psychiatric Association, 2013). A pattern of increasing ED with increasing age has
been well documented: The prevalence of erectile disorder is 1–10% under the age
of 40; 2–15% at ages 40–49; around 10–25% at ages 50–59; 20–40% at ages 60–69; and
50–100% in those aged 70 and older (McCabe et al., 2016).
Orgasmic disorders consist of orgasmic disorder (OD) in women, and delayed
ejaculation (DE) and PE in men. OD is characterized by the presence of at least one
of the following symptoms experienced in 75–100% of sexual encounters: marked
delay in, marked infrequency of, or absence of orgasm and markedly reduced inten-
sity of orgasmic sensations (American Psychiatric Association, 2013). OD affects
approximately 16–25% of women, with some studies reporting higher rates in
women over the age of 50 years (McCabe et al., 2016). The diagnostic criteria for DE
include at least one of the following symptoms experienced in 75–100% of sexual
encounters, without desiring the delay: marked delay in ejaculation and marked
infrequency or absence of ejaculation (American Psychiatric Association, 2013). One
article suggests that a median latency of 20 minutes following vaginal penetration
may be clinically useful for future definitions of DE given that this latency dis-
criminates between those with and without DE (Morgentaler et al., 2017). PE, which
affects 11–16% of men (McCabe et al., 2016), is diagnosed when a client describes a
persistent or recurrent pattern of ejaculation within approximately 1 minute follow-
ing vaginal penetration, and before the individual wishes it, that is experienced in
75–100% of partnered sexual encounters. Note that these criteria can also be applied
to nonvaginal penetration activities (e.g., anal penetration) (American Psychiatric
Association, 2013).
GPPPD is a sexual dysfunction that is currently restricted to females; there is no
parallel diagnosis for males, despite the fact that research has established the exis-
tence of genito-pelvic pain in men (Bergeron, Rosen, Pukall, & Corsini-Munt, 2020).
The diagnosis of GPPPD involves persistent or recurrent difficulties with at least one
of the following: (1) vaginal penetration during intercourse; (2) marked vulvovagi-
nal/pelvic pain during vaginal intercourse or penetration attempts; (3) marked fear/
anxiety about vulvovaginal/pelvic pain in anticipation of, during, or as a result of
vaginal penetration; and (4) marked tensing or tightening of the pelvic floor mus-
cles during attempted vaginal penetration (American Psychiatric Association, 2013).
GPPPD has a prevalence of 10–28% (Pukall et al., 2016a), but specific rates depend on
the type of condition (e.g., chronic pelvic pain, vulvodynia).
Associated Clinical Features
Sexual functioning is a complex biopsychosocial process, and these factors play
important roles in the predisposition to, and initiation and maintenance of, all sexual
dysfunctions. Biological factors include general physical health and specific health
conditions, medications and other treatments, one’s diet and exercise routine, sleep

-- 393 of 498 --

Sexual Dysfunctions 	373
habits, and alcohol and drug intake. Psychological influences include anxiety and
depressive symptoms, stress, and coping skills. Social aspects include individuals’
experiences in their family of origin (e.g., communication/education about sexual-
ity and relationships, quality of the caregiver relationship), with peers (e.g., qual-
ity, norms, expectations), with sexual and other partners (e.g., quality, interpersonal
violence, degree of support), and the larger sociocultural context in which they were
raised (e.g., traditional or liberal gender/sex roles). Historical factors can influence
current sexual function and, as discussed in “Clinical Implications” below, care
should be taken to assess both past and current experiences in order to create a
framework that can capture the client’s predisposing, initiating, and maintaining
factors, so that treatment can be targeted and individualized for optimal outcome.
Clinical Implications
Sexual dysfunction is highly prevalent, and sexual issues or concerns should rou-
tinely be part of a comprehensive assessment of one’s overall function, even if the
clinical setting is not specifically geared toward sexual health, independent of the
reason for referral. However, health care providers’ discomfort with raising issues
related to sexuality has been well documented in the literature; these conversations
tend to be limited and highly variable (Reese et al., 2017). Key issues include a lack
of time, perceiving the topic to be too personal, lack of knowledge and training in
sexual issues, and a lack of resources (reviewed in O’Connor et al., 2019). Although
many clinicians prefer to avoid embarrassing or offending clients and some prefer
to wait until the client raises the issue themselves, evidence indicates that clients are
typically comfortable with, and expect, health care providers to ask about sexual
concerns (reviewed in O’Connor et al., 2019). Unfortunately, many clients are dissat-
isfied with the depth of content and level of support given to them by their providers
even when the topic is discussed (Zhou et al., 2016).
In nonsexual health clinical settings, the clinician’s initial question should be
framed generally (e.g., “Do you have any sexual issues that you would like to discuss
with me today?”) and can be preceded by permission to ask about the patient’s sex-
ual health, depending on the context and client. If the response is “yes,” then the cli-
nician should ask general questions about the nature of the issue (see below for more
specific information), and conduct an overall assessment of sexual response (i.e.,
sexual desire/interest, sexual arousal, orgasmic frequency and pleasure, the pres-
ence of any pain). In cases in which several aspects of sexual response and pleasure
are affected, then the clinician should initiate a discussion with respect to where to
start in order to set the focus when moving forward with further assessment and
treatment of the issue or when making a referral to someone with expertise in sexual
health.
In addition, given that sexual issues often arise within a relationship context,
basic questions surrounding the partner’s sexual function and pleasure should be
posed, and the possibility of including the partner in the assessment and treatment
process should be discussed. Research has consistently demonstrated the inter-
dependence of sexual function between partners (Brotto et al., 2016). Clinically, it
is extremely useful to hear the partner’s perspective, and it is often more produc-
tive to involve partners, as they witness the sexual difficulty and may partake in

-- 394 of 498 --

374 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
its maintenance, experience its potential inconvenience, and as such, present sexual
distress and dysfunction themselves. Nevertheless, there is no rule of thumb for
when to involve the partner, and this decision must be based on a careful assessment
of the contributors to the individuals’ sexual dysfunction, as well as their therapy-
related goals and needs. Sometimes, patients consult a therapist on their own rather
than with the partner because they may be ashamed of their difficulty and not yet
ready to discuss it openly with their partner, or because they have sexual secrets
(e.g., infidelity, private sexual interests/preferences/orientation) that they have never
disclosed to their partner and prefer to explore these in a safe space of their own.
Last, if the client is in a relationship with a partner who is willing to be part of the
assessment and treatment process and the health care provider is not competent in
this area, it may be necessary to refer the client to a sexual health therapist who also
has expertise in relationship therapy; if their partner is not willing, then therapy can
continue with the individual but focus only on the individual and their perceptions
of partner responses and behaviors.
In settings with a focus on sexual health, the clinical interview is the main tech-
nique to assess and diagnose sexual dysfunctions. The interview typically starts
with the individual describing the nature of the problem and the reason for seeking
treatment. Following an open-ended description of the problem, the clinician may
ask more specific questions about when the problem started (onset), the conditions
under which it occurs (context), and the extent of the problem and amount of dis-
tress experienced because of the issue (severity). Questions are then asked about the
various biological, psychological, and social issues that might be implicated (Meana,
Binik, & Thaler, 2008). In terms of general biological factors, the clinician should
assess and take into account age, general health status (e.g., body mass index), life-
style factors (e.g., diet, cigarette smoking), hormone levels, chronic pain syndromes
(e.g., chronic prostatitis syndrome, vulvodynia), hormone levels, and medical ill-
nesses that affect vascular, sensory, central, and nervous system functions. In addi-
tion, questions regarding past surgeries and injuries, especially those in the genital
or pelvic region, and past and current medications should be posed (Meana et al.,
2008). It is commonly understood that many medications, such as antidepressants,
antipsychotics, and antihypertensives, can detrimentally affect sexual functioning.
With respect to individual psychological factors, depression and anxiety are
often comorbid with sexual dysfunction. If symptoms of mood issues are present
and treatment does not target them, then treatment will likely not be successful. Sub-
stance abuse disorders may also have a major impact on sexual functioning. Certain
maladaptive cognitive sets, unrealistic expectations, misinformation or lack of infor-
mation, and negative emotional reactions can also impinge upon sexual function.
Past sexual trauma and other negative experiences may set the foundation for sexual
problems as well (Meana et al., 2008). Socially, family-of-origin attitudes regarding
sexuality may be instilled early on and predispose the development of a sexual dys-
function. Assessing the quality of the individual’s current relationship is of utmost
importance, as problems between the members of the couple may be a cause and/
or a consequence of sexual problems. If so, these issues need to be appropriately
addressed. Areas of inquiry related to the couple should include anger, distrust, dis-
crepancies in sexual drive, and preferences, communication, and physical attraction
(Meana et al., 2008). The comorbidity of partner sexual dysfunction is common and

-- 395 of 498 --

Sexual Dysfunctions 	375
should be assessed and addressed if the partner is willing to be present in the ses-
sions. In addition, ethnocultural and religious attitudes and beliefs are important, as
they can be implicated in the development and maintenance of sexual dysfunctions
(Hall & Graham, 2013; Meana et al., 2008).
Furthermore, the topics of sexual orientation and attractions (or the lack thereof),
as well as gender identity (and pronouns), form an important part of the assessment
process, and these areas should be thoroughly evaluated. Indeed, such information
can be essential to ensuring proper diagnosis and rapport building. Take the exam-
ple of differentiating between a diagnosis of HSDD/SIAD and one’s sexual identity
as asexual (i.e., someone who feels little or no sexual attraction to others). At first
glance, the presentation of both low desire/arousal and low/lack of sexual attraction
to others may seem indistinguishable, but follow-up questions about the context, the
associated distress, and one’s identity labels will clarify whether to diagnose this cli-
ent with a sexual dysfunction.
When there are multiple sexual aspects affected, it is key to begin with what the
client feels is most important, followed by further inquiries into other aspects of the
client’s multilayered sexual experiences, while respecting their pace and boundaries.
Although it is important to gather some basic information about sexual function/dys-
function early on in the assessment, other dimensions of sexuality can be broached
when the client is more comfortable and the therapeutic alliance is in place. Sexual-
ity is an intimate and at times uncomfortable topic, even for therapists, and as such
requires patience and a nonjudgmental attitude. The clinician should keep in mind
that opening up about sexuality can make a client feel very vulnerable, as this may
be the first time that they ever discuss these aspects of their private self with anyone,
including sexual trauma and sexual preferences perceived as less traditional.
ETIOLOGIES
Male Sexual Dysfunction
Hypoactive Sexual Desire Disorder
Sexual desire is not easily defined or measured; it is considered to be the most com-
plex stage of the sexual response cycle because of its uniquely subjective aspects and
enormous variations over time, even within the same individual (Nimbi, Tripodi,
Rossi, Navarro-Cremades, & Simonelli, 2020). A commonly accepted definition of
“sexual desire” is a subjective state that includes psychological, physiological, affec-
tive, and cognitive components “to initiate and maintain human sexual behavior,
triggered by internal and/or external stimuli” (Nimbi et al., 2020, p. 59). It is linked
to sexual arousal (Nimbi, Tripodi, Rossi, & Simonelli, 2018) and comprises three bio-
psychosocial elements: drive (i.e., a biological component related to the integrity of
physiological systems involved in desire), motivation (i.e., a psychological component
consisting of mental state, relationship factors, and social context), and wish (i.e., a
cultural component that includes ideals, values, and rules related to sexual expres-
sion) (Levine, 2003).
Most current studies examining HSDD in men focus on those who are hetero-
sexual (Nimbi et al., 2020), and these studies often speak to the complex influence of

-- 396 of 498 --

376 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
biopsychosocial factors on sexual desire. Early studies on the etiology of low sexual
desire focused on the role of hormones—specifically, androgens—given that andro-
gens (particularly testosterone) play a role in male sexual desire. However, the rela-
tionship between androgens and sexual desire was found to be nonlinear: Although
a minimum level of androgens is needed to experience sexual desire, elevated levels
do not directly correlate with high levels of sexual desire (Rubio-Aurioles & Biva-
lacqua, 2013). This nonlinear relationship has been supported by recently published
consensus guidelines on male sexual desire stating that testosterone plays an impor-
tant role in modulating male sexual desire and arousal (Corona, Isidori, Aversa, Bur-
nett, & Maggi, 2016); therefore, it is clear that factors other than hormones are also
involved in the experience of desire. Indeed, HSDD has been associated with bio-
logical factors such as aging, medical conditions (e.g., androgen deficiency, cancer,
cardiovascular disease, diabetes, epilepsy, heart failure, obesity, prostatitis/chronic
pelvic pain syndrome), and psychotropic and other medications (for a review, see
Nimbi et al., 2020).
Psychosocially, mental health problems (e.g., alexithymia, anxiety, depression,
posttraumatic stress disorder), the presence of other sexual dysfunctions (e.g., ED;
Nimbi et al., 2018), and the lack of erotic thoughts and presence of sexually dysfunc-
tional beliefs (Carvalho & Nobre, 2011) have been associated with low desire. In addi-
tion, psychological functioning has been associated with dyadic and solitary sexual
desire and activity: A study demonstrated optimal psychological functioning (i.e.,
high approach motivation, secure attachment, high self-control, and high mindful-
ness) in participants with high levels of dyadic sexual desire and activity, psychologi-
cal functioning characterized by impulsivity in those with high levels of dyadic and
solitary sexual desire and activity, and high avoidance motivation, insecure attach-
ment, and poor mindfulness in those with low levels of dyadic sexual desire and
activity (Dosch, Rochat, Ghisletta, Favez, & Van der Linden, 2016). Intimate relation-
ship dynamics can also influence the experience of desire, such as the experience of
rejection, lack of emotional connection with a partner (Murray, Milhausen, Graham,
& Kuczynski, 2017), and couple conflict (Ridley et al., 2006; see Sbarra, Manvelian,
Salinger, & Whisman, Chapter 12, this volume).
Erectile Disorder
The definition of “sexual arousal” consists of psychological and physiological com-
ponents: a positive mental engagement and focus in response to a sexual stimulus,
with genital changes indicative of sexual response (e.g., penile erection, vaginal
lubrication) that perhaps occur along with other bodily changes (e.g., increased heart
rate, nipple erection) (Althof et al., 2017). In men, one of the most obvious indicators
of sexual arousal is penile erection, and for those who adhere to strict sexual scripts
that emphasizes “sex” as being performed by an erect penis, issues with penile erec-
tion can significantly and negatively impact the person and their sexual relationship
(Wiegel, Scepkowski, & Barlow, 2007).
ED has been associated with numerous biological factors, for example, neuro-
genic (central nervous system [e.g., spinal cord injury], peripheral [e.g., sensory neu-
ropathy due to diabetes], efferent [e.g., due to radical pelvic surgery]), endocrinologi-
cal (e.g., hyperprolactinaemia), vasculogenic (e.g., arterial, venous, and sinusoidal),

-- 397 of 498 --

Sexual Dysfunctions 	377
and local (e.g., cavernous fibrosis due to priapism) causes. ED is a well-established
predictor of cardiovascular disease (Kirby, 2015; Shamloul & Ghanem, 2013). In addi-
tion, aging (although there is increasing incidence among younger men; for a review,
see Nguyen, Gabrielson, & Hellstrom, 2017), certain types of medications (e.g., anti-
depressants), cigarette smoking, alcoholism, recreational drug use, systemic dis-
eases (e.g., cardiovascular disease), and general ill health (e.g., hyperlipidemia, obe-
sity, hypertension) have been associated with ED (Kirby, 2015; Shamloul & Ghanem,
2013). ED has also been associated with depression, anxiety, and lower relationship
happiness (Latini, Penson, Wallace, Lubeck, & Lue, 2006). Cognitively, men with ED
tend to make more internal and stable causal attributions for negative sexual events
than do men without ED, and they rate these events as more important (Scepkowski
et al., 2004). In addition, cognitive distraction directs attention away from erotic cues
and decreases erectile function in men with, but not in men without, ED (Abraham-
son, Barlow, & Abrahamson, 1989).
Delayed Ejaculation
DE has been associated with many biological, psychosocial, and sexual factors. Bio-
logical causes consist of genetic factors, congenital anatomical factors (e.g., Wolffian
duct abnormalities), neurogenic causes (e.g., multiple sclerosis), infections/inflam-
mations (e.g., prostatitis), endocrine issues (e.g., hypogonadism), and medications
(e.g., selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors [SSRIs]). Psychosocial and sexual factors
include the following: fear (e.g., of pregnancy, of loss of control, of intimacy), anxiety
(e.g., about performance), and an autosexual orientation (i.e., preference of masturba-
tion to partnered sexual activity), which may be related to idiosyncratic masturba-
tion habits that do not easily translate into penetrative partnered sexual activity. For
reviews, see Abdel-Hamid and Ali (2018) and Shin and Spitz (2014).
Premature (Early) Ejaculation
Potential explanations for PE include the following: genetics (that may play a role
in a sensitivity of the ejaculatory response), anxiety, faster reflexes involved in
ejaculation, prostatic inflammation/infection, sexual conditioning (e.g., masturbat-
ing quickly and efficiently, which then over time, sets up speedy ejaculation laten-
cies), low frequency of ejaculation (Donatucci, 2006), younger age, erectile disorder,
greater capacity for partial erection, greater prevalence of hyperthyroidism, and life
stressors such as relationship or work dissatisfaction (Corona et al., 2004, 2006).
Female Sexual Dysfunction
Sexual Interest/Arousal Disorder
Persistent difficulties with sexual desire and/or arousal are the most frequent reasons
for seeking sex therapy (Doss, Simpson, & Christensen, 2004; Rosen, 2000). Accord-
ing to empirically supported theories, SIAD is a result of hypofunctional excitation,
hyperfunctional inhibition, or both (Bancroft, Graham, Janssen, & Sanders, 2009;
Bitzer, Giraldi, & Pfaus, 2013; Toates, 2009). These systems are sensitive to a large

-- 398 of 498 --

378 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
number of biomedical and psychosocial factors that can hinder or facilitate sexual
desire and arousal (Bancroft et al., 2009; Toates, 2009). These factors are typically
studied separately given that SIAD is multifactorial and complex.
Lower estrogen production in menopause has been linked to lower sexual desire
and arousal (Dennerstein, Koochaki, Barton, & Graziottin, 2006; Leiblum, Koochaki,
Rodenberg, Barton, & Rosen, 2006). Although testosterone is linked to higher solitary
desire (i.e., masturbation), it is unrelated to desire for a partner (van Anders, 2012).
Oral contraceptives reduce androgen levels, yet evidence is mixed regarding the
link between contraceptive use and reduced desire (Davison, Bell, LaChina, Holden,
& Davis, 2008; Meston & Stanton, 2017; Wallwiener et al., 2010). Finally, activation
and inhibition of the sympathetic nervous system is linked to physiological sexual
arousal (Meston & Gorzalka, 1996).
Although most studies on SIAD are cross-sectional and characterized by the use
of single-occasion, retrospective measurements, distal and proximal associated fac-
tors have been identified. Societal pressures such as culture and media representa-
tions (e.g., sex is shameful, men’s desires are of higher value) may contribute to SIAD
(Richgels, 1992; Woo, Brotto, & Gorzalka, 2012), though there has been little investi-
gation in clinical samples. Childhood victimization has adverse effects on internal
sexual scripts and later sexual function, including desire and arousal (Leonard &
Mullen, 2002; Rellini, 2008). Cognitive distraction, negative sexual attitudes, perfor-
mance anxiety, and body image concerns direct attention away from erotic cues and
can interfere with desire and arousal (Barlow, 1986; DePesa & Cassisi, 2017; Dove &
Weiderman, 2000; Graham et al., 2004; Nobre & Pinto-Gouveia, 2006; Wiedermann,
2000). Depressive symptoms and low self-esteem are also correlated with SIAD
(Dennerstein et al., 2006; Hartmann, Heiser, Rüffer-Hesse, & Kloth, 2002; Shifren,
Monz, Russo, Segreti, & Johannes, 2008). However, a romantic partner is generally
the focus of sexual desire in a relationship (Rosen, Dubé, Corsini-Munt, & Muise,
2019).
Studies of interpersonal correlates are rare and focus on broad, atheoretical fac-
tors such as relationship satisfaction, and tend to exclude the partner, not taking into
account the dyadic nature of sexual desire. In a qualitative study of married women,
feelings of overfamiliarity, institutionalization of the marriage, and desexualization
of roles in the relationship were common themes for explaining their low desire
(Sims & Meana, 2010). In the limited quantitative research, sexual difficulties in the
partner, poor sexual compatibility, and relationship dissatisfaction have been linked
to SIAD (Goldstein et al., 2005; Hayes et al., 2008; Witting et al., 2008). In an early con-
trolled study of 20 couples in which the woman self-reported clinically low sexual
desire, the clinical couples reported poorer dyadic adjustment, more conflict about
sex, and a more limited sexual repertoire than did control couples (Trudel, Aubin, &
Matte, 1995; Trudel, Boulos, & Matte, 1993). Although there is evidence that relational
factors better account for women’s low desire than do biomedical correlates such as
low testosterone (Brotto, Petkau, Labrie, & Basson, 2011; Guthrie, Dennerstein, Taffe,
Lehert, & Burger, 2004; Hayes et al., 2008), the scarcity of rigorous studies on the
biopsychosocial determinants of SIAD precludes the establishment of conclusions
regarding causality. Moreover, despite the hypothesized predominant role of rela-
tionship factors in women’s sexual desire and arousal, the literature is characterized
by a significant lack of knowledge concerning the diversity of women’s relationship

-- 399 of 498 --

Sexual Dysfunctions 	379
configurations and how they may be associated with women’s sexual experiences.
Nevertheless, the limited evidence to date suggests that women in same-sex dyads
may report higher sexual desire (Holmberg & Blair, 2009).
Orgasmic Disorder
Given that the experience of orgasm requires sufficient levels of sexual arousal,
OD is also conceptualized as a result of hypofunctional excitation, hyperfunctional
inhibition, or both (Bancroft et al., 2009; Bitzer et al., 2013; Toates, 2009). Biomedical
precursors of OD generally involve illnesses that cause damage to blood vessels or
nerves, such as diabetes, hypertension, smoking, spinal cord injury, and pelvic sur-
gery for gynecological and rectal cancer—including radical hysterectomy, as well
urinary incontinence and pelvic floor dysfunction (Levin et al., 2016). Some medica-
tions may also lead to OD, such as certain classes of antidepressants, mood stabiliz-
ers, and antihypertensives. The presence of medical risk factors may not always be
associated, however, with the subjective experience of sexual distress.
Understanding the causes of OD is complicated by the difficulty in identifying
the source of stimulation and reliance on women’s self-report. Some women believe
that they reach orgasm through penile–vaginal intercourse, yet, in most cases, this
involves simultaneous stimulation of the clitoris. Lack of education concerning female
genital anatomy can further bias women’s self-reports. Another challenge lies in the
significant difference between the measurement of genital arousal and orgasm in the
laboratory versus sexual activity with a partner. In most laboratory-based studies, no
significant differences were found between women with arousal/orgasmic difficul-
ties and women without (Levin et al., 2016). Hence, apart from underlying medical
conditions, complaints of OD may stem from psychosocial factors.
Inadequate erotic stimulation during partnered sexual activity or negative
evaluations of the stimulus or context (i.e., relationship factors) are the most com-
mon causes of OD (Laan, van Driel, & van Lunsen, 2008). However, studies to date
have been cross-sectional, and none included the partner or conducted dyadic analy-
ses. Furthermore, none focused on OD alone, but rather involved samples of mixed
female sexual dysfunction. Notwithstanding these caveats, two population-based
studies showed that the major predictors of most distressing female sexual dysfunc-
tions were relationship dissatisfaction, partner sexual dysfunction, and perceived
partner sexual incompatibility (Witting et al., 2008). Intraindividual factors associ-
ated with other female sexual dysfunctions also play a role in OD: Anxiety, depres-
sion, cognitive distraction, body image/appreciation, and childhood victimization
may all alter women’s orgasm capacity.
Genito‑Pelvic Pain/Penetration Disorder
Genito-pelvic pain has traditionally been conceptualized in a dualistic fashion, as
resulting from either physical factors or psychological and sexual difficulties, despite
research and theorizing that suggest these two perspectives should be combined.
We have proposed an integrated model taking into account the interdependency of
biopsychosocial factors in GPPPD and its associated impairments (Bergeron, Rosen,
& Morin, 2011).

-- 400 of 498 --

380 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
GPPPD can result from underlying physical pathologies such as endometriosis,
interstitial cystitis, lichen sclerosus, and other genital infections (e.g., candidiasis,
herpes, bacterial vaginosis), as well as from events such as childbirth and menopause
(Bergeron et al., 2020). However, provoked vestibulodynia (PVD)—an acute recurrent
pain localized within the vulvar vestibule—is the most frequent cause of GPPPD in
premenopausal women (Harlow et al., 2009). Controlled studies point toward the
following biomedical risk factors for PVD: early puberty and pain with first tam-
pon use, inflammation, early use of oral contraceptives, nociceptor proliferation and
sensitization, lower touch and pain thresholds, and pelvic floor muscle dysfunction
(Pukall et al., 2016a). Psychosocial processes also play an important role in the onset
and maintenance of GPPPD.
Population-based studies indicate that childhood victimization is a major deter-
minant of GPPPD (Harlow & Stewart, 2005; Khandker, Brady, Stewart, & Harlow,
2014; Landry & Bergeron, 2011). Victimization may complicate women and partners’
adjustment to the sexual, psychological, and relationship repercussions of genito-
pelvic pain (Rosen & Bergeron, 2019). Anxiety and depression have been found to
be robust affective precursors, consequences, and maintenance factors in the expe-
rience of GPPPD (Khandker et al., 2014; Paquet et al., 2018). Cognitive factors are
also associated with greater pain intensity and sexual dysfunction, and include pain
catastrophizing, hypervigilance to pain, lower pain self-efficacy, negative attribu-
tions about the pain, perceived injustice, negative cognitions about penetration, pain
acceptance and self-compassion. Avoidance of pain and sexual activity is the core
behavioral factor that may exacerbate GPPPD (Bergeron, Corsini-Munt, Aerts, Ran-
court, & Rosen, 2015).
According to the interpersonal emotion regulation model of women’s sexual
dysfunction (Rosen & Bergeron, 2019), distal interpersonal factors that play a role in
GPPPD include intimacy, romantic attachment, ambivalence over emotional expres-
sion, and sexual communication. The most studied proximal factors are partner
responses to pain, which can be solicitous (e.g., expressions of attention and sympa-
thy), negative (e.g., expressions of hostility, frustration), and facilitative (e.g., affection
and encouragement of adaptive coping), with the two former associated with more
negative woman and partner outcomes and the latter, with positive outcomes. In
summary, psychosocial factors may inhibit or promote adjustment to GPPPD, with
subsequent implications for the affected couple.
Clinical Implications
Sexual dysfunctions can be highly comorbid with each other; therefore, in addition
to asking about a client’s primary complaint, all aspects of sexual response should
be comprehensively assessed. In addition, one’s current and preferred frequency of
sexual activity and level of sexual satisfaction and distress should be evaluated given
that not all difficulties with sexual dysfunction warrant a diagnosis. There are many
intraindividual variations over the life cycle, as well as couple variations over the
course of a long-term relationship, that are not problematic, as long as both partners
are comfortable with, and adapt relatively well to, these variations. Working with the
client(s) to prioritize the focus of treatment can be useful, and it can aid in establish-
ing rapport and motivation for the treatment plan.

-- 401 of 498 --

Sexual Dysfunctions 	381
Proximal and distal cognitive, affective, and behavioral factors also require care-
ful consideration. Distal cognitive factors may include issues related to social pres-
sure, value discrepancies between partners (e.g., regarding type and frequency of
sexual activity), work dissatisfaction/stress, and cultural/religious sexuality-related
norms, whereas distal affective factors may involve childhood trauma and associated
difficulties with trust and intimacy, as well as an insecure attachment style (Rosen &
Bergeron, 2019). Mood and anxiety disorders should be ruled out and/or treated when
comorbid to the sexual dysfunction. The importance of these comorbidities in the
treatment plan will depend on their severity and the extent to which they interfere
with sexuality and the individual’s overall functioning. When possible, the sexual
dysfunction and mood disorder should be treated concomitantly, as they likely share
bidirectional influences, and improvement in one area should lead to improvement
in the other. Given that many sexual dysfunctions result in negative affect (e.g., guilt,
shame, and a sense of inadequacy), these are important to assess as well. Cognitive
attributions about the sexual dysfunction may worsen the condition and create more
psychological distress (Jodoin et al., 2011); in addition, performance anxiety, preoc-
cupation with body image, and catastrophic thoughts immediately before, during,
and after sexual activity can also result in similar outcomes. Behaviorally, a pattern
of avoidance is common, both in terms of engaging in sexual interactions with the
partner and of communicating about the sexual dysfunction and its impact.
Given that any sexual problem can take a toll on the relationship, and relation-
ship factors play a role in the experience of sexual dysfunction, the dyadic or sys-
temic context requires a thorough evaluation. This evaluation could focus on partner
responses to the pain in the case of GPPPD, and for all sexual dysfunctions, on inti-
macy, attachment, sexual communication, and emotion regulation patterns. Because
more and more research indicates that relationship factors modulate sexual dysfunc-
tion, including the partner in assessment may be considered (see also the “Clinical
Implications” segment of the “Associated Clinical Features”). Finally, questions about
chronic illnesses, past surgeries, and use of medication, in addition to drug and alco-
hol consumption, are a prerequisite to the establishment of any treatment plan.
Considering the inherent biopsychosocial nature of sexual dysfunctions,
working within a multidisciplinary team with a variety of health care providers is
important for the development of a comprehensive yet individualized approach to
treatment. In addition, a full medical examination by someone in the field of sexual
medicine can streamline psychologically oriented treatments; for example, finding
evidence of retrograde ejaculation (i.e., when the ejaculate travels into the bladder
instead of through the penis) upon urological assessment in someone with what
appears to be DE can shorten therapy to work on psychoeducation, options for fertil-
ity, and any associated distress. Such an examination is not mandatory, but is likely
a relevant first step in any client over 40 years of age, where health problems and
hormonal changes begin to arise.
Last, establishing a clear understanding of sexual dysfunction within an indi-
vidual’s and/or a couple’s life requires careful consideration of sociocultural and
demographic factors, such as age, race, sexual orientation, sex assigned at birth and
gender identity, religion, trauma history, and so forth, and remaining cognizant of
how each of these will affect sexual function, satisfaction, and distress in order to
deliver culturally, diversity, and trauma competent care.

-- 402 of 498 --

382 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
COURSE AND TREATMENT OUTCOME
A common pattern with respect to course is that the frequency of sexual problems
increases with age (McCabe et al., 2016). Both medical and psychological interven-
tions exist for the treatment of sexual dysfunctions, and a combination of the two has
been shown to be more successful than one type of treatment alone for ED (Melnik,
Soares, & Nasello, 2008; Schmidt, Munder, Gerger, Frühauf, & Barth, 2014); however,
although clinically common, research studies on combination treatments are infre-
quent. Below, we briefly review medical and psychological treatments. Note that
medical treatments tend to differ depending on the sexual dysfunction reviewed,
but psychological interventions tend to highlight similar techniques and skills.
Male Sexual Dysfunction
Hypoactive Sexual Desire Disorder
Problems with sexual desire tend to increase with age in men (Angst et al., 2015) and
may be more likely in those who, for example, never marry, are widowed, are unem-
ployed, are retired, engage in low levels of physical activity, have lower testosterone
levels, and have hypertension (Martin et al., 2014).
Treatment for HSDD typically falls into two sometimes overlapping categories:
sex therapy and pharmacotherapy. Although sex therapy is a common treatment rec-
ommended for HSDD, there are no scientifically rigorous studies examining psycho-
therapy outcome for this condition. Regardless, critical components of sex therapy for
the treatment of HSDD in men include the following components: decreasing inhibi-
tory (e.g., distracting thoughts, performance anxiety) and increasing excitatory (e.g.,
sexual fantasies, focusing on pleasure) factors (Bancroft et al., 2009) via exercises such
as sensate focus (Linschoten, Weiner, & Avery-Clark, 2016); working on communica-
tion of experiences, feelings, and preferences; addressing body image and relation-
ship concerns; tackling sexual myths (e.g., “Men must always want and be ready for
sex”; “Men who refuse sex are not real men”); and increasing knowledge about sexual
techniques and pleasure/eroticism (Parish & Hahn, 2016). In contrast to the lack of rig-
orous studies in the sex therapy realm, there are numerous methodologically sound
studies of men with androgen deficiency who receive testosterone replacement that
document improvements in sexual function; however, sexual outcomes are uncertain
for those who are not hypogonadal (for a review, see Corona et al., 2014).
Erectile Disorder
For some men, the development of ED is temporary, whereas others—especially
those who are older, have higher body mass indices, and who smoke cigarettes—are
less likely to experience symptom remission (Travison et al., 2007, 2011).
The first-line option for treatment of ED is pharmacological, and within this
class, the phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE5) inhibitors (Shamloul & Ghanem, 2013)
are considered to be the treatment of choice. These oral medications have excellent
efficacy and safety profiles, especially when the dose is titrated to the maximum
tolerated dose (Hatzimouratidis et al., 2016). The most common side effects include
headache, flushing, dyspepsia, and nasal congestion (Hatzimouratidis et al., 2016),

-- 403 of 498 --

Sexual Dysfunctions 	383
and PDE5 inhibitors cannot be taken with certain other medications (e.g., nitrates)
due to potentially fatal drug interactions. Importantly, some form of sexual inter-
est/arousal is needed for the PDE5 inhibitors to work. Increases in sexual function
and satisfaction, as well as in multiple other psychosocial domains (e.g., mood, self-
esteem, quality of life), have been documented as treatment outcomes (reviewed in
Hatzimouratidis et al., 2016). However, although many men (74%) report that they are
willing to take these medications, only a small percentage (7%) do so (Mulhall, King,
Glina, & Hvidsten, 2008). In addition, research has demonstrated that 31% of men
discontinued PDE5 inhibitors after the first prescription and 48% discontinued after
3 years (Sato et al., 2007), perhaps due to reasons such as side effect profile, dissatis-
faction with treatment outcome, relationship problems, and loss of sexual interest.
Other pharmacological approaches for the treatment of ED include two second-
line treatment options: intracavernosal injections and transurethral therapy, which
lead to predictable erections in a brief period of time. In the former approach, those
with erectile disorder and/or their partners learn to inject single or combination for-
mulations of erectogenic drugs (e.g., alprostadil, papaverine) into the penis with a
small gauge needle. Erection occurs within 10 minutes and is independent of sex-
ual desire, and side effects include mild pain and priapism (Shamloul & Ghanem,
2013); however, multiple studies have shown that this type of treatment is effica-
cious and safe (for a review, see Belew, Klaassen, & Lewis, 2015). For example, in
terms of sexual outcomes, one 4-year study found that 93.1% of injections resulted in
rigid erections and satisfactory sexual intercourse (Porst, Buvat, Meuleman, Michal,
& Wagner, 1998), and an additional study reported improvements in participants’
sex lives, relationships, quality of life, and confidence in sexual intercourse attempts
(Alexandre, Lemaire, Desvaux, & Amar, 2007). Transurethral therapy consists of
the insertion of an erectogenic drug (usually alprostadil) in the form of a pellet into
the urethra with a small applicator. Side effects include penile and/or urethral pain,
syncope, and priapism (Shamloul & Ghanem, 2013). Studies have reported a rate of
49–66% for erections that are sufficient for intercourse and a rate of 64% for success-
ful intercourse, but other studies have reported much lower success rates (reviewed
in Hatzimouratidis & Hatzichristou, 2005).
Nonpharmacological approaches to the treatment of erectile disorder include
vacuum constrictive devices, penile prostheses, and sex and/or couple therapy. Vac-
uum constriction devices involve the application of negative pressure to the penis,
drawing blood into the penis which is retained by a band placed at the base of the
penis (Shamloul & Ghanem, 2013). Erections obtained with this method are not
based on physiological erection pathways (i.e., they are mechanical), and they may
be described as “cold” in terms of sensation (Shamloul & Ghanem, 2013). However,
the rate for obtaining erections that are sufficient for intercourse is as high as 90%,
and satisfaction rates range between 27 and 94% (reviewed in Hatzimouratidis and
Hatzichristou, 2005). Penile prostheses involve the surgical implantation of either a
semirigid or an inflatable prosthesis. This option is the last resort for treatment of
erectile disorder; it irreparably damages the internal tissue of the penis (Shamloul &
Ghanem, 2013). Complications can include urethral injuries, infections, and mechan-
ical malfunction (Bertero & Antunes, 2015); however, satisfaction rates for quality of
life and sexuality are high for patients and partners (Caraceni & Utizi, 2014).
Psychological intervention is often recommended for ED for three main reasons:
the lack of negative physical side effects, the reestablishment of sexual functioning,

-- 404 of 498 --

384 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
and the increase of sexual satisfaction beyond symptom reduction (Frühauf, Gerger,
Schmidt, Munder, & Barth, 2013). The meta-analysis examining the effectiveness
of psychological interventions for sexual dysfunctions indicated that effect sizes
varied from very small to large effect sizes, with the large effect sizes attributed to
interventions utilizing variants of cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) (Frühauf et
al., 2013). One such study that compared 12 weeks of rational– emotive therapy to a
wait-list condition found that participants in the active treatment group reported sig-
nificantly more sexual intercourse attempts and higher numbers of successful inter-
course attempts, as well as lower sexual anxiety, than did the control group (Mun-
jack et al., 1984). The other study compared the effectiveness of an Internet-based
CBT intervention to a wait-list condition. Results indicated that those who received
the active treatment reported significantly greater improvements in erectile function
and relationship satisfaction and quality as compared to the control group (McCabe,
Price, Piterman, & Lording, 2008).
Delayed Ejaculation
There is little information on the course of DE; however, it seems to increase after the
age of 59 years (American Psychiatric Association, 2013).
Treatment for DE is usually medical if either acquired or lifelong and due to
congenital abnormalities (Shin & Spitz, 2014). For example, the etiology of acquired
DE that appears postsurgery needs to be identified and treated; in addition, some
medications can be used off-label to treat this condition (Abdel-Hamid & Ali, 2018;
Shin & Spitz, 2014). If lifelong and congenital abnormalities have been ruled out and
off-label medications are unsuccessful or declined, then psychological intervention
targeting multiple areas specific to the client (e.g., lack of sexual knowledge, fear of
getting a partner pregnant) can be useful. Sensate focus can help in reducing perfor-
mance anxiety and maximizing pleasure, masturbation retraining can be beneficial
in the translation of pleasurable stimulation to intercourse, and stimulation to the
point of ejaculatory inevitability and the shifting immediately to vaginal penetration
can be useful in cases in which pregnancy is desired (reviewed in Shin & Spitz, 2014).
Reducing the amount of masturbation is also recommended (Perelman & Rowland,
2006).
Premature (Early) Ejaculation
Contrary to most other sexual dysfunctions, PE tends to be more common early in
life and decreases with increasing age (American Psychiatric Association, 2013).
The many medical treatments for PE include the following: topical local anes-
thetics (to diminish penile sensitivity), SSRIs (off-label, to take advantage of the com-
mon side effect of increasing ejaculatory latency time), tramadol, PDE5 inhibitors,
and alpha-adrenergic blockers (reviewed in McMahon, 2015). Some experts recom-
mend that all men presenting with PE receive psychological intervention (McMahon,
2015), which is based on supporting evidence in the research literature (Althof et al.,
2014). Psychological interventions include education to dispel myths around ejacu-
latory latency time, for example, and consist of strategies to increase latency time,
gain confidence in sexual performance, decrease performance anxiety, modify rigid

-- 405 of 498 --

Sexual Dysfunctions 	385
sexual repertoires, increase intimacy, address maintaining factors in the relation-
ship, increase effective communication, and tackle thoughts and feelings that inter-
fere with sexual function (reviewed in McMahon, 2015).
Female Sexual Dysfunction
Sexual Interest/Arousal Disorder
Although the prevalence of low sexual desire tends to increase with age, with a peak
during and after menopause, sexual distress decreases, such that SIAD is not signifi-
cantly more prevalent among older women (Parish & Hahn, 2016). Until very recently,
there were no medications for SIAD; hence, most women and couples took part in
psychological interventions. Two medications have now been approved by the U.S.
Food and Drug Administration. Flibanserin, indicated for premenopausal women,
is a systemic medication that is taken nightly and cannot be combined with any
alcohol; bremelanotide, a subcutaneous injection that is self-administered prior to
engaging in sexual activity, was approved in June 2019, such that patterns of use are
not yet established. A pooled analysis of three randomized controlled trials (RCTs)
demonstrated that flibanserin improved sexual desire and reduced sexual distress
in premenopausal women relative to placebo (Simon, Thorp, & Millheiser, 2019).
Similar findings were reported for bremelanotide (Clayton et al., 2016). A review
comparing medical—including flibanserin and bremelanotide—and psychological
treatments for low desire concluded that their effect sizes were equivalent (Pyke &
Clayton, 2018).
A recent meta-analysis examining the efficacy of mostly CBT modalities for
sexual dysfunction compared to wait-list controls indicated a large effect size for
improving women’s low desire and no significant treatment effect on their sexual
satisfaction; that is, on average, CBT was not superior to a wait-list control in enhanc-
ing sexual satisfaction for women with SIAD (Frühauf et al., 2013). One meta-analysis
indicated that including the partner in CBT may yield better outcomes (Gunzler &
Berner, 2012), and another found that evidence supporting the efficacy of psycho-
logical interventions for female sexual dysfunctions was stronger for couple and
group formats than for individual therapy (Frühauf et al., 2013). Given the strong
relational and psychosocial components of female sexual dysfunction, tackling its
dyadic dimensions head on via sex and couple therapy may be more productive than
using individual modalities that conceptualize the dysfunction as residing solely
in the woman herself, independent of context. Group formats hold the additional
advantage of normalizing many concerns that woman may hold regarding their sex-
uality, including a sense of guilt, shame, and inadequacy. Mindfulness-based group
therapy, although not studied using an RCT methodology, appears promising, as it
resulted in significant improvements in sexual desire, satisfaction, and overall sexual
function relative to a wait-list control in women with SIAD (Brotto & Basson, 2014).
Orgasmic Disorder
Treatment strategies for difficulties with orgasm include education about women’s
sexual response and sexual anatomy, directed masturbation, sexual skills training,

-- 406 of 498 --

386 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
and sensate focus in order to generalize newly learned skills to partnered sexual
activity. Findings from a meta-analysis examining the efficacy of CBT sex therapy
interventions for sexual dysfunction compared to wait-list controls indicated a
medium effect size for improving women’s orgasmic capacity and a medium effect
size for enhancing their sexual satisfaction, suggesting that psychological interven-
tions are effective in helping women achieve orgasmic success, whether secondary
or primary (Frühauf et al., 2013).
Genito‑Pelvic Pain/Penetration Disorder
Epidemiological studies suggest that genito-pelvic pain is more prevalent among
women in their twenties (Mitchell et al., 2013), and that remission rates are relatively
high, independent of treatment (Davis, Bergeron, Binik, & Lambert, 2013). Women
with GPPPD typically consult their family physicians and often receive a first-line
medical treatment, which can include topical applications and oral medications, the
most common of which are topical lidocaine and tricyclic antidepressants. How-
ever, recent guidelines suggest a lack of evidence supporting their efficacy, and
point toward psychological interventions and pelvic floor physical therapy as the
best approach (Pukall et al., 2016b). Vestibulectomy (a surgical intervention recom-
mended for PVD only) may be recommended if these are unsuccessful.
CBT generally focuses on reducing pain, improving sexual function and well-
being, as well as relationship satisfaction, by targeting the cognitive, affective,
behavioral, and relationship factors associated with GPPPD. In two RCTs, group CBT
has been shown to be superior to a first-line topical application (Bergeron, Khalifé,
Dupuis, & McDuff, 2016) and equivalent to vestibulectomy in terms of the primary
outcome of pain during intercourse (Bergeron, Khalifé, Glazer, & Binik, 2008). In
another RCT, Masheb, Kerns, Lozano, Minkin, and Richman (2009) also found that
individual CBT resulted in significantly greater reductions in pain and improve-
ments in sexual function than supportive psychotherapy. Although not studied as
rigorously, couple therapy and mindfulness-based approaches appear promising
in reducing pain and sexual distress (Brotto, Basson, Smith, Driscoll, & Sadownik,
2015).
In their systematic review of the effectiveness of physical therapy for PVD,
Morin, Carroll, and Bergeron (2017) concluded that although the literature is plagued
with methodological shortcomings, multimodal physical therapy showed consistent
effectiveness across studies, with a significant improvement in pain in 71–80% of
women. Such physical therapy typically involves education about pain and PVD;
manual therapy techniques comprise vulvar desensitization and pelvic floor muscle
stretching, myofascial release, conjunctive tissue desensitization, neuromuscular
reeducation and massage techniques in order to increase flexibility and release pel-
vic floor muscle tensions, as well as reduce vulvovaginal sensitivity; and biofeedback
to improve muscle relaxation, control, strength, speed of contraction, and endurance
(Morin et al., 2016). Furthermore, the overall effectiveness of multimodal physical
therapy surpassed that of isolated modalities (e.g., biofeedback alone). Although less
commonly included as a main endpoint, sexual function also improved after physi-
cal therapy. To this effect, the combination of physical therapy and CBT as a first-line
intervention in a multidisciplinary approach to treatment is promising.

-- 407 of 498 --

Sexual Dysfunctions 	387
Clinical Implications
Sexual dysfunctions are most often treated using second- and third-generation
CBTs, whether at individual, group or couple levels. Hypothesized mechanisms
of change and targets of intervention include (1) reconceptualizing sexuality as a
relational phenomenon whereby both members of a couple function as a team to
improve their sexual well-being, (2) facilitating intimacy (i.e., empathic response and
self-disclosure) in the relationship and securing the attachment bond, (3) reducing
sexuality-related anxiety and associated behavioral and experiential avoidance via
skills training and behavioral activation, (4) promoting communication of sexual
needs and preferences, and (5) fostering self-efficacy, as well as acceptance of, and
compassion toward, oneself and one’s partner.
The first phase of sex therapy focuses on establishing the therapeutic alliance
and setting realistic goals for treatment, working toward a multidimensional under-
standing of the biopsychosocial aspects of sexual difficulties, as well as using psy-
choeducation and exercises such as journaling to observe the thoughts, feelings,
behaviors, and couple interactions that may be linked to sexual dysfunction. This
step is crucial, as without a strong alliance, clients cannot embark on the risky jour-
ney of confronting their sexual fears and difficulties, and tolerating the vulnerability
inherent to this process. Adopting an attitude of positive regard, nonjudgment, and
curiosity can facilitate self-disclosure of the client toward not only the therapist but
also the partner or group in the case of couple and group therapy formats. More
than other psychological difficulties, sexual dysfunction as a presenting complaint
is fraught with feelings of shame and inadequacy as a sexual partner. As shame can
be a powerful barrier to openness and progress in therapy, it is important that clini-
cians foster acceptance and compassion in the therapeutic relationship. As a thera-
pist, being willing to examine one’s own values, issues, and negative affect such as
anxiety and shame around sexuality and intimacy may facilitate this work.
However, despite one’s best intentions to focus on sexual dysfunction symptom-
atology in psychotherapy, the treatment of sexual dysfunction is complex and non-
linear. For example, some clients and their partners present with significant relation-
ship difficulties; these should be addressed in couple therapy, especially since they
may interfere with ongoing treatment, such as when a partner sabotages the client’s
efforts at improving their dysfunction, or exhibits angry and critical remarks on a
regular basis. In an ideal world, this can be done by the therapist already treating the
client’s sexual difficulties, especially since there are likely bidirectional associations
between the relationship conflict and the sexual problem.
At other times, clients could present with more than one sexual dysfunction,
such as a combination of GPPPD and SIAD, or ED and PE, which is common but more
challenging to treat. Another frequent caveat in cognitive-behavioral approaches is
patients’ refusal to comply with homework assignments. This presentation is often
related to fear and avoidance, and as such needs to be worked on in therapy but
never in a blaming fashion. The therapist can take responsibility for the homework
not working out and explore with the patient in a nonjudgmental, open, and curious
manner the reasons they did not do, or did not fully complete, the homework. Expe-
riential avoidance processed directly in the here and now of the therapy session may
pave the way for more successful homework completion in the future. Last, given

-- 408 of 498 --

388 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
that sexual dysfunctions are biopsychosocial phenomena and there exists a plethora
of medical options available to clients, resistance to psychological approaches is com-
mon, as it may be easier at times for them to attribute sexual difficulties to a medical
issue rather than to engage in the challenging work of confronting their fears and
changing their behaviors and relationships. Hence, moving too quickly in sharing
our psychosocial understanding of the clients’ sexual difficulties may lead to set-
backs and alliance ruptures. Importantly, therapists are not immune to espousing
dichotomic views of sexual problems and must also work hard to hold the complex-
ity of clients’ issues without giving in to simplistic, unidimensional conceptualiza-
tions that could lead them to believe that they either have all the answers, in cases
where there is an obvious psychosocial antecedent (e.g., a history of sexual trauma),
or none at all, in cases where medical evidence supports a clear involvement of bio-
logical factors (e.g., diabetes). The truth is that most sexual difficulties involve a com-
plex combination of biomedical and psychosocial precursors and maintenance fac-
tors, that can render sex and couple therapy a delicate balancing act requiring equal
attention to therapeutic relationship factors and to empirically supported interven-
tion strategies.
COMORBIDITIES
Sexual dysfunctions are highly comorbid with other mental health and medically
relevant conditions. Mood disorders are especially common in those with sexual
dysfunctions. In men, depression was associated with increased risk of ED in a Brit-
ish population study (Polland, Davis, Zeymo, & Venkatesan, 2018). In women, a
population-based study showed that anxiety and depression were both antecedent
and consequent to GPPPD related to vulvodynia (i.e., chronic vulvar pain) (Khandker
et al., 2011). Furthermore, up to 45% of women with GPPPD report a comorbid pain
condition, and having this comorbidity is associated with increased risk of worse
overall health, sleep, pain interference, physical pain, depression, and posttraumatic
stress disorder (Reed, Plegue, Williams, & Sen, 2016). Male partners of women with
GPPPD report increased rates of psychological distress, erectile dysfunction, and
decreased sexual satisfaction (Smith & Pukall, 2014).
Relative to healthy controls, women with SIAD report more depressive symp-
toms and anxiety and lower relationship satisfaction (Parish & Hahn, 2016; Rosen et
al., 2019; Sarin, Amsel, & Binik, 2016). These symptoms can be subclinical or a full-
blown mood or anxiety disorder. Partners of women with SIAD experience more
erectile difficulties, higher sexual distress, and lower sexual and relationship satis-
faction, compared to partners of women without sexual dysfunction (Rosen et al.,
2019). In addition, female partners of men with PE report more sexual dysfunction
and more anxiety as compared with female partners of men without PE (Verze et
al., 2018). Other potential comorbid mental health issues for female sexual dysfunc-
tions include posttraumatic stress disorder following childhood trauma/victimiza-
tion, revictimization in the form of psychological, sexual, and/or physical intimate
partner violence during adolescence and adulthood, and substance abuse.
Sexual dysfunction in men can also be negatively impacted by adherence to mal-
adaptive beliefs surrounding sexual performance, such as “men must maintain an

-- 409 of 498 --

Sexual Dysfunctions 	389
erection at 100% rigidity throughout sex” (Nobre & Pinto-Gouveia, 2000). Indeed,
concerns about performance have been shown to be more common in men as com-
pared with women (Nelson & Purdon, 2011). A community sample study found that
men who experience more negative thoughts related to their sexual performance have
higher rates of psychological distress and sexual difficulties (Nelson & Purdon, 2011).
Furthermore, gay men appear to experience significantly more negative thoughts
related to their sexual performance, fears related to sexually transmitted infections,
and body image concerns relative to heterosexual men (Lacefield & Negy, 2012).
Clinical Implications
When psychological distress reaches clinical levels, this aspect of the patient’s pre-
sentation becomes more central. At this point, mental health professionals may need
to move beyond sexuality-focused psychotherapy. Clients with a preexisting or con-
sequent mood or anxiety disorder could require more intensive psychotherapy. They
may need additional psychological support and/or psychotherapy targeting their
mood or anxiety disorder directly, such as empirically validated therapies or anti-
depressant and/or anxiolytic medication. In this case, the therapist would scale back
the work on sexuality issues, at least temporarily. Depending on the severity of the
comorbidity, broad multitarget interventions that aim to improve mood as well as
sexual responsiveness, such as third-wave, acceptance-based approaches, may also
be helpful. Sexual dysfunctions are sometimes the point of entry to obtain treatment
for other disorders, such that complex cases are common, even in this specialized
area of clinical practice.
Another comorbidity that may be brought to the forefront of therapy is child-
hood and adolescent victimization, with or without posttraumatic stress symptoms.
It can become the focus of treatment if briefer interventions targeting the here and
now of the sexual dysfunction are ineffective, the clinician believes victimization is
playing a role in the client’s difficulties, and the client is ready to attend to this aspect
of the past more directly. Two lines of caution are warranted: (1) The mental health
professional needs to have sufficient experience and training in trauma-informed
psychotherapy and (2) childhood victimization must never be presented as the sole
cause of sexual difficulties given the multifactorial etiologies of sexual dysfunctions.
Focusing on victimization to the exclusion of other potential exacerbating factors
could prolong treatment unnecessarily and be less helpful.
In men with sexual dysfunction, assessing beliefs and adherence to strict models
of sexuality that focus on performance is key to providing areas of focus for therapy.
In addition, treating the couple, if appropriate, is warranted given that sexual dys-
function is experienced within a dynamic system that can influence, and be influ-
enced by, the other partner(s).
CONCLUSION
Sexuality is an integral part of one’s overall health and well-being, and it should
be assessed routinely in those who present with psychological and/or physical
complaints. Questions about the main sexual complaint, as well as other aspects of

-- 410 of 498 --

390 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
sexual function, should be asked, and appropriate referrals should be made, keep-
ing in mind the biopsychosocial and sociocultural underpinnings of sexual difficul-
ties. Assessment and treatment conducted by such referrals should focus on issues
related to distal contributing factors, for instance, child maltreatment, which may
pave the way for difficulties with sexuality and intimate relationships via more prox-
imal factors such as less adaptive thoughts, emotion regulation, and behaviors, as
they influence the experience of sexual desire, arousal, orgasm, and painful sex in
unique and varied interpersonal and sociocultural contexts. These distal and proxi-
mal factors will also shape how clients adjust to the medically based—and some-
times chronic—aspects of sexual dysfunctions and their treatment. The course of
treatment will often involve a multimodal approach targeting the different compo-
nents of the sexual dysfunction and require working with other health professionals,
such as a physician and physical therapist, with a tolerance for a multiplicity of views
and careful attention to the therapeutic alliance.
Relationship factors are an integral part of sexuality, and in many cases will
likely require specific attention via relationship therapy, or relationship-based sex
therapy. However, research in this area has only just begun, and the existing stud-
ies are methodologically limited. Although relationship factors are known to play
a role in sexual dysfunctions, the majority of treatment studies to date have not
included the partner. Furthermore, many have involved relatively short-term treat-
ment durations—from 8 to 12 weeks—that may not be sufficient to see significant
improvements in sexual satisfaction. In addition, most follow-up periods were rela-
tively short, such that the long-term effects of psychological interventions for sexual
dysfunctions were not documented. Considering the high prevalence of sexual dys-
functions, there are not enough state-of-the-art RCTs evaluating the efficacy of psy-
chological interventions in their treatment. Also, despite the fact that many individu-
als presenting with sexual dysfunctions and their partners will take part in different
models of psychotherapy, such as emotion-focused relationship therapy and psy-
chodynamic therapies, and have at least one comorbid condition, most studies have
evaluated individual and group CBT in relatively homogeneous groups of patients.
This attests to the large gap between science and practice in the field of sexual dys-
function. Finally, there remains an urgent need to better understand sexual dysfunc-
tion in the context of the individual more broadly. More research is needed among
samples that account for cultural, sexual, and gender diversity, as the lack thereof
impacts our ability to offer culturally competent and inclusive clinical care.

398
Sleep–wake disorders are prevalent and impactful conditions that are often poorly
assessed in the clinical setting. Undiagnosed sleep disorders can masquerade as
mental health conditions and worsen the outcomes associated with these conditions.
Furthermore, sleep disorders can develop from mental health conditions, and the
reverse is also true (particularly for mood disorders). Successful treatment of sleep
disorders requires a targeted approach. Therefore, understanding the difference
between normal sleep and a sleep disorder is important in the conceptualization and
treatment of mental health. Failure to effectively recognize and treat sleep disorders
leads to increased morbidity and mortality, accidents and injuries, absenteeism and
presenteeism, and reduced quality of life. Our goal in this chapter is to provide an
overview of common sleep disorders and to discuss implications for clinical practice.
NORMAL SLEEP
The three natural states of consciousness are wake, non-rapid-eye-movement
(NREM) sleep and rapid-eye-movement (REM) sleep. Normal sleep progresses
through a series of stages, beginning with light sleep (NREM1–2), followed by deep
sleep (NREM3), then to a state during which the majority of dreaming takes place
(REM). Each cycle takes 90–120 minutes, and there is often a brief awakening during
the transition from REM to NREM1. NREM Stages 1–2 are characterized by alpha and
beta brain waves, and there is considerable variability of sleep quality in NREM2.
NREM Stage 3 is less variable and characterized by theta and delta waves. The REM
stage has brain activity similar to that of the wakeful period but with the absence of
motor activity and the presence of eye movements. As the sleep period progresses,
there is a reduction in deep, delta sleep (NREM3). The entire cycle from NREM1 to
REM is repeated four to five times during a nocturnal period.
C H A P T E R 1 4
Sleep–Wake Disorders
Norah Vincent
Judith R. Davidson

-- 419 of 498 --

Sleep–Wake Disorders 	399
The quality and quantity of sleep is largely determined by two factors: whether
the sleep window overlaps the naturally reoccurring cycles of sleep and wake (or
circadian time window) and the length of time that a person has been awake prior
to the sleep period. These are sometimes referred to as Process C (endogenous cir-
cadian) and Process S (homeostasis). Process C is driven by the circadian clock that
promotes wakefulness during the day and sleep during the night. Process S deter-
mines the amount and depth of sleep and increases with the length of time spent
awake. Proper alignment of the circadian timing of sleep–wake and other behaviors
are optimal for sleep. Within this cycle, sleep can become disrupted for a variety of
reasons, leading to insomnia. In what follows, we discuss some of the more common
sleep disorders that are likely to be encountered in clinical practice: insomnia disor-
der, circadian rhythm sleep–wake disorders (CRSWDs), and parasomnias.
INSOMNIA DISORDER
Phenomenology and Symptoms
The most widely used classification systems for sleep disorders, including insom-
nia, are the third edition of the International Classification of Sleep Disorders (ICSD-3;
American Academy of Sleep Medicine, 2014) and the fifth edition of the Diagnos-
tic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-5; American Psychiatric Associa-
tion, 2013). Both systems require the presence of problems with nocturnal sleep (e.g.,
sleep onset or sleep maintenance) with daytime consequences, despite opportunity
to sleep. Both ICSD-3 and DSM-5 specify that difficulty with sleep must occur at
least 3 nights per week and persist for 3 months or longer. Although not part of any
classification system, an arbitrary rule of 30 minutes is often used to quantify insom-
nia (e.g., requiring >30 minutes to fall or return to sleep, or awakening >30 minutes
before desired time). Recent revisions of these classification systems are notable for
recognition of the need to treat insomnia as a distinct condition requiring specific
intervention despite its frequent comorbidity with other psychological and medical
diagnoses (e.g., removal of the DSM-IV primary and secondary insomnia distinc-
tion in DSM-5) (Sateia, 2014). A diagnosis of insomnia disorder depends on a thor-
ough mental and physical health evaluation to determine whether the problem is
not “better explained” by another sleep disorder, medical condition, or substance or
mental health condition (e.g., post traumatic stress disorder, personality disorder).
Additionally, the new criteria have eliminated nonrestorative sleep and added early
morning awakening as reflective of insomnia disorder. Although not yet part of any
classification system, there is growing recognition of two phenotypes of insomnia,
a short-sleeping version (<6 hours per night) and a near-normal sleep duration ver-
sion. Of these, short-sleepers have more negative health care outcomes (Vgontzas &
Fernandez-Mendoza, 2013).
There is no definitive, objective test for insomnia. Various assessment tools exist,
each with advantages and disadvantages. Laboratory sleep studies or polysomnog-
raphy assess sleep stages and cycles, along with oxygen level in blood, heart rate,
breathing, eye and leg movements, and require that the subject spend one night in a
sleep laboratory. They have long been deemed the “gold standard” of objective sleep
assessment. These assessments are costly and the artificial environment impacts

-- 420 of 498 --

400 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
upon sleep, making them unsuitable for insomnia measurement. Other low-cost, less
obtrusive devices exist (e.g., wrist actigraphy, consumer wearable devices) that can
be used in the person’s natural environment ; however, these devices measure move-
ment and can therefore inaccurately classify periods of inactivity, when the individ-
ual is lying still and awake in bed, as time asleep. This problem becomes magnified
for those with fragmented sleep, typical of insomnia. Additionally, the lack of stan-
dardizing or publishing of metrics and norms for these commercial devices hinders
interpretation of their data.
DSM-5 describes the essential feature of insomnia disorder as dissatisfaction
with sleep quantity or quality, suggesting that subjective measures may be optimal
in quantifying the problem. The Consensus Sleep Diary (Carney et al., 2012) is a help-
ful tool to capture daily reports of the sleep experience. When used as intended for
14 days, daily diaries have the advantage of being less prone than questionnaires to
recall bias and providing a more representative assessment of an individual’s sleep
over time. Global self-report questionnaires are helpful in assessing the severity of
insomnia. Both the Insomnia Severity Index (ISI; Bastien, Vallieres, & Morin, 2001)
and the Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI; Buysse, Reynolds, Monk, Berman,
& Kupfer, 1989) demonstrate strong reliability and validity, including sensitivity to
change over time. The ISI covers a period of 2 weeks and the PSQI, slightly longer
(i.e., 1 month). A disadvantage of self-report measures is that they are subject to sleep
misperception. Some individuals underestimate the amount of time they are asleep.
This may be due to spending more time in NREM1, with greater cognitive arousal
than is experienced in deeper sleep stages. Furthermore, evaluation of sleep is com-
plex, as several other sleep disorders have similar symptoms. For example, restless
legs syndrome leads to discomfort in the extremities (often the legs) beginning in
the presleep period and during the sleep period. Restless legs can lead to significant
delay in sleep onset and numerous nocturnal arousals of which an individual is
often unaware. This is associated with poor quality sleep and daytime sleepiness.
Similarly, sleep-related breathing disorders, particularly sleep apnea, are associated
with nonrestorative sleep and can coexist with insomnia; see U.S. Preventive Ser-
vices Task Force, 2017, for a review).
Associated Clinical Features
Insomnia disorder is associated with a number of biological, cognitive, emotional,
social, and physical characteristics.
Neurobiological Features
The neurotransmitters gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) and histamine are central
in promoting sleep through inhibition of arousal. The ascending reticular activating
system is inhibited by GABAergic transmission, leading to reduced activation of the
cerebral cortex and blocked transmission of thalamic relay neurons. This is a target
of many of the pharmacological agents for insomnia. Studies show that those with
insomnia disorder have lower levels of GABA (Winkelman et al., 2008), contributing
to hyperarousal. Hypocretin-1 and -2 (also known as orexin-A and -B) are neuro-
peptides produced by the hypothalamus that enhance arousal and wakefulness and
suppress REM (Saper, Fuller, Pedersen, Lu, & Scammell, 2010). Loss of these cells has

-- 421 of 498 --

Sleep–Wake Disorders 	401
been associated with the sleep disorder narcolepsy. Recently, research on adults with
insomnia disorder found them to have higher orexin-A plasma levels than normal
sleeping controls. Also, higher levels of this peptide were associated with longer
duration of insomnia disorder and with higher scores on the ISI, a self-report mea-
sure of insomnia (Tang et al., 2017). In contrast, melatonin, a hormone secreted by
the pineal gland in the absence of light, promotes sleep. Neurons originating in the
suprachiasmatic nucleus regulate the secretion of melatonin in the pineal gland. The
timing of the onset of melatonin release is important in understanding CRSWD dis-
turbance. However, evidence of decreased melatonin production among those with
insomnia disorder is mixed (see Macchi & Bruce, 2004) and most of the age-related
reduction in melatonin occurs in early adulthood, a time not typically associated with
significant sleep complaints (Kennaway et al., 1999). Biological features of insomnia
are of considerable interest, as they serve as targets for pharmacological agents.
Cognitive Features
The significant long-term health consequences of insomnia have been well docu-
mented. Some of these are in the realms of cognition, emotion and motivation, fatigue
and sleepiness, weight and hormonal fluctuation, and cardiovascular health. In the
cognitive area, a vast number of individuals with insomnia disorder report defi-
cits in attention, concentration, and memory. Recently, Cross and colleagues (2019)
studied processing speed, memory, and executive functions in 28,000 middle-aged
adults. After they adjusted for lifestyle and medical conditions, results showed that
having a probable diagnosis of insomnia disorder was associated with decreased
performance in auditory verbal learning but not inhibitory control or cognitive flex-
ibility. Other well-controlled but small studies have shown deficits in areas of work-
ing memory and divided attention among those with insomnia disorder relative to
healthy controls, but no differences in simple or sustained attention (Shekleton et al.,
2014). Notably, these deficits did not correlate with subjective sleepiness, suggest-
ing the intriguing possibility that a process other than chronic sleep deficiency may
explain the results. One possible explanation involves the effects associated with a
combination of hyperarousal and short sleep duration.
Emotional Features
Some of the puzzles in the sleep research world include why those with insomnia
disorder tend to overestimate delay in sleep onset and underestimate total sleep time.
Furthermore, many of those who awaken from objectively determined sleep (i.e.,
based on polysomnography) often deny sleeping. Additionally, those with insom-
nia disorder tend to report more improvements in sleep with hyperarousal-reducing
medication than are seen in objective sleep data. There is increasing recognition of
the role of hyperarousal in insomnia disorder.
Hyperarousal is a multidimensional construct with cognitive and somatic fea-
tures. Cognitive features of hyperarousal include worry about the sleep problem,
a tendency to misperceive sleep, and to fear the inability to sleep. Somatic features
of hyperarousal include elevations in heart rate, blood pressure, body temperature,
and cortisol. These symptoms are typical of chronic sympathetic nervous system
activation. More than two decades ago, Perlis, Giles, Mendelson, Bootzin, and Wyatt

-- 422 of 498 --

402 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
(1997) found that hyperarousal is associated with greater beta and gamma power in
electroencephalographic (EEG) recordings among those with insomnia. This type of
EEG activity tends to reduce memory related to NREM1 sleep and the sleep-onset
period. This may explain why sleep is so often misperceived.
Social Features
The social interactions of those with insomnia are less well known. Studies show
