that insomnia disorder is associated with reduced vocal expression (i.e., use of fewer
words, less expression of positive emotion), inappropriate intonation, and a percep-
tion that the sleep-deprived person is less attractive and healthy, and more sad and
fatigued (Axelsson et al., 2010; McGlinchey et al., 2011; Sundelin et al., 2013). Some
descriptions of sleep-deprived persons focus on the presence of dark circles around
the eyes, pale skin, droopy corners of the mouth, and hanging eyelids (Beattie, Kyle,
Espie, & Biello, 2015). Interpersonal distress has also been associated with difficulty
sleeping. Individuals with fear about their primary relationships have been shown
to have less NREM3 sleep and poorer sleep quality (Diamond, Hicks, & Otter-
Henderson, 2008). This has led some to suggest that individuals need to feel safe to
sleep. Furthermore, those who are lonely or socially isolated tend to have less favor-
able sleep quality (Kurina et al., 2011). Thus, there is likely a reciprocal relationship
between sleep and social connections.
Physical Health Features
Of interest is that most of those with insomnia disorder deny daytime sleepiness.
Instead, they report fatigue. The terms “sleepiness” and “fatigue” are not interchange-
able, and sleepiness refers to the propensity to doze and is, at the heart, a physiologi-
cal phenomenon. Fatigue refers to a sensation of tiredness and exhaustion associated
with maintaining activity levels or vigilance, which requires rest but not sleep for
recovery. This partially explains why those with insomnia disorder often have a very
difficult time having a daytime nap (i.e., they lack sleepiness and are hyperaroused).
Several decades of research converges on the idea that insomnia disorder can
lead to problems with blood sugar regulation, weight, and cardiovascular health.
Chronic insomnia has been shown to affect 45–71% of the diabetic population (Knut-
son, Ryden, Mander, & Van Cauter, 2006; Lamond, Tiggemann, & Dawson, 2000;
Lopes et al., 2005). Recently, variability in sleep length has been found to be strongly
associated with HbA1c in type 2 diabetes, with a U -shaped relationship between
sleep duration and HbA1c (Brouwer et al., 2020). A meta-analysis (Cappuccio, D’Elia,
Strazzullo, & Miller, 2010) of 10 prospective studies showed that gender may mod-
erate the relationship between short sleep duration and the development of type
2 diabetes, with males, more than females, at greater risk. Notably, these correla-
tional studies indicate neither a cause– effect nature nor the direction of relationship
between sleep and diabetes.
Regardless of the direction of the relationship between poor sleep and diabe-
tes, experimental laboratory studies clearly show that a deliberate reduction of sleep
time in healthy young adults (from 8 to 4 hours) leads to reduced glucose tolerance,
reduced thyrotropin concentration, and elevated evening cortisol (Spiegel, Leproult,
Van Cauter, 1999). Other investigations have shown that deliberately fragmenting

-- 423 of 498 --

Sleep–Wake Disorders 	403
the sleep of young healthy adults across varying sleep stages (while preserving total
sleep time), leads to reduced glucose metabolism, increased morning cortisol level,
and increased sympathetic nervous system activity (Stamatakis & Punjabi, 2010).
These studies show that deteriorating sleep has implications for processes important
in the development and maintenance of diabetes.
With some variability of findings, insomnia disorder (particularly in the pres-
ence of short sleep duration) has also been shown to significantly increase risk for
hypertension, coronary artery disease, and heart failure (Javaheri & Redline, 2017).
The mechanisms of action are not fully understood but there is emerging evidence
that dysregulation of the hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal (HPA) axis, increased
sympathetic nervous activation, and increased inflammation each play a role. In
normal sleepers, reducing total sleep time leads to increased blood pressure, inflam-
matory cytokines, C-reactive protein, tumor necrosis factor, and interleukin 6 (Java-
heri & Redline, 2017). These changes can cause damage to the structure of the heart,
coronary vasculature, and cardiac conduction system. In a large Norweigan epi-
demiological study, the HUNT study (Laugsand, Vatten, Platou, & Janszky, 2011),
52,610 adults were followed for 11 years to assess work performance and health sta-
tus. Results showed that insomnia increased risk of myocardial infarction (MI) by
27–45%, and that a delay in sleep onset was the most hazardous sleep symptom.
Multiple sleep problems led to greater risk estimates. Blood pressure fluctuations
within a 24-hour period follow a circadian pattern. Blood pressure is lower during
the nocturnal period and higher during the daytime. If there is <10% change in blood
pressure, this is abnormal and described as a “non-dipping” pattern. Research indi-
cates that those with insomnia are more likely to experience “non-dipping” (Jar-
rin, Alvaro, Bouchard, Jarrin, Drake, & Morin, 2018). Notably, several smaller studies
have recently shown that relatively small improvements (i.e., ≥ 30 minutes) in total
sleep time can significantly lower systolic and diastolic blood pressure. This magni-
tude of improvement can be achieved with evidence-based treatments; however, it
should be noted that outcomes tend to be less favorable for short sleepers (Bathgate,
Edinger, & Krystal, 2017). When providing such treatment to those with insomnia
disorder, the clinician should be aware that short sleepers (i.e., those sleeping <4
hours per night) may be less responsive to intervention, and may be at risk for car-
diovascular and metabolic disorders. Hence, some attention to adequate broad-based
medical assessment is warranted for this population.
Course, Outcome, and Treatment
Insomnia disorder often becomes a chronic problem. When it develops, insomnia
persists in 56–74% of adults in a 12-month period and in 46% in a 36-month period
(Morin et al., 2009; Pillai, Roth, & Drake, 2015). In a large longitudinal study, Janson,
Lindberg, Fislason, Elmasry, and Boman (2001) found that insomnia remitted in only
56% of cases over a 10-year period. Thus, insomnia disorder is more often persistent
than episodic in nature. Furthermore, individuals with sleep-maintenance insomnia,
compared to sleep-onset insomnia, tend to have the condition more reliably persist
(Pillai et al., 2015). The two most common treatments for insomnia include cognitive-
behavioral therapy (CBT-I) and pharmacotherapy. CBT-I, an evidence-based form
of psychological treatment, comprises five distinct strategies. These include sleep
restriction, stimulus control, relaxation therapy, cognitive therapy, and sleep hygiene/

-- 424 of 498 --

404 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
psychoeducation. Recent clinical practice guidelines from the American College of
Physicians recommend that all adults with insomnia disorder receive CBT-I as the
initial treatment (grade: strong, moderate-quality evidence) and that medication only
be considered after a discussion of harms, benefits, and costs, and only when CBT-I
is unsuccessful (grade: weak, low-quality evidence) (Qaseem, Kansagara, Forciea,
Cooke, & Denberg, 2016). This was echoed in recent guidelines from the European
Sleep Research Society (Riemann et al., 2017). Riemann and colleagues noted that
CBT-I has been shown to be effective in more than 15 different meta-analyses, with
positive effects on all aspects of sleep, as well as daytime fatigue, mood, and anxiety,
and with good stability of gains in the follow-up period. A number of pharmaceuti-
cal agents have been studied in the treatment of insomnia, but results have been less
favorable (with a grade of weak). A recent clinical practice guideline (Sateia, Buysse,
Krystal, Neubauer, & Heald, 2017) recommended eszopiclone, zaleplon, zolpidem,
triazolam, temazepam, and ramelteon for sleep-onset insomnia in adults. For sleep-
maintenance insomnia, suvorexant, eszopiclone, zolpidem, temazepam, and doxe-
pin were recommended. Of note, trazodone, tiagabine, diphenydramine, melatonin,
tryptophan, or valerian were not recommended for either type of insomnia.
Epidemiology
Approximately one-third of North American adults are sleeping less than recom-
mended for optimal health (i.e., 7 hours) (Chaput, Wong, & Michaud, 2017). Even
among those without sleep complaints, 21% report sleeping less than 7 hours per
night. Thus, in the absence of insomnia complaints, 1 out of 5 adults is obtaining
less sleep than needed, and recent estimates show that a large number of adults (i.e.,
25%) are dissatisfied with their sleep. Approximately 10–15% report problematic
sleep with daytime consequences (e.g., fatigue, irritability, mood disruption, cogni-
tive deficits), and 6–10% meet full criteria for insomnia disorder (Morin et al., 2011).
Using a nationally representative, cross-sectional survey approach with a sample of
22,000 adults, nocturnal sleep complaints were found to be more common among
adults than among adolescents or children, and more prevalent in women and in
those with less education and income (Chaput, Yau, Rao, & Morin, 2018). Women
tend to have better objective sleep quality than men; the latter show greater declines
in NREM3 sleep with age. Although women’s sleep tends to be of better quality,
women are more likely to have longer periods of wakefulness during the night than
men (Ohayon, Carskadon, Guilleminault, & Vitiello, 2004). There are some ethnic dif-
ferences, with Black Americans more than non-Hispanic White Americans tending
to have longer delay in falling asleep and less favorable sleep quality (Durrence &
Lichstein, 2006), and if a short sleeper, more likely to have insomnia disorder (Kalm-
bach, Pillai, Arnedt, & Drake, 2016).
In a multiyear longitudinal study in the United Kingdom, Chaput and colleagues
(2017) reported that the rate of sleep complaints among adults was found to be 24%,
and noted that this has increased from 16.8% in 2007 (42% increase). An earlier U.K.
study using standardized clinical interviews, and conducted between 1993 and 2007,
also showed an increase in the rate of sleep complaints (5%) and insomnia disorder
diagnoses, albeit smaller (3%). The problem of poor sleep may be truly accelerating in
society or the introduction of new commercial devices that purport to measure sleep,
may be leading to changes in how well we perceive we are sleeping.

-- 425 of 498 --

Sleep–Wake Disorders 	405
Comorbidity
Insomnia is frequently comorbid with a variety of psychiatric disorders (e.g., autism
spectrum disorders, major depressive disorder, bipolar disorder, generalized anxiety
disorder, posttraumatic stress disorder [PTSD], substance use disorder, borderline
personality disorder), as well as suicidality, pain (e.g., fibfromyalgia, headache, rheu-
matoid arthritis), neurological (e.g., Parkinson’s disease and parkinsonian syndromes,
Huntington’s chorea, progressive dystonia, Tourette syndrome, and epilepsy), meta-
bolic (e.g., type 2 diabetes), and cardiovascular diseases (e.g., congestive heart failure
with symptoms of paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea and orthopnea). Insomnia is also
often comorbid with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) (with symp-
tom of dyspnea), gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD; epigastric pain or burn-
ing, laryngospasm, acidic taste, sudden awakenings), and prostatic hypertrophy
(frequent nocturia). When coupled with short sleep duration, insomnia disorder is
highly associated with cardiometabolic and psychiatric disorders. This relationship
is much less significant for those with insomnia disorder and normal sleep duration
compared to those with shortened sleep duration (Kalmbach et al., 2016).
Etiology
A number of factors increase the risk of developing insomnia: female gender (Zhang
& Wing, 2006), family history of sleep problems (Dauvilliers et al., 2005), and chronic
stress (Drake, Pillai, & Roth, 2014). In the past 20 years, a number of models outlining
the development of insomnia have emerged. The first of these was Spielman’s model
(Spielman, Caruso, & Glovinsky, 1987) that describes the role of predisposing (e.g.,
hyperarousal), precipitating (e.g., stressful life events), and perpetuating factors (e.g.,
worry about sleep problem, conditioning of sleeplessness to bedroom). Perpetuating
factors are often the target of modern psychological sleep treatments. Morin’s (1993)
cognitive-behavioral model emphasizes factors that maintain the disorder. Some of
these include hyperarousal, conditioning of wakefulness to the bedroom environ-
ment, and circadian rhythm abnormalities. Last, Harvey’s (2002) cognitive model is
an adaptation of psychological anxiety treatment protocols to sleep, and emphasizes
the presence of “safety behaviors,” which refer to activities in which persons with
insomnia engage for the purpose of quelling fears of being unable to sleep. Some of
these behaviors include efforts to control thinking during the nocturnal period, con-
sumption of alcohol, napping behavior, and a tendency to avoid strenuous daytime
activities. Each of these psychological models has had a significant and enduring
impact on conceptualization and treatment approaches for insomnia disorder.
Clinical Implications
Individuals with inaccuracies in self-perception greatly benefit from receipt of nor-
mative information about sleep. For example, many sleep patients appreciate being
informed that healthy sleepers take up to least 30 minutes to fall asleep and to fully
awaken in the morning, and reach a point of wakefulness several times per night
(see Lichstein, Durrence, Riedel, Taylor, & Bush, 2004, for normative sleep data from
community-dwelling adults). Other implications are that several assessment meth-
ods may be necessary to measure this problem, and discrepancy among them is

-- 426 of 498 --

406 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
likely. Patients benefit from a discussion of the difficulty in measuring sleep, with
reference to sleep state misperception. In the clinical setting, patients’ perceptions
of sleep length and quality should be viewed as “to be determined” rather than
assumed. Frequently, patients present after attending a sleep laboratory, and have
either been directly or indirectly told that they “do not have a sleep problem.” This
invalidating message needs to be better explained at sleep centers. Others arrive at
the door of sleep centers because their commercial devices indicate that they have a
sizable sleep problem. Although enthusiastically adopted by many with sleep prob-
lems, such devices are error-prone, particularly for estimations of time awake during
the night. Relying on data from these devices is therefore not helpful.
The cognitive consequences of insomnia disorder tend to center around work-
ing memory, divided attention, and reaction time. Findings from cognitive studies
illustrate that there are far fewer objective cognitive deficits than subjectives ones. As
a result, those working with patients with sleep problems need to use cognitive tech-
niques to help individuals avoid magnification of subjective problems with atten-
tion, concentration, and memory. Rather than advise patients about recent research,
a better strategy may be to have individuals conduct experiments that test their theo-
ries that their attention, concentration, and memory are impaired. Many middle-
aged adults struggle with word finding, and attribute this common developmental
difficulty to poor sleep. Psychoeducation on working memory and divided attention
should be given, with suggestions to determine whether this processing can be mini-
mized on days following particular poor nights of sleep. Similarly, jobs that critically
emphasize working memory and divided attention (e.g., air traffic controller) might
be avoided entirely.
Laboratory findings point to problematic or infrequent social relationships
among those with insomnia. Although not part of any standardized sleep protocol,
successful treatment of sleep may require assessment of relationship quality and
encouraging patients to reduce loneliness through social engagement, particularly
in older age cohorts. Evidence-based protocols for sleep have typically not included
partners, and it remains to be seen whether this would be a helpful adjunct.
The prevalence of insomnia among endocrinology and cardiology patients sug-
gests that more emphasis on sleep in these medical clinics is warranted, particularly
for short sleepers. The finding that sleep disturbance may precipitate and worsen
comorbid medical conditions indicates that sleep treatment should be offered out-
side of traditional psychotherapy offices, particularly in the short-sleeping pheno-
types. Sleep interventions in medical clinics may require tailoring. For example,
providers might be cautious in using stimulus control interventions (specifically,
instruction to remove oneself from bed during the night when unable to sleep) in
those with cardiac conditions and in those with low blood pressure or with “non-
dipping” profiles. Similarly, those with seizure disorder or bipolar mood disor-
ders may need to avoid temporary sleep deprivation often associated with some
of the CBT-I techniques such as sleep restriction (see Johnson, Otto, Gold, & Elliott,
Chapter 10, this volume, for a discussion of the negative impact of sleep deprivation
for patients with bipolar disorders). In the family physician’s office, annual sleep
assessment (in-office) is likely warranted, and insomnia may be better viewed as
a chronic condition for some. Early intervention may reduce the likelihood of the
development of a persistent problem. Although often comorbid with other medical

-- 427 of 498 --

Sleep–Wake Disorders 	407
problems, providers should not neglect to specifically treat sleep problems. Atten-
tion to the role of medical and substance-induced causes of insomnia needs to be
considered in this medical assessment. For example, corticosteroids, opioids, and
psychotropics can all worsen sleep, and many individuals are entirely unaware of
this relationship.
Current evidence-based treatments for insomnia disorder (both psychologi-
cal and pharmacological) address a central predisposing factor (i.e., hyperarousal).
Psychological treatment also targets perpetuating factors (e.g., worry about sleep
problem, conditioning of wakefulness to the bedroom environment, circadian reen-
trainment, reduction of safety behaviors) through CBT-I. In the clinical setting, sleep
patients are often aware of the role of hyperarousal in contributing to sleep prob-
lems. Despite this awareness, interventions for reducing hyperarousal are often
looked upon with scepticism by the patient with insomnia disorder. Those who have
an exclusive problem with sleep-maintenance insomnia often do not understand
the importance of relaxing prior to sleep. In the clinical setting, patients are much
less aware of the negative impacts of conditioning of wakefulness to the bedroom
environment and circadian misalignment. The clinician, who may be aware of the
impact and interventions in each of these areas, has an array of treatment targets for
insomnia disorder (e.g., whether to prioritize arousal reduction, circadian factors, or
conditioning of wakefulness to the bedroom environment). More research is needed
to pit the impact of each of these factors against each other to determine how to prior-
itize intervention. A recent study of 100 adults with insomnia disorder who received
CBT-I through an Internet program, showed that both arousal reduction and one
type of circadian intervention (i.e., sleep scheduling) were related to improved out-
come, but efforts to de-condition the bedroom were not (Vincent & Walsh, 2013).
When treatment periods are brief due to limitations of insurance, it may make sense
to offer arousal reduction strategies initially.
Some of the implications of the widespread occurrence of sleep problems are
that many remain undiagnosed and untreated due to a perception that people should
be able to manage the condition, that their doctor will not attend to the problem, or
that it is a normal part of aging. If untreated, sleep problems can persist for decades.
CIRCADIAN RHYTHM SLEEP–WAKE DISORDERS
Circadian rhythms are common to all animal species and influence sleep and wake-
fulness, biological functioning, and activity. Generated by a pacemaker located in the
suprachiasmatic nucleus of the anterior hypothalamus, the rhythm approximates 24
hours and has inputs from light and other factors (called zeitgebers) that synchronize
the pacemaker to the environmental light–dark cycle. The most influential zeitgeber
is environmental light, but other zeitgebers are social or activity-based. Melatonin is
a marker of the timing of the endogeneous circadian system. For most people, mela-
tonin production occurs 2–3 hours before habitual bedtime, a time when core body
temperature is declining. Exogeneous melatonin administration (capsules) can also
shift the circadian rhythm. In the absence of light, the rhythm will operate with an
approximate 24-hour period. When the rhythm is synchronized to the environment,
this is called “entrainment.”

-- 428 of 498 --

408 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
Phenomenology and Symptoms
The two major sleep diagnostic systems (i.e., DSM-5, ICSD-3) define CRSWDs as per-
sistent and recurring sleep disruption primarily due to misalignment between the
internal or endogenous circadian rhythm and the external or environmental sleep
schedule. A diagnosis requires that excessive daytime sleepiness, insomnia, or both
be present, and that the sleep problem causes impaired functioning. There are a num-
ber of subtypes of this disorder. A delayed sleep-phase-type circadian rhythm dis-
order occurs in the presence of a delay in both sleep and awakening time, with an
inability to fall asleep or awaken at the time required. This delay is substantial (i.e., >3
hours) and persistent (i.e., does not fluctuate within a week). These latter two features
often help to differentiate insomnia disorder from CRSWD. An advanced-phase-type
circadian rhythm disorder occurs when there is an inability to remain awake or asleep
until the desired time. ICSD-3 (American Academy of Sleep Medicine, 2014) addition-
ally requires that CRSWD not be better explained by another medical/sleep/mental
disorder, that the duration of the problem exceed 3 months, and that when individu-
als can sleep on their preferred schedule, sleep quality and duration improve.
The CRSWDs are sometimes described as “intrinsic” (e.g., delayed sleep phase,
advanced sleep phase, non-24-hour, irregular) or “extrinsic” (e.g., shift-work subtype,
jet-lag subtype). In both delayed- and advanced-phase conditions, there is a “non-24-
hour sleep–wake subtype.” These individuals report a consistent daily drift from
one day to the next, with progressively later or earlier sleep onset and wake times.
Many of these individuals are blind, and the failure to entrain is due to a lack of light
input. This is one of the most impairing circadian rhythm sleep–wake disorders seen
in clinical practice and is fortunately quite rare. Of the advanced CRSWDs, an “irreg-
ular sleep–wake subtype” is characterized by temporary disorganization, such that
the timing of sleep–wake is variable through 24 hours. This problem occurs more
often in those with developmental disorders, and in certain neurological diseases
(Alzheimer’s, Parkinson’s, Huntington’s).
Of the extrinsic CRSWDs, a “shift-work subtype” of the CRSWDs describes
insomnia occuring during the time the individual wishes to be alert and awake.
ICSD-3 describes a “jet-lag subtype” of CRSWDs; this develops when sleep is shifted
as result of a movement between time zones and accompanying light exposure. It
should be noted that circadian disturbance is not limited to those who meet these
criteria. For example, shifting sleep–wake cycles associated with changes in sleep–
wake timing between weekdays and weekends can result in poor quality sleep.
Circadian rhythms can be assessed using measurement of plasma melatonin/
core body temperature, self-reported sleep–wake cycles using sleep diaries, sleep–
wake recording through actigraphy, or chronotype questionnaires. Laboratory-based
assessment of dim light melatonin onset (DLMO) has become the “gold standard”
for assessment of circadian rhythms, which is the time when endogenous melato-
nin levels begin to rise in dim light, on average about 2–3 hours before bedtime.
Laboratory assessment of melatonin provides a controlled environment to accurately
assess circadian rhythms. DLMO is costly, often unavailable, and occurs in an arti-
ficial environment unlike the patient’s home. Actigraphy assessment of circadian
rhythms is supported by ease of use, collection of data pertaining to environmental
light exposure, and automaticity of recording that does not rely on a conscientious

-- 429 of 498 --

Sleep–Wake Disorders 	409
patient or a patient with normal cognitive functioning. Actigraphy assessment is rec-
ommended by ICSD-3 for both daytime function (work/school) and free days (days
when individual is allowed to have unrestricted sleep time). The disadvantages of
actigraphy are that circadian rhythms can only be inferred, and environmental light
exposure can affect the results. With similar disadvantages to actigraphy, the posi-
tive features of sleep diary measurement of circadian rhythms are low cost, ease of
use, and ability to collect data on use of electronics, napping, caffeine, medications,
and alcohol (all of which may affect sleep). Chronotype questionnaires are inexpen-
sive methods to assess circadian rhythm through examination of preferred times for
daily activities and sleep. Chronotype is often assessed at the midpoint of sleep on
work-free days or days without work or school commitments. Common measures are
the Morningness–Eveningness Questionnaire, the Munich Chronotype Question-
naire, and the Munich Chronotype Questionnaire for Shift-Workers (Horne & Ost-
berg, 1976; Roenneberg, Wirz-Justice, & Merrow, 2003). The disadvantages of ques-
tionnaire assessment of circadian rhythms are that they only indirectly measure the
construct, lack detail as to the timing of sleep phases, and measure preferences and
not behaviors and thus may be unreliable.
Associated Clinical Features, Course, and Outcome
When permitted to sleep according to preference, those with delayed- and advanced-
phase CRSWDs have normal sleep architecture and sleep duration. When conven-
tion does not align with the individual’s circadian rhythm, a variety of negative
health outcomes may ensue. Some of these include increased sympathetic nervous
system activity and immune system dysfunction, as well as impairment of pancre-
atic, gastrointestinal, and endocrine function (Potter et al., 2016). CRSWDs are asso-
ciated with increased prevalence of hyperglycemia and insulin resistance, and the
shift-work subtype is associated with increased risk of MI, cardiovascular disease
(Knutsson, Hallquist, Reuterwall, Theorell, & Akerstedt, 1999), and cancer (Megdal,
Kroenke, Laden, Pukkala, & Schernhammer, 2005; Sigurdardottir et al., 2012).
Treatment for CRSWDs involves prescribing the timing of sleep and wakeful-
ness, physical activity or exercise, implementation of light therapy and/or avoidance
of light, and use of medications to phase shift. Recent clinical practice guidelines
from the American Academy of Sleep Medicine recommend evening light therapy
for advanced sleep–wake phase disorder (grade: weak), and strategically timed mel-
atonin for both delayed sleep–wake phase disorder (grade: weak) and non-24-hour
sleep–wake disorder (grade: weak). Light timed in the evenings before the minimum
core body temperature will lead to phase delays, and light timed in the mornings
after the minimum core body temperature will lead to phase advances. In general,
the more intense the light and the longer duration of exposure, the greater will
be the effect. The responsiveness of the circadian rhythm to light depends on the
recent amount of light exposure (i.e., less exposure leads to greater response to light)
and wavelength (i.e., blue light produces the most phase shifting). The guidelines
strongly advise against the use of pharmacotherapy and weakly against the use of
melatonin (either alone or in combination with light therapy) for elderly adults with
dementia with irregular sleep–wake phase disorder (Auger et al., 2015). Research
in this area has often been conducted with healthy adults, and there is a dearth of

-- 430 of 498 --

410 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
research involving actual patients with CRSWDs, leading to slow advancement of
this field. For example, a recent study contrasting use of a digital reader versus read-
ing a book prior to the sleep period illustrated that the former was significantly more
likely to disturb sleep and next-day functioning (Grønli et al., 2016). This particular
study raises the intriguing possibility that light exposure might be more powerful in
affecting sleep than conditioning of wakefulness to the bedroom environment (such
as occurs during reading in the bedroom).
Epidemiology
Delayed sleep–wake phase disorder is the most common of the CRSWDs. Estimates
are that 1.5–8.9% of the adult population (Paine, Fink, Gander, & Warman, 2014;
Schrader, Bovim, & Sand, 1993) and 7% of those with insomnia complaints (Lewy,
Cutler, & Sack, 1999) are affected. Onset of symptoms typically occurs in adolescence
or early adulthood. Advanced sleep–wake phase disorder has been less well studied.
The prevalence of this condition among those living in the community has been
shown to range from 0.25 to 7% (Paine et al., 2014) and may be even lower in sleep
clinics, suggesting underidentification of cases (Curtis et al., 2019). The prevalence
of advanced sleep–wake phase disorder is higher among men and increases with
age. The prevalence of delayed sleep–wake phase disorder is higher among those
living in more deprived areas and decreases with age. A recent large-scale epidemio-
logical study revealed that after researchers controlled for a variety of demographic
variables, individuals with advanced sleep–wake phase disorder are more likely to
report sleepiness, whereas those with delayed sleep–wake phase disorder are more
likely to report poor health (Paine et al., 2014).
Comorbidity
CRSWD has been associated with a variety of comorbid conditions. Some of these
include insomnia disorder, which can develop after extensive time spent in bed
when awake. Additionally, a variety of psychiatric diagnoses, including bipolar dis-
order, obsessive– compulsive disorder, major depressive disorder, and substance use
disorders, have been associated with a delayed circadian rhythm (Reid et al., 2012;
Robillard et al., 2013; Turner et al., 2007). Much less is known about comorbid condi-
tions associated with advanced sleep–wake phase disorder.
Etiology
The causes of CRSWDs can be both biological and behavioral. Genetic alterations in
the core circadian clock genes (for advanced phase) and in the per3 gene (for delayed
phase) have been postulated as contributors to these disorders (Hida et al., 2012), as
have the aging process, blindness, and particular disease states such as Alzheimer’s
(Potter et al., 2016). Shift work, social jet lag, early school start times, and living in
a polar region are behavioral factors that can cause CRSWDs (Potter et al., 2016).
Another common cause of delayed circadian phase is use of electronics. Many elec-
tronic devices produce blue light, to which photosensitive retinal ganglion cells are
especially sensitive. As a result, nighttime exposure to iPads, iPhones, and e-books

-- 431 of 498 --

Sleep–Wake Disorders 	411
delays sleep and melatonin production, and negatively impacts upon next day alert-
ness. Of these electronic devices, use of social media and the Internet (browsing)
are most associated with staying up later, taking longer to fall asleep, and sleeping
an objectively shorter period of time. Last, poor sleep hygiene can lead to the devel-
opment of a CRSWD if the individual “sleeps in” or takes daytime naps. Irregular
bedtimes and wakeup times, varying by hours from day to day, may lead to these
sleep disorders.
Clinical Implications
Implications of these findings are that special attention might be paid to the sleep of
older persons in geriatric medical and mental health clinics, to better detect advanced
sleep–wake phase patterns. In contrast, delayed sleep phase should be suspected
when patients, especially adolescents or young adults, present with insomnia and
have a pattern of delayed sleep and wakefulness in relation to school or work sched-
ules. Patients in a variety of settings should be encouraged to reduce use of devices
that emit blue light during critical periods (e.g., in the evenings for delayed-phase
patients, and in the mornings for advanced-phase patients). Blue-blocking glasses
and apps to filter short-wavelength emissions from electronic devices (Gringras,
Middleton, Skene, & Revell, 2015; van der Lely et al., 2015) may better regulate the cir-
cadian rhythm. Similarly, delaying school start times increases the amount of sleep
that students obtain, and might also improve mood (Minges & Redeker, 2016).
Due to comorbidity with mental health conditions, individuals with CRSWDs
may lack motivation for adhering to a prescribed, timed sleep–wake and/or a physi-
cal activity intervention or prescribed light exposure. Furthermore, medications to
assist with phase shifting, or to promote sleepiness or wakefulness, may interact
with medicines to treat the comorbid conditions. Last, the comorbid condition may
contribute to the CRSWD (e.g., depressed persons who awaken early and dwell on
negative thoughts thus advance their circadian rhythm; anxious patients who stay
up late to quell worrying delay their circadian rhythm).
PARASOMNIAS
“Parasomnias” are unwanted physical or mental events that occur during sleep or
during arousal from sleep. The states of wakefulness, NREM, and REM, are normally
distinct and occur in an organized and predictable pattern over the 24-hour period.
However, in parasomnias, aspects of more than one state co-occur and intermix.
This is referred to as a temporary “state dissociation” (American Academy of Sleep
Medicine, 2014). The nature of each parasomnia is determined by the sleep state from
which it emerges, whether NREM or REM. Although ICSD-3 (American Academy
of Sleep Medicine, 2014) recognizes 10 categories of parasomnias, DSM-5 (American
Psychiatric Association, 2013) describes four of these, including two NREM para-
somnias (sleepwalking and sleep terrors) and two REM parasomnias (nightmare dis-
order and REM sleep behavior disorder [RSBD]). It is these four parasomnias that we
discuss here, and they are the ones most likely to be encountered in psychological
clinical practice.

-- 432 of 498 --

412 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
NREM Parasomnias
Phenomenology and Symptoms
Sleepwalking and sleep terrors are NREM parasomnias, and they occur when there
is a sudden shift from deep NREM sleep, called slow-wave sleep (SWS), to partial
wakefulness. Because of this sudden and unusual shift, they are also called “dis-
orders of arousal.” Neurologically, there appears to be activation of local and sub-
cortical networks, with deactivation of prefrontal cortical control (Nobili & Arnulf,
2014). This is thought to produce automatic motor behaviors or fear responses that
in normal wakefulness are inhibited or controlled by the prefrontal cortex (Ameri-
can Academy of Sleep Medicine, 2014). Behaviors occur with limited conscious
awareness. They may include confusional arousals (person sits up in bed and looks
around, appearing confused; included as a NREM parasomnia in ICSD-3 but not
in DSM-5), ambulation (sleepwalking), or sleep terrors (person screams and exhib-
its extreme fear). In some cases, aggression, sleep-related eating, and sleep-related
sexual behavior may occur. NREM parasomnias share the features of repeated epi-
sodes of incomplete arousal from deep sleep, unresponsiveness to efforts of others
to intervene, absence of associated dream imagery, and amnesia or partial amnesia
for the episode. Because these events occur from deep NREM sleep, they occur most
frequently in the first third of sleep, when SWS is prominent.
With sleepwalking, the sleeping person leaves the bed and walks around, star-
ing blankly. Behaviors can involve routine things done at inappropriate times or
places, for example, urinating in a closet or rearranging items in the home. Calm,
quiet sleepwalking is common—especially in children; however, dangerous and life-
threatening behaviors can occur: Sleepwalkers may leave the bedroom, and even
the building, with some exiting through a window, or over a balcony. Injuries can
arise from bumping into furniture and sometimes from self-inflicted burns and cuts
(more common in adults than in children). In some cases, violent behaviors result in
injury to another person, particularly when that person tries to restrain or awaken
the sleepwalker. The sleepwalker is disoriented and usually incoherent, and slow in
responding to questions. The typical duration of episodes ranges from 1 to 20 min-
utes (Howell, 2012). The sleepwalker may return to bed on their own and continue to
sleep, or may lie down and sleep in another place (e.g., a closet or wherever they have
wandered), eventually waking up in an unexpected location.
With sleep terrors, the person has sudden episodes of intense fear that typically
begin with a piercing scream and are accompanied by signs of autonomic arousal:
tachycardia, tachypnea, pupil dilation, and sweating. If awakened, the person is dis-
oriented, remains fearful, and may take some time to settle down. Episodes typically
last 5–20 minutes (Howell, 2012). Sleep terrors are most often experienced by young
children and witnessed by their parents.
In is important to differentiate NREM parasomnias from nightmares, nocturnal
panic attacks, and seizures. Whereas NREM parasomnias occur most frequently in
the first third of the night (in SWS), nightmares are most likely to happen in the
second half of the night (in REM sleep). NREM parasomnias are not associated with
dream imagery and are not recalled, whereas nightmares are frightening, easily
recalled dreams. Awakenings from NREM parasomnias are associated with confu-
sion and disorientation, whereas awakening from a dream is rapid, and the person

-- 433 of 498 --

Sleep–Wake Disorders 	413
is not confused. Nocturnal panic attacks are episodes of waking from sleep in a
state of panic. They tend to occur during the transition from Stage NREM2 to Stage
NREM3 sleep (Hauri, Friedman, & Ravaris, 1989). Most people with nocturnal panic
attacks also experience daytime attacks. Memory of the event is vivid, most occur
1–3 hours after going to sleep, and people may delay going to bed, fearing an attack
(Craske & Tsao, 2005). Seizures associated with frontal lobe epilepsy can occur
during sleep (“nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy” [NFLE]) and can be confused with
NREM parasomnias, especially sleepwalking. NFLE seizures usually occur during
Stage NREM2 sleep (Provini et al., 1999) rather than SWS. Epileptic events are more
likely to be short (less than 2 minutes), to occur more frequently during the night
(more than three times), and to be partially recalled (Derry et al., 2006). An aura, and
dystonic or tonic posturing and repeated, stereotyped behaviors rather than complex
behaviors, are indicative of NFLE (Derry et al., 2006). A neurological assessment is
warranted if NFLE-type symptoms are present.
Associated Clinical Features
In general, NREM parasomnia events are primed by conditions that increase sleep
pressure and triggered by sleep-disrupting factors. They are more likely to occur
following sleep restriction or deprivation, when SWS rebounds. Immediate triggers
of sleepwalking in adults are sleep disruptions associated with sleep-disordered
breathing, periodic limb movements, noises and touch (Pressman, 2007). Pilon,
Montplaisir, and Zadra (2008) induced episodes in adult sleepwalkers, but not in
non-sleepwalkers, with specific auditory stimuli and this effect was accentuated
under conditions of prior sleep deprivation.
A variety of psychiatric medications, including short- or intermediate-acting
hypnotics have been implicated in NREM parasomnia events as recorded in case
reports (Pressman, 2007). Ohayon, Guilleminault, and Priest (1999) reported that
adults with NREM parasomnias, especially those with sleep terrors, were more
likely than those without, to report drinking alcohol at bedtime. This may have
contributed to a common belief that alcohol could set the stage for a NREM event
in a predisposed individual. However, current research suggests the opposite—at
least for sleepwalking: Alcohol may reduce the likelihood of sleepwalking through
inhibition of motor activity (Pressman, 2019). For people who are predisposed to
NREM parasomnias and who also have sleep-disordered breathing (i.e., sleep apnea,
upper airway resistance syndrome), alcohol can indirectly increase the occurrence of
NREM parasomnias by increasing the likelihood of breathing-related sleep disrup-
tions (American Academy of Sleep Medicine, 2014).
Course, Outcome, and Treatment
It is often a family member, rather than the person with the NREM parasomnia,
who initiates consultation with a health professional. The high incidence of NREM
arousal disorders in young children means that it is usually parents seeking help.
Indeed, parents are understandably concerned, especially in the case of sleep ter-
rors, when they hear piercing screams and witness distress signs in their child. The
affected individual is likely not aware of these events. Most cases of childhood sleep

-- 434 of 498 --

414 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
terrors and sleepwalking resolve without intervention by early adolescence; a small
proportion of individuals continue to experience NREM parasomnias in adulthood.
In adults with NREM parasomnias, the request for help may come from fam-
ily or friends, or from the individual. Sleepwalking adults may report waking up
in odd places in their home, such as a closet. Diagnosis of NREM parasomnias is
usually based on the individual’s history and observations of others, although in
some severe or forensic cases (e.g., someone has committed a crime while apparently
asleep), overnight polysomnography may be used. The latter is not a clear diagnostic
tool unless the person happens to experience an episode while in the sleep lab.
The intervention called “scheduled awakening” is a technique in which an indi-
vidual is awakened 15–30 minutes before the usual timing of an NREM parasom-
nia episode. Among children with persistent sleepwalking (Frank, Spirito, Stark, &
Owens-Stively, 1997) or sleep terrors (Durand & Mindell, 1999), this type of preemp-
tive awakening by a parent was successful at minimizing or eliminating episodes
in small, uncontrolled studies. To our knowledge, trials of scheduled awakening
for adult patients are lacking. Scheduled awakening is a promising technique that
requires further exploration through randomized controlled trials and lab research
to understand the mechanisms of its effects. Hypnosis has been tried as a psycho-
therapeutic approach for NREM parasomnias (Hauri, Silber, & Boeve, 2007; Hurwitz,
Mahowald, Schenck, Schluter, & Bundlie, 1991); however, the science and evidence
of effectiveness are not yet compelling. Pharmacotherapy, with benzodiazepines or
antidepressants, is used for persistent NREM parasomnias, but there are few ran-
domized controlled trials of medications, and the results are mixed (Howell, 2012).
Epidemiology
Prevalence estimates for NREM parasomnias vary widely across studies. Most rely
on reports by the individual or a family member via surveys. Rates are very likely
to be underestimates, since the person has no memory of these nocturnal events
and they are only recognized if they are observed and reported by another person.
NREM parasomnias are most common in childhood. For sleepwalking, peak child-
hood prevalence (13%) has been observed at age 10 (Petit et al., 2015). A recent meta-
analysis estimated the lifetime prevalence of sleepwalking at 6.9%, with the cur-
rent prevalence for children at 5.0%, and that for adults at 1.5% (Stallman & Kohler,
2016). Sleep terrors occur predominantly in very young children. Petit and colleagues
(2015) reported a prevalence of 34% at 18 months of age, followed by a sharp decrease
to 13% by age 5, and a slow decline to 5% at 13 years. The lifetime prevalence of
sleep terrors has been estimated at 10.4%, and the current prevalence in adults at
2.7% (Bjorvatn, Grønli, & Pallesen, 2010). The rates of NREM parasomnias appear not
to vary significantly by gender, either in childhood (Petit et al., 2015) or adulthood
(Ohayon et al., 1999).
Comorbidity
The age-old beliefs that sleepwalkers and those with sleep terrors are “possessed”
or mad (Riva et al., 2010) have gone by the wayside. However, people with NREM
parasomnias may be more likely than those without to have more common mental

-- 435 of 498 --

Sleep–Wake Disorders 	415
health symptoms. For example, sleepwalkers often report stressful events or strong
positive emotions as triggers, and are more likely than controls to have elevated
depressive and anxiety symptoms (Lopez et al., 2013). However, another study of 105
comprehensively screened sleepwalkers reported that mean depression and anxiety
scores were similar to those of community samples (Labelle, Desautels, Montplaisir,
& Zadra, 2013).
In a telephone survey of a representative sample (N = 4,972) of people ages 15–100
in the United Kingdom, the relationship between various mental health factors and
the occurrence of NREM parasomnias was explored (Ohayon et al., 1999). Those with
any type of NREM parasomnia were more likely than those without to find their
lives “highly stressful.” For example, 38% of those with sleep terrors reported their
lives to be highly stressful versus 17% of those without a NREM parasomnia. Hav-
ing any type of NREM parasomnia was also associated with a higher likelihood of
having a current or past mood or anxiety disorder. The rates were particularly high
in the sleep terror group, 30 and 34% of whom had a concurrent mood or anxiety
disorder, respectively. So, although the majority of adults with NREM parasomnias
do not report a stressful life or mental health problems, the rates may be elevated—
particularly in those with sleep terrors—compared to the nonaffected population.
Interestingly, treating concurrent anxiety or depression with psychiatric medication
or psychotherapy was not found to reduce the incidence of sleepwalking in a small
subsample of young adult sleepwalkers (Guilleminault et al., 2005). Similar research
on the effects of treating concurrent mental health problems on the occurrence of
sleep terrors seems to be absent.
Etiology
Family history and genetics may contribute to a person’s predisposition to NREM
parasomnias. The genotype HLA DQB1*05:01 is found in higher rates in affected
individuals (Heidbreder et al., 2016; Lecendreux et al., 2003) and an inheritance study
of a family with many sleepwalkers implicated chromosome 20q12-q13.12 (Licis, Des-
ruisseau, Yamada, Duntley, & Gurnett, 2011). Prevalence of NREM parasomnias in
children increased with the degree to which parents were affected (Petit et al., 2015).
NREM parasomnias are most prevalent in early childhood, when the central
nervous system (CNS) is undergoing rapid development. The early stages of CNS
maturational processes are believed to contribute to vulnerability to NREM para-
somnias because of reduced inhibition of motor mechanisms combined with a not-
yet-mature prefrontal cortex (Kotagal, 2009). The peak incidence of night terrors is
earlier than that of sleepwalking (Petit et al., 2015), and this possibly relates to stage
of neural development, with locomotor coordination coming later. The two disorders
share many features and can occur in the same individual. In a longitudinal study of
1,940 young children (Petit et al., 2015), one-third of those who had early-childhood
sleep terrors went on to develop sleepwalking while still in childhood.
Studies of brain structure and function in adults with NREM parasomnias have
found some apparent abnormalities: activation of motor and cingulate cortices con-
current with deactivation of frontal parietal areas (Bassetti, Vella, Donati, Wielepp,
& Weder, 2000; Castelnovo et al., 2016; Heidbreder et al., 2017). This is consistent
with an understanding of NREM parasomnias as emerging from a disassociation

-- 436 of 498 --

416 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
of neural processes that normally work together to maintain the separate states of
wakefulness and NREM sleep. Although it can happen, the emergence of a NREM
parasomnia in adulthood, without a prior history in childhood, is rare.
Clinical Implications
As we have seen, NREM parasomnias are mostly likely to occur in predisposed indi-
viduals, when there has been a buildup of SWS pressure, combined with disruption
to sleep by noise, touch, or another sleep disorder. Interventions should therefore
focus on psychoeducation, prevention of SWS pressure buildup (i.e., by avoid-
ing sleep deprivation), and minimization of sleep disturbance due to external and
internal disruptors. These three approaches are described below. Because partners
and family members are affected by NREM parasomnia events, interventions must
include them along with the patient.
PSYCHOEDUCATION
It is important to explain the nature of NREM parasomnias and to offer reassur-
ance that their occurrence does not mean there is major psychopathology. In the case
of sleepwalking, it is important for the patient and family to create a safe physical
environment, to prevent the sleepwalker from tripping, falling, exiting the home, or
otherwise moving into harm’s way. This may involve extra indoor locks on doors and
windows, moving furniture to create a clear passage, sleeping on main floors, and
avoidance of upper bunks. During a sleepwalking event, the individual should not
be awakened, touched, or forced back to bed. This is to reduce the risk of the sleep-
walker, in their disoriented state, striking out at the observer. Rather, the observer
can make a gentle verbal suggestion to return to bed and lie down, knowing that this
is the extent of reasonable intervention in the moment.
PREVENTION OF SWS BUILDUP
Because sleep deprivation builds up SWS pressure, patients need to get good sleep
each night. Having a constant bedtime and rise time 7 days a week can support sleep
stability. Patients should be advised against restricting their sleep by avoiding late
nights, early mornings, and by taking afternoon naps, if they are helpful. It some-
times happens that adults, in an effort to prevent NREM parasomnia events, can
become cautious about sleep times and conditions, avoiding late nights and sleeping
only at home. In early adulthood, this can pose a problem for dating and romantic
relationships, and the patient may need support in sorting out lifestyle routines that
prevent episodes while maintaining relationships.
MINIMIZATION OF SLEEP DISTURBANCE
Based on the evidence, sleeping in a noise-controlled environment, and avoidance of
interruptions by bed partners, family members, or pets, especially in the first third
of the night, is recommended. As for internal sleep disruptors, it is important for
the clinician to recognize and treat comorbid sleep disorders, such as periodic limb

-- 437 of 498 --

Sleep–Wake Disorders 	417
movement disorder, and especially sleep-disordered breathing (Guilleminault et
al., 2005). These sleep disorders require a medical referral. Insomnia should also be
treated, the latter with CBT-I without sleep restriction (which builds up SWS pres-
sure).
A further clinical implication of the NREM parasomnia research is that it should
not be automatically assumed that patients who have NREM parasomnias have a
major mental illness. However, clinicians need to be aware of any comorbid stress,
anxiety, and depression, especially in those with night terrors. Evidence-based treat-
ment of these psychological conditions is relevant, although this alone may not affect
the incidence of parasomnia episodes.
REM Parasomnias
Nightmare disorder and RSBD occur in REM sleep. Nightmare disorder is character-
ized by repeated, extended, very distressing dreams that involve threats to survival
or safety. Unlike sleep terrors, there is a well-remembered dream story. The person
awakens rapidly to an alert state. The nightmares and the associated awakenings
cause significant distress or impairment in functioning. RSBD is characterized by
episodes of REM sleep associated with vocalization or complex motor behaviors,
which are consistent with dream enactment. The person is alert and not confused
upon awakening. Episodes of both disorders rarely occur within the first 90 minutes
of sleep (with the exception of posttraumatic nightmares), but are most common in
the latter half of the sleep period, when most REM sleep occurs. With RSBD, poly-
somnography shows REM sleep “without atonia”; that is, there is no loss of skeletal
muscle tone. Fuller descriptions of these two REM parasomnias follow.
Nightmare Disorder
PHENOMENOLOGY AND SYMPTOMS
“Posttraumatic” nightmares are distinguished from “idiopathic” nightmares. The
former replay aspects of a traumatic event, and the person experiences emotions
similar to those experienced at the time of the trauma. Indeed, nightmares are a core
intrusive symptom of DSM-5 PTSD (see Bryant, Lott, & Keane, Chapter 6, this vol-
ume). In contrast, idiopathic nightmares have imaginative stories that do not directly
reflect actual events.
ASSOCIATED CLINICAL FEATURES AND COMORBIDITY
Most dreaming occurs in REM sleep, which occurs predominantly in the second half
of nighttime sleep, especially the early morning hours. Therefore, nightmares usu-
ally occur within this time frame. However, posttraumatic nightmares, more so than
idiopathic nightmares, sometimes occur in NREM sleep (e.g., Stage NREM2), earlier
in the night. Posttraumatic nightmares are associated with greater sleep disturbance
(Germain & Nielsen, 2003) and depression than idiopathic ones (Davis, Pruiksma,
Rhudy, & Byrd, 2011). Nightmares, of either type, are likely to be reported during
periods of increased stress and are associated with mental health symptoms of

-- 438 of 498 --

418 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
depression, anxiety, schizophrenic symptoms, and distress (Nielsen & Levin, 2007).
Importantly, recurrent nightmares are associated with a heightened risk of suicide
(Tanskanen et al., 2001).
COURSE, OUTCOME, AND TREATMENT
A longitudinal study of nightmare occurrence in 406 pregnant women followed them
6 months after giving birth and 4 years later (Schredl et al., 2019). The authors found
that nightmare frequency was very stable over time. Krakow (2006) found that adult
sleep patients with nightmares reported having had them for an average of 14.3 years
(SD = 17.5). Both of these observations suggest that nightmare problems tend to persist
if left untreated. Furthermore, Krakow found that patients often do not believe that
the nightmares can be treated. They tend to seek help from sleep labs for other prob-
lems, such as poor sleep quality, breathing problems at night, insomnia and move-
ment complaints. However, there are effective treatments for nightmare disorder.
A treatment with substantial evidence of efficacy is imagery rehearsal therapy
(IRT) (Augedal, Hansen, Kronhaug, Harvey, & Pallesen, 2013; Morgenthaler et al.,
2018). With this technique, nightmares are revised—either in writing or in the imagi-
nation only—to make them less distressing and more satisfactory, then rehearsed
on a daily basis. Such a treatment protocol is described in detail by Krakow and
Zadra (2006). Exposure-based treatments, which can include various components
but often involve writing down or imagining the nightmare in its original form, also
show evidence of efficacy. These treatments typically involve more sessions than
IRT does. Unlike IRT, the longer the exposure-based therapy, the greater the reduc-
tion in nightmare frequency (Hansen, Hofling, Kroner-Borowik, Stangier, & Steil,
2013). It is not yet clear whether IRT or exposure-based treatment is more effective
(Hansen et al., 2013). Both appear to deliver medium effect sizes (Augedal et al., 2013;
Hansen et al., 2013). Comparisons are difficult due to the heterogeneity of treatments
(variations of IRT and exposure-based PTSD or not) and measures (e.g., nightmare
frequency, distress; prospective or retrospective reports).
In a thorough examination of ideas on the possible mechanisms of action of
nightmare treatments, Rousseau and Belleville (2018) analyzed the mechanistic
themes in the literature and empirical evidence for each theme. They assembled the
themes of mastery, prevention of avoidance, belief modification, decreased arousal,
and restoration of sleep functions under the overarching theme of emotional pro-
cessing of the fear structure, which they believe to be the central mechanism respon-
sible for reducing nightmare frequency and severity.
EPIDEMIOLOGY
According to several large epidemiological studies, approximately 2–5% of the gen-
eral population experience frequent nightmares (Sandman et al., 2013). The preva-
lence is somewhat higher (i.e., 7–11%) among children (Gieselmann et al., 2019). After
young adulthood (e.g., age 25), the prevalence then increases with age (Sandman et
al., 2013). In general, the prevalence is higher in women than in men throughout the
lifespan, and there may be a peak of nightmare prevalence in the perimenopausal
years (women ages 50–55) (Sandman et al., 2013). By ages 55–60, the rate in men
approaches that of women (Sandman et al., 2013). Nightmare disorder is especially

-- 439 of 498 --

Sleep–Wake Disorders 	419
prevalent among people exposed to war (7–11%; Sandman et al., 2013), and those with
psychiatric disorders (16–67%; Gieselmann et al., 2019), particularly PTSD (50–70%;
Spoormaker & Montgomery, 2008).
ETIOLOGY
Because of the strong association between nightmares and trauma and distress, most
models concerning the development of nightmare disorder stem from psychology.
Recent models implicate hyperarousal and impaired fear extinction (Gieselmann
et al., 2019). In the neurocognitive “affective network dysfunction” model, Nielsen
and Levin (2007) propose that dreams normally serve to extinguish fear memories,
and that nightmares occur when this process fails. The dysfunction is believed to be
facilitated by “affect load” (stressful events) and “affect distress” (heightened nega-
tive emotional reactivity); childhood trauma and adversity predispose individuals to
affect distress (Carr & Nielsen, 2017).
A cognitive model has also been proposed for recurrent nightmares (Spoor-
maker, 2008). In this model, the story line of a recurrent nightmare is represented in
the memory as a “script” or fixed expectation pattern. When neutral dream elements
remind the dreamer of nightmare elements, the nightmare script replays (Spoor-
maker, 2008). This process is augmented by negatively biased interpretations and the
strength of the fear memory. Furthermore, cognitive avoidance maintains the fearful
sequence of scenes (Spoormaker, 2008). The pathway to nightmare disorder appears
to be complex and, like the function of dreaming, the dysfunction that produces
nightmares remains a topic of intensive research and debate.
CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS
Assessment is made through clinical interview and by ruling out other nocturnal
disorders such as NREM parasomnias and nocturnal panic attacks. Because of the
high rates of anxiety, depression, distress, trauma exposure, and other psychiatric
symptoms in patients with nightmare disorder, a full mental health assessment
should be done to detect these possible comorbidities. In all cases of nightmare dis-
order, it is important to let patients know that effective treatments exist, since few
realize this.
Being a principal symptom of nightmare disorder, posttraumatic nightmares
are sometimes overlooked as an entity to be separately treated. However, nightmares
often do not resolve with trauma therapy alone, and nightmare disorder is now seen
as its own disorder, requiring specific treatment (Spoormaker & Montgomery, 2008).
It is diagnosed along with PTSD if the nightmare symptoms are causing significant
distress or impairment in functioning (American Psychiatric Association, 2013).
Nightmare treatment has been shown to not only improve sleep but also reduce
PTSD symptoms (Ho, Chan, & Tang, 2016) and is associated with a reduction in sui-
cidal ideation (Ellis, Rufino, & Nadorff, 2017). Because of these benefits and its brev-
ity, IRT has been suggested as a starting point in the treatment of PTSD, and clinical
trials are under way to examine IRT as a first-line treatment for PTSD and suicidality
(Gieselmann et al., 2019).
There is an unusually high prevalence of sleep-disordered breathing, particularly
obstructive sleep apnea, among people with PTSD (Jaoude, Vermont, Porhomayon, &

-- 440 of 498 --

420 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
El-Solh, 2015) and treating the breathing disorder lowers nightmare frequency and
distress in patients with PTSD (Zhang et al., 2019). Therefore, it is important to also
check for comorbid sleep apnea in cases of nightmare disorder.
There has been some debate about the importance of exposure to nightmare
content for treatment efficacy. Certainly, the opposite—active suppression of con-
tent—is not recommended, since suppression of thoughts, especially negative ones,
can lead to their resurfacing in dreams (Taylor & Bryant, 2007). Similarly, cognitive
avoidance may facilitate maintenance of fear and nightmare recurrence (Spoor-
maker, 2008). However, lengthy exposure may be unnecessary: IRT with minimal
exposure works well to reduce nightmares (Krakow et al., 2001). Patients may prefer
IRT over exposure-based treatments in order to avoid the most unpleasant aspects of
the nightmare. Adherence to IRT may be better than that of exposure for this reason.
REM Sleep Behavior Disorder
PHENOMENOLOGY AND SYMPTOMS
In addition to REMs, a cardinal sign of REM sleep is a distinct reduction in muscle
tone. This loss of muscle tone, termed “atonia,” is due to skeletal muscle inhibition,
which prevents dreams from being acted out. In RSBD, the atonia is lost, and move-
ments consistent with dream stories are played out by the individual. A range of
vocalizations and movements is possible, from subtle hand movements to large, sud-
den movements, including punching, hitting, and kicking. Injuries to self and the
bed partner are not uncommon and can be severe. For example, violent behaviors
may occur if the person is dreaming of being threatened by a human or animal
entity and is trying to defend oneself. Despite the range of movements during REM,
people with RSBD rarely walk around the room or leave the bedroom. Unlike NREM
parasomnias (e.g., sleepwalking), most episodes of RSBD occur in the second half of
the night, the person’s eyes are closed, and there is often an abrupt awakening, with
clear recall of the dream content. Polysomnography shows an abnormally elevated
muscle tone in the electromyogram channel during REM sleep.
COURSE, OUTCOME, AND TREATMENT
Because of the relation to neurodegenerative processes (see “Etiology” section), and
the paucity of targeted treatments, the natural course of RSBD is a worsening of the
condition. The clinical emphasis is on managing the risk of inadvertent self-harm or
harm to others while acting out dreams, and medical monitoring for signs of Par-
kinson’s disease and other neurodegenerative diseases. Practice guidelines for RSBD
recommend modifying the sleep environment (Level A), clonazepam, and melatonin
(both Level B) (Aurora et al., 2010).
EPIDEMIOLOGY
The risk of RSBD increases with age, and the prevalence of RSBD in the population
over age 60 is roughly 1% (Dauvilliers et al., 2018). The population prevalence for
men and women is similar (Haba-Rubio et al., 2018), although it is possible that men
come to the attention of clinicians more often due to more violent dream enactment

-- 441 of 498 --

Sleep–Wake Disorders 	421
behaviors (Dauvilliers et al., 2018). RSBD is especially prevalent (30–70%) among peo-
ple with Parkinson’s disease, dementia with Lewy bodies, multiple system atrophy,
and narcolepsy (Dauvilliers et al., 2018).
ETIOLOGY
Many cases of RSBD are considered secondary to neurodegenerative diseases,
especially alpha-synucleinopathies, for example, Parkinson’s disease, dementia
with Lewy bodies, and multiple system atrophy (Howell, 2012; Santamaria, 2014).
RSBD can occur with other neurological and immune disorders (brainstem lesions,
stroke, dementia, narcolepsy type 1, Guillain–Barré syndrome) (Dauvilliers et al.,
2018; Nobili & Arnulf, 2014). Even in cases of RSBD without obvious neurological
disease—so-called “idiopathic” RSBD—the subsequent development of a neurode-
generative disease is likely (Dauvilliers et al., 2018; Nobili & Arnulf, 2014). Based on
postmortem examination, the likelihood of neurodegenerative pathology after diag-
nosis of idiopathic RSBD was estimated to be 35% at 5 years, 73% at 10 years, and 93%
at 14 years (Iranzo et al., 2013).
From studies of RSBD with animal models, the etiology of the disorder is
believed to involve interruption of the REM atonia pathway and/or disinhibition of
motor pattern generators in the brainstem (American Academy of Sleep Medicine,
2014). Several pathologies can lead to RSBD, including synuclein-associated and non-
synuclein-associated neurodegeneration, orexin dysfunction, and toxicity from anti-
depressant medication (Howell, 2012).
CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS
In addition to a history of episodes of complex motor behaviors and/or vocalization
during sleep, predominantly in the last half of the night, with alertness and orienta-
tion upon awakening, the diagnosis of RSBD requires polysomnographic recording
showing REM sleep without atonia. If a polysomnographic recording is not possible,
then RSBD can be diagnosed when there is a history suggestive of RSBD in a person
with a diagnosis of a synucleinopathy such as Parkinson’s disease (American Psy-
chiatric Association, 2013). In the clinical interview, RSBD is distinguishable from
NREM parasomnias by the timing of occurrence (second vs. first half of the night),
the eyes being closed, the report of dream enactment behavior, the longer duration
of episodes, the low likelihood of walking or leaving the room, the more vigorous
behaviors that are consistent with dream content and the alertness upon awakening.
Psychoeducation about RSBD is a helpful initial step. The importance of making
the sleeping environment safe (e.g., replacing hard surfaces or sharp edges near the
bed with soft ones) should be stressed. Given the potential for harm to the bed part-
ner, that person must be involved in these conversations. The bed partner is often
relieved to have the problem openly addressed, and a decision is often made to sleep
in separate locations.
An injury prevention device that provides calming audio commands (record-
ings of a familiar voice) to the person with RSBD when their movements reach a cer-
tain threshold shows preliminary evidence of efficacy (Howell, Arneson, & Schenck,
2011). Another possible injury reduction strategy for people with RSBD would be to
borrow the nightmare disorder treatment of IRT to attempt to reduce the proportion

-- 442 of 498 --

422 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
of agitated dreams and nightmares; this idea has yet to be tested. Much more needs
to be learned about how RSBD might be effectively treated or, better yet, prevented.
CONCLUSION
All sleep disorders are important to recognize, assess, and treat as early as possible.
Insomnia disorder, CRSWDs, and parasomnias are the sleep–wake disorders often
encountered in psychological practice. These conditions have widespread impacts,
and the patient usually has an incomplete understanding of the problem. Established
and emerging research in these areas informs clinical practice; however, much more
research is needed into these pervasive problems, particularly the CRSWDs and
parasomnias.
