429
Suicide is a leading cause of death worldwide. In 2016, approximately 1.4% of all
deaths were due to suicide (World Health Organization [WHO], 2020c). Moreover,
suicide is the second leading cause of death among adolescents and young adults
(ages 15–29) and accounts for more deaths in this age range than cancer, heart disease,
or homicide (WHO, 2020a). We summarize in this chapter current knowledge about
suicide, including the state of suicide research, theory, and treatment. We address
these issues in three major sections: a first section describing the phenomenology of
suicide, a second one describing factors involve in its etiology, and a third present-
ing psychological approaches that have received empirical support in reducing sui-
cidal thoughts and behaviors. We also present clinical implications and principles of
change derived from models and findings to help practitioners in their assessment,
monitoring, and management of suicidality.
PHENOMENOLOGY
Definition and Classification
It is important to begin by defining key terms. “Suicide” refers to death resulting
from self-inflicted injury, with at least some intent to die. A “suicide attempt” refers
to self-inflicted injury, with at least some intent to die, which may or may not end in
death. “Suicide ideation” refers to thoughts about suicide or suicide attempts, ranging
from passive desire for death to active intent to die by suicide. These definitions are
consistent with those offered by Turecki and Brent (2016). We begin with these defi-
nitions because, over the years, confusion and inconsistency regarding definitions
and terminology have been considerable. Terms such as “parasuicide,” “self-harm,”
“self-inflicted violence,” “self-mutilation,” “self-injury,” “suicidal gesture,” and “sui-
cidal threat” are used throughout the scientific and clinical literature in different
C H A P T E R 1 5
Suicide
Craig J. Bryan
E. David Klonsky

-- 450 of 498 --

430 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
ways by different researchers and practitioners. For example, the term “parasuicide”
is sometimes used to include all forms of self-inflicted injury, regardless of suicidal
intent (i.e., both suicidal and nonsuicidal forms of self-injury), and other times it is
used specifically to mean nonsuicidal forms of self-injury. Thus, when reading an
article or chapter that uses one or more of these terms, it is important to check the
definition being used by the authors. In general, we recommend against the terms
“committed suicide,” “completed suicide,” “failed attempt,” “nonfatal suicide,” “suc-
cessful suicide,” “suicidal gesture,” “suicide threat,” “self-mutilation,” and “parasui-
cide,” because they are considered misleading or vague, and in some cases pejorative
(Klonsky, May, & Saffer, 2016).
A key definitional issue in the field is the distinction between suicidal and non-
suicidal forms of self-harm. Historically, self-injurious behaviors with and without
suicidal intent were often lumped together into a single category of self-harm behav-
iors. However, researchers around the world are increasingly emphasizing the dis-
tinction between suicide attempts and nonsuicidal self-injury (NSSI). Specifically,
suicide attempts refer to self-injury with at least some intent to die, whereas NSSI refers
to self-injury without intent to die (International Society for the Study of Self-Injury,
2020). The distinction is important because suicide attempts and NSSI also differ
in several other important ways. These include frequency (NSSI is more frequent),
methods (common suicide attempt methods include firearm, overdose, and hanging,
whereas common NSSI methods include cutting and scratching), medical severity
(NSSI rarely requires medical attention), motivations (NSSI is most often performed
to cope with intense negative emotions), and treatment (suicide attempts or intent
sometimes require hospitalizations, including involuntary hospitalizations, which is
very rarely required for NSSI) (Klonsky, Victor, & Saffer, 2014). At the same time, it
is important to recognize that suicide attempts and NSSI can and often do co-occur
within the same person; for example, it is possible for a given individual to some-
times use NSSI to regulate intense negative emotions and at other times to have sui-
cide ideation or attempt suicide. The relationship between suicide attempts and NSSI
is the subject of considerable research and theory (Hamza, Stewart, & Willoughby,
2012; Klonsky, May, & Glenn, 2013), and for this reason is addressed further in the
“Associated Features” section.
Another important issue is the place of suicide and suicide attempts in the
diagnostic manuals that guide the classification of mental disorders. Historically,
diagnostic manuals have not included separate sections for suicide; instead, suicide,
suicide attempts, and suicide ideation are only referenced when considered relevant
for describing other mental disorders. For example, in the fourth edition of the Diag-
nostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-IV; American Psychiatric Asso-
ciation, 1994), the diagnostic criteria for borderline personality disorder included
“recurrent suicidal behavior, gestures, or threats,” and the diagnostic criteria for
major depression included “recurrent suicidal ideation without a specific plan, or
a suicide attempt or a specific plan for committing suicide” (as a side note, these
criteria use older terms—“gestures,” “threats,” “committing”—that we now consider
unhelpful and pejorative).
More recently, there have been calls for suicide to be classified as an indepen-
dent diagnostic entity. The Appendix of the most recent edition of the Diagnostic and
Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-5) includes a proposal for a psychiatric

-- 451 of 498 --

Suicide 	431
diagnosis called suicidal behavior disorder (American Psychiatric Association, 2013).
According to the proposed criteria, most individuals who have attempted suicide
during the preceding 2 years would qualify for the diagnosis. Both advantages and
limitations of this proposal have been articulated (Obegi, 2019; Oquendo & Baca-
Garcia, 2014). Since publication of DSM-5, alternative suicide diagnoses or syn-
dromes have also been proposed. Whereas the DSM-5 proposal puts the emphasis
on behavior (i.e., requiring a recent suicide attempt), other proposals elaborate on the
affective and cognitive experiences of those at risk for suicide. These include acute
suicidal affective disturbance, which emphasizes rising suicidal intent, social alien-
ation, hopelessness, and agitation, and suicide crisis syndrome, which emphasizes
the persistent feeling of entrapment (Rogers, Galynker, Yaseen, DeFazio, & Joiner,
2017). Knowledge about the validity and clinical utility of these proposals is only
beginning to accumulate. Thus, it remains unclear whether suicide will ultimately
be recognized as a distinct diagnostic entity.
A final consideration we note is whether suicide should be classified as a mental
health problem. Those who advocate viewing suicide as a mental health problem
cite data that more than 90% of those who die by suicide meet criteria for one or
more mental disorders at the time of their death (Arsenault-Lapierre, Kim, & Turecki,
2004). However, others suggest that in some populations this figure is closer to 50%,
which means that approximately half of those who die by suicide do not have a men-
tal disorder (Phillips, 2010). Moreover, it is well established that the vast majority of
individuals with mental disorders do not die by suicide, and a recent meta-analysis
found that sociodemographic characteristics and stressful life events predict suicide
more strongly than do mental disorders (Franklin et al., 2017). In short, while mental
disorders can be important contributors to suicide, a full understanding of suicide
must consider a range of sociological, interpersonal, psychological, and biological
contexts and causes.
Epidemiology
Most of what is known about the epidemiology of suicide comes from data obtained
and reported by the WHO (2020a, 2020b, 2020c). Worldwide, approximately 800,000
people die by suicide each year. In 2016, the global age-standardized suicide rate was
10.5 per 100,000. In general, suicide rates are highest among people experiencing
crises, violence, abuse, loss, or isolation, and for vulnerable groups that experience
discrimination, including lesbian, gay, bisexual, and transgender and other sexual
minorities (LGBTQ+), indigenous populations, and refugees. Suicide rates are also
higher among those with mental illness.
Suicide trends can vary greatly by geography and context. For example, suicide
rates tend to be highest in Southeast Asia, Europe, and Africa, and lowest in the East-
ern Mediterranean area. Other trends tend to be more generalizable and consistent.
For example, in most regions around the world, the suicide rate is higher in men than
in women, even though women attempt suicide more often. Globally, the suicide rate
is 1.8 times higher for men than for women. One reason for this pattern may be that
men tend to choose methods that more often end in death, such as firearms, whereas
women tend to choose methods that less often end in death, such as overdose/poi-
soning. Suicide rates in the United States mirror these global trends. Suicide in the

-- 452 of 498 --

432 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
United States also varies considerably across racial identity (rates are highest among
individuals identifying as White or Native Americans) and age groups (rates are
highest among middle-aged adults and older adults).
Most suicide attempts do not end in death. For every suicide death, there are
many who have made nonfatal attempts and even more who have considered suicide
without making an attempt. Worldwide, approximately 9% of people have seriously
considered suicide, and approximately 2% have made suicide attempts (Nock et al.,
2008). These rates are even higher in the United States, where an estimated 16% have
seriously considered suicide and 5% have made a suicide attempt (Nock et al., 2008).
It is fair to say that a large minority of people have experienced suicide ideation at
some point in their lives.
When considering the epidemiology of suicide, it is important to consider chal-
lenges in obtaining accurate data. The suicide data reported by different countries
are not always high quality, especially given that suicide is a sensitive topic—and in
some countries it is illegal. It is therefore likely that suicide is often underreported.
It is also likely that many suicide deaths are misclassified, for example, recorded as
accidental falls or overdoses. For these reasons, the WHO and others interested in
the epidemiology of suicide are continually working to improve the quality of data
collection and reporting methods.
Typical Symptoms and Associated Features
Research has identified an incredibly diverse range of correlates and predictors of
suicide, suicide attempts, and suicide ideation. However, these factors do have one
thing in common: They are very poor predictors of future suicide, suicide attempts,
and suicide ideation (Franklin et al., 2017). A comprehensive review of this topic is
far beyond the scope of this chapter. Here, we identify what are arguably the most
important symptoms and features associated with suicide, suicide attempts, and sui-
cide ideation, and also address some important misconceptions.
Motivations for Suicide
Perhaps the most consistent finding in suicidology are the motivations for suicide
reported by those who have made suicide attempts. Different theorists have proposed
different motivations for suicide. These include social isolation, pain, hopelessness,
low belongingness, perceived burdensomeness, a “cry for help,” and many others.
Importantly, a recent body of work has found evidence that two of these motiva-
tions are most common and near-universally reported by those who attempt suicide:
pain and hopelessness (May & Klonsky, 2013; May, O’Brien, Liu, & Klonsky, 2016;
May, Pachkowski, & Klonsky, 2020). Suicide is usually motivated by a combination
of overwhelming psychological pain and hopelessness that the pain can be stopped.
This finding has been replicated in both adults and adolescents who have attempted
suicide, and both clinical and nonclinical populations. Furthermore, there is both a
compelling body of literature that psychological pain is closely tied to suicide, and
especially the experience of suicide ideation (Ducasse et al., 2018; Hinze et al., 2019),
and a vast literature that suggests hopelessness predicts suicide, suicide attempts,
and suicide ideation more strongly than do depressive symptoms (Ribeiro, Huang,

-- 453 of 498 --

Suicide 	433
Fox, & Franklin, 2018). Taken together, this body of research has led one theorist to
conclude that “the combination of pain and hopelessness is what causes suicide ide-
ation to develop” (Klonsky & May, 2015b, p. 117).
Stressful Life Events
Some of the strongest near-term predictors of suicide attempts and death are stress-
ful life events, especially those of an interpersonal nature. Dozens of studies over
several decades link stressful life events to suicide ideation and attempts (Liu &
Miller, 2014). A review of “psychological autopsy studies” (studies that carefully
examine the lives of people who have died by suicide, sometimes in comparison
to the lives of people who did not die by suicide) concluded that nearly all suicide
decedents studied were found to have experienced one or more significant negative
life events in the months preceding the suicide (Foster, 2011). These events tended to
include interpersonal conflict, and to relate to suicide death independent of histories
of mental disorder. Another study using a timeline followback method revealed that
negative life events, particularly interpersonal ones involving a romantic partner,
tended to precede nonfatal suicide attempts (Bagge, Glenn, & Lee, 2013). Further-
more, meta-analytic work supports a link between stressful life events and suicide:
This work, as previously mentioned, has also found that life events predict suicide
death more strongly than do mental disorders (Franklin et al., 2017). Unfortunately,
it is challenging to study the periods of time immediately preceding suicide attempts
and death. As a result, the field still has much to learn regarding the immediate ante-
cedents and causes of suicide.
Mental Disorders
Most mental disorders have been found to be associated with suicide ideation,
attempts, and death. Interestingly, the association seems to be strongest for suicide
ideation: Rates of mental disorders are much higher in those with histories of sui-
cide ideation compared to those without, whereas rates of mental disorder are only
modestly higher in those with histories of suicide attempts compared to those with
histories of ideation but not attempts (Kessler, Borges, & Walters, 1999; May & Klon-
sky, 2016). The magnitudes of associations with suicide, suicide attempts, and sui-
cide ideation are different for different mental disorders. In general, suicide attempts
appear to be most common in bipolar disorder, posttraumatic stress disorder, and
depression (Nock et al., 2009). A full accounting of the associations of mental dis-
orders with suicide, suicide attempts, and suicide ideation is beyond the scope of
this chapter. Here, we elaborate on suicide risk in two disorders, depression and
posttraumatic stress disorder, the former because many assume almost all suicides
are due to depression, and the latter because it is somewhat unique among mental
disorders for increasing risk of not only suicide ideation but also progression from
ideation to suicide attempts.
Depression has long been emphasized as a correlate and cause of suicide. How-
ever, the overall pattern of research suggests that depression is most tightly linked
to suicide ideation (as opposed to suicide attempts or death). For example, in a large
epidemiological study in the United States, a major depressive episode was strongly

-- 454 of 498 --

434 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
associated with a history of suicide ideation, but only weakly associated with pro-
gression from ideation to a suicide attempt (Kessler et al., 1999). Similarly, a more
recent meta-analysis revealed that depression was significantly elevated in people
with histories of suicidal ideation, but that depression was similar in people who had
attempted suicide compared to people with suicidal ideation who had never made a
suicide attempt (May & Klonsky, 2016). A similar pattern is apparent in longitudinal
studies. Depression predicts future suicide ideation better than it predicts future
suicide attempts or deaths (Ribeiro et al., 2018).
Unlike depression, posttraumatic stress disorder appears to increase risk for
both the development of suicide ideation and the progression from suicide ideation
to attempts. Meta-analytically, like many disorders, posttraumatic stress disorder
exhibits a strong relationship to a history of suicide ideation (May & Klonsky, 2016).
However, in this same meta-analysis, posttraumatic stress disorder had the stron-
gest effect size when it came to distinguishing those who had attempted suicide
from those who had suicide ideation without attempts. A similar pattern emerged in
WHO data across 21 countries (Nock et al., 2009); specifically, posttraumatic stress
disorder stood out for its ability to distinguish those who attempted suicide from
those who had suicide ideation without progressing to attempts.
Impulsivity
Another variable emphasized in the suicidology literature is impulsivity. The field’s
view of impulsivity has changed over time. Initially, impulsivity was thought to be a
key factor in determining whether suicide ideation would be acted upon and lead to
a suicide attempt. For example, a highly cited clinical model of suicide suggests that
those who attempt suicide are “more impulsive and, therefore, more likely to act on
suicidal feelings” (Mann, Waternaux, Haas, & Malone, 1999, p. 181). Another early
perspective suggested that impulsivity “may actually be a more significant indica-
tor of suicide attempt than the presence of a specific suicide plan” (Bryan & Rudd,
2006). These perspectives suggest that impulsivity should be higher in those who
attempt suicide compared to those who have suicide ideation but do not attempt.
Though this perspective is intuitive and logical, it turns out to be incorrect. Mul-
tiple studies using different measures of impulsivity have found that impulsivity
is similar in those who attempt suicide and in those with suicidal ideation who do
not make suicide attempts (for a review, see Klonsky & May, 2015a). Interestingly,
these same studies indicate that impulsivity is higher in those with suicidal ideation
compared to those without suicidal ideation. In addition, few individuals report that
they attempted suicide due to impulsivity (May et al., 2020). Some speculate that
impulsivity is a marker, and possibly a cause, for a life filled with more stressful
events, which in turn raises risk for suicide ideation and attempts (Anestis, Soberay,
Gutierrez, Hernández, & Joiner, 2014).
An alternative perspective is that traditional models of impulsivity, which often
conceptualize impulsivity as a trait-like personality feature, may be limited. Indeed,
there is little debate that individuals can shift very quickly from low-risk states to
high-risk states (Bryan et al., 2020). For example, pain and hopelessness, which most
often motivate suicide, can fluctuate quite a bit over time, and can increase dramati-
cally and rapidly in response to negative life events, experiences, and contexts; in
turn, suicidal desire can fluctuate over time and at times increase dramatically and

-- 455 of 498 --

Suicide 	435
rapidly. However, rapid transition from one state to another does not, by itself, reflect
impulsivity. In other words, an individual who may reasonably be characterized
as “nonimpulsive” by traditional standards may, under the right conditions or cir-
cumstances, nonetheless experience a sudden and dramatic increase in suicide risk.
The impulsivity–suicide link has yet to be fully clarified, though it appears safe to
assume that impulsivity as traditionally conceptualized is not as central to suicide
as once thought.
Nonsuicidal Self‑Injury
The relationship of suicide attempts to NSSI has also generated considerable interest.
NSSI refers to a range of behaviors, including self-cutting, hitting, and scratching
that are likely to result in bleeding or bruising but are performed without suicidal
intent and cause only mild tissue damage. As noted earlier, suicide attempts and
NSSI differ in terms of method, frequency, medical severity, and motivation, among
other characteristics. At the same time, NSSI is a particularly strong correlate and
predictor of suicide attempts. This relationship has been demonstrated both cross-
sectionally (Klonsky et al., 2013) and longitudinally (Franklin et al., 2017).
In summary, suicide attempts and NSSI are distinct behaviors, yet often co-
occur. There are several possible explanations for this pattern (Hamza et al., 2012;
Klonsky et al., 2013). A leading possibility is that NSSI increases risk for both sui-
cide ideation and attempts. Regarding suicide ideation, NSSI is a correlate of depres-
sion, anxiety, and histories of abuse, all of which are among the top five predic-
tors of suicide ideation (Franklin et al., 2017). Thus, NSSI is a marker for increased
risk of suicide ideation, even if it does not cause suicide ideation. Regarding suicide
attempts, NSSI is a form of self-inflicted violence, and thus signifies an increased
capability to act on suicide ideation should it develop. Theorists such as Joiner (2005)
note that most people with suicide ideation do not make suicide attempts, in large
part because people are biologically and evolutionarily wired to fear and avoid pain,
injury, and death. These barriers must be overcome in order for someone to attempt
suicide. Someone who has a history of self-inflicted violence, such as NSSI, has had
opportunity to habituate to these barriers and approach them with less fear, and is
thus more able and likely to act on suicide ideation and to progress from ideation
to attempts. In short, even though NSSI is distinct from suicide attempts, it can be
viewed as a “double-trouble” suicide risk factor (Klonsky et al., 2013), in that it signi-
fies increased risk for both suicide ideation and attempts.
Prior Suicide Ideation and Attempts
In general, past suicide ideation and attempts are important predictors of future ide-
ation and attempts. In the seminal meta-analysis by Franklin and colleagues (2017),
the strongest predictor of future ideation was past ideation, and past suicide attempts
were a top predictor of future suicide attempts. Similarly, future suicide death was
predicted by prior suicide attempts and prior suicide ideation. There were some
interesting caveats to these patterns, however. Specifically, past NSSI was the stron-
gest predictor of future suicide attempts (even stronger than past suicide attempts),
and past psychiatric hospitalizations were the strongest predictor of suicide death
(even stronger than past suicide attempts and ideation). Overall, these patterns

-- 456 of 498 --

436 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
highlight the importance of distinguishing different types of self-injurious thoughts
and behaviors rather than aggregating these various thoughts and behaviors into a
composite “suicidality” construct.
These relationships between past and future instances of suicide ideation and
attempts have important caveats. First, many people who die by suicide do so on
their first attempt; thus, while it is true that past suicide attempts predict future
suicide attempts, one must not lose sight of the fact that a first suicide attempt often
results in death (Botswick, Pabbati, Geske, & McKean, 2016). Second, many people
who attempt suicide do not go on to make another attempt; in fact, a vast majority of
individuals who make suicide attempts do not go on to die by suicide. This happens
in part because people change their minds (Nordentoft, Madsen, & Erlangsen, 2016).
Many people who feel suicidal at one time will feel differently if they survive the
suicidal crisis. Third, the method used during a suicide attempt has a strong role in
whether the attempt ends in death. Specifically, suicide attempts by firearms result
in death more than 90% of the time, and hanging and drowning also frequently
result in death; in contrast, suicide attempts by poisoning or cutting rarely result in
death (Miller, Azrael, & Hemenway, 2004). For these reasons, some feel that “means
safety”—reducing access to the most lethal methods of suicide attempt—represents
the most effective and important approach to suicide prevention currently available
(Anestis et al., 2017).
Connectedness
To this point, we have focused on factors that increase risk for suicide ideation,
attempts, and death. We conclude this section with a focus on a key variable that
reduces suicide risk: connectedness. Connectedness is sometimes defined narrowly,
to focus on key relationships with family, significant others, or close friends. Oth-
ers define connectedness more broadly, to include connections to various sources
of purpose or meaning. Connectedness shows up in numerous theories of suicide
in different forms, such as the social isolation emphasized by Durkheim (1897/1951),
and the thwarted belongingness and perceived burdensomeness emphasized by
Joiner (2005). Numerous sources of connectedness have been found to be protective
against suicide risk. These include (1) one’s sense of purpose or meaning in life; (2)
key relationships, including marriage, parenthood, and other sources of social sup-
port; (3) connection to religious communities; (4) reliable and high-quality employ-
ment; and (5) pets and/or other nonhuman companions. Each of these kinds of con-
nection has layers and nuances that go beyond the scope of this chapter (for a review,
see Zareian & Klonsky, 2020). Connections to important people, communities, and
sources of meaning increase desire to live, and the absence of such connections are
common in those who consider and attempt suicide (Bryan, Bryan, Rozek, & Leifker,
2019; Frankl, 1959; Kleiman & Beaver, 2013).
Course and Outcomes
Suicide risk is an inherently dynamic construct. Although researchers and clinicians
have recognized this for decades, only in the past decade or so have technological
advances provided researchers with the tools needed to easily and reliably quantify

-- 457 of 498 --

Suicide 	437
this property. The advent of smartphones, for instance, has enabled researchers
to repeatedly assess individuals’ suicidal thoughts with very high frequency—in
some cases up to four to six times per day (Kleiman & Nock, 2018)—using ecologi-
cal momentary assessment (EMA) methods. These studies have consistently shown
that suicide ideation and other risk variables (e.g., mood, perceived burdensome-
ness, loneliness) rise and fall during the course of a typical day. Moment-to-moment
change in suicide ideation can sometimes be very large in magnitude and occur
very rapidly. In a 4-week study using EMA methods, Kleiman and colleagues (2017)
found that over 90% of suicidal individuals experienced a large (i.e., one standard
deviation or more) shift in suicide ideation at least once. Other researchers have simi-
larly observed nonlinear change processes in the social media content of individuals
who have died by suicide and, as described below, have found that fluctuations in
multiple variables tended to increase in magnitude and intensity in the months and
weeks leading up to suicide death (Bryan et al., 2018).
The fluid vulnerability theory of suicide (FVT; Rudd, 2006) provides a use-
ful model for understanding the time course of suicide. Central to the FVT is the
assumption that suicide risk is most usefully conceptualized along two dimensions:
baseline and acute. “Baseline risk” refers to the elements of suicide risk that tend
to persist or remain relatively constant over time (e.g., trauma history, biological or
genetic predispositions, previous suicidal behavior); “acute risk,” by contrast, refers
to the elements of suicide risk that tend to change (e.g., mood state, hopelessness,
situational stressors). According to the FVT, individuals with elevated baseline risk
experience more frequent and longer duration suicidal crises because they are sen-
sitized to aversive life stressors and shifts in negative internal states. In other words,
it is easier for high-baseline individuals to become—and remain—suicidal. An active
suicidal crisis is resolved when the variables that precipitate and perpetuate the cri-
sis are sufficiently disrupted, for example, through intervention or treatment.
Newer findings based on the FVT suggest that higher risk states are charac-
terized by qualitatively distinct temporal patterns in suicide ideation and other
risk variables than lower risk states. Individuals who have made multiple suicide
attempts, for example, show greater variability in suicide ideation than do individu-
als who have never attempted suicide or who made only one prior attempt (Bryan &
Rudd, 2018b; Witte, Fitzpatrick, Joiner, & Schmidt, 2005; Witte, Fitzpatrick, Warren,
Schatschneider, & Schmidt, 2006). Increased variability in suicide ideation after a
first suicide attempt has also been shown to signal vulnerability for a second suicide
attempt (Bryan, Rozek, et al., 2019). Indeed, fluctuations in suicide ideation, but not
severity of suicide ideation, distinguish patients who subsequently attempt suicide
(Bryan, Rozek, et al., 2019). These findings are not limited to suicide ideation; vari-
ability in negative affective states and negative cognitions (e.g., hopelessness, per-
ceived burdensomeness) have also been shown to distinguish between individuals
who die by suicide and those who die of nonsuicide causes (Bryan, Butner, et al.,
2018).
Recent research further suggests that higher and lower risk states also differ
from each other with respect to more complex, coordinated change processes among
multiple risk variables and/or dimensions of suicide risk. For example, the wish to
live and the wish to die have traditionally been conceptualized as competing psy-
chological forces that influence the emergence of suicidal behaviors (Brown, Steer,

-- 458 of 498 --

438 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
Henriques, & Beck, 2005; Harris, McLean, Sheffield, & Jobes, 2010; Jobes & Mann,
1999; Kovacs & Beck, 1977). Although the wish to live and the wish to die are often
inversely related to each other, this is not always the case because, like other facets
of suicide risk, the wish to live and the wish to die ebb and flow over time, but not
necessarily in a coordinated manner. In other words, it is not always the case that
when the wish to live increases, the wish to die decreases, and vice versa. Sometimes
the wish to live increases but the wish to die remains elevated; sometimes the wish
to live decreases and so does the wish to die. Indeed, these two aspects of suicide risk
often fluctuate independent of the other (Bryan, Rudd, Peterson, et al., 2018). Under
certain circumstances, however, the wish to live and the wish to die do fluctuate
in a coordinated manner. When this occurs, the probability of suicidal behavior is
reduced (Bryan, Rozek, et al., 2018). Coordinated change patterns that characterize
nonsuicidal states have been observed in other studies as well (Bryan et al., 2016),
further supporting the perspective that lower and higher risk states are character-
ized by qualitatively distinct temporal processes.
The existence of two qualitatively distinct risk states is also implicated by taxo-
metric analyses that indicate suicide risk is categorical in nature, with one category
corresponding to a high-risk state and the other category corresponding to a low-
risk state (Rufino, Marcus, Ellis, & Boccaccini, 2018; Witte, Holm-Denoma, Zurom-
ski, Gauthier, & Ruscio, 2017). Research utilizing machine learning methods have
similarly identified two distinct subgroups of suicide risk, one characterized by a
low probability of suicidal behavior and the other characterized by a much higher
probability of suicidal behavior (Kessler et al., 2015). Because these lower and higher
risk states are so distinct, the shift from the lower to the higher risk state often occurs
suddenly, seemingly coming out of the blue in response to relatively little provoca-
tion or change. If an individual attempts suicide after one of these sudden shifts,
their suicidal behavior is very likely to be characterized as “impulsive,” although
this descriptor may not be accurate, as discussed previously. The emergence of sui-
cidal behavior is not always sudden or “impulsive,” however. In some cases, suicidal
behavior emerges after an extended, gradual change process that is more likely to
be associated with prolonged and specific suicidal planning. These multiple time
courses implicate different types of interventions and prevention strategies (Bryan
et al., 2020).
Clinical Implications
Suicide results from complex interactions among multiple risk and protective factors.
Within this context, clinicians need to be aware that it disproportionately affects vul-
nerable groups who have historically experienced abuse, discrimination, and mental
illness (especially bipolar disorder, posttraumatic stress disorder, and depression).
Clinicians also need to be aware that research has led to changes in the conceptu-
alization of suicidal thoughts and behaviors, which in turn have important clini-
cal implications. Suicidal thoughts and behaviors have traditionally been concep-
tualized as existing on a spectrum; multiple lines of evidence suggest that various
forms of suicidal and self-injurious exist and have distinct risk and protective fac-
tors. Accordingly, practitioners need to assess and address various factors that may
have more or less clinical importance for some clients than others. These include

-- 459 of 498 --

Suicide 	439
the client’s level of pain and hopelessness, stressful live events, NSSI, past suicidal
ideation and attempts, access to lethal means (e.g., firearms), and connections with
others and/or with sources of meanings. Furthermore, suicidal thoughts and behav-
iors have traditionally been conceptualized as symptoms of mental disorders, but
accumulating evidence indicates they are better understood as discrete phenomena
that often co-occur with, but do not result from, psychological and behavioral dis-
orders. Consistent with this line of evidence, treatments that effectively reduce the
symptoms and features of psychological and behavioral disorders tend to reduce
suicidal thoughts but do not necessarily reduce the occurrence of suicidal behaviors.
Treatments that target factors that are specific to suicidal behaviors, regardless of an
individual’s associated mental health condition, have been shown to reliably reduce
suicidal behaviors.
First, effective treatments for suicide risk consider both the acute and baseline
risk dimensions, as articulated by the FVT. With respect to the acute risk dimension,
effective treatments must be able to disrupt positive feedback loops that exacerbate
and sustain high-risk states, thereby facilitating rapid resolution of the suicidal crisis
and a shift back to a lower risk state. Suicide risk fluctuates as a result of change in
its many associated features. For example, a stressful event can lead to momentary
shifts in emotional distress and hopelessness, thereby increasing suicide risk. This
shift in psychological state may be more pronounced for individuals with a mental
health condition such as depression or posttraumatic stress disorder. If that person
reaches out to a loved one for support, the resulting connectedness may offset or
counteract their emotional distress, thereby reducing their emotional distress, hope-
lessness, and suicide risk. If, however, the loved one rebuffs the individual, emo-
tional distress, hopelessness, and suicide risk may increase instead. Under these
conditions, the individual may perceive their emotional distress as overwhelming
or unbearable, and begin to contemplate suicide (e.g., “I should just kill myself”),
thereby increasing the probability of suicidal behavior.
Effective treatments also monitor patients’ temporal patterns through repeated
assessment of suicide ideation and/or other relevant risk variables (e.g., sleep dis-
turbance, mood state). This can enable clinicians to observe and quantify the ebb
and flow of suicide risk over the course of treatment and make appropriate adjust-
ments to the treatment plan. Symptom monitoring can be accomplished with widely
available symptoms scales that include items that directly ask about suicide-related
thoughts. Bryan, Rozek, Butner, and Rudd (2019), for instance, showed that session-
to-session monitoring of suicide ideation using a single item from a larger symptom
scale could detect change patterns that distinguished patients who subsequently
attempted suicide from those who did not. Patients whose suicide ideation rating
fluctuated from session to session were the ones most likely to eventually attempt
suicide. Patients who showed less variability in scores—even those who consistently
provided higher item ratings—were the least likely to attempt suicide.
Treatments that focus only on managing episodes of acutely elevated risk are
insufficient, however; effective treatment must also dismantle the processes and
mechanisms that activate these feedback loops in the first place, thereby reducing the
probability of shifting to a high-risk state. The most effective treatments and inter-
ventions for reducing suicidal behaviors all share a common focus on self-regulation
skills training. Critically, these treatments teach patients how to self-monitor

-- 460 of 498 --

440 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
fluctuations in suicide risk and how to recognize the early signs of feedback loops
that lead to high-risk states. The aforementioned treatments use simple strategies to
help patients understand how their many risk and protective factors can influence
one another and, under certain circumstances, result in high-risk states. Clinicians
who collaboratively engage their patients in the case conceptualization process can
help them to more readily understand how and why they are thinking, feeling, and
behaving in uncomfortable and/or undesirable ways. If the warning signs of a transi-
tion to higher risk states can be identified much sooner, acute episodes of risk can
potentially be averted.
ETIOLOGY
As for many important behavioral phenomena, the etiology of suicide is not well
established. In this section, we describe some of the leading empirical and theoreti-
cal perspectives on this topic. One way to approach the etiology of suicide is to start
with its proximal causes. As noted earlier, an increasing body of evidence suggests
that suicide attempts are motivated by overwhelming pain and by hopelessness that
the causes of one’s pain will not improve (May & Klonsky, 2013; May et al., 2016, 2020).
As a result, one theory argues that the path to suicide begins with pain (Klonsky &
May, 2015b). However, this same theory notes that even if we put the focus on pain
as a starting point, pain can have an essentially infinite number of different causes
for different people: Pain can be due to social isolation, financial difficulties, sudden
relationship dissolution, discrimination, societal inequities, abuse, physical disor-
ders, and mental disorders, in short, any context, event, or experience that someone
may experience as intensely aversive, miserable, or painful. Thus, understanding
the etiology of overwhelming pain, let alone other factors that increase suicide risk,
means embracing a variety of possible biopsychosocial causes that can have different
relevance for different people.
In this context, theories of suicide strive to identify the pathways or factors that
have near-universal relevance for causing suicide. Early theories of suicide tended
to emphasize single factors as the causes of suicide, such as Durkheim’s (1897/1951)
focus on social isolation, Beck’s (1967) and Abramson’s (Abramson et al., 2000) focus
on hopelessness, Baumeister’s (1990) focus on negative self-cognitions, and Shneid-
man’s (1993) focus on psychological pain. These theories have been tremendously
influential and helpful for providing foundational knowledge. Indeed, as will
become evident later in this section, the factors emphasized by these early theories
are also found in contemporary theories of suicide.
The Ideation‑to‑Action Framework
The early perspectives on suicide we noted earlier share a key limitation: They treated
suicidality as a single phenomenon in need of a single explanation. In contrast, con-
temporary perspectives are increasingly guided by an ideation-to-action framework
(Klonsky & May, 2014). From this perspective, (1) the development of suicide ideation
and (2) the progression from suicide ideation to attempts are viewed as distinct pro-
cesses with different predictors and explanations. Several lines of research led to

-- 461 of 498 --

Suicide 	441
this framework becoming popular. First, most people with suicide ideation do not
attempt suicide. For example, epidemiological data suggest that only about 40% of
those with serious suicide ideation eventually make a suicide attempt (Nock et al.,
2008). Thus, a strong suicide theory must explain why ideation sometimes leads to
suicide attempts but usually does not. Second, a growing body of evidence indicates
that variables commonly described as suicide risk factors are indeed strong predic-
tors of suicide ideation but are weak predictors of attempts among ideators (May
& Klonsky, 2016). For example, several studies find that depression, hopelessness,
and impulsivity, though strong correlates of suicide ideation, are similar in those
who attempt suicide compared to those with suicide ideation who do not make an
attempt (Klonsky & May, 2010, 2014; May & Klonsky, 2016). In short, many commonly
cited risk factors for suicide do not appear to be meaningfully higher in attempters
compared to ideators. Thus, contemporary researchers and theorists have sought to
identify factors that explain and predict progression from suicide ideation to suicide
attempts, and to distinguish these factors from those that explain and predict suicide
ideation (Klonsky, Qiu, & Saffer, 2017).
The Interpersonal–Psychological Theory of Suicide
The first theorist to explicitly offer distinct explanations for suicide ideation ver-
sus suicide attempts was Thomas Joiner (2005; Van Orden et al., 2010). Joiner’s
interpersonal–psychological theory of suicide (IPTS) suggests that the combination
of thwarted belongingness and perceived burdensomeness causes desire for suicide,
whereas the acquired capability to attempt suicide makes it possible for some people
to act on their ideation and make a potentially lethal suicide attempt. The construct
of acquired capability was a novel contribution to the field and is now referred to
more broadly as “capability for suicide” or “suicide capability” (May & Victor, 2018)
Joiner (2005) noted that people have evolved to have strong fears of pain, injury,
and death, and that these fears are substantial barriers to making a suicide attempt.
Thus, most people do not have the capability to attempt suicide, even if desire for sui-
cide is present. Joiner argued that this capability can be acquired over time through
exposure to “painful and provocative events” (PPEs). In short, PPEs are events that
expose people to pain, injury, or death, and that thereby habituate people to fears
of pain, injury, and death, and in turn increase their capability to overcome these
barriers and make a suicide attempt. PPEs are hypothesized to take a variety of
forms, including physical abuse; combat exposure; NSSI histories; exposure to life-
threatening injuries and death common for first responders, and exposure to pain,
injury, and death common for medical professionals, among many others. This per-
spective may help explain why past self-injurious behavior is the strongest predictor
of future suicide attempts: past self-injury is a PPE that increases comfort with and
reduces fear of self-inflicted violence.
The IPTS has been enormously influential and highly cited. This large body
of literature has led to a series of meta-analyses and systematic reviews examining
the theory’s various postulates. In brief, evidence for the theory’s various postu-
lates is mixed (Chu et al., 2017; Ma, Batterham, Calear, & Han, 2016). Perhaps the
theory’s greatest contribution is not its specifics but the heuristic value of its frame-
work: The IPTS can be viewed as the first ideation-to-action theory of suicide, and

-- 462 of 498 --

442 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
it has inspired and informed a new generation of suicide theories (Klonsky, Saffer,
& Bryan, 2018).
The Integrated Motivational–Volitional Model
The integrated motivational–volitional theory (IMV; O’Connor, 2011) represents the
second ideation-to-action theory. The IMV has a similar structure to the IPTS. It pos-
tulates that defeat and entrapment are the primary variables that motivate suicidal
desire, and lists a separate set of variables (acquired capability for suicide, access
to lethal means, planning, and impulsivity, among others) that predict and explain
the progression from ideation to attempts. Only a handful of studies examining the
theory have been conducted, though support for the theory’s tenets is promising
(Wetherall, Cleare, Zortea, & O’Connor, 2020).
The Three‑Step Theory of Suicide
The three-step theory (3ST) is the most recent ideation-to-action theory of suicide
(Klonsky & May, 2015b). The 3ST is a parsimonious theory that explains suicide in
terms of just four variables: pain, hopelessness, connectedness, and suicide capabil-
ity. Step 1 of the theory postulates that suicidal desire is caused by the combination
of pain and hopelessness. The 3ST’s emphasis on pain is based on the behavioral
principle of punishment: In short, people avoid doing things that are punished (i.e.,
paired with aversive outcomes). Thus, someone who experiences life as aversive/mis-
erable/painful is being punished for engaging with life and will want to escape their
current life circumstances. If one has hope that the pain can be stopped through
effort or time, their aim will be to achieve a better, less painful future. However, if
one does not have this hope, a desire to escape current life circumstances becomes a
desire to stop living.
Step 2 of the 3ST states that desire for suicide escalates when pain exceeds or
overwhelms connectedness. Connectedness is what makes life worth living. Con-
nectedness includes connections to family, friends, communities, valued roles or
jobs, or any sense of meaning and purpose. As defined by the 3ST, connectedness is
a broad construct and includes other important constructs, such as loneliness, social
isolation, thwarted belongingness, meaning, and anything that influences one’s feel-
ing of connection to the world. Connectedness can make life worth living even in
the presence of pain. Thus, someone with both pain and hopelessness, but also con-
nectedness, may still experience occasional desire for suicide, but they will not have
high or persistent desire for suicide. In contrast, if pain gets so great as to exceed or
impede their connectedness, suicidal desire becomes strong.
Step 3 suggests that strong desire for suicide can only progress to a suicide
attempt if one has the capability to attempt suicide. The 3ST agrees with the IPTS that
capability for suicide can be acquired over time and experience, but it also identifies
two additional contributors to suicide capability: dispositional contributors, such as
a genetically high threshold for pain or low fear of death, and practical contribu-
tors, such as knowledge of, expertise in, or access to lethal means. Practical capabil-
ity may be particularly important and changeable. For example, an Internet search
can quickly teach an adolescent about lethal over-the-counter medications that are

-- 463 of 498 --

Suicide 	443
readily available; this knowledge would represent a fast and meaningful increase
in suicide capability. Conversely, someone with suicidal desire who owns a firearm
could temporarily give the firearm or ammunition to a friend, thus drastically reduc-
ing practical capability.
In short, the 3ST explains suicide in terms of just four factors: pain, hopelessness,
connectedness, and capability. Evidence for the 3ST is growing rapidly and includes
studies indicating that pain and hopelessness are the most common motivations
for suicide (Klonsky & May, 2013; May et al., 2016, 2020), direct tests of the theory’s
three steps across three countries (Dhingra, Klonsky, & Tapola, 2019; Klonsky & May,
2015b; Yang, Liu, Chen, & Li, 2019), and studies that indicate pain and hopelessness
are the most common proximal antecedents of suicide attempts and death (Winter-
steen, 2014). The 3ST also suggests clear targets for treatment: Any intervention or
prevention method will reduce suicide risk to the extent it can (1) decrease pain, (2)
increase hope, (3) enhance connectedness, and/or (4) reduce capability for suicide.
At the same time, because the theory does not specify or limit the possible contribu-
tors to pain, hopelessness, connectedness, and suicide capability, it can account for
tremendous individual variability (the stories of pain, hopelessness, connection, and
capability will be different for an 84-year-old widow and 13-year-old boy), while still
explaining suicide risk and treatment in terms of four variables. The theory is also
consistent with a wide variety of biological, psychological, and sociological risk fac-
tors for suicide, in that any variable influencing pain, hopelessness, connectedness,
or suicide capability would be expected to influence suicide risk. Thus, the 3ST is a
parsimonious theory of suicide that is evidence-based, concise, and clinically useful,
yet adaptable to suicide risk across diverse people and contexts.
TREATMENT
Several decades of research support a handful of treatments and interventions
that reliably and consistently reduce suicidal thoughts and behaviors. Two treat-
ment packages—dialectical behavior therapy (DBT; Linehan, 2018) and cognitive-
behavioral therapy for suicide prevention (CBT-SP; Bryan & Rudd, 2018a; Wenzel,
Beck, & Brown, 2009)—have been shown in multiple randomized clinical trials and
multiple correlational studies to significantly reduce suicide attempts as compared
to treatment as usual (Brown, Ten Have, et al., 2005; Rudd et al., 2015; Sinyor et al.,
2020). One treatment package—the collaborative assessment and management of sui-
cidality (CAMS; Jobes, 2016)—has been shown in multiple randomized clinical trials
and multiple correlational studies to significantly reduce suicide ideation faster than
treatment as usual (Comtois et al., 2011; Ellis, Green, Allen, Jobes, & Nadorff, 2012;
Jobes, Wong, Conrad, Drozd, & Neal-Walden, 2005; Jobes et al., 2017). A procedure
extracted from DBT and CBT-SP called the crisis response plan (CRP) was shown in
one randomized clinical trial to significantly reduce suicide attempts and suicide
ideation as compared to treatment as usual (Bryan et al., 2017). The safety planning
intervention (SPI), which is similar in design to the CRP, was shown in two quasi-
experimental studies to reduce suicidal behaviors as compared to treatment as usual
(Miller et al., 2017; Stanley et al., 2018). Finally, mentalization-based psychotherapy
(MBP; Bateman & Fonagy, 2010) and the Attempted Suicide Short Intervention

-- 464 of 498 --

444 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
Program (ASSIP; Gysin-Maillart, Schwab, Soravia, Megert, & Michel, 2016) have each
been shown in one randomized clinical trial to significantly reduce suicide attempts
as compared to treatment as usual.
The results of multiple meta-analyses have shown that DBT and CBT-SP are
associated with significantly reduced rates of suicide attempts as compared to other
psychological treatments (DeCou, Comtois, & Landes, 2019; Hetrick, Robinson, Spit-
tal, & Carter, 2016; Kliem, Kröger, & Kosfelder, 2010; Panos, Jackson, Hasan, & Panos,
2014; Tarrier, Taylor, & Gooding, 2008). Although rates of reduction vary across stud-
ies, on average, patients receiving one of these therapies are around 50% less likely to
make a suicide attempt during or soon after treatment than patients receiving other
treatments. Although these treatments have been shown to reduce rates of suicide
attempts, reductions in suicide mortality have not yet been observed in these—or any
other—psychological treatments due to low base rates, which would require very
large sample sizes. A recent simulation study using original clinical trial data from
Rudd and colleagues’ (2015) study of brief cognitive-behavioral therapy for suicide
prevention (BCBT)—one particular type of CBT-SP—suggests that reductions in sui-
cide mortality among individuals receiving this treatment are probable, however
(Bernecker et al., 2019). In that study, researchers estimated that one to three suicide
deaths could be averted for every 100 suicidal patients receiving BCBT instead of
traditional mental health treatments. Results of a recent meta-analysis further sug-
gest that suicide-focused treatments in general are likely associated with statistically
significant reductions in suicide mortality (Hofstra et al., 2020), although we do not
yet know if any particular type of suicide-focused treatment is more or less effective
for reducing suicide mortality.
Principles of Change
DBT and CBT-SP overlap considerably with respect to their underlying conceptual
model and constituent components. Both treatments conceptualize suicide as a cop-
ing strategy for reducing or avoiding intense psychological pain, and both focus on
teaching patients how to identify, develop, and use self-regulatory skills (e.g., mind-
fulness, relaxation, cognitive restructuring) as alternatives to suicidal and other
self-injurious behaviors. Skills training is one of six principles of change that are
believed to underlie the effectiveness of these suicide-focused treatments (Bryan &
Rudd, 2018a; see Table 15.1). Newer research further suggests that therapeutic alli-
ance is correlated with better outcomes (i.e., larger reductions in suicide ideation,
lower suicide attempt rates) within suicide-focused treatments but does not appear
to be correlated with suicide-related outcomes in traditional mental health treat-
ments (Bedics, Atkins, Harned, & Linehan, 2015; Bryan, Rozek, Burch, et al., 2018),
suggesting that both interpersonal process and technique are important for reducing
suicide risk.
The primary differences between DBT and CBT-SP involve complexity and dura-
tion. DBT typically lasts for 6–12 months and involves a multimodal package that
includes a combination of individual therapy, group skills training classes, between-
session phone-based crisis support services, and clinician consultation. CBT-SP, by
contrast, typically lasts no longer than 3 months and involves individual therapy

-- 465 of 498 --

Suicide 	445
only. Group-based models of CBT-SP have recently been developed and pilot-tested
(Sinyor et al., 2020), but they have not yet been subjected to rigorous empirical exami-
nation. The CRP, MBP, and ASSIP were also associated with large reductions in sui-
cide attempts in each of their respective clinical trials, but these findings have not
yet been replicated.
Although DBT and CBT-SP consistently outperform comparison treatments with
respect to reducing suicide attempts, these treatments do not necessarily outperform
other treatments with respect to reducing suicide ideation and psychological symp-
tom severity. Patients who receive DBT and CBT-SP report significant reductions on
all of these variables, but so do patients who receive traditional mental health treat-
ments. The same pattern was reported in the MBP and ASSIP trials as well. In con-
trast, multiple CAMS trials have found the opposite pattern: Patients who receive
CAMS report significantly faster and larger reductions in suicide ideation and psy-
chological symptom severity than patients who receive other treatments, but rates of
suicide attempts across CAMS and other treatments are typically comparable. CAMS
therefore outperforms comparison treatments with respect to reducing suicide ide-
ation and emotional distress, but it does not necessarily reduce the probability of
TABLE 15.1. Six Central Principles of Change within Empirically Supported Suicide‑Focused Treatments
Change principle 	Description
Easy-to-understand model
of suicide
Effective treatments use simple case conceptualization models
that provides a platform for patients to understand how their
experiences, thoughts, feelings, and emotions interact with one
another and contribute to their decisions and behaviors. The
interventions and methods used in treatment should be based
upon this model.
Clinician fidelity 	Effective treatments outline certain procedures and methods
that are known to decrease the probability of suicidal behaviors.
Reliably administering these procedures and methods increases
the probability of reducing suicidal behaviors.
Patient adherence 	Effective treatments increase patient engagement and adherence
by addressing motivational factors and barriers to treatment.
Skills training 	Effective treatments provide opportunities to demonstrate and
practice specific activities designed to strengthen a patient’s
ability to respond more effectively to life stressors. Effective
treatments allow clinicians and patients to customize these skills
to the unique needs and circumstances of each patient.
Patient responsibility 	Effective treatments emphasize shared responsibility for decision
making and clinical outcomes across clinicians and patients,
rather than consolidating responsibility for decision making and
outcomes within clinicians.
Easy access to crisis services 	Effective treatments clearly delineate the steps to be followed
during acute crises and emergencies.

-- 466 of 498 --

446 	P s y c h o P a t h o l o g y
suicidal behaviors. Taken together, these results suggest that the mechanisms under-
lying reductions in suicide attempts are not the same mechanisms that underlie
reductions in suicide ideation and psychological symptoms.
The mechanisms that directly impact reductions in suicidal behaviors across
various treatments are as-yet unknown, but researchers have hypothesized a cen-
tral role for self-regulatory processes, notably, emotion regulation and cognitive
flexibility (Bryan & Rozek, 2018). According to this perspective, treatments that
reduce suicidal behaviors strengthen cognitive control processes involved in deci-
sion making, especially during stressful and/or uncertain situations. As cognitive
control strengthens, individuals become less sensitized to life stressors, such that
negative emotional reactions to these circumstances are less pronounced. Individu-
als also learn how to inhibit avoidance-based behavioral responses such as suicide
attempts. In other words, patients learn to “hit the brakes” before acting on intense
negative emotions and emotional pain, which affords them enough time to consider
and select alternative response patterns.
The inverse patterns observed across CAMS and DBT and CBT-SP may be
attributable to differences in their constituent components. Whereas DBT and CBT-
SP prescribe specific procedures and techniques designed to enhance patients’ self-
monitoring and self-regulatory skills, CAMS does not require or expect clinicians to
use any particular combination of techniques or procedures. CAMS instead encour-
ages clinicians to collaboratively identify and target each patient’s unique “driv-
ers” of suicide risk, then choose the procedures and techniques that they believe
will reduce these drivers. Because CAMS allows for more variability in treatment
planning than DBT and CBT-SP, clinicians may not always use procedures and tech-
niques that build or promote self-monitoring and self-regulatory skills. CAMS may,
however, encourage clinicians to use procedures and techniques that target mecha-
nisms that are more directly related to suicide ideation and emotional distress. This
does not mean that DBT and CBT-SP are overly rigid or inflexible, however. Both
treatments allow clinicians to tailor the specified procedures to the unique needs
of each patient, thereby striking a balance between the reliable administration of
empirically supported procedures and the need to address patient-specific issues.
Research focused on identifying and isolating the underlying mechanisms
of action in suicide-focused treatments is currently under way. If successful, such
research could not only lead to important advances in treatment development and
refinement but also improve our understanding of why some people attempt suicide
and others choose not to make an attempt despite experiencing significant emotional
pain and adversity.
CONCLUSION
Suicide results from complex interactions among multiple risk and protective factors,
and it affects a wide range of communities and groups. Although the science of sui-
cide prevention is relatively “young” as compared to research focused on other clini-
cal psychological and behavioral conditions, theoretical and conceptual advances
have fueled rapid growth of suicide-focused studies during the past few decades,

-- 467 of 498 --

Suicide 	447
leading to a number of critical advances in our understanding of suicide’s phenom-
enology, etiology, and treatment. Of note, researchers and theorists are coming to
better understand not only who develops suicidal ideation but also the contexts in
which ideation is most likely to progress to potentially lethal suicide attempts. In
addition, converging evidence supports the efficacy of certain types of interventions
and treatments for reducing rates of suicidal behaviors (DBT and CBT-SP) and the
intensity of suicidal thinking (CAMS). Additional treatment modalities have also
shown promise, although they have not yet been sufficiently researched. Although
some of these treatments are relatively simple and brief in duration, they are not yet
widely used by clinicians. We hope that this chapter will encourage clinicians to
learn more about these treatments and to seek out training to use them effectively.
We are also optimistic that ongoing and future research will uncover new strategies
and methods that complement existing treatments, thereby providing more options
for clinicians and those struggling with suicidal thoughts and urges.
